coreConcept: Embryological origin of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle
statement: A researcher is studying the embryonic origins of different muscle types. Which of the following correctly matches the muscle type with its primary embryological source?
options: Skeletal muscle — Lateral plate mesoderm
Cardiac muscle — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Smooth muscle of the gut — Ectoderm
Skeletal muscle of the head — Neural crest cells
Cardiac muscle — Paraxial mesoderm
correctOption: Cardiac muscle — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle is primarily derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres, rather than the lateral plate mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the abaxial domain but is not the primary source of all skeletal muscle. This distinction is critical for understanding the segmental nature of the muscular system.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm that surrounds the endothelial heart tube during early development. These cells differentiate into myoblasts that eventually form the myocardium. Unlike skeletal muscle, these cells do not fuse but remain connected via intercalated discs.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and its derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the ectoderm. Ectoderm only contributes to specific smooth muscles like the pupillary, mammary, and sweat gland muscles. Most visceral smooth muscle follows the splanchnic mesodermal lineage.

While neural crest cells contribute to some structures in the head, the skeletal musculature of the head is derived from somitomeres, which are part of the paraxial mesoderm. Neural crest cells primarily contribute to the smooth muscle of large arteries and certain connective tissues. Skeletal muscle consistently follows the paraxial mesoderm lineage.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source for skeletal muscles of the axial skeleton, body wall, and limbs. The heart's development is distinct from the segmental somitic development of the body wall.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube.

coreConcept: Somite differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome
statement: During the differentiation of a somite, which specific region undergoes an epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition to eventually form the vertebrae and ribs?
options: Dorsomedial lip (DML)
Ventrolateral lip (VLL)
Dermatome
Sclerotome
Myotome
correctOption: Sclerotome
optionExplanations: The dorsomedial lip (DML) is one of the two muscle-forming areas in the upper region of the somite. It contributes to the formation of the dermomyotome and eventually the primaxial muscle precursors. It does not form the bone-forming cells of the vertebrae.

The ventrolateral lip (VLL) is a muscle-forming area that contributes to both primaxial and abaxial muscle precursors. While it is essential for limb and body wall muscle development, it does not differentiate into the sclerotome. Its primary fate is myogenic rather than osteogenic.

The dermatome is the part of the upper somite that contributes to the dermis of the skin. It forms alongside the muscle-forming lips (VLL and DML) to create the dermomyotome. It does not participate in the formation of the axial skeleton.

The sclerotome is formed when the ventral region of the somite becomes mesenchymal again. These cells are the precursors for the vertebrae and ribs, providing the structural framework for the axial skeleton. This transition is a key step in somite maturation.

The myotome is the muscle-forming component of the somite derived from the VLL and DML regions. It gives rise to the skeletal muscles of the back and body wall. It is distinct from the sclerotome, which forms the bony elements.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and mesodermal domains
statement: The lateral somitic frontier is a critical developmental boundary. Which of the following best describes the composition of the abaxial domain?
options: Only somite-derived paraxial mesoderm cells
Only parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm cells
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Neural tube-derived cells and dorsomedial lip precursors
correctOption: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain, contains only somite-derived paraxial mesoderm cells. These cells remain in the region surrounding the neural tube and notochord. They do not cross the lateral somitic frontier.

While the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is a major component of the abaxial domain, it is not the only component. The domain also includes muscle precursor cells that have migrated from the somites. Therefore, this option is incomplete.

The abaxial domain is defined by the combination of the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm and the somite cells (from the VLL) that have crossed the lateral somitic frontier. This domain gives rise to muscles of the limbs and the infrahyoid and abdominal wall muscles. It is distinct from the primaxial domain which is purely somitic.

The visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm contributes to smooth muscle of the gut and cardiac muscle, not the abaxial skeletal muscle domain. Neural crest cells contribute to smooth muscle in large arteries but are not part of the abaxial skeletal muscle precursor population. This option confuses different mesodermal layers.

The neural tube provides signals to the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. The dorsomedial lip (DML) precursors remain in the primaxial domain and do not cross the frontier. This option incorrectly associates primaxial components with the abaxial domain.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

coreConcept: Innervation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles
statement: Regardless of whether a muscle belongs to the primaxial or abaxial domain, its innervation is determined by its relationship to the axis. Which nerve branches supply the hypaxial muscles of the body wall?
options: Dorsal primary rami
Ventral primary rami
Cranial nerves only
Sympathetic trunk ganglia
Splanchnic nerves
correctOption: Ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back. These muscles are located 'above the axis' in the traditional functional classification. They do not supply the body wall or limb muscles.

Ventral primary rami innervate the hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the body wall (like the abdominals) and the limbs. This functional definition of innervation remains consistent even with the newer embryological classification of primaxial and abaxial domains. Most skeletal muscles of the trunk and limbs fall into this category.

Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the somitomeres in the head region, such as the muscles of facial expression or mastication. They do not provide the segmental innervation for the hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs. Body wall innervation is strictly spinal.

Sympathetic trunk ganglia are part of the autonomic nervous system and provide visceral motor innervation to smooth muscle, glands, and the heart. They do not provide motor innervation to skeletal muscles like those of the body wall. Skeletal muscle requires somatic motor innervation.

Splanchnic nerves carry autonomic fibers to the viscera in the thoracic and abdominal cavities. They are not involved in the somatic innervation of the skeletal muscles of the body wall. The body wall is supplied by the somatic components of the spinal nerves.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami

statement: Which of the following is a unique histological feature of developing cardiac muscle that distinguishes it from skeletal muscle development?
options: Formation of multinucleated syncytia
Absence of myofibrils in the cytoplasm
Myoblasts do not fuse but remain connected by intercalated discs
Origin from the paraxial mesoderm
Development from the ectodermal germ layer
correctOption: Myoblasts do not fuse but remain connected by intercalated discs
optionExplanations: The formation of multinucleated syncytia is a hallmark of skeletal muscle development, where myoblasts fuse together. In contrast, cardiac myoblasts do not fuse. This fusion in skeletal muscle allows for the creation of long, continuous fibers.

Myofibrils develop in both cardiac and skeletal muscle to provide the contractile machinery. Their absence would be a pathological state or a very early undifferentiated stage, not a distinguishing feature of cardiac muscle development. Both types eventually show cross-striations.

In cardiac muscle development, myoblasts adhere to each other via special attachments that become intercalated discs, but they do not fuse into a single multinucleated cell. This allows the heart to function as a functional syncytium while maintaining individual cellular integrity. This is a key difference from the anatomical syncytium of skeletal muscle.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm, whereas skeletal muscle originates from the paraxial mesoderm. This different embryological origin is fundamental to their distinct developmental pathways. Paraxial mesoderm is reserved for the somitic lineages.

Cardiac muscle is mesodermal in origin, specifically from the visceral layer of the lateral plate. Ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system, skin epidermis, and a few specific smooth muscles, but never to cardiac muscle. This is a basic germ layer distinction.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse.

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal, which of the following muscles is an exception and originates from the ectoderm?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Dilator pupillae muscle
Smooth muscle of the jejunum
Coronary artery smooth muscle
Detrusor muscle of the bladder
correctOption: Dilator pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta is derived from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. It is not an ectodermal derivative. This muscle provides the structural integrity and contractility required for the body's largest artery.

The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are rare examples of muscles derived from the ectoderm. Other examples include the smooth muscle in mammary glands and sweat glands. This is an exception to the general rule that muscles are mesodermal.

The smooth muscle of the jejunum, like the rest of the gut wall, is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. It surrounds the endodermal lining of the gut. It is not related to the ectodermal lineage.

Smooth muscle in the coronary arteries originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. It is not derived from the ectoderm. These cells migrate to the heart to form the vascular components of the cardiac wall.

The detrusor muscle of the bladder is a smooth muscle derived from the splanchnic mesoderm associated with the urogenital system. It follows the standard mesodermal pattern for internal organ musculature. It does not have an ectodermal origin.
citation: Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm. Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm

coreConcept: Resegmentation and its effect on myotomes
statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation affect the functional arrangement of the myotomes?
options: It causes myotomes to fuse into a single continuous muscle mass
It forces myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs
It results in the loss of segmental innervation
It separates the myotome from the overlying dermatome
It converts skeletal muscle into smooth muscle
correctOption: It forces myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs
optionExplanations: Myotomes do not fuse into a single mass; they maintain a segmental arrangement, although their boundaries change relative to the bones. This segmental nature is essential for the coordinated movement of the vertebral column. Fusion would prevent localized spinal flexion.

Resegmentation of the sclerotomes (where the caudal half of one somite fuses with the cranial half of the next) ensures that the resulting vertebrae are offset from the original somite levels. This causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, allowing them to exert force across the joints and move the spine. Without this, muscles would only attach within a single bone.

Segmental innervation is strictly maintained throughout development; each myotome is supplied by the spinal nerve of its original segment. Resegmentation of the bone does not alter the nerve-muscle relationship. The nerve simply follows the muscle to its new position.

While the dermatome and myotome do eventually separate to form skin and muscle respectively, this is not the primary functional consequence of sclerotome resegmentation. Resegmentation specifically concerns the relationship between the muscles and the developing bony axis. The dermomyotome splits earlier.

Skeletal muscle and smooth muscle have entirely different lineages and developmental pathways. Resegmentation is a morphogenetic process affecting the spatial arrangement of tissues, not a change in cell type or histological identity. Skeletal muscle remains skeletal muscle.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C).

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in the vascular system
statement: Which cell populations contribute to the smooth muscle found in the proximal segments of the coronary arteries?
options: Only paraxial mesoderm
Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
Sclerotome and dermatome cells
Endoderm and visceral mesoderm
Ectoderm and somitomeres
correctOption: Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle, not the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries. The vascular system's smooth muscle has a more complex origin involving lateral plate and other migratory populations. Paraxial mesoderm stays closer to the axis.

The smooth muscle of the coronary arteries has a dual origin: proepicardial cells and neural crest cells (specifically for the proximal segments). This highlights the diverse cellular contributions required for cardiac vascularization. Neural crest cells are particularly important for the outflow tract and proximal vessels.

Sclerotome and dermatome are components of the somite that form bone and dermis/muscle, respectively. They do not contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries. Their developmental fate is restricted to the body wall and axial skeleton.

While visceral mesoderm contributes to the smooth muscle of the gut and the myocardium, the specific smooth muscle of the coronary arteries involves proepicardial and neural crest contributions. Endoderm provides the lining for the gut, not the muscle of the heart's vessels.

Ectoderm contributes to pupillary and sweat gland muscles, and somitomeres form head skeletal muscles. Neither is involved in the formation of coronary artery smooth muscle. The coronary vasculature is derived from cells associated with the heart's outer layers and the neural crest.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments).

coreConcept: Primaxial vs. Abaxial signaling and precursors
statement: A group of muscle precursor cells remains in the paraxial mesoderm and does not cross the lateral somitic frontier. From which structures will these primaxial cells primarily receive their differentiation signals?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Ectoderm and endoderm
Proepicardial organ
Parietal mesoderm only
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals to the abaxial muscle precursors that have crossed the lateral somitic frontier. It does not signal the primaxial cells that remain near the axis. This spatial separation of signaling environments is a key developmental principle.

Primaxial muscle precursors, which include all DML cells and some VLL cells, remain in the paraxial mesoderm. They are positioned close to the midline and thus receive their primary developmental cues from the neural tube and the notochord. These signals direct them to become the muscles of the back and shoulder girdle.

While the ectoderm and endoderm provide signals for various other tissues, the specific differentiation of primaxial muscle precursors is driven by the axial structures (neural tube/notochord). Ectoderm is more involved in signaling the dermatome. Endoderm signals the ventral somite/sclerotome.

The proepicardial organ is involved in the development of the heart's epicardium and coronary smooth muscle. It has no role in signaling the skeletal muscle precursors of the somites. It is located far from the paraxial mesoderm domain.

Parietal mesoderm is part of the lateral plate mesoderm and is the environment for the abaxial domain. Primaxial cells, by definition, do not enter this environment and therefore do not rely on its signals for their initial differentiation. They stay within the somitic environment.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord

statement: At what point during human embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
End of the sixth month
At birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: By the end of the first month, somites are still forming and differentiating into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblasts have not yet fused into the mature fibers required for striations to be visible. This is too early in the myogenic process.

During the second month, myoblasts are actively migrating and beginning to fuse into multinucleated fibers. While myofibrils are starting to appear in the cytoplasm, the organized sarcomeric structure that produces visible cross-striations is not yet fully established. The muscle is still very immature.

Cross-striations, which reflect the highly organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments into sarcomeres, typically appear by the end of the third month of development. This follows the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers and the synthesis of contractile proteins. It marks a significant stage in functional maturation.

The end of the sixth month is well into the fetal period, and skeletal muscles are already well-developed and striated by this time. Striations appear much earlier than this to allow for the fetal movements that occur in the second trimester. This timing is too late.

Skeletal muscle is fully striated and functional long before birth. Fetal movements, which require striated muscle, are a key indicator of health during pregnancy and begin months before delivery. Waiting until birth for striations would be incompatible with normal development.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear.

coreConcept: The old concept of epimeres and hypomeres
statement: The traditional classification of muscles into 'epimeres' and 'hypomeres' was primarily based on which of the following criteria?
options: The embryological origin of the cells
The functional definition of innervation
The presence or absence of intercalated discs
The type of mesoderm (paraxial vs. lateral plate)
The timing of myoblast fusion
correctOption: The functional definition of innervation
optionExplanations: The embryological origin of the cells (primaxial vs. abaxial) is the basis for the *new* description of muscle development, not the old one. The new system focuses on whether cells stay in the paraxial domain or migrate into the lateral plate. This distinction is more accurate for lineage tracing.

The old concept of epimeres and hypomeres was based on a functional definition of innervation. Epimeres were defined as muscles innervated by dorsal primary rami (back muscles), and hypomeres were those innervated by ventral primary rami (limb and body wall). This system is now considered less embryologically precise.

Intercalated discs are a feature of cardiac muscle, not skeletal muscle. The epimere/hypomere classification applies specifically to the skeletal muscles derived from the myotomes. It has no relevance to cardiac histology.

The distinction between paraxial and lateral plate mesoderm is the foundation of the modern primaxial/abaxial classification. The old epimere/hypomere system did not account for the migration of somitic cells into the lateral plate mesoderm. It focused on the final nerve supply instead.

The timing of myoblast fusion is a general feature of skeletal muscle development and does not vary between epimeric and hypomeric muscles. Both groups form multinucleated fibers through fusion. This was never a criterion for their classification.
citation: The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles), which was based on a functional de fi nition of innervation: Epimeric muscles were innervated by dorsal primary rami, and hypomeric muscles by ventral primary rami.

coreConcept: Innervation of myotomes by spinal nerves
statement: Which of the following statements regarding the innervation of developing myotomes is correct?
options: Myotomes are innervated by nerves from distant segments
Innervation is determined by the final location of the muscle only
Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its own segment
Abaxial muscles lose their original segmental innervation
Primaxial muscles are innervated by cranial nerves
correctOption: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its own segment
optionExplanations: Myotomes are not innervated by distant segments; they maintain a strict relationship with the spinal nerves of their segment of origin. This segmental pattern is a fundamental feature of vertebrate body organization. Even when muscles migrate, they 'drag' their nerve with them.

While the final location of a muscle determines which branch of the nerve (dorsal or ventral ramus) reaches it, the identity of the nerve itself is determined by the muscle's segment of origin. Innervation is established early and remains constant. This is why the diaphragm is innervated by cervical nerves.

Each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. This segmental relationship is maintained regardless of whether the muscle cells belong to the primaxial or abaxial domain. It is one of the most reliable rules in musculoskeletal embryology.

Abaxial muscles, such as those in the limbs, migrate significantly from their origin, but they do not lose their original segmental innervation. They are supplied by the ventral rami of the spinal nerves corresponding to the somites from which they originated. This is the basis for dermatome and myotome maps.

Primaxial muscles of the trunk are innervated by spinal nerves, not cranial nerves. Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the somitomeres in the head. Primaxial muscles include the deep back muscles and intercostals, which are clearly spinal-supplied.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description is based on the actual embryological origin of muscle cells from two different populations of muscle cell precursors, the abaxial and primaxial cells, and not their innervation.

statement: The dermomyotome is formed by the migration and proliferation of cells from which two specific areas of the somite?
options: Sclerotome and notochord
Ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips
Neural tube and parietal mesoderm
Visceral mesoderm and endoderm
Occipital and sacral somitomeres
correctOption: Ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips
optionExplanations: The sclerotome forms the vertebrae and ribs, and the notochord provides signaling but does not contribute cells to the dermomyotome. These structures are located ventrally or axially relative to the muscle-forming regions. They are not part of the dermomyotome itself.

The dermomyotome is formed when cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips of the upper somite migrate and proliferate. These cells form a layer of progenitor muscle cells (the myotome) just ventral to the dermatome. Together, these layers constitute the dermomyotome.

The neural tube and parietal mesoderm are external to the somite itself. While they provide essential signaling for differentiation, they do not provide the cellular material for the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is an intrinsic somitic structure.

Visceral mesoderm and endoderm are involved in the development of internal organs and the gut, not the skeletal muscles of the body wall and back. The dermomyotome is a product of the paraxial mesoderm. This option confuses different embryonic layers.

Occipital and sacral somitomeres/somites represent the longitudinal extent of the paraxial mesoderm, but they are not the specific sub-regions within a single somite that form the dermomyotome. The VLL and DML are the specific intra-somitic areas responsible. This is a confusion of scale.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome

coreConcept: Embryological origin of the muscular system
statement: A researcher is studying the embryonic origins of various muscle types. Which of the following correctly matches the muscle type with its primary embryological source?
options: Skeletal muscle — Lateral plate mesoderm
Cardiac muscle — Paraxial mesoderm
Smooth muscle of the gut — Visceral mesoderm
Pupillary muscles — Somitic mesoderm
Limb muscles — Neural crest cells
correctOption: Smooth muscle of the gut — Visceral mesoderm
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle is primarily derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres, rather than the lateral plate mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the connective tissue of limbs but not the primary skeletal muscle fibers themselves. This distinction is fundamental to understanding the segmental nature of the musculoskeletal system.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm layer that surrounds the endothelial heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is dedicated to forming the axial skeleton and skeletal muscles. The cardiac progenitor cells migrate early in development to form the primary heart field.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gastrointestinal tract and its derivatives is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This layer surrounds the endodermal tube and differentiates into the muscularis externa and muscularis mucosae. This developmental pathway is distinct from the somitic origin of skeletal muscles.

The pupillary muscles (sphincter and dilator pupillae) are unique because they are derived from the ectoderm of the optic cup, not the somitic mesoderm. Most other muscles in the body have a mesodermal origin, making the pupillary, mammary, and sweat gland muscles notable exceptions. This ectodermal origin is shared with the neural tissues of the eye.

Limb muscles are derived from the ventrolateral cells of the somites (paraxial mesoderm) that migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. Neural crest cells contribute to many structures in the head and neck, including some smooth muscle in large arteries, but they do not form the skeletal muscles of the limbs. The migration of somitic cells is a key step in limb bud development.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

coreConcept: Somite differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome
statement: During the differentiation of a somite, the ventral region undergoes a transition from epithelial to mesenchymal tissue. What is the primary fate of the cells resulting from this specific ventral transformation?
options: Formation of the dermis of the back
Development of the vertebrae and ribs
Formation of the epaxial back muscles
Development of the abdominal wall muscles
Formation of the limb musculature
correctOption: Development of the vertebrae and ribs
optionExplanations: The dermis of the back is formed by the dermatome, which is located in the upper or dorsal region of the somite. The ventral region specifically transforms into the sclerotome, which has a different fate. The dermatome remains epithelial longer than the sclerotome before contributing to the skin.

The ventral region of the somite becomes mesenchymal to form the sclerotome, which consists of bone-forming cells. These cells migrate medially to surround the notochord and neural tube, eventually forming the vertebrae and the ribs. This process is essential for the segmental organization of the axial skeleton.

Epaxial back muscles are derived from the myotome, specifically from the dorsomedial and ventrolateral lips of the upper somite region. While the myotome is closely associated with the sclerotome, it arises from the dorsal part of the somite rather than the ventral mesenchymal transformation. These muscles will eventually be innervated by dorsal primary rami.

Abdominal wall muscles are derived from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the dermomyotome, which is part of the upper somite. These cells migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm to form the abaxial muscles. They do not arise from the ventral sclerotome population that forms bone.

Limb musculature originates from the ventrolateral cells of the dermomyotome that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. Like the abdominal muscles, they are part of the abaxial domain and are not derived from the ventral sclerotome. The sclerotome is strictly dedicated to the formation of axial skeletal elements.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center. The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome, the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome.

statement: A developmental biologist identifies a population of muscle precursor cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Which characteristic best describes these cells?
options: They are primaxial muscle cell precursors
They receive differentiation signals from the notochord
They will eventually form the intercostal muscles
They comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors
They are derived exclusively from the dorsomedial lip
correctOption: They comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors
optionExplanations: Primaxial muscle cell precursors are defined as those that remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. These cells stay in the region surrounding the neural tube. In contrast, the cells described in the scenario have crossed the frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm.

Cells that remain in the primaxial domain receive signals from the neural tube and notochord for their differentiation. Abaxial cells, having migrated into the lateral plate mesoderm, receive their primary differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm itself. This environmental change is crucial for their specific developmental fate.

Intercostal muscles are formed by cells that remain in the primaxial domain (the remaining VLL cells and DML cells). Although they are part of the body wall, they do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Therefore, they are not derived from the migrated abaxial population described.

The abaxial muscle cell precursors are specifically defined as the cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the myotome that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. Once they enter the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, they differentiate into muscles such as the infrahyoids, abdominal wall, and limbs. This domain is characterized by its unique signaling environment.

The cells that migrate to form the abaxial domain are derived from the ventrolateral lip (VLL), not the dorsomedial lip (DML). The DML cells remain entirely within the primaxial domain and contribute to the muscles of the back. The VLL has a dual fate, with some cells migrating and others staying behind.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm; those that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors.

coreConcept: Innervation of Epaxial and Hypaxial Muscles
statement: Despite the modern classification of muscle development into primaxial and abaxial domains, the classical functional definition of innervation remains valid. Which of the following correctly describes the innervation of the hypaxial muscles?
options: Dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves
Ventral primary rami of spinal nerves
Cranial nerves from the brainstem
Autonomic fibers from the sympathetic trunk
Sensory fibers from the dorsal root ganglion only
correctOption: Ventral primary rami of spinal nerves
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the intrinsic muscles of the back. These muscles are located 'above the axis' and are derived from the primaxial domain. The hypaxial muscles, located 'below the axis,' are served by a different branch of the spinal nerve.

Hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the body wall (abdominal and intercostal) and the limbs, are innervated by the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves. This relationship is established early in development as the spinal nerves grow into the developing myotomes. This pattern persists regardless of whether the muscle belongs to the primaxial or abaxial embryological domain.

Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres and branchial arches, such as the muscles of facial expression or mastication. They do not provide the primary innervation for the hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs. The hypaxial muscles are strictly associated with the spinal cord segments.

Autonomic fibers from the sympathetic trunk provide motor innervation to smooth muscles, cardiac muscle, and glands, but they do not provide the voluntary motor innervation to skeletal muscles. Hypaxial muscles are skeletal muscles and require somatic motor innervation from the ventral rami. Autonomic fibers may travel with these nerves but do not innervate the muscle fibers themselves.

While hypaxial muscles do have sensory innervation from the dorsal root ganglion, they must also have motor innervation to function. The question asks for the primary innervation, which includes the motor component found in the ventral primary rami. A 'sensory only' description is incomplete and incorrect for muscle function.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami. Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment.

statement: A histology student is comparing the development of skeletal and cardiac muscle. Which developmental feature is unique to cardiac muscle compared to skeletal muscle?
options: The presence of myofibrils in the cytoplasm
The formation of long, multinucleated fibers via fusion
The development of intercalated discs between myoblasts
The origin from the paraxial mesoderm
The appearance of cross-striations by the third month
correctOption: The development of intercalated discs between myoblasts
optionExplanations: The presence of myofibrils is a common feature of both skeletal and cardiac muscle, as they are both types of striated muscle. Myofibrils are the contractile units composed of actin and myosin filaments. Therefore, this feature is not unique to cardiac muscle development.

The formation of long, multinucleated fibers through the fusion of myoblasts is a hallmark of skeletal muscle development, not cardiac muscle. In cardiac muscle, myoblasts adhere to each other but do not fuse into a syncytium. This lack of fusion allows for the individual cellular nature of cardiomyocytes.

Intercalated discs are unique to cardiac muscle and develop from special attachments where myoblasts adhere to one another. These structures contain gap junctions and desmosomes that allow for electrical and mechanical coupling between cells. This is a critical distinction from skeletal muscle, where cells fuse rather than maintaining these specialized junctions.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, whereas skeletal muscle originates from the paraxial mesoderm (somites). This difference in embryological origin is a fundamental distinction between the two tissue types. Paraxial mesoderm does not contribute to the myocardium.

Both skeletal and cardiac muscle exhibit cross-striations due to the organized arrangement of sarcomeres within their myofibrils. In skeletal muscle, these striations are clearly visible by the end of the third month of development. Since both types are striated, this is not a unique feature of cardiac muscle.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myofibrils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible.

statement: Smooth muscle has diverse embryological origins depending on its anatomical location. Which of the following structures contains smooth muscle derived, in part, from neural crest cells?
options: The wall of the small intestine
The muscularis of the urinary bladder
The proximal segments of the coronary arteries
The myometrium of the uterus
The sphincter pupillae muscle
correctOption: The proximal segments of the coronary arteries
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle in the wall of the small intestine is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This layer surrounds the endodermal gut tube during development. Neural crest cells contribute to the enteric nervous system within the gut wall, but not the smooth muscle itself.

The muscularis of the urinary bladder, like most of the internal organs, is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Neural crest cells do not contribute to the smooth muscle of the pelvic viscera. Their contribution to the vascular system is limited to specific cranial and cardiac outflow regions.

Smooth muscle in the proximal segments of the coronary arteries is derived from both proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. This dual origin is characteristic of the great vessels and their immediate branches near the heart. This highlights the significant role of neural crest cells in cardiovascular development.

The myometrium of the uterus is derived from the mesoderm associated with the paramesonephric ducts, which is a derivative of the lateral plate mesoderm. Neural crest cells are not involved in the formation of the smooth muscle of the female reproductive tract. Their migration patterns do not typically extend to these pelvic structures.

The sphincter pupillae muscle is derived from the ectoderm of the optic cup, not neural crest cells. While neural crest cells contribute to many structures in the eye (like the stroma of the iris), the actual contractile smooth muscle of the pupil is one of the few examples of an ectodermal muscle. This makes it a unique exception in muscle development.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm.

statement: What is the significance of the lateral somitic frontier in the development of the musculoskeletal system?
options: It marks the boundary between the ectoderm and the mesoderm
It separates the sclerotome from the overlying dermatome
It defines the border between somite-derived cells and the lateral plate mesoderm
It is the site where the neural tube closes during neurulation
It separates the epaxial muscles from the hypaxial muscles based on innervation
correctOption: It defines the border between somite-derived cells and the lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The lateral somitic frontier is an intra-mesodermal boundary, not a boundary between different germ layers like ectoderm and mesoderm. It specifically exists within the mesodermal layer to organize different populations of muscle precursors. Ectoderm-mesoderm boundaries are established much earlier during gastrulation.

The separation of the sclerotome and dermatome occurs within the somite itself, with the sclerotome being ventral and the dermatome being dorsal. The lateral somitic frontier is located lateral to the somite, separating the entire somitic domain from the lateral plate mesoderm. It is not an internal somite divider.

The lateral somitic frontier is the well-defined border that separates the primaxial domain (containing only somite-derived cells) from the abaxial domain (containing lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells). This frontier is crucial because it determines the signaling environment and the eventual fate of the muscle precursors. It represents a fundamental shift in how we understand muscle patterning.

The closure of the neural tube occurs at the midline of the embryo during the process of neurulation. The lateral somitic frontier is located much further laterally, at the junction between the paraxial and lateral plate mesoderm. These are two distinct topographical and developmental events.

While the frontier helps define muscle groups, the separation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles is traditionally based on innervation (dorsal vs. ventral rami). The frontier defines domains based on embryological origin (primaxial vs. abaxial). Although there is overlap, the frontier itself is a physical and developmental boundary, not a functional innervation line.
citation: Initially, there is a well-defined border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier. This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells.

statement: Which principle governs the initial innervation of developing muscle cells from the myotome?
options: Muscles are innervated by the nearest cranial nerve
Innervation is determined by the final adult position of the muscle
Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its own segment
Abaxial muscles lose their original segmental innervation after migration
Innervation is provided by the lateral plate mesoderm signals
correctOption: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its own segment
optionExplanations: Cranial nerves only innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres and pharyngeal arches. The muscles derived from the somites (occipital to sacral) are innervated by spinal nerves. Therefore, cranial nerve innervation is not a general principle for the myotomes of the body.

Innervation is established early in development based on the segmental origin of the muscle cells, not their final adult position. Even if a muscle migrates a long distance (like the diaphragm or limb muscles), it carries its original segmental nerve supply with it. This is why the phrenic nerve originates in the neck but travels to the thorax.

A fundamental rule of muscle development is that each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. This segmental relationship is maintained throughout development, regardless of whether the cells belong to the primaxial or abaxial domains. This allows clinicians to use muscle testing to localize spinal cord lesions.

Abaxial muscles, such as those in the limbs or abdominal wall, migrate extensively, but they do not lose their original segmental innervation. They 'drag' their nerves with them as they move into the lateral plate mesoderm. This preservation of segmental nerve supply is a key feature of vertebrate anatomy.

The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the differentiation of abaxial muscle precursors, but it does not provide the innervation itself. Innervation is provided by axons growing out from the developing spinal cord. The mesoderm influences the muscle's identity, but the nervous system provides the functional connection.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaxial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles).

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most muscle tissue is mesodermal in origin, certain specific smooth muscles are derived from the ectoderm. Which of the following is an example of such a muscle?
options: Arrector pili muscles of the skin
Smooth muscle of the mammary glands
Smooth muscle of the coronary arteries
Internal anal sphincter
Trachealis muscle
correctOption: Smooth muscle of the mammary glands
optionExplanations: Arrector pili muscles are smooth muscles associated with hair follicles, but they are generally considered to be of mesodermal origin. The text specifically lists the pupillary, mammary, and sweat gland muscles as the primary ectodermal exceptions. Arrector pili are not included in this specific ectodermal list.

The muscle tissue found in the mammary glands and sweat glands is a rare example of muscle derived from the ectoderm. This occurs because these glands themselves are ectodermal derivatives, and the associated contractile cells (myoepithelial cells) differentiate from the same germ layer. This is an exception to the general rule of mesodermal muscle origin.

Smooth muscle of the coronary arteries originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells, both of which are mesodermal or neural-fold derivatives. They are not derived from the surface ectoderm. The vascular system is almost entirely mesodermal, with some neural crest contribution in specific regions.

The internal anal sphincter is composed of smooth muscle derived from the visceral mesoderm surrounding the hindgut. It follows the standard developmental pattern for the gastrointestinal tract. The external anal sphincter, by contrast, is skeletal muscle derived from the cloacal region's mesoderm.

The trachealis muscle is the smooth muscle located at the posterior aspect of the trachea, and it is derived from the visceral mesoderm of the foregut. Like the rest of the respiratory and digestive tracts, its muscular components are mesodermal. It does not share the unique ectodermal origin of the pupillary or sweat gland muscles.
citation: Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

coreConcept: Myoblast fusion and skeletal muscle formation
statement: What is the cellular mechanism that results in the characteristic multinucleated state of mature skeletal muscle fibers?
options: Repeated nuclear division without cytokinesis
Fusion of individual mononuclear myoblasts
Uptake of nuclei from surrounding connective tissue
Fragmentation of a single large primary nucleus
Migration of satellite cells into the fiber sarcoplasm
correctOption: Fusion of individual mononuclear myoblasts
optionExplanations: Repeated nuclear division without cytokinesis (endomitosis) occurs in some cell types, such as megakaryocytes, but it is not the mechanism for skeletal muscle formation. Skeletal muscle fibers are formed by the physical merging of separate cells. This results in a syncytium rather than a single polyploid cell.

The multinucleated state of skeletal muscle is achieved through the fusion of individual, mononuclear precursor cells called myoblasts. As these cells fuse, they form long myotubes where the nuclei share a common cytoplasm. This process is essential for creating the long, continuous fibers required for skeletal muscle contraction.

Nuclei are not 'taken up' from the surrounding connective tissue (fibroblasts). All nuclei within a skeletal muscle fiber originate from the myoblasts that fused to form that fiber. The surrounding connective tissue (epimysium, perimysium) remains cellularly distinct from the muscle fibers themselves.

The nuclei in a skeletal muscle fiber do not come from the fragmentation of a single nucleus. Each nucleus represents an original myoblast that contributed to the formation of the fiber. This allows the large muscle fiber to have localized genetic control over different regions of its long sarcoplasm.

While satellite cells are important for muscle repair and can fuse with existing fibers, they are not the primary mechanism for the initial embryonic formation of the multinucleated fiber. The initial formation is driven by the mass fusion of embryonic myoblasts. Satellite cells remain as a quiescent population for later regenerative needs.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fibers. Myofibrils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

coreConcept: Development of the cardiac conducting system
statement: Which of the following statements accurately describes the origin and nature of Purkinje fibers in the developing heart?
options: They are specialized nerve cells that migrate from the neural crest
They are modified cardiac muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils
They are derived from the endocardial cushions of the heart tube
They are connective tissue strands that conduct electrical impulses
They are formed by the fusion of multiple cardiac myoblasts
correctOption: They are modified cardiac muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are often confused with nervous tissue because of their conducting function, but they are not neurons and do not migrate from the neural crest. They are myogenic in origin, meaning they develop from the same mesodermal precursors as the rest of the myocardium. Their electrical properties are specialized versions of muscle cell properties.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that appear during later development. They are characterized by having fewer and more irregularly distributed myofibrils compared to standard contractile cardiomyocytes. Their primary role is the rapid conduction of electrical impulses through the ventricular walls.

The endocardial cushions contribute to the formation of the heart valves and the septation of the heart chambers, but they do not form the Purkinje fibers. The conducting system differentiates from the myocardial layer (visceral mesoderm). The cushions are primarily composed of endocardial-derived mesenchymal tissue.

Purkinje fibers are not made of connective tissue; they are living, specialized muscle cells. While connective tissue (the fibrous skeleton of the heart) helps insulate the electrical signals, the actual conduction is performed by these modified muscle cells. Connective tissue lacks the membrane channels necessary for rapid action potential propagation.

Unlike skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle cells—including Purkinje fibers—do not fuse to form multinucleated structures. They remain as individual cells connected by intercalated discs. The Purkinje fibers maintain this cellular individuality while forming the specialized bundles of the conducting system.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fibers, form the conducting system of the heart. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

coreConcept: Resegmentation of sclerotomes and myotome function
statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation affect the functional relationship between the myotomes and the vertebral column?
options: It ensures each myotome is attached to only one vertebra
It allows myotomes to bridge the gap between adjacent vertebrae
It prevents the myotomes from interacting with the intervertebral discs
It causes the myotomes to degenerate and be replaced by tendons
It forces the myotomes to migrate into the limb buds
correctOption: It allows myotomes to bridge the gap between adjacent vertebrae
optionExplanations: If each myotome were attached to only one vertebra, muscle contraction would not result in movement of the spine, as there would be no joint or flexible point to act upon. Resegmentation is specifically required to change this one-to-one relationship. This shift is what enables the mechanical leverage needed for spinal flexion and extension.

Resegmentation involves the caudal half of one sclerotome fusing with the cranial half of the next. This shift causes the myotomes, which originally aligned with the sclerotomes, to now bridge the intervertebral discs between the newly formed definitive vertebrae. This anatomical arrangement is what allows the muscles to contract and move the vertebral column.

The myotomes must interact with the region of the intervertebral discs to bridge the gap between vertebrae. Resegmentation ensures that the muscle spans the flexible disc space rather than being confined to a single rigid bone. Without this bridging, the spine would be a rigid, immobile rod.

Myotomes do not degenerate during resegmentation; rather, they differentiate into the functional skeletal muscles of the axial skeleton. Tendons do form to attach these muscles to the bone, but the myotome itself remains the contractile muscle mass. Resegmentation is a reorganization, not a degenerative process.

While some cells from the myotome (specifically the VLL) migrate into the limb buds, this migration is not caused by the resegmentation of the sclerotomes. Resegmentation is a process specific to the formation of the axial skeleton (vertebrae). The migration to limbs is a separate event involving the abaxial muscle precursors.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine. During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fibers.

coreConcept: Muscle derivatives of the VLL and DML
statement: Which of the following muscle groups is formed by cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) that have migrated into the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Intrinsic muscles of the back
Intercostal muscles
Rectus abdominis muscle
Erector spinae muscles
Rhomboid muscles
correctOption: Rectus abdominis muscle
optionExplanations: The intrinsic muscles of the back (epaxial muscles) are derived from the primaxial domain, which includes all cells from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and some from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) that do not migrate. They do not involve migration into the lateral plate mesoderm. These muscles remain close to the neural tube.

Intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial domain. Although they are part of the body wall, the cells that form them do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are formed by the 'remaining cells' of the myotome that stay within the paraxial domain.

The rectus abdominis is a classic example of an abaxial muscle. It is formed by cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the somite that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier and into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This group also includes the obliques and transversus abdominis.

The erector spinae muscles are intrinsic back muscles and are therefore primaxial in origin. They develop from the myotome cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm near the vertebral column. They are innervated by the dorsal primary rami, consistent with their epaxial position.

The rhomboid muscles, along with the shoulder girdle muscles, are listed as being formed by the cells that remain in the myotome (primaxial domain). While they are located superficially, their embryological origin is from the somite cells that do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are considered primaxial precursors.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles.

statement: A mutation that disrupts signaling from the neural tube and notochord during early development would most likely impair the differentiation of which muscle group?
options: Muscles of the abdominal wall
Muscles of the pelvic limb
Intrinsic muscles of the back
Infrahyoid muscles
Rectus abdominis
correctOption: Intrinsic muscles of the back
optionExplanations: The muscles of the abdominal wall are abaxial muscles. They receive their differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm after migrating across the lateral somitic frontier. Therefore, they would be less affected by a loss of signals from the neural tube and notochord compared to primaxial muscles.

Limb muscles are abaxial and, like the abdominal wall, depend on signals from the lateral plate mesoderm for their development. They migrate far from the neural tube and notochord early in development. Their patterning is governed by the limb bud environment.

The intrinsic muscles of the back are primaxial muscles. These cells remain in the paraxial mesoderm and receive their essential developmental signals directly from the neural tube and the notochord. Disruption of these signaling centers would specifically and severely impair the formation of this muscle group.

Infrahyoid muscles are derived from the abaxial domain (migrated VLL cells). As abaxial precursors, they rely on signals from the lateral plate mesoderm rather than the neural tube/notochord. Their development is tied to the migration into the ventral neck region.

The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle of the ventral body wall. It differentiates from VLL cells that have entered the lateral plate mesoderm and are thus under the influence of lateral plate signaling. It would not be the primary target of a neural tube/notochord signaling defect.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm; those that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord.

statement: At what point in human embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: By the end of the first month
By the end of the second month
By the end of the third month
During the fifth month
Only after birth
correctOption: By the end of the third month
optionExplanations: By the end of the first month, the somites are still forming and undergoing epithelization and early differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblasts have not yet fused into the long fibers necessary for striations to be visible. This is too early for mature muscle histology.

During the second month, myoblasts are actively proliferating and beginning to fuse into myotubes. While myofibrils begin to appear in the cytoplasm during this time, the highly organized sarcomere arrangement that produces visible cross-striations is not yet fully established. The muscle is still in an early formative stage.

The end of the third month marks a significant milestone in skeletal muscle development where cross-striations become visible. This indicates that the myofibrils have reached a level of organization where the A-bands and I-bands are aligned across the fiber. This histological appearance is characteristic of mature skeletal muscle.

While muscle development continues through the fifth month and beyond (including growth in size and number of myofibrils), the initial appearance of cross-striations occurs earlier. Waiting until the fifth month would miss the standard developmental window described in embryological texts. By this time, the fetus is already capable of significant movement.

Cross-striations are definitely present long before birth. Fetal movements (quickening), which usually begin around the fourth or fifth month, require functional, striated skeletal muscle. The structural basis for these contractions is established by the end of the first trimester.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fibers. Myofibrils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

coreConcept: Embryological origin of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle
statement: A researcher is studying the embryonic origins of different muscle types. Which of the following correctly matches the muscle type with its primary embryological source?
options: Skeletal muscle — Lateral plate mesoderm
Cardiac muscle — Paraxial mesoderm
Smooth muscle of the gut — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Pupillary muscles — Neural crest cells
Skeletal muscle of the limbs — Ectoderm
correctOption: Smooth muscle of the gut — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle is primarily derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres, rather than the lateral plate mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the connective tissue of the limbs but not the primary muscle fibers themselves. This distinction is fundamental in early embryological patterning.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm that surrounds the endothelial heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is dedicated to forming the axial skeleton and skeletal musculature. Misidentifying this source would overlook the specific cardiogenic mesoderm's role.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and its derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. This layer provides the mesenchymal cells that differentiate into the circular and longitudinal layers of the gastrointestinal tract. It is a classic example of splanchnic mesoderm differentiation.

While many head structures derive from neural crest cells, the pupillary muscles (sphincter and dilator pupillae) are unique because they are derived from the ectoderm of the optic cup. Most other smooth muscles in the body are mesodermal in origin. This makes the pupillary muscles a notable exception in developmental biology.

Skeletal muscle of the limbs is derived from the ventrolateral cells of the somites (paraxial mesoderm) that migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system and skin epidermis, as well as specific muscles like those in the mammary glands. Limb muscles are never derived from the ectodermal germ layer.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube.

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and Mesodermal Domains
statement: The lateral somitic frontier is a critical boundary in the developing embryo. Which of the following best describes the 'primaxial domain' established by this frontier?
options: It contains only cells derived from the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm.
It consists of somite-derived cells that have migrated across the frontier into the lateral plate.
It comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived cells.
It is the domain where muscle cells receive signals exclusively from the lateral plate mesoderm.
It is the primary source for the development of the abdominal wall and limb muscles.
correctOption: It comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived cells.
optionExplanations: The primaxial domain is characterized by its exclusive somitic origin, whereas the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is part of the abaxial domain. Cells in the primaxial domain do not mix with lateral plate mesoderm at this stage. Therefore, this option incorrectly identifies the tissue composition of the domain.

Cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm are defined as abaxial, not primaxial. The primaxial domain is strictly composed of cells that remain within the paraxial environment. Migration across the frontier is the defining feature of the abaxial population.

The primaxial domain is the embryonic region surrounding the neural tube that consists entirely of paraxial mesoderm (somite) cells. These cells do not cross the lateral somitic frontier and are thus isolated from the lateral plate mesoderm environment. This domain gives rise to muscles such as those of the back and the intercostals.

Muscle cells in the primaxial domain receive their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord, not the lateral plate mesoderm. Signals from the lateral plate mesoderm are specific to the abaxial domain. This signaling difference is crucial for the distinct patterning of the two domains.

The abdominal wall and limb muscles are derived from the abaxial domain, which consists of somitic cells that have migrated across the frontier. The primaxial domain contributes to the axial muscles like the rhomboids and levator scapulae. Thus, the primaxial domain is not the source for limb or ventral body wall musculature.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells

coreConcept: Developmental origin of specific abaxial muscles
statement: During the development of the muscular system, certain progenitor cells migrate from the ventrolateral (VLL) lip of the somite. Which group of muscles is formed by these migrating abaxial cells?
options: Muscles of the back (erector spinae)
Intercostal muscles
Rectus abdominis and limb muscles
Rhomboid muscles
Levator scapulae
correctOption: Rectus abdominis and limb muscles
optionExplanations: The muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are derived from the primaxial domain, not the migrating abaxial cells. These muscles develop from cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm near the neural tube. They are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves.

Intercostal muscles are classified as primaxial muscles because they develop from somitic cells that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. Although they are located in the body wall, their embryological origin is distinct from the abaxial abdominal muscles. They receive signals from the neural tube and notochord.

The rectus abdominis, along with the external and internal obliques, transversus abdominis, and all limb muscles, are formed by VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. These are termed abaxial muscles because they develop within the environment of the lateral plate mesoderm. This migration is a key step in forming the ventral body wall.

The rhomboid muscles are part of the primaxial muscle group, meaning they develop from cells that stay within the paraxial mesoderm domain. They do not involve the migration of VLL cells into the lateral plate mesoderm. Their development is governed by signals from the axial structures.

Levator scapulae is a primaxial muscle that originates from somite cells that remain medial to the lateral somitic frontier. It is not formed by the abaxial cell population that migrates into the lateral plate. Like other primaxial muscles, it maintains a closer developmental relationship with the neural tube.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles.

coreConcept: Cardiac muscle differentiation and Purkinje fibers
statement: Which of the following statements accurately describes the differentiation of cardiac muscle compared to skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated fibers similar to skeletal muscle.
Intercalated discs in cardiac muscle develop from special attachments between non-fusing myoblasts.
Purkinje fibers are derived from neural crest cells that migrate into the heart tube.
Cardiac muscle is derived from the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm.
Cross-striations are absent in cardiac muscle throughout its entire development.
correctOption: Intercalated discs in cardiac muscle develop from special attachments between non-fusing myoblasts.
optionExplanations: Unlike skeletal muscle, where myoblasts fuse to form a syncytium of multinucleated fibers, cardiac myoblasts do not fuse. They remain as individual cells that are joined end-to-end. This structural difference is essential for the functional syncytium required for coordinated heart contractions.

Cardiac myoblasts adhere to each other through specialized cell membrane attachments that eventually differentiate into intercalated discs. These discs allow for rapid electrical coupling and mechanical adhesion between adjacent cardiac cells. This is a unique feature of cardiac muscle development that distinguishes it from skeletal muscle.

Purkinje fibers, which form the heart's conducting system, are actually specialized cardiac muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils, not neural crest derivatives. While neural crest cells contribute to the heart's septation and outflow tract, the conducting system itself is myogenic. This is a common point of confusion in cardiac embryology.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the parietal layer. The visceral layer specifically surrounds the endothelial heart tube to form the myocardium. The parietal layer contributes to the serous membranes lining the body cavities.

Cardiac muscle does possess cross-striations, as myofibrils develop in a manner similar to skeletal muscle. These striations reflect the organized sarcomeric structure necessary for contraction. The absence of striations is a characteristic of smooth muscle, not cardiac muscle.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart.

coreConcept: Origins of smooth muscle in different organs
statement: A medical student is reviewing the diverse origins of smooth muscle. Which of the following is the correct embryological source for the smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta?
options: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm only
Ectoderm and paraxial mesoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Proepicardial cells and endoderm
Somitomere-derived mesenchymal cells
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: While the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut, the dorsal aorta has a more complex origin. It involves contributions from both the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. Restricting the origin to only the visceral layer is incomplete.

Ectoderm gives rise to specific smooth muscles like the pupillary muscles, but it does not contribute to the dorsal aorta's muscular wall. Paraxial mesoderm primarily forms skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton. Therefore, this combination is incorrect for vascular development.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries is uniquely derived from a combination of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. Neural crest cells are particularly important for the smooth muscle in the arch of the aorta and the great vessels. This dual origin is a key feature of the vascular system's development.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, not the dorsal aorta. Endoderm provides the epithelial lining of the gut and respiratory tract but does not differentiate into muscle tissue. This option confuses different vascular and visceral origins.

Somitomeres in the head region give rise to skeletal muscles of the face and jaws, not the smooth muscle of the major systemic arteries. The dorsal aorta develops further caudally and relies on different cell populations. Mesenchymal cells from somitomeres have a distinct fate in craniofacial development.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm.

coreConcept: Innervation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles
statement: Regardless of whether muscle cells belong to the primaxial or abaxial domains, their innervation follows a specific segmental pattern. Which statement correctly describes this innervation?
options: Epaxial muscles are innervated by ventral primary rami.
Hypaxial muscles are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
Back muscles (epaxial) are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
Limb muscles are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
All myotomes are innervated by cranial nerves regardless of their segment.
correctOption: Back muscles (epaxial) are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
optionExplanations: Epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back, are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves, not the ventral rami.
