The epimere/hypomere concept is based on dorsal vs. ventral primary rami innervation. The frontier defines the primaxial and abaxial domains.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells

statement: Which of the following muscle groups is classified as belonging to the abaxial domain, having developed from precursors that crossed the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Rectus abdominis
Deep muscles of the back
Intercostal muscles
Shoulder girdle muscles
Rhomboid muscles
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle because its precursors originate from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the somite and migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. In this new environment, they receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate. This group also includes the infrahyoid and limb muscles.

The deep muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are primaxial muscles. They develop from precursors (DML and some VLL cells) that remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They receive their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord.

Intercostal muscles are considered primaxial muscles because they develop from somite cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm domain. Although they are located in the body wall, they do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are innervated by the ventral primary rami but remain primaxial in origin.

Shoulder girdle muscles, such as the levator scapulae and rhomboids, are primaxial muscles. Their precursors do not migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are derived from the somitic cells that stay within the primaxial domain surrounding the neural tube.

Rhomboid muscles are part of the primaxial domain, as they are derived from somite cells that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They are often grouped with other shoulder girdle muscles that maintain a primaxial developmental origin. Their signaling is derived from the axial structures like the notochord.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors

statement: Precursors of the primaxial muscle domain, which include the dorsomedial lip cells and some ventrolateral lip cells that do not migrate across the lateral somitic frontier, primarily receive their developmental signals from which structures?
options: Neural tube and notochord
Lateral plate mesoderm
Ectoderm and endoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
Proepicardial cells
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: Primaxial muscle precursors remain in the paraxial mesoderm and are physically close to the axial structures of the embryo. Consequently, they receive their primary inductive signals for differentiation from the neural tube and the notochord. This signaling environment is distinct from that of the abaxial domain.

The lateral plate mesoderm provides differentiation signals to the abaxial muscle precursors that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier. Primaxial cells do not enter this domain and thus do not rely on these specific signals. The frontier acts as a boundary for these different signaling environments.

While the ectoderm and endoderm provide various signals during early gastrulation and organogenesis, they are not the primary signaling sources for the differentiation of primaxial muscle precursors. The neural tube and notochord are the specific axial structures responsible for this induction. Ectoderm signals are more relevant to the dermatome.

The sclerotome and dermatome are regions within the same somite as the myotome, but they are not the primary sources of the signals that drive primaxial muscle differentiation. Instead, the external axial structures (neural tube/notochord) provide the necessary cues. The sclerotome itself is responding to similar axial signals.

Proepicardial cells are involved in the development of the heart, specifically contributing to the epicardium and coronary artery smooth muscle. They have no role in the signaling or development of the primaxial skeletal muscle precursors of the somites. Their location is restricted to the developing thoracic cavity.
citation: those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord (Fig. 11.3). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment

statement: A patient presents with a loss of motor function in the deep muscles of the back (epaxial muscles). Based on the developmental organization of the myotome, which nerves are responsible for the innervation of these specific muscles?
options: Dorsal primary rami
Ventral primary rami
Phrenic nerve
Intercostal nerves
Sympathetic chain ganglia
correctOption: Dorsal primary rami
optionExplanations: Epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back, are developmentally defined by their innervation from the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. This relationship is established early in development as the myotome receives innervation from the spinal nerve of the same segment. This pattern remains consistent regardless of the primaxial/abaxial classification.

Ventral primary rami innervate the hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the limbs and the ventrolateral body wall. While many primaxial muscles are innervated by ventral rami (like the intercostals), the epaxial back muscles are strictly the domain of the dorsal rami. This is a key functional distinction in anatomy.

The phrenic nerve is derived from the ventral rami of C3, C4, and C5 and innervates the diaphragm, which is a hypaxial muscle. It does not provide motor innervation to the epaxial muscles of the back. Its path is entirely ventral and internal to the thoracic cavity.

Intercostal nerves are the ventral primary rami of the thoracic spinal nerves and innervate the intercostal muscles and the skin of the thorax. Since they are ventral rami, they innervate hypaxial structures, not the epaxial muscles of the back. The epaxial muscles are located posterior to the transverse processes of the vertebrae.

Sympathetic chain ganglia are part of the autonomic nervous system and provide visceral motor innervation to smooth muscle, glands, and the heart. They do not provide voluntary motor innervation to skeletal muscles like the epaxial back muscles. Skeletal muscle innervation is always via somatic motor fibers in the rami.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

statement: In the development of cardiac muscle from visceral mesoderm, how does the cellular differentiation process differ significantly from that of skeletal muscle?
options: Myoblasts adhere via intercalated discs but do not fuse
Myoblasts fuse to form long multinucleated fibers
Myoblasts are derived from the neural crest
Myoblasts develop cross-striations only after birth
Myoblasts differentiate into Purkinje fibers before forming the myocardium
correctOption: Myoblasts adhere via intercalated discs but do not fuse
optionExplanations: Unlike skeletal muscle where myoblasts fuse into syncytial multinucleated fibers, cardiac myoblasts remain as individual cells. They adhere to each other through specialized junctions that eventually mature into intercalated discs. This allows for electrical coupling while maintaining individual cellular integrity.

The fusion of myoblasts to form long, multinucleated fibers is a hallmark of skeletal muscle development, not cardiac muscle. In the heart, the cells remain distinct and are joined by intercalated discs. This structural difference is vital for the unique physiological properties of cardiac tissue.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds the heart tube. Neural crest cells contribute to the septation of the heart and some smooth muscle in the great vessels, but they do not form the myocardium itself. The myocardium is purely mesodermal.

Cross-striations in cardiac muscle, much like in skeletal muscle, develop during embryonic life as myofibrils are organized within the cytoplasm. They do not wait until after birth to appear. By the end of the third month, these striations are typically visible in skeletal muscle, and a similar timeline applies to cardiac muscle.

Purkinje fibers are specialized conducting cells that differentiate from cardiac muscle cells later in development. They do not form before the myocardium; rather, they are a specialized subset of the cardiac muscle lineage. The bulk of the myocardium forms the contractile walls of the heart first.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is derived from mesoderm, certain specific smooth muscles have an ectodermal origin. Which of the following muscles is derived from the ectoderm?
options: Sphincter pupillae
Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Smooth muscle of the jejunum
Coronary artery smooth muscle
Rectus abdominis
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae
optionExplanations: The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are rare examples of muscle tissue derived from the ectoderm (specifically the neuroectoderm of the optic cup). Most other muscles in the body are mesodermal. This also includes the smooth muscle associated with mammary and sweat glands.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta is derived from a combination of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. It is not an ectodermal derivative. Neural crest cells are ectodermal in origin but are considered a distinct population from the surface ectoderm that forms glands.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut, such as in the jejunum, is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This mesoderm surrounds the endodermal gut tube during development. It is never derived from the ectoderm.

Smooth muscle in the coronary arteries originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. Proepicardial cells are mesodermal in origin. While neural crest cells have an ectodermal source, the primary bulk of vascular smooth muscle is mesodermal or crest-derived, not from surface ectoderm.

The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle, not a smooth muscle, and it is derived from the abaxial precursors of the somitic myotomes (paraxial mesoderm). It has no ectodermal component. Skeletal muscles are universally mesodermal in origin.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

coreConcept: Development of the cardiac conducting system
statement: During the maturation of the heart, certain specialized bundles of muscle cells develop irregularly distributed myofibrils and eventually form the cardiac conducting system. What are these specialized cells called?
options: Purkinje fibers
Myoblasts
Sclerotome cells
Proepicardial cells
Intercalated discs
correctOption: Purkinje fibers
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are specialized cardiac muscle cells that form the heart's conducting system. They are characterized by having fewer and more irregularly distributed myofibrils compared to typical cardiomyocytes. They are essential for the rapid propagation of electrical impulses through the ventricles.

Myoblasts are the undifferentiated precursor cells that give rise to muscle fibers. In skeletal muscle, they fuse, while in cardiac muscle, they adhere but remain separate. They are the starting point for muscle development, not the final specialized conducting cells.

Sclerotome cells are mesenchymal cells derived from the ventral part of the somite that form the vertebrae and ribs. They are involved in skeletal development, not the development of the heart or its conducting system. They are part of the paraxial mesoderm lineage.

Proepicardial cells are a population of cells that migrate onto the surface of the heart to form the epicardium and contribute to the coronary vasculature. While they are vital for heart development, they do not differentiate into the Purkinje fibers of the conducting system. The conducting system arises from the myocardium itself.

Intercalated discs are not cells, but rather the specialized junctional complexes that connect individual cardiac muscle cells. They facilitate mechanical and electrical coupling between cardiomyocytes. They are a structural feature of cardiac tissue, not a cell type of the conducting system.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in large arteries
statement: The smooth muscle found in the walls of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries has a complex embryological origin. Which of the following pairs of tissues contribute to the formation of smooth muscle in these vessels?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Paraxial mesoderm and ectoderm
Visceral mesoderm and endoderm
Sclerotome and myotome
Proepicardial cells and paraxial mesoderm
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large vessels is uniquely derived from both the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. This dual origin reflects the complex integration of different cell populations during the formation of the great vessels. Neural crest cells specifically contribute to the segments closest to the heart.

Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton, while ectoderm forms the nervous system and skin. Neither is the primary source for the smooth muscle of the large arteries. While some head muscles are paraxial, the vascular smooth muscle follows a different developmental path.

Visceral mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut and the cardiac muscle, but the large arteries like the aorta specifically involve lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest. Endoderm forms the epithelial lining of the gut and respiratory tract, not muscle tissue. The endoderm provides the internal lining of organs, not the contractile wall.

The sclerotome and myotome are subdivisions of the somite (paraxial mesoderm). The sclerotome forms bone, and the myotome forms skeletal muscle. Neither contributes to the smooth muscle of the large arteries. These structures are dedicated to the axial skeleton and voluntary musculature.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, but not the dorsal aorta. Paraxial mesoderm is not a source for the smooth muscle of the large arteries. The dorsal aorta's smooth muscle is specifically linked to the lateral plate and neural crest populations.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm.

statement: What developmental event allows the myotome-derived muscles to effectively move the vertebral column?
options: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae
Fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers
Migration of VLL cells across the lateral somitic frontier
Formation of the lateral somitic frontier
Innervation by the ventral primary rami
correctOption: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae
optionExplanations: During development, the sclerotomes undergo resegmentation, where the caudal half of one sclerotome fuses with the cranial half of the next. This process shifts the position of the vertebrae relative to the myotomes, causing the muscles to bridge the intervertebral joints. This bridging is what allows the muscles to exert force and move the spine.

The fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers is the process of muscle cell maturation, but it does not explain the mechanical advantage needed to move the spine. Without the resegmentation of the skeleton, the muscles would be attached within a single vertebral segment. Resegmentation provides the necessary skeletal framework for movement.

The migration of VLL cells across the lateral somitic frontier creates the abaxial muscles, such as those in the limbs and abdominal wall. While important for body wall movement, it is not the mechanism that allows axial muscles to move the spine. Spine movement is specifically tied to the relationship between the myotome and the sclerotome.

The formation of the lateral somitic frontier defines the primaxial and abaxial domains but does not directly facilitate the mechanical action of spinal movement. It is a boundary for cell signaling and origin. The mechanical ability to move the spine is a result of the spatial realignment of muscle and bone precursors.

Innervation by the ventral primary rami (or dorsal rami) provides the electrical signal for muscle contraction. However, the physical capacity to move the spine depends on the anatomical attachment across joints. This attachment is created by the resegmentation of the sclerotomes, not the innervation pattern itself.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). With the exception of some smooth muscle tissue (see p. 164), the muscular system develops from the mesodermal germ layer and consists of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle.

statement: The dermomyotome is formed when cells from which two specific areas of the somite migrate and proliferate beneath the dermatome?
options: Ventrolateral and dorsomedial lips
Sclerotome and notochord
Neural tube and lateral plate
Ectoderm and endoderm
Epimere and hypomere
correctOption: Ventrolateral and dorsomedial lips
optionExplanations: The dermomyotome is a composite structure formed from the dermatome and the underlying myotome precursors. These precursors originate from two specific regions of the somite: the ventrolateral lip (VLL) and the dorsomedial lip (DML). Cells from these lips migrate medially to form the muscle layer beneath the dermatome.

The sclerotome is the ventral part of the somite that forms bone, and the notochord is an axial signaling structure. Neither migrates to form the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is a dorsal somitic structure, while the sclerotome is ventral and mesenchymal.

The neural tube and lateral plate are structures external to the somite that provide signaling for its differentiation. They do not contribute cells to the formation of the dermomyotome itself. The dermomyotome is composed entirely of cells derived from the paraxial mesoderm of the somite.

Ectoderm and endoderm are primary germ layers. While the ectoderm lies dorsal to the somite and influences the dermatome, it does not provide the cells that form the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is a purely mesodermal structure arising from the somite.

Epimere and hypomere are older terms used to describe the functional divisions of the myotome based on innervation. They are not the names of the specific lips or edges of the somite that migrate to form the dermomyotome. The VLL and DML are the correct embryological terms for these muscle-forming areas.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome.

statement: In the development of skeletal muscle, at what point do the characteristic cross-striations typically become visible within the cytoplasm of the multinucleated muscle fibers?
options: By the end of the third month
During the first week of gestation
Immediately after the formation of the somite
After the first year of life
During the process of epithelization
correctOption: By the end of the third month
optionExplanations: Cross-striations are the result of the highly organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments into sarcomeres. In human development, this organization reaches a level visible by light microscopy by the end of the third month of gestation. This marks a significant stage in the functional maturation of skeletal muscle.

The first week of gestation is the period of cleavage and blastocyst formation; no tissues or organs have formed yet. Muscle development does not begin until the somites appear during the third and fourth weeks. Striations require a much longer period of cellular differentiation.

Immediately after somite formation, the cells are still epithelial or just beginning to differentiate into sclerotome and dermomyotome. They have not yet fused into myoblasts or synthesized the myofibrils necessary for striations. Striations appear much later in the second trimester.

Skeletal muscle is well-developed and functional at birth, meaning striations appear long before the first year of life. Infants are born with the ability to move their limbs and breathe, both of which require striated skeletal muscle. The third month of gestation is the correct developmental milestone.

Epithelization is the process where somitomeres form the epithelial somite 'ball'. This occurs very early in the third week of development. At this stage, the cells are undifferentiated and do not contain the complex contractile apparatus required for cross-striations.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

statement: Muscles of the body wall and limbs, classified as hypaxial muscles, are innervated by which specific neural structures?
options: Ventral primary rami
Dorsal primary rami
Cranial nerves
Splanchnic nerves
Gray rami communicantes
correctOption: Ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Hypaxial muscles include those of the limbs and the ventrolateral body wall. These muscles are consistently innervated by the ventral primary rami of the spinal nerves. This anatomical arrangement is established as the myotome cells migrate to their final positions while dragging their nerve supply with them.

Dorsal primary rami are responsible for the innervation of the epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back. They do not supply the muscles of the limbs or the abdominal wall. This division between dorsal and ventral rami is a fundamental principle of spinal nerve distribution.

While some cranial nerves provide motor innervation to muscles in the head and neck, the hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs are supplied by spinal nerves. Cranial nerves are not the primary source of innervation for the trunk and limb musculature. Their distribution is limited to the cephalic region.

Splanchnic nerves carry autonomic fibers to the viscera and are not involved in the voluntary motor innervation of skeletal muscles. They supply smooth muscle and glands in the body cavities. Hypaxial skeletal muscles are part of the somatic nervous system, not the autonomic system.

Gray rami communicantes are part of the sympathetic nervous system, carrying postganglionic fibers to spinal nerves for distribution to blood vessels and sweat glands. They do not provide motor innervation to skeletal muscles. Skeletal muscle contraction is controlled by somatic motor neurons in the rami.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in the gut
statement: A medical student is reviewing the histology of the esophagus and stomach. The smooth muscle layers found within the walls of these gut-derived organs originate from which embryonic source?
options: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Ectoderm
Sclerotome
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract and its derivatives (like the respiratory system) is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This layer closely invests the endodermal tube during development. It differentiates into the circular and longitudinal muscle layers of the gut wall.

Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle via the somites. It does not contribute to the smooth muscle of the internal organs like the stomach or esophagus. Its derivatives are primarily associated with the body wall and axial skeleton.

Neural crest cells migrate into the gut wall to form the enteric nervous system (Auerbach's and Meissner's plexuses), but they do not form the smooth muscle itself. The muscle tissue is mesodermal. The crest cells provide the regulatory neural network for the muscle.

Ectoderm forms the nervous system and the epidermis. Its contribution to smooth muscle is limited to specific glands and the eye, not the gastrointestinal tract. The gut wall is a combination of endoderm (lining) and mesoderm (muscle and connective tissue).

The sclerotome is the part of the somite that forms the vertebrae and ribs. It is a paraxial mesoderm derivative and has no role in the formation of the visceral smooth muscle of the gut. It is located dorsally and medially compared to the visceral lateral plate mesoderm.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: Which of the following embryonic layers is the primary source for the development of the skeletal muscular system, including the muscles of the axial skeleton and limbs?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Surface ectoderm
Neural crest cells
Endoderm
correctOption: Paraxial mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is the correct answer as it organizes into somites and somitomeres, which are the precursors for all skeletal muscles of the body. These segments extend from the occipital region to the tail bud, providing the myogenic cells for the trunk and limbs. The other options do not contribute to the bulk of the skeletal muscular system.

Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm is incorrect because it primarily gives rise to cardiac muscle and the smooth muscle layers of the gastrointestinal tract and its derivatives. While it is a mesodermal derivative, its fate is restricted to involuntary muscle types. It does not form the voluntary skeletal muscles of the axial or appendicular skeleton.

Surface ectoderm is incorrect as its primary derivatives include the epidermis and nervous system components. Although it gives rise to specific smooth muscles like the pupillary and mammary gland muscles, it does not contribute to skeletal muscle formation. Skeletal muscle is exclusively a mesodermal derivative.

Neural crest cells are incorrect because they are multipotent cells that contribute to the peripheral nervous system, craniofacial structures, and smooth muscle of large arteries. They do not differentiate into the skeletal muscle fibers of the axial skeleton or limbs. Their role in the muscular system is limited to specific vascular smooth muscle populations.

Endoderm is incorrect as this germ layer primarily forms the epithelial lining of the respiratory and digestive tracts. It does not have a myogenic potential for skeletal, smooth, or cardiac muscle. Muscle tissue is derived from the mesoderm and, in very specific cases, the ectoderm.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in the gastrointestinal tract
statement: A developmental biologist is investigating the origin of the smooth muscle layers within the wall of the descending colon. From which specific embryonic source do these muscle cells differentiate?
options: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm (somites)
Neural crest cells
Surface ectoderm
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is the correct source for smooth muscle in the gut wall. As the gut tube forms, this mesodermal layer surrounds the endodermal lining and differentiates into the circular and longitudinal muscle layers. This process is consistent throughout the gastrointestinal tract and its derivatives.

The parietal (somatic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is incorrect because it contributes to the body wall and the skeletal elements of the limbs. It forms the dermis of the body wall and the parietal peritoneum but does not differentiate into the smooth muscle of the internal organs. Its myogenic contribution is limited to providing the environment for abaxial skeletal muscle precursors.

Paraxial mesoderm is incorrect as it is the source of skeletal muscle via the somites. While somites are essential for the axial skeleton and voluntary muscles, they do not contribute to the smooth muscle of the gut. The differentiation pathways for skeletal and smooth muscle are distinct and arise from different mesodermal subdivisions.

Neural crest cells are incorrect in this context because, while they migrate into the gut wall to form the enteric nervous system (Auerbach's and Meissner's plexuses), they do not form the smooth muscle layers themselves. Their role is regulatory and sensory rather than structural within the muscularis externa. Smooth muscle of the gut is purely mesodermal.

Surface ectoderm is incorrect because it only gives rise to a very limited set of smooth muscles, such as those in the eye and certain glands. The vast majority of smooth muscle, especially that of the digestive system, is derived from the visceral mesoderm. Ectoderm does not contribute to the muscularis of the colon.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: The myocardium, which constitutes the thick muscular layer of the heart, originates from which embryonic tissue during early development?
options: Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Somatic (parietal) mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm
Intermediate mesoderm
Neural crest
correctOption: Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
optionExplanations: Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm is the correct answer as it surrounds the endothelial heart tube during the early stages of cardiogenesis. These mesodermal cells differentiate into myoblasts that form the cardiac muscle fibers. This layer is also responsible for forming the epicardium and the smooth muscle of the gut.

Somatic (parietal) mesoderm is incorrect because it forms the lining of the body cavities (pleural, pericardial, and peritoneal) and the skeletal components of the limbs. It does not contribute to the formation of the heart's muscular wall. Its role is primarily structural for the body wall and limb buds.

Paraxial mesoderm is incorrect as it is the precursor for somites, which give rise to skeletal muscle, vertebrae, and the dermis of the back. It does not participate in the formation of the heart or its associated muscular tissues. Cardiac muscle development is localized to the splanchnic mesoderm near the anterior end of the embryo.

Intermediate mesoderm is incorrect because it is the embryonic source for the urogenital system, including the kidneys and gonads. It has no myogenic potential for cardiac or skeletal muscle. The differentiation of the heart occurs independently of the intermediate mesoderm's developmental pathway.

Neural crest is incorrect as these cells contribute to the septation of the heart (conotruncal ridges) and the autonomic innervation of the heart. They do not differentiate into the contractile cardiac muscle cells of the myocardium. The myocardium is derived strictly from the splanchnic mesoderm.
citation: Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. Musculature of the axial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites, which initially form as somitomeres and extend from the occipital region to the tail bud.

coreConcept: Somite differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome
statement: During somite differentiation, the ventral portion of the somite undergoes an epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition to form which specific structure?
options: Sclerotome
Dermatome
Myotome
Dermomyotome
Lateral somitic frontier
correctOption: Sclerotome
optionExplanations: The sclerotome is the correct answer, formed when the ventral cells of the somite lose their epithelial characteristics and become mesenchymal. These cells eventually migrate to surround the notochord and neural tube to form the vertebrae and ribs. This transition is a critical step in the development of the axial skeleton.

The dermatome is incorrect because it is formed from the dorsal part of the somite, not the ventral part. It eventually gives rise to the dermis of the skin over the back. The dermatome remains closely associated with the myotome in the early stages, forming the dermomyotome.

The myotome is incorrect as it refers specifically to the muscle-forming component of the somite. While the myotome is derived from the somite, it originates from the dorsomedial and ventrolateral lips of the upper region, not the ventral mesenchymal transition that forms the sclerotome. The myotome provides the precursors for skeletal muscle.

The dermomyotome is incorrect because it is the combined structure of the dermatome and the underlying myotome precursors before they fully differentiate. It is located in the dorsal and lateral regions of the somite. The ventral region is dedicated to the sclerotome, which has a different developmental fate (bone).

The lateral somitic frontier is incorrect as it is not a structure formed by the ventral somite, but rather a boundary between the somite-derived cells and the lateral plate mesoderm. It defines the primaxial and abaxial domains. It is a conceptual and physical border, not a tissue derivative like the sclerotome.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips

statement: Which specific population of somitic cells is responsible for the formation of the rectus abdominis and the external oblique muscles of the abdominal wall?
options: Ventrolateral (VLL) lip cells
Dorsomedial (DML) lip cells
Sclerotome cells
Neural crest cells
Visceral mesoderm cells
correctOption: Ventrolateral (VLL) lip cells
optionExplanations: Ventrolateral (VLL) lip cells are the correct answer as they migrate from the somite across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. These cells are the precursors for the abaxial muscles, which include the infrahyoid, abdominal wall, and limb muscles. Their migration is essential for the development of the ventral body wall.

Dorsomedial (DML) lip cells are incorrect because they remain in the primaxial domain and do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. They contribute to the formation of the muscles of the back (epaxial muscles) and the shoulder girdle. They receive different signaling cues compared to the VLL cells.

Sclerotome cells are incorrect as they are the precursors for the vertebrae and ribs. They do not have a myogenic fate and instead differentiate into chondrocytes and osteoblasts. Their migration is directed toward the midline to surround the axial structures, not toward the body wall to form muscles.

Neural crest cells are incorrect because they do not contribute to the skeletal muscles of the abdominal wall. Their role in the trunk is related to the formation of the peripheral nervous system, melanocytes, and adrenal medulla. Skeletal muscle formation is strictly a function of the paraxial mesoderm derivatives.

Visceral mesoderm cells are incorrect as they give rise to smooth muscle and cardiac muscle, not skeletal muscle. The abdominal wall consists of skeletal muscle, which must originate from the somites (paraxial mesoderm). Visceral mesoderm is associated with the internal organs and the gut tube.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles.

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and domains
statement: The lateral somitic frontier defines two distinct mesodermal domains. Which domain is characterized by containing exclusively cells derived from the paraxial mesoderm?
options: Primaxial domain
Abaxial domain
Splanchnic domain
Ectodermal domain
Neural crest domain
correctOption: Primaxial domain
optionExplanations: The primaxial domain is the correct answer as it is the region immediately surrounding the neural tube and notochord. It consists solely of cells derived from the somites (paraxial mesoderm) that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. This domain gives rise to muscles like the intercostals and the deep muscles of the back.

The abaxial domain is incorrect because it is a mixed domain consisting of both the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm and somite cells that have migrated across the frontier. This domain is responsible for forming the muscles of the limbs and the ventral body wall. It is defined by the interaction between these two different mesodermal sources.

The splanchnic domain is incorrect as it refers to the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, which is involved in forming the heart and gut muscle. It is not one of the two domains defined by the lateral somitic frontier in the context of skeletal muscle development. The frontier specifically separates paraxial derivatives from the somatic lateral plate.

The ectodermal domain is incorrect because the lateral somitic frontier is a boundary within the mesodermal layer. Ectoderm is a separate germ layer that lies superficial to the mesoderm. While ectoderm provides signals for muscle development, it is not a domain defined by the somitic frontier.

The neural crest domain is incorrect as neural crest cells are a distinct population of migratory cells and do not form a 'domain' defined by the lateral somitic frontier. The frontier is specifically concerned with the organization of mesodermal derivatives into primaxial and abaxial regions. Neural crest cells have a much wider and different distribution pattern.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

statement: Abaxial muscle cell precursors, which migrate across the lateral somitic frontier, receive their primary developmental signals for differentiation from which source?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm
Neural tube
Notochord
Endoderm
Floor plate
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The lateral plate mesoderm is the correct answer as it provides the signaling environment for the abaxial muscle precursors once they cross the frontier. These signals are distinct from those in the primaxial domain and guide the differentiation of muscles like the rectus abdominis and limb muscles. This interaction is a hallmark of the abaxial domain.

The neural tube is incorrect because it primarily provides signals (such as Wnt and Shh) for the primaxial muscle precursors that remain close to the midline. It does not provide the primary signals for the cells that have migrated far laterally into the abaxial domain. The neural tube's influence is restricted to the primaxial region.

The notochord is incorrect as it, along with the neural tube, serves as a signaling center for the primaxial domain and the sclerotome. It does not reach the abaxial domain where the lateral plate mesoderm resides. Its signals are essential for axial patterning but not for abaxial muscle differentiation.

Endoderm is incorrect because, while it may provide some early patterning signals for the mesoderm, it is not the primary source of differentiation signals for abaxial muscle precursors. The lateral plate mesoderm itself is the immediate environment providing these cues. Endoderm is more involved in the patterning of the visceral mesoderm.

The floor plate is incorrect as it is a specialized part of the neural tube that secretes signals like Shh to pattern the ventral neural tube and adjacent sclerotome. It does not influence the abaxial muscle precursors that are located in the lateral plate mesoderm. Its signaling range is limited to the most medial structures.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier

coreConcept: Innervation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles
statement: According to the functional definition of innervation, which nerves are responsible for the supply of hypaxial muscles, such as those of the body wall and limbs?
options: Ventral primary rami
Dorsal primary rami
Cranial nerves
Sympathetic rami communicantes
Gray rami
correctOption: Ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Ventral primary rami are the correct answer as they innervate the hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the limbs and the ventral and lateral body wall. This innervation pattern is established early in development as the spinal nerves grow into the developing myotomes. This remains true regardless of the primaxial/abaxial classification.

Dorsal primary rami are incorrect because they specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back (erector spinae group). They do not supply the muscles of the limbs or the abdominal wall. The division of the spinal nerve into dorsal and ventral rami reflects the division of the myotome into epaxial and hypaxial parts.

Cranial nerves are incorrect as they innervate the muscles of the head and neck derived from the somitomeres and pharyngeal arches. They do not provide the segmental innervation for the trunk and limb muscles derived from the somites. The axial and limb muscles are supplied by spinal nerves.

Sympathetic rami communicantes are incorrect because they carry autonomic (visceral) fibers to and from the sympathetic trunk. They do not provide motor innervation to skeletal muscles. Skeletal muscle innervation is strictly somatic, provided by the primary rami of spinal nerves.

Gray rami are incorrect as they are part of the autonomic nervous system, carrying postganglionic sympathetic fibers back to the spinal nerves for distribution to the body wall (e.g., to sweat glands and blood vessels). they do not innervate the skeletal muscle fibers of the hypaxial group. Motor innervation is via the somatic efferent fibers in the rami.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4).

statement: What is the characteristic cellular process that leads to the formation of the long, multinucleated fibers seen in mature skeletal muscle?
options: Fusion of myoblasts
Incomplete cytokinesis of myocytes
Hypertrophy of single-nucleated cells
Endoreplication of DNA without division
Fragmentation of large syncytial cells
correctOption: Fusion of myoblasts
optionExplanations: The fusion of myoblasts is the correct answer. During skeletal muscle development, individual mononuclear myoblasts align and their cell membranes fuse to form a syncytium, which is a single cell with multiple nuclei. This process allows for the creation of the long, continuous fibers necessary for skeletal muscle contraction.

Incomplete cytokinesis is incorrect because this would imply that a single cell divides its nucleus but fails to divide its cytoplasm. In skeletal muscle, the multinucleated state is achieved by the merging of many separate cells (myoblasts), not by the failure of a single cell to complete division. This is a fundamental distinction in myogenic biology.

Hypertrophy of single-nucleated cells is incorrect as hypertrophy refers to an increase in the size of existing cells, not an increase in the number of nuclei per cell through fusion. While muscle fibers do undergo hypertrophy during growth and exercise, their initial multinucleated structure is formed by fusion during embryogenesis.

Endoreplication is incorrect because it involves repeated rounds of DNA replication within a single nucleus without nuclear or cytoplasmic division, leading to polyploidy. Skeletal muscle fibers contain many individual, diploid nuclei that were originally in separate myoblasts. The nuclei themselves do not become polyploid through endoreplication.

Fragmentation of large syncytial cells is incorrect as it describes the opposite of the actual process. Skeletal muscle fibers are built up from smaller units (myoblasts) through fusion, rather than being broken down from a larger mass. The term syncytium correctly describes the final fiber, but it is formed by assembly, not fragmentation.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fibers. Myofibrils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear.

coreConcept: Cellular characteristics of cardiac muscle development
statement: Which of the following features distinguishes the cellular development of cardiac muscle from that of skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse into multinucleated fibers
Cardiac muscle lacks cross-striations
Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectoderm
Cardiac muscle cells lack myofibrils
Cardiac muscle cells do not form specialized attachments
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse into multinucleated fibers
optionExplanations: The fact that cardiac myoblasts do not fuse is the correct answer. Unlike skeletal muscle, where myoblasts merge to form long syncytia, cardiac muscle cells remain as individual units. They are joined by intercalated discs, which allow for electrical and mechanical coupling while maintaining cellular boundaries.

Cardiac muscle lacks cross-striations is incorrect because cardiac muscle is a striated muscle type. It contains organized sarcomeres with myofibrils similar to those found in skeletal muscle. The presence of striations is a key histological feature of both cardiac and skeletal muscle.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectoderm is incorrect as cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm. Ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system and epidermis, and only a few specific smooth muscles. All striated muscle (skeletal and cardiac) is mesodermal in origin.

Cardiac muscle cells lack myofibrils is incorrect because myofibrils are the contractile elements of the cell and are present in cardiac muscle. They develop in a manner very similar to skeletal muscle. Without myofibrils, the heart would be unable to generate the force required for contraction.

Cardiac muscle cells do not form specialized attachments is incorrect because they form highly specialized attachments known as intercalated discs. These discs contain desmosomes and gap junctions, which are essential for the coordinated contraction of the myocardium. These attachments are a defining characteristic of cardiac tissue.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myofibrils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse.

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal, which of the following muscles is a notable exception and is derived from the ectoderm?
options: Pupillary sphincter muscle
Rectus abdominis muscle
Myocardium
Smooth muscle of the aorta
Trachealis muscle
correctOption: Pupillary sphincter muscle
optionExplanations: The pupillary sphincter muscle is the correct answer as it, along with the dilator pupillae and muscles of the mammary and sweat glands, is derived from the ectoderm. This is a rare exception to the rule that muscle tissue is mesodermal. These muscles develop from the neural ectoderm of the optic cup or the surface ectoderm.

Rectus abdominis muscle is incorrect because it is a skeletal muscle derived from the paraxial mesoderm (specifically the VLL lip of the somite). It is part of the abaxial muscle group. Skeletal muscles are never derived from the ectoderm.

Myocardium is incorrect as it is cardiac muscle derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm. It forms the muscular wall of the heart. Like most muscle types, its origin is mesodermal, not ectodermal.

Smooth muscle of the aorta is incorrect because it is derived from a combination of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. While neural crest cells are ectodermal in origin, the question asks for a muscle derived directly from ectoderm like the pupillary muscles. The aorta's muscle is primarily considered a mesodermal/neural crest derivative.

Trachealis muscle is incorrect as it is the smooth muscle of the respiratory tract, which is derived from the visceral mesoderm surrounding the lung buds. Like the smooth muscle of the gut, it follows the standard mesodermal developmental pathway. It does not have an ectodermal origin.
citation: Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube.

coreConcept: Development of the cardiac conducting system
statement: What are the specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that differentiate to form the heart's conducting system called?
options: Purkinje fibers
Intercalated discs
Sclerotomes
Myofibrils
Somitomeres
correctOption: Purkinje fibers
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are the correct answer. They are specialized cardiac muscle cells that have fewer myofibrils and a higher glycogen content than regular cardiomyocytes. They are responsible for the rapid conduction of electrical impulses throughout the ventricles, ensuring coordinated contraction.

Intercalated discs are incorrect because they are the junctional complexes that connect individual cardiac muscle cells. They are not 'bundles of cells' but rather structural features of the cell membrane. They facilitate electrical coupling but do not constitute the conducting system itself.

Sclerotomes are incorrect as they are the ventral parts of somites that give rise to the vertebrae and ribs. They are composed of mesenchymal cells and are not related to the heart or its conducting system. Sclerotomes are part of the developing axial skeleton.

Myofibrils are incorrect because they are the intracellular contractile structures composed of actin and myosin filaments. They are found within all muscle cells, including Purkinje fibers (though irregularly distributed there). They are not a specialized bundle of cells but a component of the cytoplasm.

Somitomeres are incorrect as they are the partially segmented whorls of mesoderm in the head region that give rise to cranial skeletal muscles. They are not involved in the development of the heart or its specialized conducting tissues. The heart develops from the splanchnic mesoderm in the thoracic region.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fibers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

coreConcept: Resegmentation of sclerotomes and myotome function
statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation affect the functional relationship between myotomes and the developing vertebral column?
options: It causes myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, enabling spinal movement
It causes myotomes to fuse into a single longitudinal muscle mass
It forces myotomes to migrate to the limbs, leaving the spine without muscle
It results in the loss of segmental innervation for the myotomes
It converts myotomes into the cartilaginous precursors of the ribs
correctOption: It causes myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, enabling spinal movement
optionExplanations: The correct answer is that resegmentation causes myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs. During resegmentation, the caudal half of one sclerotome fuses with the cranial half of the next, forming a vertebra. This shift ensures that the muscle derived from a single myotome now spans the gap between two vertebrae, allowing it to act on the joint (the intervertebral disc) and move the spine.

It causes myotomes to fuse into a single mass is incorrect because, although some muscles like the erector spinae form long columns, the segmental origin and innervation are maintained. The bridging of discs is a specific anatomical consequence of resegmentation that allows for discrete segmental movement. Fusion would not explain the ability to move individual spinal segments.

It forces myotomes to migrate to the limbs is incorrect as the myotomes that form the axial muscles remain in the trunk. While some VLL cells do migrate to the limbs, this is not caused by sclerotome resegmentation. The axial myotomes are essential for the support and movement of the vertebral column.

It results in the loss of segmental innervation is incorrect because the relationship between a myotome and its original spinal nerve is strictly maintained. Even as the sclerotomes resegment and the muscles shift their positions, the nerves follow their respective muscle segments. This segmental innervation is a key principle of musculoskeletal development.

It converts myotomes into cartilaginous precursors is incorrect because myotomes are committed to a myogenic (muscle) fate. The sclerotome is the structure that forms the cartilaginous and bony elements of the ribs and vertebrae. Myotomes and sclerotomes have distinct developmental trajectories despite their common origin in the somite.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in large arteries
statement: Which two embryonic cell populations contribute to the formation of smooth muscle in the wall of the dorsal aorta?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Paraxial mesoderm and endoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
Ectoderm and visceral mesoderm
Proepicardial cells and myotome
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells are the correct sources. The neural crest cells contribute significantly to the smooth muscle of the great vessels near the heart (aortic arches), while the lateral plate mesoderm provides the smooth muscle for the rest of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries. This dual origin is unique to the vascular system.

Paraxial mesoderm and endoderm are incorrect because paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle and endoderm forms epithelial linings. Neither contributes to the smooth muscle of the large elastic arteries. The vascular smooth muscle has a distinct lineage from the axial skeletal muscles.

Sclerotome and dermatome are incorrect as these are somitic derivatives that form bone and dermis, respectively. While they are adjacent to the developing aorta, they do not provide the myogenic cells for the vascular wall. The aorta develops within the mesenchyme derived from the lateral plate and neural crest.

Ectoderm and visceral mesoderm are incorrect because, although visceral mesoderm forms smooth muscle in the gut, the large arteries like the aorta specifically utilize the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest. Ectoderm's direct contribution to smooth muscle is limited to specific glands and the eye, not the major systemic arteries.

Proepicardial cells and myotome are incorrect because proepicardial cells specifically contribute to the coronary arteries, not the dorsal aorta. The myotome is the source of skeletal muscle and does not contribute to the vascular smooth muscle. The dorsal aorta's development is independent of the myotome's differentiation.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments).

statement: Primaxial muscle precursors, which include all cells from the dorsomedial lip (DML), receive their primary developmental signals from which embryonic structures?
options: Neural tube and notochord
Lateral plate mesoderm
Surface ectoderm
Yolk sac endoderm
Amniotic membrane
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The neural tube and notochord are the correct signaling centers for the primaxial domain. They secrete factors like Wnt and Shh that induce the differentiation of the DML and the medial VLL cells into the muscles of the back and intercostals. This medial signaling environment is distinct from the lateral environment of the abaxial domain.

Lateral plate mesoderm is incorrect because it provides signals for the abaxial muscle precursors, not the primaxial ones. The primaxial cells remain in the paraxial mesoderm and are physically separated from the lateral plate by the lateral somitic frontier. Therefore, they are not exposed to the same signaling molecules.

Surface ectoderm is incorrect as its signals (like Wnt) primarily influence the dorsal part of the somite to form the dermatome and the VLL lip. While it plays a role in early somite patterning, the specific signals for the primaxial muscle precursors are dominated by the midline structures (neural tube and notochord).

Yolk sac endoderm is incorrect because it is located far from the developing somites and is involved in early nutrient transfer and hematopoiesis. It does not serve as a signaling center for the differentiation of skeletal muscle precursors in the trunk. Muscle patterning is controlled by local axial and lateral signals.

Amniotic membrane is incorrect as it is an extraembryonic membrane that provides a protective environment for the fetus but does not participate in the molecular signaling required for myogenesis. The signals for muscle development are intrinsic to the embryo's germ layers and axial structures.
citation: those that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord (Fig. 11.3).

statement: A researcher is studying the embryological origins of the human muscular system. Which of the following embryonic layers is primarily responsible for the formation of the skeletal muscles of the limbs and axial skeleton?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Visceral mesoderm
Neural crest
Ectoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm (parietal layer)
correctOption: Paraxial mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is the primary source of skeletal muscle, forming somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. These structures eventually differentiate into the myotomes that give rise to the musculature of the axial skeleton, body wall, and limbs. This layer is distinct from the lateral plate mesoderm, which contributes to other connective tissues and smooth muscle.

Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm is the source of cardiac muscle and most smooth muscle, such as that surrounding the gut. It does not contribute to the formation of skeletal muscle fibers in the limbs or axial skeleton. This layer surrounds the endodermal tube during early development.

Neural crest cells contribute to various structures including the peripheral nervous system and certain smooth muscles of the large arteries. However, they are not the primary source of skeletal muscle tissue. Their role in the muscular system is limited to specific vascular and craniofacial components.

Ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system and the epidermis, as well as specific smooth muscles like the pupillary and mammary gland muscles. It is not involved in the development of the vast majority of skeletal muscles. Skeletal muscle is almost exclusively mesodermal in origin.

While the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm provides the environment for abaxial muscle precursors, the muscle cells themselves originate from the paraxial mesoderm (somites). The lateral plate mesoderm provides the dermis of the body wall and bones of the limbs, but not the primary myogenic cells. The somite-derived cells must migrate into this layer to form specific muscles.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal in origin, certain specific smooth muscle tissues are derived from the ectoderm. Which of the following muscles shares this unique ectodermal origin?
options: Sphincter pupillae
Rectus abdominis
Myocardium
Detrusor muscle
Vascular smooth muscle of the aorta
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae
optionExplanations: The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are rare examples of muscle tissue derived from the ectoderm rather than the mesoderm. This also includes the smooth muscle associated with mammary glands and sweat glands. This developmental exception is critical for understanding the diverse origins of the ocular and integumentary systems.

The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle derived from the paraxial mesoderm, specifically from the ventrolateral (VLL) cells of the somite that migrate into the abaxial domain. It is not a smooth muscle and does not have an ectodermal origin. It is part of the hypaxial muscle group innervated by ventral primary rami.

The myocardium consists of cardiac muscle, which is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. Cardiac muscle cells do not fuse like skeletal muscle but are joined by intercalated discs. It is entirely mesodermal and never derived from the ectoderm.

The detrusor muscle is the smooth muscle of the urinary bladder wall, which is derived from the splanchnic mesoderm. Like most visceral smooth muscle, it follows the standard mesodermal developmental pathway. It is not included in the specific list of ectoderm-derived muscles.

Vascular smooth muscle in the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from a combination of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. While neural crest cells are ectodermal in origin, the question refers to the direct ectodermal differentiation seen in pupillary muscles. Aortic smooth muscle is generally classified by its mesodermal and neural crest contributions.
citation: Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. Musculature of the axial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites

coreConcept: Cardiac muscle development and intercalated discs
statement: During the development of the heart, cardiac myoblasts undergo a specific differentiation process that distinguishes them from skeletal muscle fibers. Which of the following best describes this process?
options: Myoblasts adhere via intercalated discs without fusing
Myoblasts fuse to form multinucleated syncytia
Myoblasts differentiate from the neural crest
Myoblasts lose their myofibrils to become Purkinje fibers
Myoblasts originate from the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: Myoblasts adhere via intercalated discs without fusing
optionExplanations: Cardiac myoblasts are unique because they adhere to one another through specialized attachments that evolve into intercalated discs. Unlike skeletal muscle, these cells do not fuse into a single multinucleated fiber, maintaining their individual cellular boundaries. This allows for the coordinated but distinct electrical and mechanical coupling required for heart function.

Fusion into multinucleated syncytia is a hallmark of skeletal muscle development, not cardiac muscle. In skeletal muscle, individual myoblasts merge their membranes to form long fibers with many nuclei. Cardiac muscle remains composed of individual cells joined by junctions.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not the neural crest. While neural crest cells contribute to the septation of the heart and some vascular smooth muscle, they do not form the myocardium. The primary contractile tissue of the heart is mesodermal.

Purkinje fibers are indeed specialized cardiac muscle cells, but they do not 'lose' their myofibrils; rather, they have irregularly distributed myofibrils. They represent a specialized conducting system rather than the standard differentiation pathway for all cardiac myoblasts. Most cardiac myoblasts become typical contractile cardiomyocytes.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the parietal layer. The parietal layer contributes to the body wall and limb structures. The visceral layer specifically surrounds the developing endocardial heart tube to form the myocardium.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myofibrils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible.

statement: The lateral somitic frontier is a critical boundary established during early musculoskeletal development. What is the primary significance of this frontier in the developing embryo?
options: It separates somite-derived cells from the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
It marks the division between the sclerotome and the dermatome
It defines the boundary between the endoderm and the visceral mesoderm
It separates the neural tube from the paraxial mesoderm
It acts as the site where myoblasts fuse into multinucleated fibers
correctOption: It separates somite-derived cells from the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The lateral somitic frontier serves as the boundary between the primaxial domain (containing only somite-derived cells) and the abaxial domain (containing lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells). This frontier is essential for determining the signaling environment that muscle precursors will encounter. Cells crossing this frontier enter a different developmental niche.

The division between the sclerotome and the dermatome occurs within the somite itself, with the ventral part becoming the sclerotome and the dorsal part becoming the dermomyotome. This is an internal somitic reorganization. The lateral somitic frontier is an external boundary between the somite and the lateral plate.

The boundary between the endoderm and visceral mesoderm is located deeper in the embryo, surrounding the gut tube. The lateral somitic frontier is located more laterally, between the paraxial mesoderm and the parietal lateral plate mesoderm. These are distinct anatomical regions in the early embryo.

The neural tube is separated from the paraxial mesoderm by the primaxial domain, but the frontier itself is the lateral-most boundary of the somite. The neural tube and notochord provide signals to the primaxial cells. The frontier specifically marks the transition to the lateral plate mesoderm territory.

Myoblast fusion is a cellular differentiation process that occurs within the myotome or after migration into the abaxial domain. It is not a function of the lateral somitic frontier itself. The frontier is a spatial and developmental boundary, not a site of cellular fusion.
citation: Initially, there is a well-defined border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells

statement: A developmental biologist identifies a group of muscle precursor cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Which of the following muscles will these 'abaxial' cells eventually form?
options: Rectus abdominis
Intercostal muscles
Deep muscles of the back
Rhomboids
Erector spinae
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is a classic example of an abaxial muscle, formed by VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Other abaxial muscles include the infrahyoid and limb muscles. These muscles receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm rather than the neural tube.

Intercostal muscles are considered primaxial muscles because they remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. Although they are part of the body wall, their precursors do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. They receive signals from the neural tube and notochord.

The deep muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are primaxial muscles derived from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and the remaining ventrolateral (VLL) cells. They stay close to the neural tube and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They are innervated by the dorsal primary rami.

The rhomboids and the levator scapulae are primaxial muscles, despite their location in the shoulder region. They are derived from somite cells that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are part of the primaxial domain and receive signals accordingly.

The erector spinae is a primaxial muscle group that develops from the myotome cells remaining in the paraxial mesoderm. These cells do not migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. They are functionally classified as epaxial muscles and are innervated by dorsal rami.
citation: The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier. Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors

coreConcept: Signaling sources for primaxial muscle precursors
statement: Muscle precursors that remain within the primaxial domain do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. From which structures do these primaxial precursors primarily receive their developmental signals for differentiation?
options: Neural tube and notochord
Lateral plate mesoderm
Ectoderm and endoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
Heart tube and visceral mesoderm
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: Primaxial muscle precursors remain in the paraxial mesoderm and are positioned close to the axial structures of the embryo. Consequently, they receive their primary inductive signals for differentiation from the neural tube and the notochord. This signaling environment is distinct from that of the abaxial domain.

The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals to the abaxial muscle precursors, which are the cells that have crossed the lateral somitic frontier. Primaxial cells are sequestered from these signals by the frontier. The lateral plate mesoderm influences muscles like the rectus abdominis and limb muscles.

While the overlying ectoderm can provide some signals to the dermomyotome, the specific differentiation of primaxial muscle is dominated by the neural tube and notochord. Endoderm is located too far ventrally to be the primary signaling source for these dorsal paraxial structures. The primaxial domain is defined by its proximity to the neural axis.

The sclerotome and dermatome are regions within the same somite as the myotome, but they are not the primary external signaling sources for primaxial differentiation. The sclerotome cells themselves respond to signals from the notochord to form bone. The myotome's fate is determined by the axial structures.

The heart tube and visceral mesoderm are involved in the development of cardiac and gut smooth muscle, respectively. They do not provide the signals for the differentiation of skeletal muscle precursors in the primaxial domain. These structures are part of the splanchnic mesoderm system.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier... receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord

statement: Despite the complex migration of muscle precursors into different domains (primaxial and abaxial), what fundamental rule governs the innervation of the resulting skeletal muscles?
options: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its original segment
All abaxial muscles are innervated by cranial nerves
Primaxial muscles lose their original segmental innervation
Innervation is determined solely by the final destination of the muscle
Only dorsal primary rami provide innervation to all somite-derived muscles
correctOption: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its original segment
optionExplanations: The innervation of skeletal muscle is strictly segmental, meaning that regardless of how far a muscle cell migrates (e.g., into the limbs), it carries the nerve supply from its somite of origin. This explains why nerves often travel long distances to reach their target muscles. This segmental relationship is established very early in development.

Abaxial muscles, such as those in the limbs and abdominal wall, are innervated by spinal nerves, specifically the ventral primary rami. Cranial nerves only innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres or specific branchial arches. Migration across the lateral somitic frontier does not change the nerve source to a cranial one.

Primaxial muscles do not lose their segmental innervation; they maintain a very direct relationship with the spinal nerves of their segment. Because they do not migrate far, their nerves (dorsal primary rami) have a relatively short path to the deep back muscles. Segmental integrity is a core feature of the vertebrate body plan.

Innervation is not determined by the final destination but by the embryological origin of the muscle cells. If a muscle cell originates from the C5 somite, it will be innervated by the C5 spinal nerve, even if it migrates to the distal upper limb. This 'memory' of origin is a key principle in neuroembryology.
