Limb muscles
correctOption: Intercostal muscles
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle. It forms from VLL cells that migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm to form the ventral body wall.
The external oblique is part of the abdominal wall musculature. Like the rectus abdominis, it is derived from the abaxial domain after VLL cell migration.
Infrahyoid muscles are located in the neck but are derived from the abaxial domain. They originate from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier.
Intercostal muscles are primaxial derivatives. Even though they are located in the body wall, the cells that form them do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm.
Limb muscles are the quintessential example of abaxial muscles. They are formed by somite-derived cells that migrate extensively into the lateral plate mesoderm of the limb buds.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells... The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles (Table 11.1, p. 162).

statement: What morphological change occurs in the somitomeres immediately following segmentation, before they differentiate into sclerotome and dermomyotome?
options: Apoptosis of the central core
Epithelization into a hollow ball of cells
Direct migration into the lateral plate
Fusion into a single multinucleated syncytium
Immediate differentiation into myoblasts
correctOption: Epithelization into a hollow ball of cells
optionExplanations: Apoptosis is not the primary event following segmentation. Instead, the cells undergo a structural reorganization to form an epithelial structure.
Immediately after segmentation, the somitomeres undergo epithelization. This process transforms the loose mesenchyme into a 'ball' of epithelial cells organized around a small central cavity.
Migration into the lateral plate occurs much later and only for a specific subset of cells (the VLL). It is not the immediate step following segmentation.
Fusion into a multinucleated syncytium is a process specific to the later histogenesis of skeletal muscle fibers, not the early organization of the somite.
Differentiation into myoblasts occurs later within the myotome region of the dermomyotome. The initial step for the whole somite is epithelization.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A). The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs.

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in the gut
statement: The smooth muscle layer of the esophagus and intestines is derived from which embryonic source?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Surface ectoderm
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscles of the trunk and head, not the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract.
The parietal (somatic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the body wall and the abaxial muscle domain, but not to the muscle of the internal organs.
The visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm surrounds the endodermal gut tube. It is the specific source of the smooth muscle found in the walls of the gut and its derivatives.
Neural crest cells contribute to the smooth muscle of large arteries and the autonomic ganglia of the gut (enteric nervous system), but the bulk of the gut's smooth muscle is mesodermal.
Surface ectoderm gives rise to the epidermis and specific smooth muscles like those in the mammary and sweat glands, but not the muscle of the gut wall.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

coreConcept: Purkinje fibers and the cardiac conducting system
statement: Which statement best describes the developmental nature of Purkinje fibers in the heart?
options: They are specialized nerve fibers derived from the neural tube.
They are modified cardiac muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils.
They are derived from neural crest cells migrating into the heart tube.
They are smooth muscle cells that have migrated from the dorsal aorta.
They are formed by the fusion of multiple cardiac myoblasts.
correctOption: They are modified cardiac muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils.
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are not nerve fibers; they are specialized muscle cells. Although they function in conduction, their embryological origin is myogenic, not neurogenic.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells. They are characterized histologically by having irregularly distributed myofibrils and they form the heart's intrinsic conducting system.
While neural crest cells contribute to the septation of the heart and some vascular smooth muscle, the Purkinje fibers themselves develop from the cardiac myogenic lineage.
Smooth muscle cells from the dorsal aorta do not contribute to the internal conducting system of the heart. The conducting system arises from the heart's own mesodermal wall.
Cardiac muscle cells, including those that become Purkinje fibers, do not fuse. They maintain individual cell boundaries and connect via intercalated discs, unlike skeletal muscle fibers.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fibers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

statement: Which of the following embryonic layers is the primary source for the development of skeletal muscles in the trunk and limbs?
options: Ectoderm
Paraxial mesoderm
Intermediate mesoderm
Endoderm
Neural crest cells
correctOption: Paraxial mesoderm
optionExplanations: Ectoderm primarily gives rise to the nervous system and epidermis, though it does contribute to specific smooth muscles like the pupillary and sweat gland muscles. It is not the source of the vast majority of skeletal muscle tissue in the body. Skeletal muscle is almost exclusively mesodermal in origin.

Paraxial mesoderm is the correct answer as it organizes into somites and somitomeres which are the precursors for skeletal muscle. Somites differentiate into myotomes that eventually form the musculature of the axial skeleton, body wall, and limbs. This layer provides the foundational progenitor cells for all voluntary striated muscle.

Intermediate mesoderm is responsible for the development of the urogenital system, including the kidneys and gonads. It does not contribute to the formation of skeletal, cardiac, or smooth muscle tissues. Its role is strictly limited to the excretory and reproductive tracts.

Endoderm forms the epithelial lining of the gastrointestinal and respiratory tracts, as well as the parenchyma of glands like the liver and pancreas. It does not have a myogenic role in the development of the musculoskeletal system. Muscle tissue surrounding endodermal structures is derived from the adjacent splanchnic mesoderm.

Neural crest cells contribute to various structures including the peripheral nervous system and certain craniofacial bones and muscles. While they contribute to some smooth muscle in large arteries, they are not the primary source of the trunk and limb skeletal musculature. The bulk of skeletal muscle arises from the paraxial mesoderm.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

statement: During the differentiation of a somite, which specific regions are responsible for the formation of the dermomyotome?
options: The central sclerotome and the ventral lip
The dorsomedial and ventrolateral lips of the upper somite
The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm only
The endodermal lining and the neural tube
The notochord and the intermediate mesoderm
correctOption: The dorsomedial and ventrolateral lips of the upper somite
optionExplanations: The sclerotome is the ventral portion of the somite that undergoes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition to form the vertebrae and ribs. It does not contribute to the dermomyotome, which is the precursor for skin and muscle. The sclerotome is specifically bone-forming rather than muscle-forming.

The dorsomedial (DML) and ventrolateral (VLL) lips are the two muscle-forming areas in the upper region of the somite. Cells from these edges migrate beneath the dermatome to establish the dermomyotome. This structure eventually gives rise to the myotome, which provides the muscle cells for the body.

The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm receives migrating cells from the somite but does not form the dermomyotome itself. It provides the environment for the abaxial muscle precursors. The dermomyotome is a direct derivative of the paraxial mesoderm somite.

The endodermal lining and neural tube provide signaling cues for muscle development but are not the structural precursors of the dermomyotome. The neural tube influences the primaxial muscle precursors specifically. The dermomyotome is a mesodermal structure, not an endodermal or ectodermal one.

The notochord provides essential signals for the differentiation of the sclerotome but does not form the dermomyotome. Intermediate mesoderm is involved in urogenital development. Neither of these structures contains the progenitor cells that constitute the muscle-forming lips of the somite.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome

coreConcept: Abaxial muscle precursors and their derivatives
statement: Which group of muscles is derived from the ventrolateral (VLL) cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Deep muscles of the back
Intercostal muscles
Abdominal wall muscles (e.g., rectus abdominis)
Shoulder girdle muscles
Intrinsic muscles of the vertebral column
correctOption: Abdominal wall muscles (e.g., rectus abdominis)
optionExplanations: Deep muscles of the back are derived from primaxial muscle precursors that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain. They do not migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. These muscles are typically innervated by the dorsal primary rami.

Intercostal muscles are formed by the remaining cells in the myotome that do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are considered part of the primaxial domain. While they are part of the body wall, they do not share the same abaxial origin as the abdominal wall muscles.

The abdominal wall muscles, including the rectus abdominis and obliques, are formed by VLL cells that migrate into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. These are classified as abaxial muscles. They receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm rather than the neural tube.

Shoulder girdle muscles are generally derived from the primaxial domain cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm. They do not involve the migration of VLL cells across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate. Their development is distinct from the abaxial abdominal wall muscles.

Intrinsic muscles of the vertebral column are classic examples of primaxial muscles derived from the dorsomedial and remaining ventrolateral lips. They stay close to the neural tube and notochord during development. They are never part of the abaxial domain that migrates into the lateral plate mesoderm.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles.

statement: What is the significance of the lateral somitic frontier in embryonic muscle development?
options: It separates the ectoderm from the underlying endoderm
It marks the boundary between the sclerotome and the dermatome
It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
It defines the limit of the neural crest cell migration
It acts as the site for the formation of the heart tube
correctOption: It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
optionExplanations: The frontier is a boundary within the mesodermal layer and does not involve the separation of ectoderm and endoderm. Those layers are separated by the intraembryonic coelom and other mesodermal structures. The frontier is specific to the organization of somitic and lateral plate mesoderm.

The sclerotome and dermatome are both subdivisions within the somite itself, located medial to the lateral somitic frontier. The frontier is the lateral boundary of the entire somite where it meets the lateral plate mesoderm. It does not define the internal divisions of the somite.

The lateral somitic frontier is the critical boundary separating the primaxial domain (somite-derived cells only) from the abaxial domain (lateral plate mesoderm plus migrated somite cells). This distinction is vital because cells in these two domains receive different signaling cues for differentiation. It represents a fundamental shift in how we understand muscle patterning.

Neural crest cells migrate through various pathways, but the lateral somitic frontier is not the primary anatomical landmark used to define their migration limits. Neural crest migration is more related to the somite's sclerotome and the space between the somite and ectoderm. The frontier is specifically a mesodermal boundary.

The heart tube forms from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm in the cardiogenic area, which is far removed from the lateral somitic frontier. The frontier involves the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm and the paraxial mesoderm. Cardiac development occurs independently of this specific somitic boundary.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells

coreConcept: Innervation of axial and limb muscles
statement: Regardless of their embryological domain (primaxial or abaxial), how are the hypaxial muscles (body wall and limb muscles) innervated?
options: Dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves
Ventral primary rami of spinal nerves
Cranial nerves only
Autonomic postganglionic fibers
Phrenic nerve only
correctOption: Ventral primary rami of spinal nerves
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back. They do not supply the muscles of the limbs or the ventral body wall. This division is based on the functional split of the spinal nerve as it exits the intervertebral foramen.

Ventral primary rami are responsible for the innervation of all hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the limbs and the ventrolateral body wall. This remains true even though these muscles may have different embryological origins (some primaxial, some abaxial). The innervation pattern follows the functional hypaxial/epaxial split.

Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres and branchial arches, such as the muscles of facial expression and mastication. They do not provide the primary innervation for the trunk's hypaxial muscles or the limb muscles. Those are supplied by spinal nerves.

Autonomic postganglionic fibers provide motor supply to smooth muscles, cardiac muscle, and glands, but not to skeletal muscles. Skeletal muscles require somatic motor innervation for voluntary contraction. The hypaxial muscles are skeletal and thus receive somatic innervation from the ventral rami.

The phrenic nerve is a specific ventral ramus derivative (C3-C5) that innervates the diaphragm. While the diaphragm is a hypaxial muscle, the question asks for the general innervation of all hypaxial muscles. The ventral primary rami of all spinal segments involved in the limbs and body wall provide this innervation.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4).

statement: Which characteristic distinguishes the development of cardiac muscle from that of skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form multinucleated syncytia
Cardiac muscle is derived from the paraxial mesoderm
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but are joined by intercalated discs
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils during early development
Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectodermal germ layer
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but are joined by intercalated discs
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated fibers, but cardiac myoblasts do not undergo this fusion process. Instead, cardiac cells remain as individual units. The syncytial nature of skeletal muscle is a key histological difference from cardiac muscle.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source for skeletal muscle. This different embryological origin reflects their different locations and functions in the body.

In cardiac muscle development, myoblasts adhere to each other via specialized attachments that evolve into intercalated discs, allowing for electrical coupling. Unlike skeletal muscle, these myoblasts do not fuse into a single multinucleated cell. This allows the heart to function as a functional, rather than anatomical, syncytium.

Both skeletal and cardiac muscles develop myofibrils in their cytoplasm during differentiation to enable contraction. The presence of myofibrils is a shared feature of all striated muscles. The timing and appearance of these fibrils are similar in both tissue types.

Cardiac muscle is entirely mesodermal in origin, specifically from the visceral mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. Ectoderm contributes to some smooth muscles (like the iris) but never to cardiac muscle. The heart is one of the first functional organs to develop from the mesoderm.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse.

statement: Which of the following smooth muscles has an ectodermal rather than a mesodermal origin?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Smooth muscle in the wall of the stomach
Sphincter pupillae muscle
Smooth muscle of the coronary arteries
Smooth muscle of the urinary bladder
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta is derived from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. It is not ectodermal in origin. Most vascular smooth muscle follows this mesodermal or neural crest pattern.

The smooth muscle in the wall of the stomach and the rest of the gut is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This layer surrounds the endodermal heart tube and gut tube. It is a classic example of mesodermal differentiation.

The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are unique because they are derived from the ectoderm of the optic cup. This is an exception to the general rule that muscle tissue is mesodermal. Other ectodermal muscles include those in the mammary and sweat glands.

Coronary artery smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. While neural crest cells are ectodermal in origin, the question specifically targets the classic ectodermal derivatives mentioned in the text like the pupillary muscles. Proepicardial cells are mesodermal.

The smooth muscle of the urinary bladder (detrusor muscle) is derived from the splanchnic mesoderm associated with the urogenital sinus. It follows the standard pattern for visceral smooth muscle. It does not have an ectodermal origin.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: What is the embryological nature and function of Purkinje fibers in the heart?
options: They are specialized nerve cells that initiate the heartbeat
They are connective tissue strands that support the valves
They are specialized cardiac muscle cells forming the conducting system
They are smooth muscle cells that regulate blood flow
They are endocardial cells that line the heart chambers
correctOption: They are specialized cardiac muscle cells forming the conducting system
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are not nerve cells; they are modified cardiac muscle cells. While they function in electrical conduction, their histological origin is myogenic. They lack the typical structure of neurons found in the peripheral nervous system.

Connective tissue in the heart forms the fibrous skeleton and chordae tendineae, but not the Purkinje fibers. Purkinje fibers contain myofibrils, although they are irregularly distributed. Their primary role is electrical, not structural support.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that develop irregularly distributed myofibrils. They are the primary components of the heart's internal conducting system, ensuring coordinated ventricular contraction. They represent a functional specialization of the visceral mesoderm.

Smooth muscle is found in the walls of the great vessels but not in the Purkinje system of the heart. Purkinje fibers are striated, albeit modified, and are derived from the same lineage as contractile cardiomyocytes. They do not perform the vasomotor functions of smooth muscle.

Endocardial cells form the inner endothelial lining of the heart and are distinct from the myocardial layer where Purkinje fibers reside. The endocardium is a simple squamous epithelium. Purkinje fibers are located in the subendocardial layer but are muscular in origin.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart.

statement: By which time during embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically appear?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
End of the sixth month
Just before birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: By the end of the first month, the somites are still undergoing segmentation and initial differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblasts have not yet fused into mature fibers. Cross-striations are not visible at this very early stage.

During the second month, myoblasts are actively migrating and beginning to fuse, but the internal organization of myofibrils into sarcomeres is not yet complete. The tissue is myogenic but lacks the definitive striated appearance. The third month is the key milestone for this feature.

The end of the third month marks the point where myofibrils have organized sufficiently within the multinucleated muscle fibers to exhibit cross-striations. This indicates a high level of structural maturity in the skeletal muscle tissue. It coincides with the early fetal period of development.

The end of the sixth month is well into the fetal period, and while muscles are growing and contracting, the striations appeared much earlier. Waiting until the sixth month would imply a significant delay in muscle maturation. Striations are a relatively early histological feature.

Skeletal muscle is functional and striated long before birth, allowing for fetal movements (quickening) which usually begin in the second trimester. If striations only appeared just before birth, the fetus would be unable to move in utero. The third month is the correct developmental window.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear.

statement: From which structures do the primaxial muscle cell precursors receive their primary developmental signals for differentiation?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm and ectoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Endoderm and yolk sac
Amniotic fluid and placenta
Neural crest and heart tube
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial muscle precursors, not the primaxial ones. Ectoderm provides signals for the dermatome and some muscle areas, but the neural tube/notochord are the primary drivers for the primaxial domain. This spatial signaling ensures correct patterning.

Primaxial muscle precursors remain in the paraxial mesoderm close to the midline of the embryo. Consequently, they are positioned to receive inductive signals from the neural tube and the notochord. These signals direct their differentiation into the deep muscles of the back and other primaxial structures.

The endoderm and yolk sac are involved in the development of the gut and early hematopoiesis, respectively. They are located ventrally and do not provide the specific patterning signals required for the dorsal paraxial mesoderm. Muscle differentiation is driven by more immediate neighbors like the neural tube.

Amniotic fluid and the placenta provide a protective environment and nutrition but do not act as localized signaling centers for tissue-specific differentiation like myogenesis. Morphogenetic signaling requires close-range interactions between embryonic tissues. The neural tube and notochord serve this role for the somites.

Neural crest cells and the heart tube are involved in different developmental pathways. While neural crest cells migrate through the somites, they are not the primary source of signals for the primaxial muscle precursors. The neural tube and notochord are the established signaling centers for the medial somite derivatives.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord

statement: Which of the following correctly describes the composition of the abaxial domain?
options: Only somite-derived cells located near the notochord
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm and endoderm
Neural tube cells and neural crest cells
Sclerotome cells and dermatome cells only
correctOption: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: Cells located near the notochord are part of the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. The primaxial domain is characterized by its purely somitic origin and its proximity to the embryonic midline. These cells do not migrate across the lateral somitic frontier.

The abaxial domain is a hybrid region formed by the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm and the muscle precursor cells that have migrated into it from the somites. This domain gives rise to muscles of the limbs and the ventral body wall. It is defined by its location lateral to the lateral somitic frontier.

The visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is involved in the formation of cardiac muscle and smooth muscle of the gut. It is not part of the abaxial domain, which specifically involves the parietal layer. Endoderm is a separate germ layer entirely.

The neural tube and neural crest are ectodermal derivatives. While they interact with the mesoderm, they do not constitute the abaxial domain. The abaxial domain is strictly a mesodermal concept involving the lateral plate and somitic cells.

Sclerotome and dermatome are parts of the somite. While some dermatome cells and VLL cells contribute to the abaxial domain, the sclerotome remains primaxial to form the vertebrae. The abaxial domain must include the lateral plate mesoderm by definition.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

statement: What are the paraxial mesoderm structures in the head region that give rise to skeletal muscle?
options: Somites
Somitomeres
Sclerotomes
Lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest
correctOption: Somitomeres
optionExplanations: Somites are the segmented blocks of paraxial mesoderm found from the occipital region down to the tail bud. In the head region, the paraxial mesoderm does not form fully segmented somites. Instead, it forms less distinct structures known as somitomeres.

Somitomeres are the partially segmented whorls of paraxial mesoderm found in the head. They contribute to the musculature of the face and jaws. They are the cranial counterparts to the somites found in the trunk.

Sclerotomes are the ventral portions of somites that form bone. While they exist in the trunk somites, the question asks for the primary paraxial structures in the head. Somitomeres are the organizational units in the head that precede muscle formation.

Lateral plate mesoderm is located lateral to the paraxial mesoderm and contributes to the body wall and viscera. It is not the primary source of skeletal muscle in the head. Head skeletal muscles are almost entirely derived from paraxial mesoderm (somitomeres).

Neural crest cells contribute to the connective tissue and some skeletal elements of the head, but the muscle fibers themselves are derived from the paraxial mesoderm. The myogenic cells in the head originate from the somitomeres. Neural crest cells often provide the patterning for these muscles.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut

coreConcept: Smooth muscle of the large arteries
statement: Which two sources contribute to the smooth muscle in the wall of the dorsal aorta?
options: Paraxial mesoderm and endoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Ectoderm and visceral mesoderm
Sclerotome and myotome
Proepicardial cells and somites
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle, and endoderm forms the lining of the gut. Neither is a primary contributor to the smooth muscle of the large arteries. Vascular smooth muscle has a more complex origin involving lateral plate and neural crest.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries is uniquely derived from a combination of the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. This dual origin is characteristic of the great vessels. Neural crest cells specifically contribute to the segments of the arteries closer to the heart.

While ectoderm gives rise to neural crest cells, the term 'ectoderm' usually refers to the surface layer. Visceral mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut, not the dorsal aorta. The dorsal aorta is associated with the dorsal body wall and lateral plate mesoderm.

Sclerotome and myotome are derivatives of the somite (paraxial mesoderm) and form bone and skeletal muscle, respectively. They do not contribute to the vascular smooth muscle of the aorta. The aorta develops in the mesenchyme lateral to the somites.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the coronary arteries, but not the dorsal aorta. Somites form skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton. The dorsal aorta's smooth muscle is derived from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells as specified in the text.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments).

statement: Which principle governs the primary innervation of developing myotomes by spinal nerves?
options: Muscles are innervated by the nearest available nerve regardless of origin
Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its own segment
All myotomes are innervated by a single master cranial nerve
Innervation only occurs after the muscle has reached its final adult position
The domain (primaxial or abaxial) determines which spinal segment provides the nerve
correctOption: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its own segment
optionExplanations: Innervation is not random or based purely on proximity in the adult; it is strictly determined by the embryonic segment of origin. This is why nerves often travel long distances to reach muscles that have migrated (e.g., the diaphragm). The segmental relationship is established very early.

The fundamental rule of myotome innervation is that each muscle group is supplied by the spinal nerve derived from the same embryonic segment as the muscle cells. This segmental relationship is maintained even if the muscle cells migrate far from their original somite. This is a key concept in understanding dermatomes and myotomes.

There is no single master nerve for all myotomes. Each segment of the spinal cord provides nerves for its corresponding somite-derived muscles. Cranial nerves only innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres and branchial arches.

Innervation begins early in development, often while the muscle precursors are still migrating or just after they have formed the myotome. The nerve follows the muscle to its final destination. This is why the path of a nerve can reveal the migratory history of a muscle.

The primaxial or abaxial domain determines the signaling and the final muscle type, but it does not change the segmental origin of the innervation. Both domains receive nerves from the spinal segment they originated from. The difference lies in whether they are supplied by the dorsal or ventral ramus of that segmental nerve.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres

coreConcept: Embryological origin of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle
statement: A researcher is studying the embryonic origins of different muscle types. Which of the following correctly matches the muscle type with its primary embryological precursor?
options: Skeletal muscle — Lateral plate mesoderm
Cardiac muscle — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Smooth muscle of the gut — Ectoderm
Pupillary muscles — Paraxial mesoderm
Skeletal muscle of the limbs — Neural crest cells
correctOption: Cardiac muscle — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle is primarily derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres, rather than the lateral plate mesoderm. While some limb muscle precursors migrate into the lateral plate, the fundamental origin remains the paraxial mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm typically contributes to the connective tissue and bones of the limbs.

Cardiac muscle develops from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm that surrounds the endothelial heart tube. This mesodermal layer gives rise to the myoblasts that will eventually form the myocardium. Unlike skeletal muscle, these cells do not fuse but remain connected via intercalated discs.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and its derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm only contributes to specific smooth muscles such as those in the iris (pupillary) and certain glands. The gut wall is a classic example of splanchnic mesodermal differentiation.

The pupillary muscles (sphincter and dilator pupillae) are unique because they are derived from the ectoderm of the optic cup. Most other muscles in the body are mesodermal in origin. Paraxial mesoderm specifically forms the skeletal muscles of the head, trunk, and limbs.

Limb muscles are derived from the ventrolateral cells of the somites, which are part of the paraxial mesoderm. These cells migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm to form the limb musculature. Neural crest cells contribute to various structures like the peripheral nervous system and certain craniofacial bones, but not limb skeletal muscle.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube.

coreConcept: Somite differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome
statement: During the differentiation of a somite, which specific region undergoes a transition back to mesenchymal tissue to eventually form the vertebrae and ribs?
options: Dorsomedial lip
Ventrolateral lip
Ventral region (Sclerotome)
Dermatome
Lateral somitic frontier
correctOption: Ventral region (Sclerotome)
optionExplanations: The dorsomedial lip (DML) is one of the two muscle-forming areas in the upper region of the somite. It contributes to the formation of the dermomyotome and eventually the primaxial muscle precursors. It does not form the bone-forming sclerotome.

The ventrolateral lip (VLL) is the other muscle-forming area that contributes to the dermomyotome. Cells from this region can migrate across the lateral somitic frontier to form abaxial muscles. It is not responsible for the formation of the axial skeleton.

The ventral region of the somite undergoes a process where it becomes mesenchymal again to form the sclerotome. These sclerotome cells are the precursors for the vertebrae and ribs. This transition is a critical step in the development of the axial skeleton.

The dermatome is formed from the upper region of the somite and gives rise to the dermis of the back. It is distinct from the sclerotome, which forms the skeletal elements. The dermatome remains closely associated with the developing myotome.

The lateral somitic frontier is a boundary between the primaxial and abaxial domains, not a tissue region that undergoes mesenchymal transition. It serves as a dividing line for muscle precursor migration and signaling. It does not contribute to the formation of the vertebrae or ribs.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A). The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas.

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and muscle domains
statement: A developmental biologist is tracking the migration of muscle precursors. Which of the following best describes the 'abaxial domain' in the developing embryo?
options: The region containing only somite-derived cells surrounding the neural tube
The area where muscle cells receive signals exclusively from the notochord
The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm combined with migrated somite cells
The region that gives rise only to the muscles of the back and intercostals
The mesenchymal ball formed immediately after somitomere epithelization
correctOption: The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm combined with migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The region containing only somite-derived cells surrounding the neural tube is defined as the primaxial domain. This domain does not include cells from the lateral plate mesoderm. It is the site where muscles like the intercostals and back muscles develop.

Muscle cells in the primaxial domain receive signals from the neural tube and notochord. In contrast, cells in the abaxial domain receive many of their differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm. Therefore, this description applies to the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain.

The abaxial domain is defined by the lateral somitic frontier and consists of the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm along with somite cells (from the VLL) that have migrated across the frontier. This domain is responsible for forming muscles of the limbs and abdominal wall. It represents a mix of two different mesodermal sources.

The muscles of the back and intercostals are derived from the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. These muscles are formed by cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. Abaxial muscles include the infrahyoid and abdominal wall muscles.

The mesenchymal ball formed after epithelization is the early somite structure before it differentiates into sclerotome and dermomyotome. This stage precedes the establishment of the lateral somitic frontier and the subsequent primaxial/abaxial domains. It is a transient morphological stage in somite development.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier. Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors.

coreConcept: Innervation of axial and limb muscles
statement: Regardless of their embryological domain (primaxial or abaxial), how is the innervation of the body wall and limb muscles (hypaxial muscles) characterized?
options: They are innervated by dorsal primary rami
They are innervated by ventral primary rami
They are innervated by cranial nerves only
They are innervated by the sympathetic trunk
They lack direct innervation until the second trimester
correctOption: They are innervated by ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the muscles of the back. These muscles are located 'above the axis' and are derived from the primaxial domain. Hypaxial muscles are innervated by a different branch of the spinal nerve.

Hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the body wall and the limbs, are innervated by the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves. This functional definition remains consistent even with the newer embryological classification of primaxial and abaxial domains. Each myotome receives innervation from the spinal nerve of its corresponding segment.

Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres, such as the muscles of facial expression or mastication. Body wall and limb muscles are derived from somites in the trunk and are innervated by spinal nerves. Cranial nerves do not provide the primary motor supply to the limbs.

The sympathetic trunk is part of the autonomic nervous system and provides visceral motor innervation to smooth muscle, glands, and the heart. It does not provide the somatic motor innervation required for skeletal muscles of the body wall and limbs. Skeletal muscle requires somatic motor neurons.

Innervation of myotomes occurs early in development, with each myotome receiving its spinal nerve supply as it forms. By the end of the third month, skeletal muscle already shows cross-striations and functional development. Innervation is established well before the second trimester.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

statement: Which of the following is a unique characteristic of cardiac muscle development that distinguishes it from skeletal muscle development?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form multinucleated fibers
Cardiac muscle is derived from the paraxial mesoderm
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but are joined by intercalated discs
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils during early development
Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectodermal germ layer
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but are joined by intercalated discs
optionExplanations: In skeletal muscle development, myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated muscle fibers. In contrast, cardiac myoblasts do not undergo fusion. They remain as individual cells that are functionally coupled through specialized junctions.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source for skeletal muscles of the trunk and limbs. The visceral mesoderm is a component of the lateral plate mesoderm.

A defining feature of cardiac muscle development is that the myoblasts adhere to one another via special attachments that eventually become intercalated discs. Unlike skeletal muscle, these cells do not fuse into a syncytium. This allows for the coordinated contraction of the heart while maintaining individual cell integrity.

Myofibrils develop in cardiac muscle in a manner very similar to skeletal muscle. Both muscle types rely on these contractile structures for function. The presence of myofibrils is not a distinguishing factor, but rather their organization and the lack of cell fusion are.

Cardiac muscle is entirely mesodermal in origin, specifically from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm contributes to very few muscle types, such as the pupillary and sweat gland muscles. The heart is a primary derivative of the embryonic mesoderm.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal, which of the following muscles is derived from the ectoderm?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Smooth muscle of the coronary arteries
Sphincter pupillae muscle
Rectus abdominis muscle
Trachealis muscle
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. It is not an ectodermal derivative. This muscle provides the structural integrity and vasomotor control for major vessels.

Smooth muscle in the coronary arteries originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. Like the aorta, it is mesodermal or neural crest-derived. It does not share the ectodermal origin seen in the iris muscles.

The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are rare examples of muscles derived from the ectoderm (specifically the optic cup). Most other smooth muscles in the body arise from the mesoderm. This makes the pupillary muscles an important exception in embryology.

The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle derived from the abaxial domain of the somites (paraxial mesoderm). It is not a smooth muscle and is not derived from ectoderm. It forms part of the ventral body wall.

The trachealis muscle is a smooth muscle associated with the respiratory tract, which is a gut derivative. Therefore, it is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. It follows the general rule for smooth muscle in internal organs.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: What is the embryological origin of the Purkinje fibers that form the conducting system of the heart?
options: Neural crest cells migrating into the heart
Specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells
Endocardial cushions
Ectodermal invaginations
Paraxial mesoderm from the cervical region
correctOption: Specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells
optionExplanations: While neural crest cells contribute to the septation of the heart and the smooth muscle of the great vessels, they are not the source of Purkinje fibers. Purkinje fibers are modified muscle cells, not neural cells. Their function is electrical conduction, but their origin is myogenic.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that develop irregularly distributed myofibrils. They differentiate from the same visceral mesoderm that forms the rest of the myocardium. They are essential for the coordinated electrical conduction within the heart.

Endocardial cushions are involved in the formation of the heart valves and the atrial and ventricular septa. They are composed of mesenchymal tissue and do not differentiate into the Purkinje fibers of the conducting system. The conducting system is specifically muscular in origin.

Ectoderm does not contribute to the internal structures of the heart tube or its conducting system. The heart is almost entirely a mesodermal structure. Ectodermal derivatives in the muscular system are limited to specific smooth muscles like those in the eye and glands.

Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton, but it does not contribute to the heart. The heart develops from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. The cervical somites form muscles of the neck and contribute to the diaphragm, but not the heart's conduction system.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

statement: Which of the following muscle groups is correctly classified as being derived from the 'primaxial' muscle cell precursors?
options: Rectus abdominis
Limb muscles
Infrahyoid muscles
Intercostal muscles
External oblique
correctOption: Intercostal muscles
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle. It is formed by cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Therefore, it is not a primaxial muscle.

Limb muscles are classic examples of abaxial derivatives. Precursor cells from the somites migrate extensively into the lateral plate mesoderm of the limb buds. They receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate environment rather than the neural tube.

The infrahyoid muscles are derived from the abaxial domain. Like the abdominal wall muscles, their precursors migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. They are not part of the primaxial population that remains near the neural tube.

The intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial muscle cell precursors. These cells remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They receive their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord.

The external oblique is one of the abdominal wall muscles, all of which are abaxial in origin. These muscles form from VLL cells that have migrated into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. They are not primaxial derivatives.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors.

statement: What is the functional significance of the resegmentation of sclerotomes during the development of the vertebral column?
options: It allows spinal nerves to exit the vertebral canal
It ensures each vertebra is innervated by two different spinal nerves
It enables myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs for spinal movement
It prevents the formation of the nucleus pulposus
It triggers the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers
correctOption: It enables myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs for spinal movement
optionExplanations: While resegmentation does create the pathways for spinal nerves to exit, the primary functional consequence for the muscular system is related to the attachment of myotomes. The exit of nerves is a secondary anatomical result of the sclerotome shift. The question asks for the significance regarding the myotomes.

Resegmentation does not result in dual innervation of a single vertebra by two nerves in a functional sense. Each myotome retains its original segmental innervation from the spinal nerve of the same level. The segmental nature of the nerves is preserved.

Resegmentation of the sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae causes the associated myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs. This anatomical arrangement is crucial because it allows the muscles to span the joints between vertebrae, thereby providing the mechanical leverage necessary to move the spine. Without this shift, muscles would only attach within a single vertebral segment.

The nucleus pulposus is derived from the notochord, not from the process of sclerotome resegmentation. Resegmentation involves the condensation and shift of the mesenchymal sclerotome cells around the notochord. It does not prevent the formation of the disc's core.

The fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers is a cellular differentiation process inherent to skeletal muscle development. It is regulated by myogenic factors and is independent of the gross anatomical resegmentation of the sclerotomes. Resegmentation is a tissue-level structural change.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells.

statement: Which structures provide the primary developmental signals for the differentiation of primaxial muscle precursors?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm and ectoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Endoderm and yolk sac
Amniotic membrane and chorion
Sclerotome and dermatome
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial muscle precursors, not the primaxial ones. Ectoderm provides signals for the dermatome and some abaxial muscles but is not the primary source for the primaxial domain. Primaxial cells are located closer to the midline.

Primaxial muscle precursors, which include all cells from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and some from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) that do not migrate, receive their differentiation signals from the neural tube and the notochord. These midline structures are essential for the proper patterning of the medial somite derivatives. This distinguishes them from the abaxial cells.

The endoderm and yolk sac are involved in early germ layer formation and the development of the gut tube and hematopoiesis. They do not play a direct role in signaling the differentiation of the paraxial mesoderm into specific muscle domains. Muscle patterning is driven by adjacent axial and lateral tissues.

The amniotic membrane and chorion are extraembryonic structures. While they are vital for the survival and protection of the embryo, they do not provide the localized molecular signals required for the differentiation of somitic myotomes. Patterning signals must come from within the embryo's body axis.

The sclerotome and dermatome are regions within the somite itself that differentiate alongside the myotome. While there is some interaction between these compartments, the primary inductive signals for the primaxial muscle precursors come from the external axial structures like the neural tube. The somite regions are the targets of these signals.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord.

statement: By which embryonic time point do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically appear?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
End of the fifth month
At the time of birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: By the end of the first month, the somites are still forming and differentiating into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblasts have not yet fused extensively or developed the organized myofibrillar structure necessary for striations. This is too early in development.

During the second month, myoblasts are actively migrating and beginning to fuse into multinucleated fibers. While myofibrils begin to appear in the cytoplasm, the highly organized sarcomeric pattern that produces visible cross-striations is not yet fully established. Differentiation is still ongoing.

The characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle appear by the end of the third month of development. This indicates that the myofibrils have become sufficiently organized into sarcomeres within the multinucleated muscle fibers. This timing marks a significant milestone in the maturation of fetal skeletal muscle.

The end of the fifth month is well after the initial appearance of cross-striations. By this time, the fetus is capable of significant movement (quickening), which requires functional, striated muscle. Striations are already a well-established feature by this stage.

Cross-striations are not a postnatal development; they are a fundamental feature of mature skeletal muscle that develops early in the fetal period. Waiting until birth for striations would mean the fetus lacked functional skeletal muscle throughout gestation. Striations are present by the end of the first trimester.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in large arteries
statement: Which two cell populations contribute to the formation of smooth muscle in the dorsal aorta?
options: Paraxial mesoderm and ectoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Visceral mesoderm and endoderm
Sclerotome and myotome
Proepicardial cells and paraxial mesoderm
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle, not the smooth muscle of the large vessels. Ectoderm contributes to very specific smooth muscles like those in the eye, but not the aorta. The aorta's smooth muscle has a more complex origin involving lateral plate and neural crest.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries is derived from a combination of the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. Neural crest cells specifically contribute to the smooth muscle of the great vessels near the heart. This dual origin is a key feature of vascular development.

Visceral mesoderm (splanchnic mesoderm) forms the smooth muscle of the gut wall, but the aorta is typically associated with the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest. Endoderm provides the epithelial lining of the gut but does not contribute to muscle tissue. The aorta is a mesodermal/neural crest structure.

Sclerotome and myotome are derivatives of the somites (paraxial mesoderm). Sclerotome forms bone, and myotome forms skeletal muscle. Neither of these populations contributes to the smooth muscle found in the walls of the dorsal aorta or large arteries.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, but they are not the primary source for the dorsal aorta. Paraxial mesoderm is not involved in vascular smooth muscle formation. The aorta relies on lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells instead.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm.

statement: The muscles of the back, classified as epaxial muscles, are innervated by which specific nervous structures?
options: Ventral primary rami
Dorsal primary rami
White rami communicantes
Grey rami communicantes
Cranial nerve XI
correctOption: Dorsal primary rami
optionExplanations: Ventral primary rami innervate the hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the limbs and the ventral body wall. They do not supply the true back muscles (epaxial muscles). This division is a fundamental principle of spinal nerve distribution.

Epaxial muscles, which are the intrinsic muscles of the back located 'above the axis,' are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. This relationship is established early in development as the myotome differentiates. It remains a consistent anatomical landmark in the adult.

White rami communicantes carry preganglionic sympathetic fibers from the spinal nerve to the sympathetic trunk. They are part of the autonomic nervous system and do not provide motor innervation to skeletal muscles. They are only found in the T1-L2 segments.

Grey rami communicantes carry postganglionic sympathetic fibers from the sympathetic trunk back to the spinal nerves for distribution to the body wall (sweat glands, etc.). They do not provide the somatic motor supply to the epaxial muscles. They are found at all levels of the spinal cord.

Cranial nerve XI (the accessory nerve) innervates the trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles. While the trapezius is located on the back, it is not an intrinsic (epaxial) back muscle; it is a limb-related muscle that migrated to the back. True epaxial muscles are supplied by dorsal rami.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves.

statement: How is the dermomyotome formed during somite development?
options: By the fusion of the sclerotome with the neural tube
By cells from the VLL and DML migrating ventral to the dermatome
By the invagination of the overlying ectoderm into the somite
By the migration of neural crest cells into the ventral somite
By the direct transformation of the notochord into muscle tissue
correctOption: By cells from the VLL and DML migrating ventral to the dermatome
optionExplanations: The sclerotome and the neural tube do not fuse. The sclerotome is the ventral part of the somite that forms bone, while the neural tube is the precursor to the central nervous system. The dermomyotome is a separate dorsal component of the somite.

The dermomyotome is formed when cells from the two muscle-forming areas—the ventrolateral lip (VLL) and the dorsomedial lip (DML)—migrate and proliferate to form a layer of progenitor muscle cells (the myotome) just ventral to the dermatome. This combined structure is a key intermediate in muscle and skin development.

Ectoderm does not invaginate to form the dermomyotome. While the ectoderm provides signaling factors (like Wnt) that influence the development of the dorsal somite, the dermomyotome itself is entirely derived from the paraxial mesoderm. Ectoderm forms the epidermis overlying the dermatome.

Neural crest cells migrate through the somites to form various structures like sensory ganglia, but they do not form the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is a primary derivative of the somitic mesoderm. Neural crest cells use the somites as a pathway but are not their source.

The notochord is a signaling center and the precursor to the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral discs. It does not transform into muscle tissue. It provides essential inductive signals (like SHH) that pattern the ventral somite into sclerotome, but not the dermomyotome.
citation: Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome (Fig. 11.1B,C; Fig. 11.2). Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall.

statement: What are the specific structures in the head region, derived from paraxial mesoderm, that give rise to skeletal musculature?
options: Pharyngeal arches
Somitomeres
Neural crest cells
Optic vesicles
Sclerotomes
correctOption: Somitomeres
optionExplanations: Pharyngeal arches are complex structures containing mesoderm, ectoderm, endoderm, and neural crest cells. While they contain the muscle precursors for the head, the paraxial mesoderm specifically organizes into somitomeres in this region. The arches are the anatomical location where these muscles develop.

In the head region, the paraxial mesoderm does not form distinct, segmented somites as it does in the trunk. Instead, it forms loosely organized structures called somitomeres. These somitomeres are the source of the skeletal muscles of the head, including the extraocular and branchiomeric muscles.

Neural crest cells contribute to the connective tissue, tendons, and skeletal elements of the head, but they do not form the skeletal muscle fibers themselves. The muscle fibers are derived from the paraxial mesoderm (somitomeres). This is a common point of confusion in craniofacial development.

Optic vesicles are outgrowths of the forebrain (neuroectoderm) that form the retina and optic nerve. They do not contribute to the formation of skeletal muscle. The muscles that move the eye (extraocular muscles) are derived from the head somitomeres.

Sclerotomes are the ventral portions of somites in the trunk that form the vertebrae and ribs. In the head, the paraxial mesoderm (somitomeres) contributes to the skull, but the term 'sclerotome' is specifically used for the segmented trunk derivatives. The question asks for the muscle-forming structures.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

coreConcept: Embryological origin of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle
statement: Which of the following correctly matches the muscle type with its primary embryological tissue of origin?
options: Skeletal muscle — Lateral plate mesoderm
Cardiac muscle — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Smooth muscle of the gut — Ectoderm
Skeletal muscle of the head — Neural crest cells
Pupillary muscles — Paraxial mesoderm
correctOption: Cardiac muscle — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle is primarily derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres, rather than the lateral plate mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the connective tissue of limbs but not the primary skeletal muscle fibers themselves. This distinction is fundamental in early embryonic patterning of the musculoskeletal system.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral or splanchnic layer of the lateral plate mesoderm that specifically surrounds the endothelial heart tube. This mesodermal layer differentiates into the myoepicardial mantle, which eventually forms the myocardium. This is a critical developmental step occurring early in the formation of the cardiovascular system.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the ectoderm. Ectoderm only contributes to specific smooth muscles like those in the mammary glands, sweat glands, and the pupil. Most visceral smooth muscle follows the patterning of the endodermal tube it surrounds.

Skeletal muscle of the head is derived from somitomeres, which are part of the paraxial mesoderm, not neural crest cells. While neural crest cells contribute significantly to the craniofacial skeleton and connective tissues, the actual muscle fibers are mesodermal in origin. This reflects the conserved nature of paraxial mesoderm in forming skeletal muscle across the body axis.

The pupillary muscles (sphincter and dilator pupillae) are unique because they are derived from the ectoderm of the optic cup. Most other muscles in the body are mesodermal, making this a notable exception in embryology. Paraxial mesoderm forms the extrinsic ocular muscles, but not the intrinsic pupillary muscles.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube.

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and Mesodermal Domains
statement: A developmental biologist is studying the 'abaxial domain' of a developing embryo. Which of the following best describes the cellular composition of this specific domain?
options: Exclusively somite-derived cells located around the neural tube
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm mixed with migrated somite cells
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Ectodermal cells and paraxial mesoderm that has not yet segmented
Sclerotome cells that have undergone epithelization to form the vertebrae
correctOption: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm mixed with migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The region around the neural tube containing only somite-derived cells is defined as the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. These cells receive their developmental signals primarily from the neural tube and notochord. This domain is restricted to the immediate vicinity of the embryonic axis.

The abaxial domain is defined by the lateral somitic frontier and consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm combined with somite cells (from the VLL) that have migrated across this boundary. These cells eventually form muscles of the body wall and limbs. This domain is characterized by signals originating from the lateral plate mesoderm.

The visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm is involved in forming smooth muscle of the gut and cardiac muscle, but it is not part of the abaxial domain definition. Neural crest cells contribute to various structures but are not the defining somitic component of the abaxial domain. The abaxial domain specifically involves the parietal layer.

The abaxial domain consists of differentiated somitic cells and lateral plate mesoderm, not unsegmented paraxial mesoderm or ectoderm. By the time these domains are established, the paraxial mesoderm has already segmented into somites. Ectoderm lies superficial to these mesodermal domains.

Sclerotome cells are the mesenchymal cells that form the vertebrae and ribs, but they are not the defining feature of the abaxial domain. The abaxial/primaxial distinction is primarily used to describe the development and migration of muscle precursors. Sclerotome cells generally remain in the primaxial region to form the axial skeleton.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier. This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

coreConcept: Derivatives of the Abaxial Muscle Cell Precursors
statement: Which of the following muscles is derived from the abaxial muscle cell precursors?
options: Intercostal muscles
Muscles of the back (erector spinae)
Rectus abdominis
Rhomboids
Levator scapulae
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial muscle cell precursors, specifically those that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain. Although they are part of the body wall, they do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They receive their developmental cues from the neural tube and notochord.

The muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are classic examples of primaxial muscles. They develop from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and the remaining ventrolateral lip (VLL) cells that do not migrate across the frontier. These muscles are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves.

The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle because its precursor cells migrate from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the somite across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Other abaxial muscles include the infrahyoid and limb muscles. These muscles are typically innervated by the ventral primary rami.

The rhomboids are considered primaxial muscles because they are part of the shoulder girdle musculature that develops from cells remaining in the paraxial mesoderm. They do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm during their early development. Their signaling environment is distinct from that of abaxial muscles.

The levator scapulae is a muscle of the shoulder girdle and is classified as being derived from primaxial precursors. Like the rhomboids, these cells do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the abaxial domain. They are part of the group that receives signals from the axial structures of the embryo.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors.

coreConcept: Innervation of Epaxial and Hypaxial Muscles
statement: Despite the modern classification into primaxial and abaxial domains, the functional definition of innervation remains consistent. Which statement correctly describes the innervation of these muscle groups?
options: Epaxial muscles are innervated by ventral primary rami.
Hypaxial muscles are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
All muscles derived from a single myotome are innervated by spinal nerves of the same segment.
Abaxial muscles are exclusively innervated by dorsal primary rami.
Primaxial muscles are exclusively innervated by cranial nerves.
correctOption: All muscles derived from a single myotome are innervated by spinal nerves of the same segment.
optionExplanations: Epaxial muscles, which are the muscles of the back, are actually innervated by the dorsal primary rami, not the ventral primary rami. The ventral rami are reserved for the hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs. This anatomical relationship is established early during the outgrowth of spinal nerves.

Hypaxial muscles, including those of the limbs and abdominal wall, are innervated by the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves. The dorsal primary rami only supply the epaxial (back) muscles. This division reflects the split of the spinal nerve as it exits the intervertebral foramen.

Regardless of whether muscle cells belong to the primaxial or abaxial domain, they maintain a segmental relationship with their origin. Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves derived from the same embryonic segment. This ensures that even as muscles migrate, they carry their original nerve supply with them.

Abaxial muscles, which include the limb and body wall muscles, are hypaxial and therefore innervated by ventral primary rami. Dorsal primary rami only innervate the epaxial muscles of the back. The abaxial/primaxial classification is based on embryological origin, while epaxial/hypaxial is based on innervation.

Primaxial muscles are innervated by spinal nerves (specifically dorsal or ventral rami depending on the specific muscle), not exclusively by cranial nerves.
