While some head muscles from somitomeres are innervated by cranial nerves, the vast majority of primaxial muscles in the trunk are supplied by spinal nerves. This distinction is based on the level of the somite along the body axis.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells.

statement: During the development of cardiac muscle, which of the following features distinguishes it from the development of skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form long multinucleated fibers.
Cardiac muscle is derived from the paraxial mesoderm.
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain connected by intercalated discs.
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils during the third month of development.
Cardiac muscle cells are derived from the neural crest cells of the heart tube.
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain connected by intercalated discs.
optionExplanations: Unlike skeletal muscle, where myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated syncytia, cardiac myoblasts do not undergo fusion. Instead, they maintain their individual cellular boundaries throughout development and into adulthood. This lack of fusion is a key histological difference between the two striated muscle types.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source for skeletal muscles of the axis and limbs. The visceral mesoderm specifically wraps around the endocardial heart tube to form the myocardium.

In cardiac muscle development, myoblasts adhere to each other through specialized cell-to-cell junctions. These attachments eventually mature into intercalated discs, which allow for electrical and mechanical coupling. This structural arrangement is essential for the coordinated contraction of the heart as a functional syncytium.

Cardiac muscle, like skeletal muscle, develops myofibrils within the cytoplasm of the myoblasts. These myofibrils are responsible for the contractile properties of the heart. By the time the heart begins to beat early in development, these contractile elements are already being organized.

While neural crest cells contribute to the septation of the heart and the formation of the outflow tract, the actual cardiac muscle cells (myocytes) are derived from visceral mesoderm. The neural crest does not differentiate into the contractile myocardium. The myocardium forms from the mesodermal mantle surrounding the heart tube.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

coreConcept: Ectodermal Origin of Specific Smooth Muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal in origin, certain specific smooth muscles are derived from the ectoderm. Which of the following is an example of such an ectoderm-derived muscle?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Smooth muscle of the coronary arteries
Sphincter pupillae muscle
Smooth muscle in the wall of the ileum
Bronchial smooth muscle
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries is derived from a combination of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. It is not derived from the surface ectoderm. This reflects the complex origins of the vascular system depending on the anatomical location.

Smooth muscle in the coronary arteries originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. It is not an ectodermal derivative. The proepicardium is a mesothelial structure that contributes significantly to the external layers of the heart and its vasculature.

The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are rare examples of muscles that originate from the ectoderm (specifically the neuroectoderm of the optic cup). Most other muscles in the human body are of mesodermal origin. This unique embryological source is a common point of emphasis in developmental biology.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut, such as in the ileum, is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This mesoderm surrounds the endodermal gut tube and differentiates into the muscularis externa. It is never derived from the ectoderm.

Bronchial smooth muscle, like the muscle of the gut, is derived from the visceral mesoderm that surrounds the developing respiratory diverticulum. The respiratory system is a derivative of the foregut, and thus its associated smooth muscle follows the same mesodermal patterning. Ectoderm does not contribute to the respiratory musculature.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: Which process leads to the formation of the dermomyotome from a newly segmented somite?
options: Epithelization of the ventral region to form the sclerotome
Migration of cells from the VLL and DML to a position ventral to the dermatome
Fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers within the central cavity
Invasion of neural crest cells into the dorsal region of the somite
Transformation of the entire somite into mesenchymal bone-forming cells
correctOption: Migration of cells from the VLL and DML to a position ventral to the dermatome
optionExplanations: The ventral region of the somite actually undergoes a process of becoming mesenchymal again (de-epithelization) to form the sclerotome, not epithelization. The sclerotome cells then migrate to surround the notochord and form the vertebrae. This is a separate process from dermomyotome formation.

The dermomyotome is formed when cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips of the somite's upper region migrate and proliferate. These cells position themselves beneath the remaining dorsal epithelial layer (the dermatome). This combined structure is the precursor for both the dermis of the back and the skeletal muscles.

Fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers is a later stage of muscle differentiation that occurs after the myotome has been established. It does not describe the initial formation of the dermomyotome structure itself. The dermomyotome consists of progenitor cells that have not yet fully differentiated into mature fibers.

Neural crest cells do not form the dermomyotome; they contribute to the peripheral nervous system and other structures. The dermomyotome is entirely a product of the paraxial mesoderm (somite). While neural crest cells migrate through the somites, they are not the source of the muscle or dermis precursors.

Only the ventral portion of the somite (the sclerotome) transforms into mesenchymal bone-forming cells. The dorsal portion remains epithelial for a longer period to form the dermatome and the muscle-forming lips. If the entire somite became bone, no muscle or dermis would develop from that segment.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome.

coreConcept: The Conducting System of the Heart
statement: What is the embryological origin and nature of Purkinje fibers in the heart?
options: They are specialized nerve fibers derived from the neural crest.
They are modified cardiac muscle cells derived from visceral mesoderm.
They are connective tissue strands derived from the endocardial cushions.
They are smooth muscle cells that have migrated from the dorsal aorta.
They are ectodermal cells that invaginate into the myocardium.
correctOption: They are modified cardiac muscle cells derived from visceral mesoderm.
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are often confused with nervous tissue because of their conductive function, but they are not derived from the neural crest. They are actually specialized cardiac muscle cells. Their primary role is the rapid conduction of electrical impulses through the ventricles.

Purkinje fibers develop from the same visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm that forms the rest of the myocardium. They are characterized by having fewer and more irregularly distributed myofibrils compared to typical contractile cardiomyocytes. This specialization allows them to function as the heart's internal conducting system.

Endocardial cushions contribute to the formation of heart valves and the septation of the heart chambers, but they do not form the Purkinje fibers. The conducting system is an integral part of the muscular wall (myocardium). Connective tissue in the heart serves a structural rather than a conductive role.

Smooth muscle cells from the dorsal aorta do not contribute to the internal conducting system of the heart. While the aorta and heart are connected, their muscular components have different developmental trajectories and functions. Purkinje fibers are unique to the cardiac chambers.

Ectoderm does not contribute to the internal structures of the heart like the Purkinje fibers. The heart is almost entirely a mesodermal organ, with some neural crest contribution to the outflow tract. The conducting system arises via the differentiation of existing cardiac mesodermal cells.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

coreConcept: Signaling and Differentiation of Muscle Precursors
statement: Which of the following correctly identifies the primary source of developmental signals for primaxial muscle cell precursors?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Surface ectoderm only
Endodermal lining of the gut
Proepicardial cells
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial muscle cell precursors, which are the cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. Primaxial cells, by definition, stay within the paraxial domain and do not interact with the lateral plate mesoderm in the same way. This signaling difference helps determine the different fates of these two cell populations.

Primaxial muscle cell precursors remain in the paraxial mesoderm surrounding the embryonic axis. Consequently, they receive their essential developmental and differentiation signals from the nearby axial structures, specifically the neural tube and the notochord. This signaling environment is distinct from the one encountered by migrating abaxial cells.

While the surface ectoderm does provide signals to the underlying dermatome and some muscle precursors, it is not the primary source of signals for the primaxial group. The neural tube and notochord are the dominant signaling centers for the medial portion of the somite. The ectoderm's influence is more pronounced on the dorsal-most cells.

The endodermal lining of the gut is located ventrally and provides signals for the development of the gut-associated smooth muscle and some ventral structures. It is not the primary signaling source for the primaxial skeletal muscle precursors located dorsally around the neural tube. The spatial separation limits its influence on these specific cells.

Proepicardial cells are involved in the development of the heart's epicardium and coronary vasculature. They have no role in providing signals for the differentiation of the primaxial skeletal muscle precursors of the somites. These are entirely different developmental systems located in different regions of the embryo.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord.

statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation affect the functional arrangement of the myotomes?
options: It causes myotomes to fuse into a single continuous muscle mass along the spine.
It allows myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, enabling spinal movement.
It forces myotomes to migrate into the limbs to form the appendicular skeleton.
It results in the loss of segmental innervation from the spinal nerves.
It converts the myotomes into cartilaginous precursors of the ribs.
correctOption: It allows myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, enabling spinal movement.
optionExplanations: Myotomes do not fuse into a single mass; they maintain their segmental identity even as they develop into complex muscle groups. The segmental nature of the muscles is essential for the refined control of the vertebral column. Fusion would prevent the localized movement required for spinal flexibility.

During resegmentation, the caudal half of one sclerotome fuses with the cranial half of the next. This shift causes the muscles derived from the myotomes, which were originally aligned with the somites, to now span across the newly formed intervertebral joints. This 'bridging' is what allows the muscles to exert force across the joints and move the spine.

Resegmentation is a process specific to the formation of the axial skeleton (vertebrae). While some muscle cells do migrate to the limbs, this is not caused by the resegmentation of the sclerotomes. Limb muscle migration is a separate event involving the ventrolateral lips of the somites.

Resegmentation does not change the innervation pattern; each myotome continues to be supplied by the spinal nerve of its original segment. The nerves actually exit the vertebral column between the resegmented vertebrae. This ensures that the functional relationship between the nerve and its muscle is preserved.

Myotomes are the precursors of muscle, not cartilage or bone. The sclerotome is the part of the somite that forms the cartilaginous and bony components of the ribs and vertebrae. The myotome and sclerotome have distinct fates, although they develop in close proximity within the somite.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells.

coreConcept: Smooth Muscle of the Vascular System
statement: Which of the following is a source of smooth muscle cells for the coronary arteries?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Proepicardial cells
Endoderm of the heart tube
Sclerotome of the thoracic somites
Dermatome of the cervical somites
correctOption: Proepicardial cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton, but it does not contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries. The vascular smooth muscle of the heart has more specialized origins. Paraxial mesoderm is restricted to the somitic and somitomeric regions.

The smooth muscle in the walls of the coronary arteries has a dual origin: it arises from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells (specifically for the proximal segments). The proepicardium is a cluster of cells that migrates over the heart to form the epicardium and its associated vessels. This is a specialized developmental pathway distinct from the gut or large vessels.

The endoderm of the heart tube is a misnomer, as the heart tube is lined by endothelium (which is mesodermal). Even so, the inner lining of the heart does not differentiate into the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries. The coronary vessels develop on the external surface of the heart within the epicardial layer.

The sclerotome forms the vertebrae and ribs and does not contribute to the vascular smooth muscle of the heart. While the heart is located in the thorax, its tissues are derived from the lateral plate mesoderm (visceral layer) and other specific cardiac precursors. Sclerotome cells remain associated with the axial skeleton.

The dermatome is the precursor for the dermis of the skin and does not contribute to internal vascular structures like the coronary arteries. Each part of the somite has a highly specified fate. The dermatome's contribution is limited to the integumentary system of the dorsal body wall.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments).

coreConcept: Differentiation of Myoblasts into Muscle Fibers
statement: At what point in human embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
End of the fifth month
At the time of birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: By the end of the first month, the somites are still forming and differentiating into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblasts have not yet begun the extensive fusion and myofibril organization necessary for striations to be visible. The embryo is still in the very early stages of organogenesis.

During the second month, myoblasts are actively proliferating and beginning to fuse into myotubes. While myofibrils are starting to appear in the cytoplasm, the highly organized sarcomeric structure that produces visible cross-striations is not yet fully mature. This is a period of rapid muscle mass growth.

Cross-striations, which are the histological hallmark of skeletal muscle, appear by the end of the third month of development. This indicates that the myofibrils have become sufficiently organized into sarcomeres. This timing coincides with the maturation of the multinucleated muscle fibers formed by the fusion of myoblasts.

The end of the fifth month is well after the initial appearance of cross-striations. By this time, the fetus is capable of significant movement (quickening), which requires functional, striated muscle. The basic histological structure of the muscle is already well-established by this stage.

Skeletal muscle is fully striated and functional long before birth. If striations only appeared at birth, the fetus would be unable to move in utero. The developmental timeline ensures that the musculoskeletal system is prepared for the physical demands of the postnatal environment.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

coreConcept: The Ventrolateral Lip (VLL) and Limb Muscle Development
statement: Which specific region of the somite provides the progenitor cells that migrate to form the muscles of the limbs?
options: Dorsomedial lip (DML)
Ventrolateral lip (VLL)
Central sclerotome
Dermatome only
Notochordal process
correctOption: Ventrolateral lip (VLL)
optionExplanations: The dorsomedial lip (DML) cells remain in the primaxial domain and contribute to the muscles of the back and shoulder girdle. They do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm or contribute to the limb muscles. Their migration is much more restricted compared to the VLL cells.

Cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the somite are the ones that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. Once they enter the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, they differentiate into the muscles of the limbs and the abdominal wall. This migration is a key step in the development of the appendicular musculature.

The sclerotome is the ventral part of the somite that forms the bone and cartilage of the axial skeleton. It does not contain the progenitor cells for skeletal muscle. Muscle precursors are found exclusively in the myotome portions (VLL and DML) of the somite.

The dermatome is the epithelial layer that forms the dermis of the back. While it is part of the dermomyotome, the specific cells that migrate to form limb muscles come from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) area, not the central dermatome itself. The dermatome remains relatively stationary compared to the migrating VLL cells.

The notochordal process is an axial structure that provides signaling for the patterning of the somites, but it does not provide the actual cells for muscle formation. It is a transient structure that eventually becomes the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral discs. Muscle cells are always mesodermal somite derivatives.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back.

coreConcept: Comparison of Primaxial and Abaxial Muscle Groups
statement: According to the primaxial/abaxial classification, which of the following muscles is correctly categorized as primaxial?
options: Rectus abdominis
External oblique
Infrahyoid muscles
Intercostal muscles
Triceps brachii
correctOption: Intercostal muscles
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle because its precursors migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. It is part of the ventral body wall musculature. Abaxial muscles are typically those that have moved furthest from the original somite position.

The external oblique is a member of the abdominal wall muscles and is classified as abaxial. Like the rectus abdominis, it originates from VLL cells that have migrated into the lateral plate mesoderm. These muscles receive signals from the lateral plate rather than the neural tube.

The infrahyoid muscles are derived from abaxial precursors that migrate into the cervical region. They are not primaxial. This group of muscles is involved in the movement of the hyoid bone and larynx and is innervated by the ventral rami (via the ansa cervicalis).

Intercostal muscles are classified as primaxial because they develop from somite cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain. Even though they are located in the body wall, they do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. This makes them distinct from the abaxial abdominal wall muscles.

The triceps brachii is a limb muscle and is therefore derived from abaxial precursors. All limb muscles originate from VLL cells that migrate into the limb buds (which are formed from lateral plate mesoderm). This migration is a hallmark of the abaxial muscle group.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

coreConcept: Developmental Origin of Smooth Muscle in the Gut
statement: The smooth muscle found in the wall of the esophagus and stomach is derived from which embryonic tissue?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Surface ectoderm
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is responsible for skeletal muscle, not the smooth muscle of the internal organs. While some skeletal muscle exists in the upper esophagus, the question refers to the general smooth muscle of the gut wall. Paraxial mesoderm forms the somites.

The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm forms the dermis of the body wall, the lining of the body cavities, and the skeletal components of the limbs. It does not contribute to the smooth muscle of the gut. The gut is associated with the visceral layer of the mesoderm.

The visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is the specific source for the smooth muscle and connective tissue of the gut wall. As the gut tube forms, this mesoderm wraps around the endoderm. This relationship is maintained throughout the length of the alimentary canal.

Neural crest cells contribute to the enteric nervous system (ganglia) within the gut wall, but they do not form the smooth muscle layers themselves. The muscle layers (circular and longitudinal) are entirely mesodermal. The neural crest cells migrate into this mesodermal layer later.

Surface ectoderm forms the epidermis and certain specialized muscles like those in the sweat glands, but it does not contribute to the gut wall. The gut is an internal structure derived from endoderm and its associated visceral mesoderm. Ectoderm is restricted to the exterior and specific sensory structures.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: A researcher is studying the development of the human muscular system. Which of the following embryonic layers is the primary source for the development of skeletal muscles in the trunk and limbs?
options: Ectoderm
Paraxial mesoderm
Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Parietal (somatic) mesoderm
Neural crest cells
correctOption: Paraxial mesoderm
optionExplanations: Ectoderm is primarily responsible for the nervous system and epidermis, though it does contribute to specific smooth muscles like the pupillary and sweat gland muscles. It does not contribute to the formation of skeletal muscle fibers in the trunk or limbs. Therefore, this option is incorrect for the origin of skeletal muscle.

Paraxial mesoderm is the correct origin for skeletal muscle, forming somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. These somites further differentiate into myotomes, which provide the progenitor cells for the musculature of the axial skeleton, body wall, and limbs. This developmental pathway is distinct from the origins of cardiac and smooth muscle.

Visceral or splanchnic mesoderm is the embryonic source for cardiac muscle and the smooth muscle surrounding the gut and its derivatives. While it is a mesodermal layer, it does not give rise to the skeletal muscles of the body wall or limbs. Skeletal muscle is specifically derived from the paraxial division of the mesoderm.

Parietal or somatic mesoderm forms the body wall structures and the parietal layer of serous membranes, but the muscle cells themselves migrate into this layer from the somites. While the lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for differentiation, the actual muscle precursors originate from the paraxial mesoderm. Thus, it is not the primary source of the muscle cells.

Neural crest cells contribute to various structures including the peripheral nervous system and certain smooth muscles in the large arteries. However, they are not the source of skeletal muscle fibers in the axial or appendicular skeleton. Skeletal muscle development is strictly a product of the paraxial mesoderm.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

coreConcept: Somite differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome
statement: During the differentiation of a somite, which specific region undergoes a mesenchymal transition to eventually form the vertebrae and ribs?
options: Dorsomedial lip (DML)
Ventrolateral lip (VLL)
Ventral region (Sclerotome)
Dermatome
Dermomyotome
correctOption: Ventral region (Sclerotome)
optionExplanations: The dorsomedial lip (DML) is one of the two muscle-forming areas located at the edges of the upper somite region. It contributes to the formation of the myotome and specifically provides precursors for primaxial muscles. It does not form the skeletal elements like vertebrae or ribs.

The ventrolateral lip (VLL) is a muscle-forming area that contributes to both primaxial and abaxial muscle populations. Cells from this region migrate to form muscles of the abdominal wall and limbs. It is not involved in the formation of the bony axial skeleton.

The ventral region of the somite undergoes a transition back to mesenchymal tissue to form the sclerotome. These sclerotome cells are the specific progenitors for the bone-forming cells of the vertebrae and ribs. This process occurs immediately after the initial epithelization of the somite.

The dermatome is formed from the upper region of the somite and eventually gives rise to the dermis of the skin. While it is closely associated with the muscle-forming areas, it does not contribute to the formation of the vertebrae or ribs. Its primary role is the development of the integumentary system's deeper layer.

The dermomyotome is a composite structure formed by the dermatome and the underlying progenitor muscle cells from the VLL and DML. It represents a stage in the development of skin and muscle precursors. It is distinct from the sclerotome, which is the ventral portion dedicated to skeletal development.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges).

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and Mesodermal Domains
statement: The lateral somitic frontier serves as a critical boundary during embryonic development. Which of the following best describes the 'abaxial domain' created by this frontier?
options: A region containing only cells derived from the neural tube
A region around the neural tube containing only paraxial mesoderm cells
A region consisting of the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
A region containing parietal lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
A region formed exclusively by the dorsomedial lip of the somite
correctOption: A region containing parietal lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The abaxial domain is not derived from the neural tube; rather, the neural tube provides signals to the primaxial domain. The abaxial domain is defined by its relationship with the lateral plate mesoderm. Therefore, this description is embryologically incorrect.

The region around the neural tube containing only paraxial mesoderm cells defines the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. Primaxial cells receive signals from the neural tube and notochord. This domain is located medial to the lateral somitic frontier.

The abaxial domain involves the parietal (somatic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the visceral (splanchnic) layer. The visceral layer is involved in the formation of smooth muscle of the gut and cardiac muscle. This distinction is vital for understanding the different mesodermal territories.

The abaxial domain is correctly defined as the territory consisting of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm combined with somite cells (from the VLL) that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier. These cells receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm rather than the neural tube. This domain gives rise to muscles of the limbs and abdominal wall.

The dorsomedial lip (DML) cells remain in the primaxial domain and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. Only a portion of the ventrolateral lip (VLL) cells migrate to form the abaxial precursors. Thus, the DML is not associated with the formation of the abaxial domain.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

coreConcept: Muscles derived from the Abaxial Domain
statement: Based on the modern embryological classification of muscle domains, which of the following muscles is considered a component of the abaxial muscle group?
options: Intercostal muscles
Muscles of the back (epaxial)
Rectus abdominis
Shoulder girdle muscles
Prevertebral muscles
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial domain, consisting of cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They receive their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord. They are not part of the abaxial group.

Muscles of the back, also known as epaxial muscles, are the classic examples of the primaxial domain. They are derived from somite cells that stay medial to the lateral somitic frontier. These muscles are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves.

The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle because it develops from ventrolateral lip (VLL) cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm. Other examples include the infrahyoid and limb muscles. These muscles receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm.

Shoulder girdle muscles are classified as primaxial muscles according to the modern domain-based description. Although they are associated with the limb skeleton, their cellular precursors do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. They remain within the paraxial mesoderm territory.

Prevertebral muscles are located deep in the neck and trunk and are derived from the primaxial domain. They develop from cells that do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. Like other primaxial muscles, they are distinct from the abaxial muscles like the rectus abdominis.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles.

coreConcept: Innervation of myotomes and spinal nerves
statement: Which principle governs the primary innervation of developing muscle cells, regardless of whether they belong to the primaxial or abaxial domains?
options: Innervation is determined by the final functional position of the muscle
Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its segment of origin
Abaxial muscles are innervated by cranial nerves, while primaxial are spinal
Innervation is provided by the lateral plate mesoderm signals
All muscles are initially innervated by dorsal primary rami only
correctOption: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from its segment of origin
optionExplanations: While the final position of a muscle determines whether it is innervated by a dorsal or ventral ramus, the fundamental embryological principle is segmental. The functional position (epaxial vs. hypaxial) follows the segmental origin. Therefore, functional position is a result, not the primary governing principle.

The core developmental principle is that each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. This segmental relationship is maintained even as cells migrate far from their origin, such as into the limbs. This ensures that the nerve supply follows the muscle precursors during development.

Both primaxial and abaxial muscles of the trunk and limbs are innervated by spinal nerves, not cranial nerves. Cranial nerves primarily innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres. The domain classification (abaxial vs. primaxial) does not change the spinal nature of the innervation for trunk muscles.

The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the differentiation of abaxial muscles, but it does not provide innervation. Innervation is a function of the nervous system, specifically the spinal nerves emerging from the neural tube. Mesodermal signals and neural innervation are distinct processes.

Muscles are not all innervated by dorsal rami; instead, the innervation splits into dorsal and ventral primary rami. Epaxial muscles are supplied by dorsal rami, while hypaxial muscles (including abaxial and some primaxial) are supplied by ventral rami. This division occurs early in development.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles).

statement: In the traditional functional description of muscle development, which nerves innervate the hypaxial muscles, such as those of the limb and body wall?
options: Dorsal primary rami
Ventral primary rami
Sympathetic trunk ganglia
White rami communicantes
Cranial nerve XI
correctOption: Ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the true muscles of the back. They do not supply the hypaxial muscles of the body wall or limbs. This distinction is a fundamental aspect of the functional organization of the peripheral nervous system.

Ventral primary rami are the nerves that innervate the hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the limbs and the ventral body wall. This functional definition remains valid even with the newer embryological classification of primaxial and abaxial domains. Most skeletal muscles in the body are hypaxial and thus supplied by ventral rami.

Sympathetic trunk ganglia are part of the autonomic nervous system and provide visceral motor innervation to smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands. They do not provide the somatic motor innervation required for skeletal muscles like the hypaxial group. Skeletal muscle requires somatic motor neurons.

White rami communicantes carry preganglionic sympathetic fibers from the spinal nerves to the sympathetic trunk. They are not involved in the direct motor innervation of skeletal muscles. Their role is strictly limited to the autonomic pathway.

Cranial nerve XI (the accessory nerve) innervates specific muscles like the trapezius and sternocleidomastoid. While these are skeletal muscles, they are not the general group of hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs. The hypaxial group is defined by its innervation from spinal ventral rami.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

statement: Which of the following characteristics distinguishes the development of cardiac muscle from that of skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm
Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form multinucleated fibers
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but adhere via intercalated discs
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils in its cytoplasm
Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectodermal germ layer
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but adhere via intercalated discs
optionExplanations: Cardiac muscle is derived from visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm, not paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source for skeletal muscle. This difference in embryological origin is a key distinction between the two muscle types.

Unlike skeletal muscle, where myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated fibers, cardiac myoblasts do not fuse. They remain as individual cells throughout development and adulthood. The multinucleated nature of skeletal muscle is a result of this fusion process which is absent in the heart.

The defining developmental feature of cardiac muscle is that the myoblasts adhere to one another through special attachments that evolve into intercalated discs. This allows for electrical and mechanical coupling without the fusion of cell membranes. This structural arrangement is essential for the coordinated contraction of the heart.

Cardiac muscle does contain myofibrils, which develop in a manner similar to those in skeletal muscle. These myofibrils are responsible for the contraction of the cardiomyocyte. The presence of myofibrils is a shared feature of all striated muscles, including cardiac and skeletal.

Cardiac muscle is mesodermal in origin, specifically from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system and certain specific smooth muscles, but it does not contribute to the myocardium. Mesoderm is the primary source for the bulk of the muscular system.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most muscle tissue is mesodermal, which of the following smooth muscles is uniquely derived from the ectoderm?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut
Sphincter pupillae muscle
Coronary artery smooth muscle
Bronchial smooth muscle
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta is derived from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. It is not an ectodermal derivative. The large vessels of the body generally follow this mesodermal/neural crest pattern of development.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This layer surrounds the endodermal heart tube and gut tube during development. It is one of the most extensive sources of smooth muscle in the body.

The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil, along with the muscle tissue in mammary and sweat glands, are rare exceptions in the muscular system as they are derived from ectoderm. Most other muscles in the human body originate from the mesodermal germ layer. This makes the pupillary muscles a frequent point of anatomical interest.

Coronary artery smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. It is not derived from the ectoderm. The development of the coronary vasculature involves complex migrations of cells to the surface of the heart.

Bronchial smooth muscle, like the muscle of the gut, is derived from the visceral mesoderm that surrounds the developing respiratory diverticulum. Since the lungs are a derivative of the foregut, they share the same mesodermal source for their stromal and muscular components.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in large arteries
statement: What are the primary embryonic sources for the smooth muscle found in the dorsal aorta and other large arteries?
options: Paraxial mesoderm and endoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Ectoderm and visceral mesoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
Myotome and somitomeres
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle, and endoderm forms the epithelial lining of the gut and respiratory systems. Neither of these layers is the primary source for the smooth muscle of the large arteries. The vascular system has a distinct developmental origin.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from a combination of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. Neural crest cells are particularly important for the proximal segments of the great vessels. This dual origin is characteristic of the major vascular structures.

Ectoderm only contributes to very specific smooth muscles like those in the eye and skin glands. Visceral mesoderm contributes to the smooth muscle of the gut and the heart. While related, they are not the primary sources for the large arterial walls described.

Sclerotome and dermatome are subdivisions of the somite (paraxial mesoderm). Sclerotome forms bone, and dermatome forms dermis. They do not contribute to the smooth muscle of the vascular system, which arises from more lateral mesodermal populations.

Myotome and somitomeres are the precursors for skeletal muscle. Myotomes form the trunk and limb muscles, while somitomeres form the head muscles. They are not involved in the formation of the involuntary smooth muscle found in the walls of arteries.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer.

coreConcept: Development of the cardiac conducting system
statement: Which specialized cardiac structures, characterized by irregularly distributed myofibrils, form the heart's conducting system?
options: Intercalated discs
Sinoatrial node cells only
Purkinje fibers
Papillary muscles
Chordae tendineae
correctOption: Purkinje fibers
optionExplanations: Intercalated discs are the specialized cell-to-cell junctions between cardiomyocytes that allow for electrical coupling. They are a structural feature of all cardiac muscle cells, not a specific bundle forming the conducting system itself. They facilitate the signal but are not the 'fibers' described.

While the sinoatrial node is part of the conducting system, the specific bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils described in the developmental context are the Purkinje fibers. The SA node is the pacemaker, but the Purkinje fibers are the specialized distal conduction pathways.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that develop irregularly distributed myofibrils. They function as the conducting system of the heart, ensuring rapid spread of electrical impulses. They are modified cardiomyocytes rather than nervous tissue.

Papillary muscles are regular cardiac muscle structures that contract to prevent the inversion of heart valves during systole. They are not part of the specialized conducting system. They consist of standard contractile myocardium rather than the specialized conduction cells.

Chordae tendineae are fibrous cords of connective tissue that connect the papillary muscles to the heart valves. They are not composed of muscle cells and do not conduct electrical impulses. Their role is purely mechanical in the function of the heart valves.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

statement: By which embryonic time point do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
End of the sixth month
At the time of birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: The end of the first month is too early for the appearance of cross-striations. At this stage, the somites are still undergoing initial segmentation and differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblast fusion has barely begun.

By the end of the second month, myoblasts are fusing and myofibrils are beginning to appear in the cytoplasm. However, the organized arrangement required for visible cross-striations is usually not complete until later in the first trimester. The third month is the definitive milestone.

Cross-striations, which are the hallmark of mature skeletal muscle, appear by the end of the third month of development. This follows the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers and the subsequent synthesis and organization of myofibrillar proteins. This marks a significant stage in functional muscle maturation.

The end of the sixth month is well into the fetal period, long after the initial appearance of cross-striations. By this time, the muscles are well-developed and the fetus is capable of significant movement. Striations are already a established feature by this stage.

Cross-striations appear much earlier than birth. Waiting until birth for muscle maturation would mean the fetus would have no contractile skeletal muscle function throughout gestation. The developmental process is largely completed in the first and second trimesters.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

statement: According to the domain-based model of muscle development, what is the primary source of differentiation signals for primaxial muscle cell precursors?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Ectoderm and yolk sac
Endoderm and heart tube
Amniotic fluid and chorion
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: Lateral plate mesoderm provides the differentiation signals for the abaxial muscle precursors, which are the cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. It does not signal the primaxial cells. This spatial separation of signaling sources is a key feature of the domain model.

Primaxial muscle cell precursors, which include all DML cells and some VLL cells that stay within the paraxial mesoderm, receive their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord. These structures are located medially, close to the primaxial domain. This ensures proper patterning of the axial musculature.

Ectoderm and the yolk sac are not the primary signaling centers for the differentiation of somite-derived muscle precursors. While the ectoderm is adjacent to the dermatome, the specific signals for the primaxial myotome come from the deeper axial structures. The yolk sac is involved in early nutrition and hematopoiesis.

Endoderm and the heart tube are involved in signaling for other tissues, such as the gut and cardiac muscle. They do not provide the specific patterning signals required for the skeletal muscle precursors in the primaxial domain. The neural tube and notochord are the dominant organizers for the axial mesoderm.

Amniotic fluid and the chorion are extraembryonic components that provide protection and exchange but do not act as localized signaling centers for tissue-specific differentiation like myogenesis. Muscle differentiation is controlled by internal embryonic signaling gradients.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors.

statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation affect the functional relationship between myotomes and the vertebral column?
options: It causes myotomes to disappear and be replaced by bone
It ensures each myotome is attached to only one vertebra
It allows myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs to enable spinal movement
It prevents the spinal nerves from reaching the muscles
It forces all myotomes to migrate to the limbs
correctOption: It allows myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs to enable spinal movement
optionExplanations: Myotomes do not disappear; they differentiate into the muscles of the axial skeleton. The sclerotome is the part that forms the bone. The two structures develop in tandem to create the musculoskeletal system of the trunk.

If a myotome were attached to only one vertebra, it would be unable to produce movement across a joint. The resegmentation process is specifically designed to shift the relationship so that muscles can act on multiple skeletal elements. This is the basis for spinal flexibility.

Resegmentation of the sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae results in a shift where the myotomes come to bridge the intervertebral discs. This anatomical arrangement is crucial because it allows the muscles to span the joints between vertebrae. Consequently, this gives the muscles the mechanical leverage needed to move the spine.

Resegmentation does not block spinal nerves; in fact, the nerves pass between the newly formed vertebrae to reach their corresponding myotomes. The segmental innervation is preserved throughout this complex skeletal rearrangement. The nerves and muscles remain linked.

While some myotome cells do migrate to the limbs (the abaxial cells), many remain in the trunk to form the axial musculature. Resegmentation is a process specifically related to the formation of the vertebral column and the axial muscles. It is not the trigger for limb migration.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). With the exception of some smooth muscle tissue (see p. 164), the muscular system develops from the mesodermal germ layer and consists of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle.

statement: Which structure is formed when progenitor muscle cells from the VLL and DML migrate and proliferate beneath the dermatome?
options: Sclerotome
Dermomyotome
Lateral somitic frontier
Endothelial heart tube
Neural crest
correctOption: Dermomyotome
optionExplanations: The sclerotome is formed from the ventral region of the somite, not the upper region. It undergoes a mesenchymal transition to form the vertebrae and ribs. It is distinct from the muscle-forming areas of the upper somite.

The dermomyotome is the correct term for the structure formed by the dermatome and the underlying progenitor muscle cells derived from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips. This structure serves as the common precursor for both the dermis of the back and the skeletal muscles of the trunk and limbs. It represents a key stage in somite maturation.

The lateral somitic frontier is a boundary or border between the somite-derived cells and the lateral plate mesoderm. It is not a proliferative structure formed by the migration of VLL and DML cells. It defines the domains (primaxial and abaxial) rather than being a tissue layer itself.

The endothelial heart tube is the primitive structure of the heart, derived from the visceral mesoderm. It is not related to the somites or the dermomyotome. The heart tube develops independently in the thoracic region of the embryo.

The neural crest is a population of cells that migrates from the edges of the neural folds. While they contribute to some smooth muscles and the peripheral nervous system, they are not part of the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is strictly a paraxial mesoderm derivative.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome (Fig. 11.1B,C; Fig. 11.2).

statement: In the context of the lateral somitic frontier, what defines the 'abaxial muscle cell precursors'?
options: Cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and receive signals from the notochord
Cells from the DML that migrate into the neural tube
Cells from the VLL that cross the frontier into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm
Cells that form the smooth muscle of the gut wall
Cells that differentiate into the vertebrae and ribs
correctOption: Cells from the VLL that cross the frontier into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: Cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and receive signals from the notochord are primaxial muscle cell precursors, not abaxial. This group includes all DML cells and the remaining VLL cells. They form the muscles of the back and intercostals.

DML (dorsomedial lip) cells do not migrate into the neural tube; they stay within the primaxial domain of the paraxial mesoderm. The neural tube is a signaling center for these cells, not a destination. This option misrepresents the migratory path of somite cells.

Abaxial muscle cell precursors are specifically defined as those cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the myotome that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. Once they enter the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, they receive new differentiation signals. This population gives rise to the limb and abdominal wall muscles.

Smooth muscle of the gut wall is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not from migrated somite cells. The abaxial domain involves the parietal layer. These are two different layers of the lateral plate mesoderm with different fates.

Cells that differentiate into the vertebrae and ribs are derived from the sclerotome, which is the ventral part of the somite. These are bone-forming cells, not muscle precursors. The abaxial domain is specifically concerned with the development of certain skeletal muscles.
citation: The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier. Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm.

coreConcept: Embryological origin of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle
statement: A medical student is reviewing the embryological origins of the muscular system. Which of the following correctly matches the muscle type with its primary embryonic source?
options: Skeletal muscle — Paraxial mesoderm
Cardiac muscle — Ectoderm
Smooth muscle of the gut — Somatic mesoderm
Pupillary muscles — Visceral mesoderm
Skeletal muscle of the limbs — Neural crest cells
correctOption: Skeletal muscle — Paraxial mesoderm
optionExplanations: Option A is correct because skeletal muscle is derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres. This tissue provides the precursors for the musculature of the axial skeleton, body wall, and limbs. The paraxial mesoderm is the fundamental source for nearly all skeletal muscle in the body.

Option B is incorrect because cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not the ectoderm. Ectoderm primarily gives rise to the nervous system and epidermis. Only very specific smooth muscles, like those in the eye and certain glands, are ectodermal in origin.

Option C is incorrect because smooth muscle of the gut is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Somatic mesoderm typically contributes to the body wall structures rather than the internal organ musculature. The visceral layer specifically surrounds the endodermal gut tube during development.

Option D is incorrect because the pupillary muscles (sphincter and dilator pupillae) are unique exceptions derived from the ectoderm. Most smooth muscle comes from mesoderm, but these ocular muscles are among the few derived from the neuroectoderm of the optic cup. Visceral mesoderm forms the muscle of the internal viscera.

Option E is incorrect because limb skeletal muscles are derived from the ventrolateral lip of the somites (paraxial mesoderm). While neural crest cells contribute to many structures in the head and neck, they do not form the skeletal muscle of the limbs. These muscle precursors migrate from the somites into the lateral plate mesoderm of the limb buds.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

coreConcept: Somite differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome
statement: During the differentiation of a somite, the ventral region undergoes a transition from epithelial back to mesenchymal tissue. What is the primary fate of the cells resulting from this specific transition?
options: Formation of the dermis of the back
Formation of the vertebrae and ribs
Formation of the intrinsic back muscles
Formation of the abdominal wall muscles
Formation of the limb musculature
correctOption: Formation of the vertebrae and ribs
optionExplanations: Option B is correct because the ventral region of the somite becomes mesenchymal to form the sclerotome. These sclerotome cells are the precursors for the bone-forming cells of the axial skeleton, specifically the vertebrae and ribs. This process occurs immediately after the initial epithelization of the somite.

Option A is incorrect because the dermis of the back is derived from the dermatome, which is part of the upper (dorsal) region of the somite. The dermatome remains epithelial longer than the sclerotome before contributing to the skin. It does not arise from the ventral mesenchymal transition described.

Option C is incorrect because the intrinsic back muscles are derived from the myotome, specifically the primaxial muscle precursors. While the myotome is part of the somite, it originates from the dorsomedial and ventrolateral lips, not the ventral sclerotome region. The sclerotome is dedicated to skeletal, not muscular, structures.

Option D is incorrect because abdominal wall muscles are derived from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the dermomyotome. These cells migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm to form the abaxial muscles. They are distinct from the ventral sclerotome cells that form the axial skeleton.

Option E is incorrect because limb musculature also originates from the ventrolateral lip of the dermomyotome. These cells migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the limb buds. The ventral sclerotome cells stay more medial to form the vertebral column.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A). The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs.

coreConcept: Formation of the dermomyotome from VLL and DML
statement: Which two specific regions of the somite contribute cells that migrate and proliferate to form the dermomyotome?
options: Sclerotome and dermatome
Ventrolateral lip (VLL) and dorsomedial lip (DML)
Parietal and visceral lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest and somitomeres
Epimere and hypomere
correctOption: Ventrolateral lip (VLL) and dorsomedial lip (DML)
optionExplanations: Option B is correct because the dermomyotome is formed by cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) and the dorsomedial lip (DML) of the somite. These cells migrate beneath the dermatome to form the muscle-forming layer. This layer eventually gives rise to all the skeletal muscles derived from that somite level.

Option A is incorrect because the sclerotome forms the vertebrae and ribs, while the dermatome forms the dermis. While the dermatome is part of the dermomyotome complex, the muscle-forming cells specifically come from the lips (VLL and DML). The sclerotome is a separate ventral population.

Option C is incorrect because the lateral plate mesoderm is a different mesodermal layer altogether. While the VLL cells may migrate into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, the lateral plate itself does not form the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is strictly a paraxial mesoderm derivative.

Option D is incorrect because neural crest cells and somitomeres are distinct populations. Somitomeres are the unsegmented paraxial mesoderm in the head, while neural crest cells are ectodermal. Neither is responsible for the formation of the dermomyotome in the trunk somites.

Option E is incorrect because epimere and hypomere are older functional terms for muscle groups based on innervation. They are not the names of the specific somite regions that migrate to form the dermomyotome. The modern embryological description uses VLL and DML to define the precursor populations.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and mesodermal domains
statement: A researcher is studying the 'Lateral Somitic Frontier' in a developing embryo. Which of the following best describes the composition of the abaxial domain relative to this frontier?
options: It contains only cells derived from the dorsomedial lip of the somite.
It consists of the neural tube and the surrounding paraxial mesoderm.
It consists of parietal lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells.
It is composed entirely of visceral mesoderm surrounding the gut tube.
It contains only sclerotome cells that have undergone epithelization.
correctOption: It consists of parietal lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells.
optionExplanations: Option C is correct because the abaxial domain is defined as the region lateral to the frontier. It contains the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm as well as the muscle precursor cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) that have migrated across the frontier. This domain gives rise to muscles of the limbs and body wall.

Option A is incorrect because cells from the dorsomedial lip (DML) do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. DML cells remain in the primaxial domain to form the muscles of the back and shoulder girdle. Only a subset of VLL cells migrates into the abaxial domain.

Option B is incorrect because the region around the neural tube containing only somite-derived cells is the primaxial domain. The primaxial domain is medial to the lateral somitic frontier. It does not include lateral plate mesoderm components.

Option D is incorrect because the visceral mesoderm is involved in forming the smooth muscle of the gut and cardiac muscle. It is not part of the abaxial domain defined by the lateral somitic frontier. The frontier specifically involves the parietal (somatic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm.

Option E is incorrect because sclerotome cells form the axial skeleton and are generally considered part of the primaxial environment. Furthermore, the sclerotome undergoes a mesenchymal transition, not epithelization, to form bone. The abaxial domain is primarily concerned with muscle and dermis development.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

coreConcept: Signaling sources for primaxial vs. abaxial muscle precursors
statement: The differentiation of muscle precursors is highly dependent on their environment. From which structures do the primaxial muscle cell precursors receive their primary developmental signals?
options: Parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Endodermal gut tube and visceral mesoderm
Ectoderm and proepicardial cells
Sclerotome and dermatome
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: Option B is correct because primaxial muscle precursors remain in the paraxial mesoderm environment. Consequently, they receive their inductive signals for differentiation from the midline structures, specifically the neural tube and the notochord. These signals guide the development of the back and intercostal muscles.

Option A is incorrect because the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm provides signals to the abaxial muscle precursors. These are the cells that have crossed the lateral somitic frontier. Primaxial cells are sequestered from these signals by the frontier itself.

Option C is incorrect because the endodermal gut tube and visceral mesoderm are involved in the development of smooth muscle and internal organs. They do not provide the primary signals for the skeletal muscle precursors of the somite. Skeletal muscle signaling is dominated by the neural tube, notochord, and lateral plate.

Option D is incorrect because proepicardial cells contribute to the smooth muscle of coronary arteries, not skeletal muscle. While ectoderm can signal to various tissues, the specific signals for primaxial skeletal muscle differentiation are well-documented to come from the neural tube and notochord. Ectoderm is more involved in abaxial signaling for the dermis.

Option E is incorrect because the sclerotome and dermatome are fellow derivatives of the somite, not the primary signaling sources for the myotome's differentiation. While there is local interaction, the major inductive signals that define the primaxial domain come from the axial structures. The myotome forms from the lips of the dermomyotome.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord

coreConcept: Innervation of myotomes and spinal nerves
statement: Which of the following statements regarding the innervation of developing muscle is correct?
options: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from the adjacent superior segment.
Innervation is the primary criteria for the modern primaxial/abaxial classification.
Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from the same segment as the muscle cells.
Abaxial muscles lose their original segmental innervation after migration.
Primaxial muscles are innervated by cranial nerves, while abaxial are by spinal nerves.
correctOption: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from the same segment as the muscle cells.
optionExplanations: Option C is correct because there is a strict segmental relationship between the myotome and its nerve supply. Regardless of whether the muscle cells migrate (abaxial) or stay put (primaxial), they are always innervated by spinal nerves derived from the same embryonic segment. This ensures consistent motor control based on the origin of the tissue.

Option A is incorrect because the innervation is segmental, meaning it comes from the same level, not the superior or inferior level. If a muscle is derived from the C5 somite, it will be innervated by the C5 spinal nerve. This segmental mapping is a fundamental principle of vertebrate anatomy.

Option B is incorrect because the modern primaxial/abaxial classification is based on the embryological origin and the lateral somitic frontier, not innervation. The older epimere/hypomere system was the one based on functional innervation (dorsal vs. ventral rami). The new system focuses on the source of the cells and their signaling environment.

Option D is incorrect because abaxial muscles maintain their original segmental innervation even after migrating long distances into the limbs or body wall. This is why the nerve supply to a muscle can often be used to trace its embryological origin. Migration does not result in the acquisition of new nerves from different segments.

Option E is incorrect because both primaxial and abaxial muscles of the trunk and limbs are innervated by spinal nerves. Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the somitomeres in the head. The distinction between primaxial and abaxial is not related to the choice between cranial and spinal nerves.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles), which was based on a functional de fi nition of innervation.

statement: A patient presents with weakness in the intrinsic back muscles. Based on embryological development, these epaxial muscles are innervated by which specific nerve branches?
options: Ventral primary rami
Dorsal primary rami
White rami communicantes
Gray rami communicantes
Cranial nerves
correctOption: Dorsal primary rami
optionExplanations: Option B is correct because epaxial muscles, which are the intrinsic muscles of the back, are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. The term 'epaxial' literally means 'above the axis.' These muscles develop from the primaxial precursors that remain near the neural tube.

Option A is incorrect because ventral primary rami innervate the hypaxial muscles. Hypaxial muscles include those of the limbs and the ventrolateral body wall. While some primaxial muscles are hypaxial (like intercostals), the intrinsic back muscles are strictly epaxial and dorsal rami-supplied.

Option C is incorrect because white rami communicantes are part of the autonomic nervous system, specifically carrying preganglionic sympathetic fibers. They do not provide motor innervation to skeletal muscles. Their role is in the visceral motor pathway, not the somatic motor pathway.

Option D is incorrect because gray rami communicantes carry postganglionic sympathetic fibers back to the spinal nerves for distribution to the body wall (sweat glands, etc.). They are not involved in the voluntary motor innervation of the epaxial skeletal muscles. They are part of the sympathetic chain connections.

Option E is incorrect because cranial nerves innervate muscles of the head and neck derived from somitomeres or branchial arches. The intrinsic back muscles are derived from trunk somites and are thus innervated by spinal nerves. The segmental nature of the trunk precludes cranial nerve involvement.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

coreConcept: Cardiac muscle development and intercalated discs
statement: Which of the following is a unique characteristic of cardiac muscle development that distinguishes it from skeletal muscle development?
options: Derivation from the paraxial mesoderm
Formation of multinucleated fibers through myoblast fusion
Myoblasts adhere via special attachments that become intercalated discs
Absence of myofibrils in the mature cytoplasm
Development from the ectodermal germ layer
correctOption: Myoblasts adhere via special attachments that become intercalated discs
optionExplanations: Option C is correct because in cardiac muscle development, myoblasts do not fuse into syncytia like they do in skeletal muscle. Instead, they remain as individual cells that adhere to one another through specialized junctions. These junctions eventually mature into the intercalated discs characteristic of adult cardiac tissue.

Option A is incorrect because cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle. The visceral mesoderm is part of the lateral plate mesoderm.

Option B is incorrect because the formation of multinucleated fibers through fusion is a hallmark of skeletal muscle development. In cardiac muscle, the myoblasts do not fuse; they remain distinct cells. This is a key histological difference between the two striated muscle types.

Option D is incorrect because cardiac muscle cells do contain myofibrils, which are the contractile units of the cell. In fact, the text states that myofibrils develop in cardiac muscle just as they do in skeletal muscle. The presence of these fibrils is what gives cardiac muscle its striated appearance.

Option E is incorrect because cardiac muscle is mesodermal in origin. Ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system, skin, and a few specific smooth muscles, but never to cardiac muscle. The heart and its muscular walls are entirely products of the mesodermal germ layer.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

coreConcept: Origins of smooth muscle in different regions
