Sclerotome and myotome are derivatives of the somite (paraxial mesoderm) and form bone and skeletal muscle, respectively. They do not contribute to the vascular smooth muscle of the aorta. The aorta's development is linked more closely to the lateral plate mesoderm.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the coronary arteries, not the dorsal aorta. Somitomeres are the precursors to head skeletal muscles and do not form the smooth muscle of the major systemic arteries. Each vascular bed has a specific and sometimes unique embryological source.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments).

coreConcept: Definition of Epaxial and Hypaxial muscles
statement: Based on the functional definition of innervation, how are 'hypaxial' muscles characterized?
options: Muscles of the back innervated by dorsal primary rami
Muscles of the limbs and body wall innervated by ventral primary rami
Muscles of the head innervated by cranial nerves
Cardiac muscles innervated by the autonomic nervous system
Smooth muscles of the gut innervated by the enteric nervous system
correctOption: Muscles of the limbs and body wall innervated by ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Muscles of the back innervated by dorsal primary rami are defined as epaxial (or epimeric) muscles. These are located 'above the axis' of the vertebral column. They are primarily derived from the primaxial domain of the somite.

Hypaxial (or hypomeric) muscles are those located 'below the axis,' which includes the muscles of the limbs and the ventral body wall. These muscles are functionally defined by their innervation from the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves. This group includes both primaxial (intercostals) and abaxial (abdominal) components.

Head muscles are derived from somitomeres and are innervated by cranial nerves, but they are not typically classified using the epaxial/hypaxial terminology used for the trunk. The epaxial/hypaxial division is specific to the spinal nerve distribution. Cranial nerves have a different organizational pattern.

Cardiac muscle is involuntary and regulated by the autonomic nervous system, but it does not fall into the epaxial/hypaxial classification. That classification is strictly for skeletal muscles derived from somites. Cardiac muscle has a completely different embryological origin (splanchnic mesoderm).

Smooth muscles of the gut are also involuntary and not part of the skeletal muscle system derived from somites. Therefore, they are never referred to as hypaxial or epaxial. These terms are reserved for the voluntary muscles of the body wall and limbs.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4).

coreConcept: Embryological origins of different muscle types
statement: A researcher is studying the embryonic origins of various muscle tissues. Which of the following correctly matches the muscle type with its primary embryological source?
options: Skeletal muscle — Lateral plate mesoderm
Cardiac muscle — Paraxial mesoderm
Smooth muscle of the gut — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Pupillary muscles — Neural crest cells
Abdominal wall muscles — Ectoderm
correctOption: Smooth muscle of the gut — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle is primarily derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres, rather than the lateral plate mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the connective tissue and some muscle precursors in the abaxial domain, but it is not the primary source of all skeletal muscle. Paraxial mesoderm is the fundamental source for the axial and limb musculature.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm that surrounds the endothelial heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is dedicated to forming somites which give rise to skeletal muscle, dermis, and bone. The heart's muscular wall, the myocardium, develops specifically from the splanchnic layer of the lateral plate.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gastrointestinal tract and its derivatives is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This mesoderm surrounds the endodermal gut tube and differentiates into the circular and longitudinal muscle layers. This is a classic example of splanchnic mesoderm differentiation into involuntary muscle.

The pupillary muscles (sphincter and dilator pupillae) are unique because they are derived from the ectoderm of the optic cup, not neural crest cells. While neural crest cells contribute to many head structures, these specific smooth muscles are among the few derived from the ectodermal germ layer. Other ectodermal muscles include those of the mammary and sweat glands.

Abdominal wall muscles, such as the rectus abdominis, are skeletal muscles derived from the ventrolateral lip of the somites (paraxial mesoderm). While these cells migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm, their cellular origin is mesodermal, not ectodermal. Ectoderm only gives rise to specific smooth muscles like those in sweat glands.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

statement: During the differentiation of a somite, which specific regions are responsible for the formation of the progenitor muscle cells that eventually constitute the dermomyotome?
options: The central cavity and the sclerotome
The ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips
The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm
The endodermal lining and the neural tube
The notochord and the overlying ectoderm
correctOption: The ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips
optionExplanations: The central cavity of the somite is transient and disappears as the somite undergoes epithelization and subsequent differentiation. The sclerotome is the ventral portion of the somite that becomes mesenchymal to form the vertebrae and ribs, not the muscle progenitors. Muscle formation is restricted to the dorsal regions of the somite.

The dermomyotome is formed by cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips of the upper somite region. These cells migrate and proliferate beneath the dermatome to create the myotome layer. This process is essential for establishing the precursors of both axial and limb musculature.

The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm provides the environment for abaxial muscle development but does not form the initial progenitor muscle cells of the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is strictly a product of the paraxial mesoderm (somite). Lateral plate mesoderm only receives migrating cells from the VLL lip.

The endodermal lining and the neural tube provide signaling cues for differentiation but do not physically contribute cells to the dermomyotome. The neural tube is involved in signaling the dorsomedial lip cells to become primaxial muscle. The dermomyotome itself is entirely mesodermal in origin.

The notochord and overlying ectoderm are critical signaling centers that influence somite patterning but are not the cellular source of the dermomyotome. The notochord specifically influences the ventral somite to become sclerotome. The muscle-forming areas are intrinsic parts of the somite structure itself.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively. Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome.

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and Mesodermal Domains
statement: A developmental biologist is mapping the 'lateral somitic frontier.' Which of the following best describes the significance of this boundary in the developing embryo?
options: It separates the ectoderm from the underlying mesodermal germ layer
It defines the border between the sclerotome and the dermatome
It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
It marks the transition between the somite and the neural tube
It distinguishes between the epaxial and hypaxial functional muscle groups
correctOption: It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
optionExplanations: The lateral somitic frontier is an intra-mesodermal boundary, not a boundary between different germ layers like ectoderm and mesoderm. It specifically exists within the mesodermal layer to organize muscle precursor migration. Germ layer boundaries are established much earlier during gastrulation.

The sclerotome and dermatome are internal subdivisions of the somite itself, whereas the lateral somitic frontier is a boundary between the somite and the lateral plate mesoderm. The sclerotome forms ventrally and the dermatome dorsally. The frontier is a lateral landmark relative to the somite's position.

The lateral somitic frontier is the critical boundary separating the primaxial domain (containing only somite-derived cells) from the abaxial domain (containing lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells). This frontier determines the environment and signaling sources for developing muscles. It is the modern embryological basis for classifying muscle groups.

The transition between the somite and the neural tube is a medial relationship, whereas the lateral somitic frontier is located laterally. The neural tube is a midline structure that provides signals to the primaxial domain. The frontier is located where the paraxial mesoderm meets the lateral plate mesoderm.

While the frontier relates to muscle origins, the terms epaxial and hypaxial are based on functional innervation by dorsal and ventral rami, respectively. The frontier defines embryological domains (primaxial and abaxial) which do not perfectly overlap with the functional epaxial/hypaxial classification. The frontier is based on cell lineage and signaling environment.
citation: Initially, there is a well-defined border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier. This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells.

statement: Which of the following muscles is classified as belonging to the abaxial domain based on its migration across the lateral somitic frontier?
options: Intercostal muscles
Rectus abdominis
Rhomboids
Erector spinae
Levator scapulae
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial domain, meaning they develop from somite cells that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They remain within the paraxial mesoderm environment during their differentiation. These muscles are part of the thoracic wall but retain a primaxial origin.

The rectus abdominis is a classic example of an abaxial muscle because its precursors migrate from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Once there, they receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate environment. Other abaxial muscles include the infrahyoid and limb muscles.

The rhomboids are considered primaxial muscles because they develop from somite cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain. They do not migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Their development is influenced by signals from the neural tube and notochord.

The erector spinae muscles are deep back muscles that belong to the primaxial domain. They are derived from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and the remaining VLL cells that do not cross the frontier. As primaxial muscles, they are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves.

The levator scapulae is a muscle of the shoulder girdle that is classified as primaxial. It originates from somite cells that stay within the paraxial mesoderm territory. It does not involve the migration of precursors into the lateral plate mesoderm characteristic of the abaxial domain.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm; those that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors.

statement: A patient presents with a loss of motor function in the deep muscles of the back (epaxial muscles). Which nerve structures are most likely involved, and what is the developmental basis for this innervation?
options: Ventral primary rami; they innervate all muscles derived from the primaxial domain
Dorsal primary rami; they innervate muscles located above the axis (epaxial)
Cranial nerves; they innervate all muscles derived from the occipital somites
Sympathetic ganglia; they provide motor innervation to the myotome
Ventral primary rami; they innervate muscles that cross the lateral somitic frontier
correctOption: Dorsal primary rami; they innervate muscles located above the axis (epaxial)
optionExplanations: Ventral primary rami do not innervate the epaxial (back) muscles; they are responsible for the hypaxial muscles, which include the limb and body wall muscles. Furthermore, the primaxial domain includes some muscles innervated by ventral rami (like intercostals), so this generalization is incorrect. Innervation is tied to the epaxial/hypaxial position.

Epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back, are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. This is a functional classification based on the muscle's position relative to the vertebral axis. Regardless of whether they are primaxial or abaxial in origin, their final position determines this specific innervation pattern.

Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the somitomeres and occipital somites of the head and neck, but the deep back muscles (epaxial) of the trunk are innervated by spinal nerves. The question specifies back muscles, which are segmental and correspond to spinal cord levels. Occipital somites contribute to the tongue and neck, not the entire back.

Sympathetic ganglia are part of the autonomic nervous system and provide visceral motor innervation to smooth muscle and glands, not skeletal muscle. Skeletal muscles of the myotome receive somatic motor innervation from the ventral horn of the spinal cord via spinal nerves. Autonomic nerves do not control voluntary back muscles.

Ventral primary rami do innervate muscles that cross the lateral somitic frontier (abaxial muscles), but they also innervate some primaxial muscles like the intercostals. More importantly, they do not innervate the epaxial back muscles mentioned in the scenario. The epaxial muscles are strictly the domain of the dorsal primary rami.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami.

statement: Which of the following is a unique characteristic of cardiac muscle development that distinguishes it from skeletal muscle development?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form long multinucleated fibers
Cardiac muscle is derived from the paraxial mesoderm
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but are joined by intercalated discs
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils and cross-striations
Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectodermal germ layer
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but are joined by intercalated discs
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated fibers, but cardiac myoblasts do not undergo this fusion process. In the heart, cells remain individual units throughout development and adulthood. This lack of fusion is a key histological difference between the two striated muscle types.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source for skeletal muscles of the trunk and limbs. The visceral mesoderm specifically surrounds the heart tube to form the myocardium.

In cardiac muscle development, myoblasts adhere to each other via specialized attachments that eventually become intercalated discs, rather than fusing into a syncytium. This allows for electrical coupling between individual cells while maintaining separate cellular identities. This is a hallmark of cardiac tissue architecture.

Cardiac muscle does possess myofibrils and exhibits cross-striations, just like skeletal muscle. The internal contractile apparatus is very similar in both types of striated muscle. The primary differences lie in the cellular arrangement and the presence of intercalated discs in the heart.

Cardiac muscle is entirely mesodermal in origin, specifically from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm only gives rise to very specific smooth muscles, such as those in the iris and certain glands. No part of the heart's muscular wall is derived from ectoderm.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myofibrils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse.

statement: While most muscle tissue is mesodermal, a few specific muscles are derived from the ectoderm. Which of the following is an example of an ectoderm-derived muscle?
options: Rectus abdominis
Dilator pupillae
Myocardium
Biceps brachii
Trachealis muscle
correctOption: Dilator pupillae
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle derived from the paraxial mesoderm (specifically the abaxial domain). It is not an ectodermal derivative. Skeletal muscles are almost exclusively mesodermal in origin.

The dilator pupillae and the sphincter pupillae of the eye are among the rare muscles derived from the ectoderm (specifically the neuroectoderm of the optic cup). Other examples include the smooth muscles of the mammary and sweat glands. This is an exception to the general rule that muscles are mesodermal.

The myocardium is the muscular layer of the heart and is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm. It is a specialized form of striated muscle. It has no ectodermal contribution in its muscular components.

The biceps brachii is a skeletal muscle of the limb, derived from the abaxial muscle precursors of the somites. These cells migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm of the limb bud. Like all limb muscles, it is mesodermal.

The trachealis muscle is a smooth muscle found in the wall of the trachea, which is a gut derivative (respiratory diverticulum). Therefore, it is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. It is not an ectodermal structure.
citation: Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm. Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures.

coreConcept: Purkinje Fibers and the Cardiac Conducting System
statement: What is the developmental origin and nature of Purkinje fibers in the heart?
options: They are specialized nerve cells derived from the neural crest
They are modified muscle cells derived from visceral mesoderm
They are connective tissue strands derived from the epicardium
They are endothelial cells derived from the heart tube lining
They are skeletal muscle fibers that migrated from the somites
correctOption: They are modified muscle cells derived from visceral mesoderm
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are often confused with nerve fibers because of their conducting function, but they are not derived from the neural crest or the nervous system. They are specialized cardiac muscle cells. Their embryological origin is the same as the rest of the myocardium.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that develop from the visceral mesoderm. They are characterized by irregularly distributed myofibrils and are responsible for the heart's conducting system. They represent a functional specialization of the myogenic lineage.

While the epicardium provides some cells to the heart (like coronary smooth muscle), it does not form the Purkinje fibers. Purkinje fibers are located subendocardially and are part of the muscular wall. They are not primarily composed of connective tissue.

The endothelial heart tube gives rise to the endocardium (the inner lining of the heart), not the muscular conducting system. The Purkinje fibers develop within the myocardial layer. Endothelial cells are specialized for lining vessels and heart chambers.

Skeletal muscle fibers from somites do not contribute to the heart. The heart develops independently from the visceral mesoderm in the cardiogenic area. There is no migration of somitic myotome cells into the developing heart tube.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fibers, form the conducting system of the heart. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation affect the functional relationship between myotomes and the vertebral column?
options: It causes each myotome to attach to only one vertebra, limiting spinal rotation
It allows myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs, enabling spinal movement
It forces myotomes to fuse into a single continuous muscle mass along the spine
It disconnects the myotomes from their original segmental spinal nerves
It converts myotome cells into intervertebral disc fibrocartilage
correctOption: It allows myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs, enabling spinal movement
optionExplanations: If a myotome attached to only one vertebra, it would not be able to produce movement between vertebrae. Resegmentation is specifically what prevents this 'one-to-one' lock. The shift in vertebral boundaries is essential for creating the mechanical leverage needed for spinal flexion and extension.

Resegmentation involves the fusion of the caudal half of one sclerotome with the cranial half of the next. This shift ensures that the myotomes, which remain segmentally aligned with the original somite boundaries, now bridge the newly formed intervertebral discs. This anatomical arrangement is what allows muscles to move the vertebral column.

Myotomes do not fuse into a single mass; they maintain their segmental identity, which is reflected in the segmental innervation by spinal nerves. While some muscles (like erector spinae) form long columns, they are still composed of segmentally derived units. Resegmentation facilitates movement, not fusion.

Innervation is never lost during resegmentation; each myotome continues to be supplied by the spinal nerve from its original segment. The nerve actually exits the vertebral column between the new vertebrae. The relationship between the nerve and its muscle is one of the most stable features in development.

Myotome cells do not become fibrocartilage; they are committed to the myogenic lineage and form skeletal muscle. The intervertebral discs are formed from the mesenchymal cells located between the resegmented sclerotome masses. The myotomes remain muscular and provide the force to move these joints.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine. Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment.

coreConcept: Signaling for Primaxial vs. Abaxial Differentiation
statement: Which of the following correctly identifies the primary signaling sources for the differentiation of primaxial muscle precursors?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm and the overlying ectoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Endoderm and the yolk sac
The heart tube and proepicardial organ
The sclerotome and the dermatome
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial domain, not the primaxial domain. Primaxial cells are those that stay close to the midline and do not interact with the lateral plate environment. Ectoderm provides some signals for the dermatome but is not the primary driver for primaxial muscle.

Primaxial muscle precursors, which include all cells from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and some from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) that stay in the paraxial mesoderm, receive their developmental cues from the neural tube and notochord. These midline structures are essential for patterning the medial portion of the somite. This ensures the proper development of back and intercostal muscles.

The endoderm and yolk sac are involved in early patterning and gut development but do not provide the specific signals required for myotome differentiation in the paraxial mesoderm. Muscle patterning is primarily driven by adjacent axial structures. The yolk sac is far removed from the dorsal somites.

The heart tube and proepicardial organ are involved in cardiac and coronary development, respectively. They have no role in signaling the skeletal muscle precursors of the somites. Skeletal muscle signaling is localized to the dorsal body wall and limb buds.

While the sclerotome and dermatome are parts of the same somite, they are not the primary 'external' signaling sources that drive the differentiation of the muscle precursors. Instead, they all respond to signals from the neural tube, notochord, and lateral plate. The somite is the responder, not the primary signal source for its own muscle cells.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm; those that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord.

statement: In the development of skeletal muscle, by which time point do the characteristic cross-striations typically become visible?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
End of the sixth month
At the time of birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: The end of the first month is too early for cross-striations; at this stage, the somites are still undergoing initial segmentation and differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblast fusion is just beginning or hasn't started in many regions. Striations require a high degree of internal organization.

By the end of the second month, myoblasts are fusing and myofibrils are appearing, but the organized sarcomeric structure that produces visible cross-striations is not yet fully established. The muscle fibers are still in an early stage of maturation. The third month is the definitive milestone.

According to embryological timelines, cross-striations typical of skeletal muscle appear by the end of the third month of development. This follows the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers and the subsequent synthesis and organization of myofibrils. This marks a significant stage in the functional maturation of the muscular system.

The end of the sixth month is well after the appearance of cross-striations. By this time, the fetus is capable of significant movement, and the muscles are well-developed. Striations are a much earlier developmental feature, appearing in the first trimester.

Cross-striations are present long before birth. They are visible by the end of the third month, allowing for the fetal movements that can be detected later in pregnancy. Waiting until birth for striations would mean the muscles were not functional throughout gestation.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fibers. Myofibrils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear.

statement: Which of the following groups of muscles are all derived from the abaxial domain?
options: Erector spinae, rhomboids, and latissimus dorsi
Rectus abdominis, infrahyoid, and limb muscles
Intercostal muscles, longus colli, and scalenes
Quadratus lumborum, levator ani, and coccygeus
Trapezius, splenius capitis, and semispinalis
correctOption: Rectus abdominis, infrahyoid, and limb muscles
optionExplanations: The erector spinae and rhomboids are primaxial muscles, not abaxial. While the latissimus dorsi is often grouped with back muscles, the core of this list (erector spinae and rhomboids) consists of muscles that do not migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. They receive signals from the neural tube.

The rectus abdominis, infrahyoid muscles, and all limb muscles are derived from the abaxial domain. These muscles originate from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This migration is the defining characteristic of the abaxial domain.

Intercostal muscles are a classic example of primaxial muscles, despite their location in the body wall. They develop from somite cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain. Therefore, this group cannot be classified as abaxial.

The quadratus lumborum and pelvic floor muscles (levator ani) have complex origins, but they are not the primary examples used to define the abaxial domain in standard embryological texts. The rectus abdominis and limb muscles are the definitive examples of abaxial migration. The pelvic floor has different developmental constraints.

The trapezius (partially derived from somitomeres/cranial nerves) and the deep back muscles like splenius capitis and semispinalis are primaxial. They develop in the region around the neural tube and do not involve migration into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are innervated by dorsal rami or specific cranial nerves.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles.

statement: What structural transformation occurs in the somite immediately following its segmentation from the paraxial mesoderm?
options: It immediately differentiates into mature skeletal muscle fibers
It undergoes epithelization to form a hollow ball of cells
It migrates directly to the limb buds to form the skeleton
It fuses with the neural tube to form the spinal cord
It undergoes apoptosis to create space for the spinal nerves
correctOption: It undergoes epithelization to form a hollow ball of cells
optionExplanations: Mature skeletal muscle fibers do not appear until much later, after the myoblasts have fused and synthesized myofibrils. The immediate post-segmentation phase is characterized by tissue organization, not terminal differentiation. The somite must first form the dermomyotome.

Immediately after segmentation, the somitomeres/somites undergo epithelization, transforming from a loose mesenchymal cluster into a structured 'ball' of epithelial cells with a central cavity. This epithelial stage is a prerequisite for the subsequent patterning into sclerotome and dermomyotome. This organization allows for distinct signaling responses.

Somites do not migrate as a whole to the limb buds. Only specific cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) migrate to the limb buds to form muscle, while the sclerotome stays medial to form the vertebrae. The skeleton of the limbs actually comes from the lateral plate mesoderm, not the somite.

The somite and the neural tube are distinct structures derived from different germ layers (mesoderm and ectoderm, respectively). They do not fuse. The neural tube provides signals to the somite, but they remain anatomically separate entities throughout development.

Somites do not undergo mass apoptosis; they are the primary source of the axial skeleton, dermis, and musculature. While some programmed cell death occurs during development, the somite's main trajectory is proliferation and differentiation into vital body structures. Spinal nerves grow into the existing somitic segments.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center. The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome, the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs.

coreConcept: The Old vs. New Concept of Muscle Development
statement: How does the modern 'primaxial/abaxial' model of muscle development differ from the traditional 'epimere/hypomere' model?
options: The new model is based on functional innervation, while the old was based on cell lineage
The new model is based on embryological origin and signaling, while the old was based on innervation
The new model only applies to cardiac muscle, while the old applied to skeletal muscle
The new model suggests all muscles are derived from ectoderm, unlike the old model
The new model eliminates the distinction between dorsal and ventral primary rami
correctOption: The new model is based on embryological origin and signaling, while the old was based on innervation
optionExplanations: This is the reverse of the truth. The old model (epimere/hypomere) was the one based on functional innervation (dorsal vs. ventral rami). The new model (primaxial/abaxial) focuses on the actual embryological source of the cells and the environment where they differentiate.

The modern description uses the lateral somitic frontier to define domains based on the embryological origin of muscle cells (whether they stay in the paraxial mesoderm or migrate into the lateral plate) and their signaling sources. The older epimere/hypomere model was strictly a functional classification based on which branch of the spinal nerve (dorsal or ventral ramus) supplied the muscle.

Both models apply specifically to the development of skeletal muscle derived from somites. Cardiac muscle development is described separately and does not use the primaxial/abaxial or epimere/hypomere terminology. These concepts are central to understanding the axial and limb skeletal musculature.

Neither model suggests that skeletal muscles are derived from ectoderm; both correctly identify the mesoderm as the source. The shift in models represents a deeper understanding of mesodermal patterning and cell migration, not a change in the identified germ layer. Ectodermal muscle remains a rare exception.

The new model does not eliminate the distinction between dorsal and ventral primary rami; these anatomical structures still exist and innervate specific muscle groups. However, the new model recognizes that innervation does not always perfectly align with the embryological origin of the muscle cells. It decouples the developmental domain from the nerve supply.
citation: The new description of muscle development characterized by primaxial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles), which was based on a functional definition of innervation: Epimeric muscles were innervated by dorsal primary rami, and hypomeric muscles by ventral primary rami.

statement: A researcher is studying the embryological origins of the craniofacial muscles. Which of the following structures is the primary source of skeletal muscle in the head region?
options: Somitomeres
Lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Splanchnic mesoderm
Ectoderm
correctOption: Somitomeres
optionExplanations: Somitomeres are partially segmented whorls of paraxial mesoderm in the head region that give rise to the skeletal muscles of the face and skull. Unlike the somites found in the trunk, these structures do not undergo full epithelialization but follow a similar myogenic pathway. They are distinct from the occipital-to-sacral somites that form the rest of the axial skeleton.

Lateral plate mesoderm primarily contributes to the smooth muscle of the circulatory system and the skeletal elements of the limbs, rather than the skeletal muscles of the head. While it forms the parietal and visceral layers, it is not the source of craniofacial voluntary muscle.

Neural crest cells contribute significantly to the connective tissues and bones of the face, but the actual contractile skeletal muscle fibers are derived from paraxial mesoderm. They may provide the connective tissue template for muscle attachment but not the muscle cells themselves.

Splanchnic mesoderm, or visceral mesoderm, is the source of cardiac muscle and the smooth muscle surrounding the gut tube. It does not contribute to the voluntary skeletal muscles of the head or limbs.
Ectoderm is the source of the nervous system and skin epidermis, and while it forms specific smooth muscles like the pupillary muscles, it is not the origin of the skeletal muscles of the head.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

statement: During the transition from somitomeres to somites in the trunk region, what morphological change occurs immediately following segmentation?
options: Formation of a mesenchymal sclerotome
Epithelization into a ball with a central cavity
Migration of cells to the lateral plate
Fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers
Development of intercalated discs
correctOption: Epithelization into a ball with a central cavity
optionExplanations: Immediately after segmentation, the paraxial mesoderm undergoes epithelization, transforming from a loose mesenchymal state into organized epithelial spheres called somites. These spheres contain a small central cavity and represent the first stage of somite maturation before further differentiation. This process is essential for establishing the segmental pattern of the body.

The formation of the mesenchymal sclerotome occurs after epithelization, when the ventral and medial cells of the somite lose their epithelial characteristics to become migratory bone-forming cells. This is a secondary step in somite differentiation.

Migration to the lateral plate mesoderm is a characteristic of abaxial muscle precursors that occurs much later in development, after the dermomyotome has formed. It is not the immediate result of segmentation.

The fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers is a late stage of muscle differentiation that occurs after the muscle progenitor cells have reached their final destination. It is not related to the initial epithelization of the somite.

Intercalated discs are unique features of cardiac muscle development and are not found in the developing somites or skeletal muscle precursors. They represent specialized cell-to-cell attachments in the heart tube.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A). The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs.

statement: Which of the following best describes the formation of the dermomyotome during somite differentiation?
options: Cells from the sclerotome migrate dorsally to cover the neural tube
Cells from the VLL and DML lips migrate and proliferate ventral to the dermatome
Ectodermal cells invaginate to form a layer between the somite and the skin
Lateral plate mesoderm cells migrate medially to fuse with the sclerotome
Neural crest cells differentiate into myoblasts within the central somite cavity
correctOption: Cells from the VLL and DML lips migrate and proliferate ventral to the dermatome
optionExplanations: The dermomyotome is formed when progenitor muscle cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips of the somite migrate and proliferate beneath the remaining epithelial layer, which becomes the dermatome. This combined structure serves as the source for both the dermis of the back and the skeletal muscles of the body wall and limbs.

The sclerotome cells migrate ventromedially to surround the notochord and neural tube to form the vertebrae, rather than migrating dorsally to form the dermomyotome. The sclerotome is primarily osteogenic, not myogenic.

The dermomyotome is entirely mesodermal in origin; ectodermal cells do not invaginate to form this layer, although the overlying ectoderm does provide signals for its differentiation. The dermatome part of the dermomyotome will eventually form the dermis.

Lateral plate mesoderm does not migrate medially to form the dermomyotome; instead, some cells from the VLL lip of the dermomyotome migrate laterally into the lateral plate mesoderm. This lateral migration defines the abaxial muscle population.

Neural crest cells do not form myoblasts within the somite; they primarily contribute to the peripheral nervous system and various connective tissues. Myoblasts are derived exclusively from the paraxial mesoderm in this region.
citation: Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome (Fig. 11.1B,C; Fig. 11.2). Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B).

statement: A developmental defect specifically affecting the abaxial muscle cell precursors would most likely result in the malformation of which muscle?
options: Erector spinae
Intercostal muscles
Rectus abdominis
Rhomboids
Latissimus dorsi
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is a classic example of an abaxial muscle, as it is derived from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. These muscles receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate rather than the neural tube.

The erector spinae muscles are primaxial muscles that remain near the neural tube and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They are derived from the DML and VLL cells that stay within the paraxial mesoderm domain.

Intercostal muscles are considered primaxial because they develop within the paraxial mesoderm domain, even though they are located in the body wall. They do not involve migration into the lateral plate mesoderm.

The rhomboids and other shoulder girdle muscles are derived from primaxial precursors that remain in the paraxial mesoderm. They are not part of the abaxial population that migrates into the lateral plate.

The latissimus dorsi is a primaxial muscle that migrates to its final position but does not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm during its early specification. It remains under the signaling influence of the primaxial domain.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles.

coreConcept: Signaling sources for primaxial vs abaxial
statement: Which of the following correctly identifies the primary source of developmental signals for the primaxial muscle cell precursors?
options: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Overlying surface ectoderm only
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Proepicardial cells
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: Primaxial muscle precursors are those that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. Consequently, they are positioned to receive inductive signals from the midline structures, specifically the neural tube and the notochord.

The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial muscle precursors, which are the cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. Primaxial cells are sequestered from these signals by the frontier itself.

While the surface ectoderm does provide some signals for the dermomyotome, the neural tube and notochord are the dominant signaling centers for the primaxial muscle population. The ectoderm is more involved in specifying the dermatome and some VLL cells.

The visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is involved in the development of smooth muscle of the gut and cardiac muscle, but it does not provide the primary signals for the skeletal muscle precursors of the somite.

Proepicardial cells are specifically involved in the formation of the epicardium and the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries. they have no role in the signaling or differentiation of primaxial skeletal muscle precursors.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord.

statement: In the traditional functional classification of axial muscles, which nerve branches are responsible for the innervation of epaxial (back) muscles?
options: Ventral primary rami
Dorsal primary rami
Sympathetic chain ganglia
Phrenic nerve
Cranial nerves
correctOption: Dorsal primary rami
optionExplanations: Epaxial muscles, which are the intrinsic muscles of the back, are defined by their innervation from the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. This relationship is maintained regardless of the newer embryological classification into primaxial and abaxial domains.

Ventral primary rami innervate the hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the limbs and the ventrolateral body wall. This division is the basis for the old epimere/hypomere functional classification system.

Sympathetic chain ganglia provide autonomic innervation to smooth muscles and glands, but they do not provide motor innervation to the skeletal muscles of the back. Skeletal muscle requires somatic motor fibers.
The phrenic nerve is a specific nerve (C3-C5) that innervates the diaphragm, which is a hypaxial muscle. It is not responsible for the innervation of the epaxial back muscles.
Cranial nerves innervate the muscles of the head and neck derived from somitomeres and pharyngeal arches, but they do not innervate the epaxial muscles of the trunk, which are spinal nerve-dependent.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

statement: At what point during human embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
Beginning of the second trimester
Just before birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: Cross-striations in skeletal muscle result from the highly organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments into sarcomeres. This histological feature typically appears by the end of the third month of gestation as myoblasts fuse and myofibrils mature.

By the end of the first month, the somites are still in the process of differentiating into dermomyotomes, and myoblasts have not yet fused into the multinucleated fibers necessary for striation. The tissue is still largely mesenchymal or early myogenic.

The end of the second month marks the period when myoblasts are actively fusing to form primary myotubes, but the internal organization of myofibrils is not yet sufficient to produce visible cross-striations.

While the end of the third month is technically the beginning of the second trimester, the specific developmental milestone is classically cited as the end of the third month. Striations are well-established by the time the second trimester is underway.

Waiting until just before birth would be far too late, as skeletal muscles must be functional and organized much earlier for fetal movements. Fetal breathing and limb movements occur long before the third trimester.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

statement: Which of the following is a key histological difference between the development of cardiac muscle and skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form syncytia
Skeletal muscle lacks myofibrils
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain as individual cells
Skeletal muscle develops from visceral mesoderm
Cardiac muscle lacks intercalated discs
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain as individual cells
optionExplanations: Unlike skeletal muscle, where myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated fibers (syncytia), cardiac myoblasts remain as individual cells. They are joined by specialized cell junctions called intercalated discs, which allow for electrical and mechanical coupling.

Skeletal muscle is characterized by the presence of myofibrils, which are the contractile units of the cell. Both skeletal and cardiac muscles develop myofibrils in a similar fashion to produce striations.

Cardiac myoblasts adhere to each other via intercalated discs but maintain their individual cell membranes and nuclei. This lack of fusion is a defining characteristic that distinguishes cardiac muscle from the multinucleated fibers of skeletal muscle.

Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm (somites and somitomeres), whereas cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This reflects their different embryological origins.

Intercalated discs are a unique and essential feature of cardiac muscle, providing the structural integrity and gap junctions needed for the heart to contract as a functional syncytium. Skeletal muscle does not possess these structures.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

statement: What are the specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that form the heart's conducting system and contain irregularly distributed myofibrils?
options: Sinoatrial nodes
Purkinje fibers
Bundle of His
Papillary muscles
Trabeculae carneae
correctOption: Purkinje fibers
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are specialized cardiac muscle cells that have differentiated for electrical conduction rather than forceful contraction. They are histologically distinct due to their larger size and the irregular, sparse distribution of myofibrils in their cytoplasm.

The sinoatrial node is the primary pacemaker of the heart, and while it is part of the conducting system, the specific bundles described with irregularly distributed myofibrils that form the terminal conduction pathways are the Purkinje fibers.

The Bundle of His is the proximal part of the ventricular conducting system, but the term 'Purkinje fibers' specifically refers to the specialized muscle cell bundles that distribute the impulse throughout the ventricles.

Papillary muscles are regular contractile cardiac muscles that anchor the chordae tendineae; they do not have the specialized conduction properties or the irregular myofibril distribution of Purkinje fibers.
Trabeculae carneae are the muscular ridges on the internal walls of the ventricles. While they contain cardiac muscle, they are not specialized conducting bundles like Purkinje fibers.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

statement: The smooth muscle in the wall of the dorsal aorta has a dual embryological origin. Which two cell populations contribute to this tissue?
options: Paraxial mesoderm and ectoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Visceral mesoderm and somitomeres
Proepicardial cells and endoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: The dorsal aorta and other large arteries receive smooth muscle contributions from both the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. This dual origin is unique to the major vascular structures and reflects the complex integration of different embryonic tissues.
Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle, and while ectoderm forms some specific smooth muscles (like in sweat glands), it is not a primary contributor to the dorsal aorta's muscular wall.
Visceral mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut, and somitomeres form the skeletal muscle of the head. Neither is the primary source for the smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta.
Proepicardial cells contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, not the dorsal aorta. Endoderm provides the epithelial lining of the gut but does not form muscle tissue.
The sclerotome and dermatome are subdivisions of the somite that form bone and dermis/muscle, respectively. They do not contribute to the smooth muscle of the large systemic arteries.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm.

statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal, which of the following muscles is derived from the ectoderm?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Sphincter pupillae muscle
Smooth muscle of the jejunum
Cardiac conducting system
Rectus abdominis
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are rare examples of muscles derived from the ectoderm (specifically the neuroectoderm of the optic cup). This is an exception to the general rule that muscle tissue is mesodermal.
The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells, both of which are mesodermal or ectomesenchymal, but not pure surface ectoderm.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut, such as in the jejunum, is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This is the most common origin for smooth muscle in the body.
The cardiac conducting system, including Purkinje fibers, is derived from visceral mesoderm, just like the rest of the heart muscle. It is not an ectodermal derivative.
The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle derived from the abaxial precursors of the paraxial mesoderm. It is not a smooth muscle and is not derived from ectoderm.
citation: Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. Musculature of the aXial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites.

statement: What is the functional significance of the resegmentation of sclerotomes during the development of the vertebral column?
options: It allows spinal nerves to exit the vertebral canal
It enables myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs and move the spine
It triggers the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers
It separates the primaxial and abaxial muscle domains
It induces the formation of the nucleus pulposus
correctOption: It enables myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs and move the spine
optionExplanations: Resegmentation involves the fusion of the caudal half of one sclerotome with the cranial half of the next. This process shifts the position of the vertebrae relative to the myotomes, allowing each myotome to span the gap between two vertebrae (the intervertebral disc) and thus act on the joint to move the spine.

While resegmentation does create pathways for spinal nerves to exit between vertebrae, its primary effect on the muscular system is the repositioning of myotomes to allow for spinal mobility. The nerves actually pass through the middle of the resegmented vertebrae initially.

Myoblast fusion is a cellular differentiation process that occurs within the myotome and is not directly caused by the gross morphological resegmentation of the sclerotomes. These are independent developmental events.

The separation of primaxial and abaxial domains is established by the lateral somitic frontier and signaling from the lateral plate mesoderm, not by the resegmentation of the bony vertebral precursors.

The nucleus pulposus is derived from the notochord, which is surrounded by the sclerotome cells. While resegmentation organizes the vertebral bodies around the notochord, it is not the trigger for the formation of the nucleus pulposus itself.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head.

statement: Which of the following components characterizes the abaxial mesodermal domain in the developing embryo?
options: Only somite-derived paraxial mesoderm cells
Parietal lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
Visceral lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Neural tube and notochord signaling centers
Ectodermal cells and sclerotome-derived mesenchyme
correctOption: Parietal lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The abaxial domain is defined as the region lateral to the lateral somitic frontier, consisting of the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm mixed with myogenic cells that have migrated from the VLL lip of the somites. This domain is distinct from the primaxial domain in both its cellular composition and signaling environment.
The primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain, is characterized by containing only somite-derived paraxial mesoderm cells and being located immediately adjacent to the neural tube.
Visceral lateral plate mesoderm is involved in gut and heart development and is not a component of the abaxial domain, which specifically involves the parietal (somatic) layer.
The neural tube and notochord are the primary signaling centers for the primaxial domain. The abaxial domain receives its signals from the lateral plate mesoderm.

The abaxial domain does not include sclerotome-derived mesenchyme, which forms the axial skeleton, nor is it defined by ectodermal cells, although the overlying ectoderm does influence the underlying mesoderm.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier. Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors.

statement: Which specific cell population contributes to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, distinguishing them from the smooth muscle of the gut?
options: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Proepicardial cells
Paraxial mesoderm
Sclerotome
Dermatome
correctOption: Proepicardial cells
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the coronary arteries has a unique origin from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells (for the proximal segments). This differs from most other vascular smooth muscle and the smooth muscle of the internal organs.
The visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is the source of smooth muscle for the gut and its derivatives, but it is not the source for the coronary artery musculature.
Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle and does not contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries or the gut.
The sclerotome is the bone-forming part of the somite and does not differentiate into smooth muscle. It forms the vertebrae and ribs.
The dermatome forms the dermis of the back and contributes to the dermomyotome, but it is not involved in the formation of vascular smooth muscle.
citation: In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: Despite the migration of some muscle precursors into the abaxial domain, what fundamental rule governs the innervation of all myotome-derived muscles?
options: They are all innervated by cranial nerves
They are innervated by nerves from the same segment as the muscle cells
They lose their original innervation upon crossing the lateral somitic frontier
They are all innervated by the dorsal primary rami
They receive innervation only after reaching their final adult position
correctOption: They are innervated by nerves from the same segment as the muscle cells
optionExplanations: A key principle of muscle development is that muscle cells carry their original segmental innervation with them, regardless of how far they migrate. Therefore, every myotome-derived muscle is supplied by spinal nerves from the same embryonic segment as the muscle cells themselves.
Cranial nerves only innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres and pharyngeal arches, not the muscles derived from the trunk myotomes. Trunk muscles are always supplied by spinal nerves.
Crossing the lateral somitic frontier into the abaxial domain does not change the segmental origin of the muscle cells; they maintain their connection to the spinal nerves of their original segment.
Only the epaxial muscles (back muscles) are innervated by the dorsal primary rami. Hypaxial muscles, which include many abaxial derivatives, are innervated by the ventral primary rami.
Innervation is established early in development as the myotomes form and is maintained throughout the migration process. The nerves follow the muscle cells to their final destinations.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles), which was based on a functional de fi nition of innervation.

coreConcept: Embryological origin of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle
statement: A medical student is reviewing the embryological origins of different muscle types. Which of the following correctly matches the muscle type with its primary embryonic source?
options: Skeletal muscle — Lateral plate mesoderm
Cardiac muscle — Paraxial mesoderm
Smooth muscle of the gut — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Pupillary muscles — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Musculature of the limbs — Ectoderm
correctOption: Smooth muscle of the gut — Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle is primarily derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres, rather than the lateral plate mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm contributes to the connective tissue of the limbs but not the primary muscle fibers themselves. This distinction is fundamental in understanding the segmental nature of skeletal muscle development.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm that surrounds the endothelial heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is reserved for the development of the axial skeleton and skeletal muscles. Misidentifying this source would lead to a misunderstanding of cardiogenesis.

Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and its derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. This layer provides the mesenchymal precursors that differentiate into the circular and longitudinal layers of the gastrointestinal tract. It is a key example of splanchnic mesoderm differentiation.

The pupillary muscles (sphincter and dilator pupillae) are unique because they are derived from the ectoderm of the optic cup, not the visceral mesoderm. Most other smooth muscles in the body are mesodermal in origin, making the pupillary, mammary, and sweat gland muscles notable exceptions. This ectodermal origin is a high-yield anatomical fact.

Limb musculature is derived from the ventrolateral cells of the somites (paraxial mesoderm) that migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system and epidermis, but not the contractile skeletal muscle of the limbs. The migration of these somitic cells is a critical step in limb bud development.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

coreConcept: Differentiation of the somite into sclerotome and dermomyotome
statement: During the differentiation of a somite, which specific region undergoes an epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition to eventually form the vertebrae and ribs?
options: Dorsomedial lip (DML)
Ventrolateral lip (VLL)
Dermatome
Sclerotome
Dermomyotome
correctOption: Sclerotome
optionExplanations: The dorsomedial lip (DML) is one of the two muscle-forming areas in the upper region of the somite. It contributes to the formation of the myotome and eventually the primaxial muscles of the back. It does not undergo the transition to form bone-forming cells for the ribs or vertebrae.

The ventrolateral lip (VLL) is the second muscle-forming area of the somite, providing precursors for both primaxial and abaxial muscles. While these cells migrate extensively, they are destined to become muscle tissue rather than the skeletal elements of the axial column. They form the ventral portion of the dermomyotome.

The dermatome is the part of the somite that gives rise to the dermis of the skin for a specific segment. It remains in the upper region of the somite and does not contribute to the formation of the vertebrae or ribs. It is distinct from the muscle-forming and bone-forming regions.

The sclerotome is formed when the ventral region of the somite becomes mesenchymal again, allowing these cells to migrate and surround the notochord and neural tube. These cells are the precursors for the vertebrae and ribs. This transition is a critical step in the development of the axial skeleton.

The dermomyotome is a composite structure formed by the dermatome and the underlying myotome precursors from the VLL and DML. It represents the dorsal portion of the somite after the sclerotome has migrated away. It does not form the bone-forming cells of the vertebrae.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome, the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively.

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and muscle domains
statement: The lateral somitic frontier is a critical embryological boundary. Which of the following best describes the 'abaxial domain' created by this frontier?
options: It contains only cells derived from the paraxial mesoderm that remain near the neural tube.
It consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm combined with migrated somite cells.
It is the region where muscle cells receive differentiation signals exclusively from the notochord.
It is the domain that gives rise only to the muscles of the back and the intercostal muscles.
It is a transient structure that disappears before the formation of the dermomyotome.
correctOption: It consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm combined with migrated somite cells.
optionExplanations: The region containing only somite-derived cells near the neural tube is the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. These cells do not cross the lateral somitic frontier and maintain a closer relationship with the axial structures. This domain is responsible for the development of the epaxial muscles.

The abaxial domain is defined by the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm and the somite cells (from the VLL) that have crossed the lateral somitic frontier. These migrated cells will form muscles of the body wall and limbs. This domain is characterized by signals originating from the lateral plate mesoderm.

Cells in the abaxial domain receive their differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm, not the notochord. The notochord and neural tube provide signals primarily to the primaxial domain. This signaling difference is a key functional distinction between the two domains.

The muscles of the back and some intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. The abaxial domain is responsible for the infrahyoid, abdominal wall, and limb muscles. Understanding which muscles belong to which domain is essential for clinical embryology.

The lateral somitic frontier is not a transient structure that disappears before dermomyotome formation; rather, it is established as the somite differentiates. It serves as a lasting boundary that defines the developmental environment for different muscle groups. It is fundamental to the modern understanding of muscle patterning.
citation: Initially, there is a well-defined border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier. This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain.

coreConcept: Innervation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles
statement: A researcher is studying the innervation of the axial musculature. Which statement correctly describes the relationship between the muscle groups and their nerve supply?
options: Epaxial muscles are innervated by ventral primary rami.
Hypaxial muscles are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
All muscles derived from the abaxial domain are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
Epaxial muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
Innervation is determined by the lateral somitic frontier rather than the spinal segment.
correctOption: Epaxial muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami.
optionExplanations: Epaxial muscles, which are the muscles of the back, are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves, not the ventral rami. The ventral rami are reserved for the hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs. This anatomical division is consistent throughout the spinal column.

Hypaxial muscles, including those of the body wall and limbs, are innervated by the ventral primary rami, not the dorsal rami. The dorsal rami only supply the true back muscles and the overlying skin. This distinction is a core concept in gross anatomy and embryology.

Muscles derived from the abaxial domain are hypaxial muscles and are therefore innervated by ventral primary rami, not dorsal rami. The abaxial domain includes the abdominal wall and limb muscles. The dorsal rami never supply these migrated muscle groups.

Epaxial muscles, located 'above the axis,' consist of the deep muscles of the back and are strictly innervated by the dorsal primary rami of the spinal nerves. This relationship is established early in development as the myotome divides. It remains a reliable landmark for identifying muscle groups.

Innervation is fundamentally linked to the spinal segment from which the muscle cells originated, regardless of whether they cross the lateral somitic frontier. While the frontier defines the domain (primaxial vs. abaxial), the segmental origin determines the specific spinal nerve. The nerve follows the muscle cells during their migration.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami.

statement: Which of the following is a unique developmental characteristic of cardiac muscle that distinguishes it from skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated fibers.
Cardiac muscle is derived from the paraxial mesoderm.
Cardiac myoblasts adhere via special attachments but do not fuse.
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils during early development.
Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectodermal germ layer.
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts adhere via special attachments but do not fuse.
optionExplanations: In skeletal muscle, myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated muscle fibers, but this does not occur in cardiac muscle. Cardiac muscle cells remain as individual units, although they are highly interconnected. This lack of fusion is a defining histological and developmental difference.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle. The two muscle types have distinct mesodermal origins within the early embryo.

Cardiac myoblasts are characterized by their adherence to one another through specialized junctions that eventually become intercalated discs. Unlike skeletal muscle, these myoblasts do not fuse into a syncytium, allowing for the unique branched structure of cardiac tissue. This allows for coordinated contraction without losing individual cell integrity.

Myofibrils develop in cardiac muscle in a manner very similar to skeletal muscle, providing the contractile apparatus. They are present and essential for the early beating of the heart tube. The statement that they are lacking is incorrect.

Cardiac muscle is entirely mesodermal in origin, specifically from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm contributes to the nervous system and certain smooth muscles, but not the myocardium. The heart is one of the first functional organs to develop from the mesoderm.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myofibrils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible.

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal, certain specific smooth muscles are derived from the ectoderm. Which of the following is an example of such a muscle?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Smooth muscle of the bladder wall
Dilator pupillae muscle
Smooth muscle of the coronary arteries
Rectus abdominis muscle
correctOption: Dilator pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta is derived from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells, not the ectoderm. Neural crest cells are often considered a fourth germ layer but are distinct from the surface ectoderm that forms these specific smooth muscles. The aorta's origin is complex and multi-source.

The smooth muscle of the bladder wall is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm that surrounds the urogenital sinus. It follows the general rule that smooth muscle of internal organs is mesodermal. It is not an ectodermal derivative.

The dilator pupillae and sphincter pupillae muscles of the eye are classic examples of smooth muscles derived from the ectoderm (specifically the neuroectoderm of the optic cup). Other examples include the smooth muscle in mammary and sweat glands. This is a rare exception to the rule of mesodermal muscle origin.

Smooth muscle in the coronary arteries originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. It is not derived from the surface ectoderm. The proepicardium is a mesothelial structure that contributes significantly to the heart's vasculature.

The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle, not a smooth muscle, and it is derived from the ventrolateral cells of the somites (paraxial mesoderm). It belongs to the abaxial domain of skeletal muscle development. It has no ectodermal component.
