optionExplanations: This option incorrectly reverses the signaling sources for the two domains. Primaxial cells are located near the axial structures (neural tube/notochord) and receive signals from them. Abaxial cells migrate into the lateral plate and are influenced by that environment.

Primaxial muscle cell precursors remain in the paraxial mesoderm and are influenced by signals from the neural tube and notochord. In contrast, abaxial precursors migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm, where they receive their differentiation signals. This spatial separation ensures the development of distinct muscle groups.

While the overlying ectoderm does provide some developmental cues, the primary distinction between these two domains is their reliance on either axial structures (neural tube/notochord) or the lateral plate mesoderm. The signals are not identical, which allows for the diversification of muscle types. The frontier acts as a boundary for these different signaling environments.

The neural tube signals the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. The yolk sac endoderm is not a primary signaling center for skeletal muscle differentiation in the somitic domains. Abaxial cells are specifically influenced by the lateral plate mesoderm they inhabit.

The proepicardial organ is involved in cardiac and coronary development, not the signaling of primaxial skeletal muscle. The sclerotome forms bone and does not serve as the primary signaling source for the differentiation of the abaxial muscle precursors. Abaxial cells depend on the lateral plate mesoderm for their cues.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord (Fig. 11.3).

statement: Which of the following processes directly leads to the formation of the dermomyotome during somite differentiation?
options: The fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers within the sclerotome region.
The migration of cells from the VLL and DML lips to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome.
The epithelization of the ventral region of the somite to form a mesenchymal ball.
The migration of neural crest cells into the dorsal region of the paraxial mesoderm.
The transformation of the entire somite into bone-forming cells for the vertebrae.
correctOption: The migration of cells from the VLL and DML lips to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome.
optionExplanations: Myoblast fusion occurs later in development during the differentiation of muscle fibers, not during the initial formation of the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is a precursor structure containing progenitor cells. Sclerotome is the bone-forming region, not the site of dermomyotome formation.

The dermomyotome is formed when cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips of the upper somite migrate and proliferate. These cells position themselves ventral to the dermatome. This combined structure then serves as the source for both dermis and muscle tissues.

Epithelization occurs when somitomeres first form somites, but the ventral region actually undergoes the opposite process—becoming mesenchymal—to form the sclerotome. The dermomyotome forms from the upper (dorsal) region of the somite. The 'ball' of epithelial cells is the initial state of the somite before differentiation.

Neural crest cells do not form the dermomyotome; they contribute to other structures like the peripheral nervous system and certain smooth muscles. The dermomyotome is derived entirely from the paraxial mesoderm of the somite. It is the precursor for skeletal muscle and the dermis of the back.

Only the ventral region of the somite (the sclerotome) transforms into bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. The upper region is reserved for the dermatome and myotome. If the entire somite became bone, no skeletal muscle or dermis would develop from that segment.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome (Fig. 11.1B,C; Fig. 11.2).

coreConcept: Cardiac muscle development and intercalated discs
statement: In contrast to skeletal muscle development, which of the following is a unique characteristic of cardiac muscle cell differentiation?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated syncytia.
Cardiac muscle cells lack myofibrils in their cytoplasm during development.
Cardiac myoblasts adhere via special attachments that become intercalated discs instead of fusing.
Cardiac muscle is derived from the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm.
Cardiac muscle cells lose their nuclei entirely by the end of the third month.
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts adhere via special attachments that become intercalated discs instead of fusing.
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle myoblasts fuse to form multinucleated fibers, but cardiac myoblasts do not. Cardiac muscle remains composed of individual cells that are functionally connected but physically distinct. This lack of fusion is a hallmark difference between the two muscle types.

Cardiac muscle cells do develop myofibrils, and the process is very similar to that seen in skeletal muscle. These myofibrils are responsible for the contractile nature of the heart. Only the Purkinje fibers have 'irregularly distributed' myofibrils, but they are still present.

A defining feature of cardiac muscle development is that the myoblasts adhere to one another through specialized attachments. These attachments eventually mature into intercalated discs, which allow for rapid electrical and mechanical coupling. Unlike skeletal muscle, these cells do not fuse into a single multinucleated fiber.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the parietal layer. The visceral layer surrounds the endothelial heart tube. The parietal layer is involved in forming the body wall and limb muscles.

Cardiac muscle cells do not lose their nuclei; they remain nucleated throughout their lifespan to maintain cellular functions. Skeletal muscle fibers are also nucleated, though they are multinucleated due to fusion. No muscle type becomes anucleated like a mature red blood cell.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

statement: What is the fundamental rule regarding the innervation of developing myotomes by spinal nerves?
options: Each myotome is innervated by nerves from the adjacent cranial segment.
Innervation is determined solely by whether the muscle is in the primaxial or abaxial domain.
Each myotome receives innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells.
Myotomes lose their original segmental innervation as they migrate into the limbs.
All axial muscles are innervated by a single collective nerve plexus regardless of origin.
correctOption: Each myotome receives innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells.
optionExplanations: Innervation is not derived from the cranial segment; it is strictly segmental. The nerve follows the muscle cells as they develop and migrate from their original somite level. This ensures that the functional relationship between the spinal cord and muscle is maintained.

The domain (primaxial vs. abaxial) describes the embryological origin and signaling environment, but it does not change the rule of innervation. Both domains follow the same segmental innervation pattern. The nerve supply is linked to the somite of origin, not the final domain.

The embryological rule is that each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves that originate from the same segment as the muscle cells themselves. This segmental relationship is maintained even if the muscle cells migrate far from their point of origin, such as into the limbs. This is a key principle in understanding dermatomes and myotomes.

Myotomes do not lose their original segmental innervation when they migrate. For example, limb muscles carry their segmental nerve supply with them as they move into the limb buds. This is why specific spinal cord levels correspond to specific muscle groups in the adult.

There is no single collective nerve plexus for all axial muscles. Instead, the innervation is highly organized and segmental, reflecting the original somitic pattern. While plexuses do form for the limbs, they are still composed of specific segmental nerves.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles), which was based on a functional de fi nition of innervation.

statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation affect the functional capacity of the associated myotomes?
options: It causes myotomes to fuse into a single continuous muscle sheet along the entire spine.
It forces myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, allowing them to move the vertebral column.
It results in the total loss of segmental innervation for the axial muscles.
It converts the myotomes into cartilaginous precursors for the ribs.
It separates the myotomes from the neural tube, preventing further differentiation.
correctOption: It forces myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, allowing them to move the vertebral column.
optionExplanations: Resegmentation does not cause the myotomes to fuse into a single sheet; they remain segmentally organized. This organization is crucial for the fine motor control of the vertebral column. The muscles remain distinct units associated with specific segments.

Resegmentation involves the caudal half of one sclerotome fusing with the cranial half of the next. This shift causes the myotomes, which were originally aligned with the sclerotomes, to bridge the newly formed intervertebral discs. This anatomical arrangement is what enables the muscles to exert force across the joints of the spine and produce movement.

Resegmentation does not affect the segmental innervation; the spinal nerves still exit between the vertebrae to reach their respective myotomes. The relationship between the nerve and the muscle segment remains intact. In fact, the nerves now pass through the intervertebral foramina created by this process.

Myotomes do not convert into cartilage; they are the precursors for muscle tissue. The sclerotome is the structure that forms the cartilaginous and bony components of the ribs and vertebrae. The myotome remains myogenic throughout development.

Resegmentation does not separate the myotomes from the neural tube in a way that prevents differentiation. The myotomes continue to receive signals and innervation from the neural tube and spinal cord. The process is a structural reorganization of the skeletal elements, not a disruption of muscle development.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells.

coreConcept: Origin of coronary artery smooth muscle
statement: Which specific cell populations contribute to the formation of smooth muscle in the coronary arteries?
options: Only the paraxial mesoderm from the occipital somites.
Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells.
Visceral lateral plate mesoderm and endodermal cells.
Ectodermal cells from the mammary gland primordia.
Sclerotome cells and DML-derived myoblasts.
correctOption: Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells.
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm from occipital somites contributes to skeletal muscles of the tongue and neck, not the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries. Coronary vessels have a distinct developmental origin related to the heart's epicardial layer. Somites do not contribute to the coronary vasculature.

The smooth muscle of the coronary arteries has a dual origin: the majority arises from proepicardial cells that migrate over the heart, while the proximal segments receive contributions from neural crest cells. This is distinct from the smooth muscle of the gut or the dorsal aorta. Understanding these origins is vital for cardiac embryology.

While visceral lateral plate mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut, it is not the primary source for coronary artery smooth muscle. Endodermal cells provide the lining for the gut but do not differentiate into muscle. The coronary vessels develop within the epicardial environment.

Ectodermal cells contribute to the smooth muscle of the pupil and certain glands, but they do not participate in the formation of coronary artery smooth muscle. The coronary arteries are primarily mesodermal and neural crest in origin. Ectoderm's role in musculature is very limited.

Sclerotome cells form bone and cartilage, and DML-derived myoblasts form skeletal muscles of the back. Neither of these populations contributes to the smooth muscle found in the walls of blood vessels. Smooth muscle differentiation follows different pathways than skeletal muscle formation.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures.

coreConcept: Abaxial muscle precursors and their derivatives
statement: Which of the following muscle groups is derived from the abaxial muscle cell precursors that migrate into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Muscles of the back (erector spinae)
Intercostal muscles
Abdominal wall muscles (e.g., rectus abdominis)
Muscles of the shoulder girdle
Cardiac muscle of the ventricles
correctOption: Abdominal wall muscles (e.g., rectus abdominis)
optionExplanations: Muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are derived from the primaxial domain. These cells do not cross the lateral somitic frontier and remain in the paraxial mesoderm. They are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves.

Intercostal muscles are considered part of the primaxial domain, as they remain within the paraxial mesoderm environment during development. They do not migrate into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm. They are derived from the remaining cells in the myotome after VLL migration.

The abdominal wall muscles, including the rectus abdominis, internal and external obliques, and transversus abdominis, are classic examples of abaxial derivatives. These muscles form from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm. This migration is a key step in body wall development.

Muscles of the shoulder girdle are listed as being formed by the remaining cells in the myotome that do not migrate into the lateral plate, placing them in the primaxial domain. They are distinct from the limb muscles which are abaxial. Their development is influenced by signals from the neural tube and notochord.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not from the abaxial skeletal muscle precursors. While both are mesodermal, they represent entirely different lineages and developmental processes. Abaxial precursors only form skeletal muscle.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles (Table 11.1, p. 162).

statement: Immediately following segmentation, what morphological change do somitomeres undergo to form somites?
options: They undergo apoptosis to create space for the neural tube.
They undergo epithelization to form a 'ball' of epithelial cells with a central cavity.
They immediately differentiate into multinucleated myoblasts.
They migrate to the lateral plate mesoderm to form the heart tube.
They fuse with the overlying ectoderm to form the dermis.
correctOption: They undergo epithelization to form a 'ball' of epithelial cells with a central cavity.
optionExplanations: Somitomeres do not undergo apoptosis; they are the precursors to the somites which form the bulk of the axial skeleton and musculature. Apoptosis is a programmed cell death that occurs in other developmental contexts. The formation of somites is a constructive process of organization.

The transition from somitomeres to somites is characterized by epithelization. The mesenchymal cells organize into a spherical 'ball' of epithelial cells surrounding a small central cavity. This organized structure is the starting point for further differentiation into sclerotome, dermatome, and myotome.

Differentiation into myoblasts is a much later process that occurs after the somite has further subdivided into the dermomyotome. The initial formation of the somite is purely a structural reorganization into an epithelial state. Myoblasts are the result of later myogenic commitment.

Somitomeres do not migrate to the lateral plate to form the heart; the heart develops from the visceral mesoderm in the cardiogenic area. Somites remain in the paraxial position to form axial structures. Only specific cells from the somite (VLL) migrate later in development.

Somites do not fuse with the ectoderm. While the dermatome part of the somite will eventually contribute to the dermis of the back, it remains a distinct mesodermal layer beneath the ectoderm. The initial epithelization is an internal reorganization of the paraxial mesoderm.
citation: Musculature of the aXial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites, which initially form as somitomeres and extend from the occipital region to the tail bud. Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A).

coreConcept: Development of the cardiac conducting system
statement: What are the specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that form the heart's conducting system called, and what is their histological characteristic?
options: Intercalated discs; they provide mechanical strength.
Myofibrils; they are organized into dense cross-striations.
Purkinje fibers; they contain irregularly distributed myofibrils.
Sclerotomes; they are mesenchymal cells that form bone.
Myoblasts; they fuse to form multinucleated syncytia.
correctOption: Purkinje fibers; they contain irregularly distributed myofibrils.
optionExplanations: Intercalated discs are the specialized cell-to-cell junctions between cardiac myocytes, not the conducting bundles themselves. They facilitate electrical and mechanical coupling but do not constitute the Purkinje system. They are a feature of all cardiac muscle cells, not just the conducting system.

Myofibrils are the contractile elements found in all muscle cells. While they are present in the conducting system, their 'irregular distribution' is what characterizes Purkinje fibers. In regular cardiac and skeletal muscle, myofibrils are highly organized to produce striations.

Purkinje fibers are specialized cardiac muscle cells that make up the heart's conducting system. Histologically, they are distinguished by having fewer and more irregularly distributed myofibrils compared to standard contractile cardiomyocytes. They are specialized for electrical conduction rather than forceful contraction.

Sclerotomes are the ventral parts of somites that give rise to the vertebrae and ribs. They are not related to the cardiac conducting system or muscle tissue. They represent the osteogenic lineage of the paraxial mesoderm.

Myoblasts are the precursor cells for muscle, but in the heart, they do not fuse to form multinucleated syncytia. The conducting system cells (Purkinje fibers) are specialized myocytes, not fused myoblasts. Fusion is a unique characteristic of skeletal muscle development.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

statement: According to the functional definition of innervation, which nerves supply the epaxial and hypaxial muscles, respectively?
options: Epaxial by ventral primary rami; Hypaxial by dorsal primary rami.
Epaxial by dorsal primary rami; Hypaxial by ventral primary rami.
Both are innervated by the sympathetic trunk.
Epaxial by cranial nerves; Hypaxial by spinal nerves.
Both are innervated exclusively by the phrenic nerve.
correctOption: Epaxial by dorsal primary rami; Hypaxial by ventral primary rami.
optionExplanations: This option incorrectly swaps the innervation. Epaxial muscles, which are located dorsally (above the axis), are supplied by the dorsal rami. Hypaxial muscles, located ventrally (below the axis), are supplied by the ventral rami.

The traditional functional classification of muscles into epaxial and hypaxial groups is based on their innervation. Epaxial muscles (the deep muscles of the back) are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. Hypaxial muscles (including those of the body wall and limbs) are innervated by the ventral primary rami.

The sympathetic trunk provides autonomic innervation to smooth muscles and glands, not motor innervation to the skeletal muscles of the epaxial and hypaxial groups. Skeletal muscles are supplied by somatic motor fibers within the primary rami. The sympathetic system is not involved in this segmental motor pattern.

While some head muscles are innervated by cranial nerves, the epaxial and hypaxial muscles of the trunk and limbs are strictly supplied by spinal nerves. The division into dorsal and ventral rami is a feature of the spinal nerve organization. Cranial nerves do not follow this epaxial/hypaxial rami pattern.

The phrenic nerve specifically innervates the diaphragm, which is a hypaxial muscle derived from cervical somites. It does not innervate the epaxial muscles of the back or all other hypaxial muscles. Innervation is segmental across the entire length of the spinal cord.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

statement: During the differentiation of a somite, what is the primary fate of the cells in its ventral region?
options: They form the dermis of the skin.
They become the sclerotome, which forms the vertebrae and ribs.
They differentiate into the cardiac muscle of the heart tube.
They form the VLL and DML muscle-forming lips.
They remain epithelial and form the lining of the gut.
correctOption: They become the sclerotome, which forms the vertebrae and ribs.
optionExplanations: The dermis of the skin (specifically the back) is formed by the dermatome, which is located in the upper (dorsal) region of the somite, not the ventral region. The ventral region has a different osteogenic fate. The dermatome and myotome together form the dermomyotome.

The ventral region of the somite undergoes a transition back to mesenchyme to become the sclerotome. These cells are the precursors for the axial skeleton, specifically the vertebrae and the ribs. This process is distinct from the development of the dorsal part of the somite.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral lateral plate mesoderm, not from the somites. Somites are paraxial mesoderm and contribute to skeletal muscle and bone. The heart develops in a completely different mesodermal domain.

The VLL and DML muscle-forming lips are located in the upper (dorsal) region of the somite, as part of the dermomyotome. They are not derived from the ventral sclerotome region. These lips are the source of all skeletal muscle cells from the somite.

The lining of the gut is derived from the endoderm, not the mesodermal somites. The somites provide the skeletal and muscular components of the body, while the endoderm provides the epithelial linings. The ventral somite cells are committed to a skeletal (mesenchymal) fate.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B).

statement: Which of the following is a defining characteristic of the 'primaxial domain' in muscle development?
options: It contains a mixture of neural crest and lateral plate mesoderm cells.
It is located far from the neural tube in the developing limb buds.
It contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells.
It is the region where smooth muscle of the gut originates.
It is defined by cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.
correctOption: It contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells.
optionExplanations: The primaxial domain does not contain neural crest or lateral plate mesoderm cells; it is composed exclusively of paraxial mesoderm. Neural crest cells contribute to other structures, and lateral plate mesoderm is the defining component of the abaxial domain. The primaxial domain is 'pure' in its somitic origin.

The primaxial domain is located immediately around the neural tube and notochord. It is the abaxial domain that extends into the limb buds and body wall. The proximity to the neural tube is why primaxial cells receive signals from axial structures.

By definition, the primaxial domain consists only of cells derived from the somites (paraxial mesoderm) that remain in their original location. These cells do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. This domain gives rise to muscles like those of the back and the intercostals.

Smooth muscle of the gut originates from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, which is not part of the primaxial domain. The primaxial domain is strictly related to skeletal muscle development. Gut development occurs in the splanchnic mesoderm.

Cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier become part of the abaxial domain, not the primaxial domain. The frontier serves as the boundary that separates these two distinct developmental regions. Primaxial cells are those that stay 'behind' the frontier.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

coreConcept: Smooth muscle of the gut and visceral mesoderm
statement: From which specific embryonic tissue does the smooth muscle in the wall of the gastrointestinal tract develop?
options: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm (somites)
Ectoderm of the neural plate
Endoderm of the primitive gut
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm forms the body wall and contributes to the abaxial skeletal muscle domain. It does not form the smooth muscle of the gut. The visceral layer is the one that closely invests the internal organs.

The smooth muscle of the gut and its derivatives (like the respiratory tract) is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This layer surrounds the endodermal tube and differentiates into the muscularis externa and other smooth muscle layers. This is a fundamental concept in organogenesis.

Paraxial mesoderm (somites) gives rise to skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton, not the smooth muscle of the internal organs. While some head muscles are paraxial, the gut musculature is entirely lateral plate in origin. Somites are located dorsally, while the gut is more ventral.

Ectoderm only gives rise to a few specific smooth muscles, such as those in the eye and certain glands. It does not contribute to the extensive smooth muscle found in the gastrointestinal tract. The neural plate specifically forms the central nervous system.

The endoderm provides the epithelial lining of the gut and its glands, but it does not form the muscle. The muscle is always mesodermal (with rare ectodermal exceptions). The interaction between the endoderm and the visceral mesoderm is crucial for gut development.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

coreConcept: Derivatives of the abaxial muscle cell precursors
statement: A researcher is studying the migration of ventrolateral lip (VLL) cells across the lateral somitic frontier. Which of the following muscle groups is exclusively formed by these migrated cells within the abaxial domain?
options: Intercostal muscles
Muscles of the back
Shoulder girdle muscles
Rectus abdominis muscle
Intrinsic muscles of the tongue
correctOption: Rectus abdominis muscle
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial domain, consisting of cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. These cells receive their developmental signals primarily from the neural tube and notochord rather than the lateral plate mesoderm. They are functionally categorized as hypaxial but embryologically belong to the primaxial population.

Muscles of the back are classic examples of primaxial derivatives that develop from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and the remaining ventrolateral lip (VLL) cells that do not migrate. These muscles are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. They remain in the region surrounding the neural tube throughout their development.

Shoulder girdle muscles are formed by primaxial muscle cell precursors that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. While they are located near the limb, their embryological origin is distinct from the abaxial limb muscles. They rely on signaling environments provided by the paraxial mesoderm and axial structures.

The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle formed by VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This domain transition allows these cells to respond to signals from the lateral plate mesoderm for their differentiation. Other muscles in this group include the external and internal obliques and the transversus abdominis.

Intrinsic muscles of the tongue are derived from occipital somites but are not considered part of the abaxial domain formed by migration into the lateral plate mesoderm. Their development is closely tied to the cranial nerve innervation and the pharyngeal arch environment. They do not follow the same VLL migration pattern into the body wall as the abdominal muscles.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles

coreConcept: Cellular characteristics of developing cardiac muscle
statement: Which of the following cellular features distinguishes the development of cardiac muscle from that of skeletal muscle?
options: Origin from the paraxial mesoderm
Formation of long, multinucleated syncytia through fusion
Absence of myofibrils in the cytoplasm
Development of intercalated discs from cell attachments
Derivation from the ectodermal germ layer
correctOption: Development of intercalated discs from cell attachments
optionExplanations: Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the specific source for skeletal muscles of the axis and limbs. This distinction in germ layer sub-specialization is fundamental to the different signaling environments each muscle type encounters.

The formation of multinucleated syncytia through the fusion of myoblasts is a hallmark of skeletal muscle development, not cardiac muscle. In cardiac development, myoblasts remain as individual cells that adhere to one another rather than merging their cytoplasms. This allows for the distinct cellular architecture required for cardiac conduction and contraction.

Myofibrils are present in both cardiac and skeletal muscle as the primary contractile apparatus. In both tissues, these fibrils develop within the cytoplasm to provide the machinery for contraction. The presence of myofibrils is what eventually leads to the striated appearance of both muscle types by the end of the third month.

Cardiac myoblasts adhere to one another via specialized attachments that eventually differentiate into intercalated discs. These discs are critical for mechanical and electrical coupling between individual cardiac myocytes, ensuring synchronized contraction of the heart. This feature is unique to cardiac muscle and is not found in skeletal muscle fibers.

Cardiac muscle is mesodermal in origin, specifically from the splanchnic layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm only gives rise to very specific smooth muscles, such as those in the iris and certain glands. The heart tube and its surrounding muscular mantle are entirely products of the mesodermal germ layer.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myofibrils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal, which of the following muscles is derived from the ectodermal germ layer?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Dilator pupillae muscle
Muscularis externa of the esophagus
Smooth muscle of the coronary arteries
Trachealis muscle
correctOption: Dilator pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: Smooth muscle in the dorsal aorta is derived from a combination of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. It is not an ectodermal derivative, although neural crest cells themselves originate from the neural folds. The primary structural components of large vessels are mesodermal in nature.

The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are unique because they differentiate from the ectoderm of the optic cup. This is an exception to the general rule that muscle tissue is mesodermal. Other ectodermal muscles include those associated with the mammary and sweat glands.

The muscularis externa of the esophagus, like the rest of the gut wall smooth muscle, is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This mesoderm surrounds the endodermal heart tube and gut tube during early folding. It does not have an ectodermal contribution.

Smooth muscle in the coronary arteries originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. Proepicardial cells are mesodermal derivatives that migrate over the heart surface. This complex origin does not involve the surface ectoderm or the neuroectoderm that forms the pupillary muscles.

The trachealis muscle is a smooth muscle derivative of the visceral mesoderm that surrounds the respiratory diverticulum. Since the respiratory system is a derivative of the foregut, its associated smooth muscle follows the same developmental pattern as the gut wall. It is strictly mesodermal in origin.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and mesodermal domains
statement: The 'lateral somitic frontier' is a critical developmental boundary. Which of the following best describes the composition of the abaxial domain relative to this frontier?
options: It contains only cells derived from the paraxial mesoderm
It consists of the neural tube and the surrounding notochord
It comprises the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
It is composed exclusively of cells from the dorsomedial lip (DML)
It represents the epithelial ball formed immediately after somitomere segmentation
correctOption: It comprises the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The abaxial domain is defined by the presence of migrated somite cells, so it cannot contain only paraxial mesoderm. The primaxial domain is the one that contains only somite-derived cells. The abaxial domain is a mixed population of cells from two different mesodermal sources.

The neural tube and notochord are axial structures that provide signaling for the primaxial domain, but they are not part of the abaxial domain itself. The abaxial domain is located more laterally in the embryo, within the body wall and limb buds. These axial structures are ectodermal and mesodermal, respectively, but do not define the abaxial territory.

The abaxial domain is defined as the region consisting of the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm along with the somite cells (from the VLL) that have crossed the lateral somitic frontier. This domain is distinct because its muscle precursors receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm. This concept replaces the older, purely functional epimere/hypomere classification.

Cells from the dorsomedial lip (DML) do not cross the lateral somitic frontier; they remain in the primaxial domain. The DML contributes to the muscles of the back and intercostals. Only a specific subset of cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) migrates to form the abaxial population.

The epithelial ball is the transient state of the somite immediately after segmentation from the somitomeres. This structure later differentiates into the sclerotome and dermomyotome. The lateral somitic frontier and the resulting domains only become relevant after this initial epithelial stage as cells begin to migrate.
citation: Initially, there is a well-defined border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells

statement: A developmental biologist observes that certain muscle precursor cells fail to differentiate because they are isolated from the neural tube and notochord. These cells most likely belong to which population?
options: Abaxial muscle cell precursors
Primaxial muscle cell precursors
Smooth muscle cells of the gut
Cardiac myoblasts
Ectodermal pupillary muscles
correctOption: Primaxial muscle cell precursors
optionExplanations: Abaxial muscle cell precursors receive their primary differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm, not the neural tube or notochord. Because they have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier, they are exposed to a different signaling environment. Their development would be less affected by the absence of axial structures compared to primaxial cells.

Primaxial muscle cell precursors remain in the paraxial mesoderm near the axis and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord. This population includes all cells from the DML and the non-migratory cells from the VLL. Without these axial signals, these cells would fail to differentiate into the muscles of the back and shoulder girdle.

Smooth muscle cells of the gut differentiate from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Their differentiation is governed by signals from the endodermal gut tube they surround. They are not dependent on the neural tube or notochord for their primary myogenic specification.

Cardiac myoblasts differentiate from the visceral mesoderm surrounding the heart tube in the cranial region of the embryo. Their signaling environment is dominated by the endoderm of the anterior foregut and the heart tube itself. They do not rely on the neural tube or notochord for their initial differentiation into cardiac tissue.

Ectodermal pupillary muscles differentiate from the neuroectoderm of the optic cup. Their development is tied to the morphogenesis of the eye and local signals within the optic vesicle. They are entirely independent of the paraxial mesoderm signaling pathways involving the notochord.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors

statement: What is the functional significance of the resegmentation of sclerotomes during the development of the axial skeleton?
options: It allows the spinal nerves to exit the vertebral column
It ensures that each muscle is innervated by multiple spinal segments
It enables myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs to facilitate spinal movement
It triggers the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers
It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
correctOption: It enables myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs to facilitate spinal movement
optionExplanations: While resegmentation does create the pathways for spinal nerves to exit, the primary functional consequence for the muscular system is the relationship between myotomes and vertebrae. The nerves actually pass through the intervertebral foramen created by the resegmentation process. However, the question asks for the significance regarding the myotomes' capacity.

Resegmentation does not change the segmental nature of innervation; each myotome continues to be innervated by the spinal nerve from its original segment. The segmental pattern is strictly maintained throughout development. This ensures a one-to-one relationship between the spinal cord segment and the muscle derivative.

Resegmentation involves the caudal half of one sclerotome fusing with the cranial half of the next. This shift causes the myotomes, which were originally aligned with the sclerotomes, to now span the gap between the newly formed definitive vertebrae. This bridging of the intervertebral discs is what allows the muscles to exert leverage and move the vertebral column.

The fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers is a cellular differentiation process that occurs within the myotome. It is independent of the macroscopic resegmentation of the sclerotomes. Striations and fusion are governed by myogenic regulatory factors rather than the skeletal arrangement of the vertebrae.

The separation of the primaxial and abaxial domains is established by the lateral somitic frontier, which is a boundary between the somite and the lateral plate mesoderm. Resegmentation is a process occurring within the paraxial mesoderm (sclerotomes) and does not define the boundary with the lateral plate. These are two distinct developmental events.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells

statement: During somite differentiation, where are the progenitor muscle cells that form the dermomyotome located relative to the dermatome?
options: Dorsal to the dermatome
Ventral to the dermatome
Medial to the sclerotome
Lateral to the lateral plate mesoderm
Inside the central cavity of the epithelial ball
correctOption: Ventral to the dermatome
optionExplanations: The dermatome is the most dorsal part of the somite after the sclerotome has formed ventrally. Muscle cells do not migrate to a position dorsal to the dermatome. Instead, the dermatome remains the outer layer that will eventually contribute to the dermis of the back.

Progenitor muscle cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips migrate and proliferate to form a layer of cells directly ventral to the dermatome. This combined structure is known as the dermomyotome. This arrangement is essential for the subsequent separation of the myotome from the dermatome.

The sclerotome is located in the ventral region of the somite and becomes mesenchymal to form bone. The muscle-forming cells are located more dorsally than the sclerotome. While they are medial to the lateral plate, they are not described as being medial to the sclerotome in the context of dermomyotome formation.

The lateral plate mesoderm is located lateral to the somite. While some VLL cells migrate into it, the dermomyotome itself is a somitic structure and remains medial to the lateral plate mesoderm. The abaxial domain is where somite cells and lateral plate mesoderm meet.

The epithelial ball has a small cavity initially, but this structure is lost as the somite differentiates into the sclerotome and dermomyotome. The progenitor muscle cells form a distinct layer rather than filling the primitive cavity. The cavity is a transient feature of the early somite stage.
citation: Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome (Fig. 11.1B,C; Fig. 11.2). Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B)

coreConcept: Innervation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles
statement: A patient presents with weakness in the muscles of the back (epaxial muscles). Which nerve components are most likely responsible for the innervation of these specific muscles?
options: Ventral primary rami
Dorsal primary rami
Cranial nerves
Sympathetic trunk
Lateral cord of the brachial plexus
correctOption: Dorsal primary rami
optionExplanations: Ventral primary rami innervate the hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the body wall and the limbs. Although some hypaxial muscles are primaxial in origin (like the intercostals), they are still supplied by the ventral rami. The division into dorsal and ventral rami is a functional and anatomical one.

Epaxial muscles, which are the muscles of the back located 'above the axis,' are strictly innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. This relationship is maintained regardless of the modern classification of these muscles into the primaxial domain. This anatomical rule is a key feature of segmental innervation.

Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the somitomeres in the head and the pharyngeal arches. The epaxial muscles of the back are derived from somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and are supplied by spinal nerves. Cranial nerves do not extend to the true back muscles.

The sympathetic trunk is part of the autonomic nervous system and provides visceral motor innervation to smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands. It does not provide the somatic motor innervation required for skeletal muscles like the epaxial group. Skeletal muscle is always supplied by somatic motor fibers.

The lateral cord of the brachial plexus is formed by ventral primary rami and supplies muscles of the upper limb (hypaxial muscles). It does not contribute to the innervation of the epaxial muscles of the back. The brachial plexus is entirely a ventral ramus structure.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in large vessels
statement: The smooth muscle found in the wall of the dorsal aorta has a dual embryological origin. What are the two primary sources?
options: Paraxial mesoderm and ectoderm
Visceral mesoderm and endoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Sclerotome and dermatome
Proepicardial cells and paraxial mesoderm
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm primarily forms skeletal muscle, and ectoderm forms only very specific smooth muscles like the pupillary muscles. The dorsal aorta does not receive contributions from the paraxial mesoderm. The vessel walls are formed from more lateral mesodermal populations.

Visceral mesoderm (splanchnic) forms the smooth muscle of the gut and the heart, but the dorsal aorta specifically involves the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest. Endoderm provides the epithelial lining of the gut and respiratory tract but does not contribute to muscle tissue. The aorta is a mesodermal and neural crest structure.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries is uniquely derived from both the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. This dual origin is significant for understanding certain vascular pathologies. Neural crest cells contribute particularly to the vessels near the heart and the arch of the aorta.

The sclerotome and dermatome are subdivisions of the somite (paraxial mesoderm). The sclerotome forms bone and the dermatome forms dermis. Neither of these somitic components contributes to the smooth muscle of the large arterial walls. Somites are focused on the axial skeleton and its associated skeletal muscles.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, not the dorsal aorta. While neural crest cells contribute to both, the second source for the dorsal aorta is the lateral plate mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is not involved in the formation of the great vessels.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer

coreConcept: Development of the cardiac conducting system
statement: What are Purkinje fibers in the heart embryologically derived from, and what is their histological characteristic?
options: Neural crest cells; they contain dense, regular myofibrils
Modified cardiac muscle cells; they have irregularly distributed myofibrils
Endocardial cells; they lack myofibrils entirely
Paraxial mesoderm; they form multinucleated syncytia
Visceral mesoderm; they are identical to skeletal muscle fibers
correctOption: Modified cardiac muscle cells; they have irregularly distributed myofibrils
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are not derived from neural crest cells, although neural crest cells do contribute to the septation of the heart. Histologically, they do not have dense, regular myofibrils; instead, their myofibrils are sparse and irregularly arranged. They are specialized for conduction rather than forceful contraction.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that differentiate during later development. They are characterized by having irregularly distributed myofibrils and a larger diameter than typical cardiomyocytes. They form the essential conducting system that coordinates the heartbeat.

Endocardial cells form the inner lining of the heart (endothelium) and do not differentiate into the muscular conducting system. Purkinje fibers are muscular in origin, not endothelial. While they are located subendocardially, their lineage is myogenic.

Purkinje fibers are a type of cardiac muscle, which is derived from visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm, not paraxial mesoderm. They do not form multinucleated syncytia because cardiac myoblasts do not fuse. They maintain individual cell boundaries marked by intercalated discs.

While Purkinje fibers are derived from visceral mesoderm, they are not identical to skeletal muscle fibers. Skeletal muscle fibers are multinucleated and formed by fusion, whereas Purkinje fibers are modified cardiac cells. Their myofibril distribution is also distinct from the highly organized pattern in skeletal muscle.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fibers, form the conducting system of the heart. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube

statement: What is the first morphological change that occurs in somitomeres immediately after they segment from the paraxial mesoderm?
options: Migration into the lateral plate mesoderm
Fusion into multinucleated myoblasts
Epithelization into a ball of cells with a central cavity
Differentiation into the definitive vertebrae
Formation of intercalated discs
correctOption: Epithelization into a ball of cells with a central cavity
optionExplanations: Migration into the lateral plate mesoderm is a later event that only involves a subset of cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL). It does not occur immediately after segmentation. The somite must first organize itself into an epithelial structure before any migration begins.

Fusion into multinucleated myoblasts is a much later stage of skeletal muscle differentiation. This occurs after the myotome has been established and cells have committed to the myogenic lineage. The initial somite formation is about structural organization, not cellular fusion.

Immediately after segmentation, somitomeres undergo epithelization. They transform from a loose mesenchymal arrangement into a organized 'ball' of epithelial cells. This ball contains a small central cavity and represents the early somite before it further divides into sclerotome and dermomyotome.

Differentiation into definitive vertebrae is the end result of sclerotome development and resegmentation. This is a complex process involving the transition of the ventral somite back into mesenchyme. It is not the immediate first step after segmentation.

Intercalated discs are specific to cardiac muscle development and are not found in the somites or the skeletal muscle they produce. Cardiac muscle develops from the visceral mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. Somites are the source of skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A). The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome

statement: At what point during human embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
Beginning of the second trimester
At the time of birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: By the end of the first month, the somites are still forming and differentiating into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblasts have not yet fused into the long fibers necessary for striations to be visible. The muscular system is in its very early stages of specification.

During the second month, myoblasts are actively fusing and myofibrils are beginning to appear in the cytoplasm. However, the highly organized arrangement that produces visible cross-striations is not yet complete. The muscle tissue is still maturing at the cellular level.

Cross-striations, which are the result of the highly ordered arrangement of actin and myosin filaments into sarcomeres, appear by the end of the third month of development. This marks a significant milestone in the functional maturation of skeletal muscle fibers. By this time, the fibers are multinucleated and contain numerous myofibrils.

While the end of the third month is technically the end of the first trimester, the specific developmental milestone is traditionally cited as the end of the third month. Striations are already present as the fetus enters the second trimester. This allows for the early fetal movements that occur during this period.

Skeletal muscle is well-developed and striated long before birth. Fetal movements, which require functional striated muscle, are felt by the mother (quickening) usually in the second trimester. Waiting until birth for striations would mean the fetus would be unable to move in utero.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fibers. Myofibrils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear

statement: Which of the following muscles is considered a derivative of the primaxial domain?
options: Rectus abdominis
Infrahyoid muscles
Shoulder girdle muscles
External oblique
Limb muscles
correctOption: Shoulder girdle muscles
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle. It is formed by VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. It is part of the ventral body wall musculature that receives signals from the lateral plate.

The infrahyoid muscles are also abaxial derivatives. Like the abdominal wall muscles, they originate from somite cells that migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are located in the anterior neck region, far from the primaxial territory.

Shoulder girdle muscles, along with the muscles of the back and the intercostals, are primaxial derivatives. These cells remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They are characterized by their developmental reliance on signals from the neural tube and notochord.

The external oblique is one of the three flat muscles of the abdominal wall, all of which are abaxial. These muscles form from the VLL cells that migrate into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. They are functionally hypaxial and embryologically abaxial.

Limb muscles are the classic example of abaxial derivatives. Precursor cells from the VLL migrate extensively into the limb buds, which are formed by the lateral plate mesoderm. There, they differentiate in response to local limb bud signals.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraxial mesoderm) cells. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier

statement: What is the fundamental rule governing the innervation of developing myotomes by spinal nerves?
options: Innervation is determined by the final adult position of the muscle
Each myotome is innervated by the nerve from its segment of origin
Primaxial muscles are innervated by cranial nerves, and abaxial by spinal nerves
Innervation only occurs after the muscle has reached its final destination
All myotomes are initially innervated by a single plexiform nerve
correctOption: Each myotome is innervated by the nerve from its segment of origin
optionExplanations: Innervation is not determined by the final position; rather, the nerve follows the muscle as it migrates. This is why the diaphragm, which originates in the cervical region, is innervated by the phrenic nerve (C3-C5) even though it ends up in the thorax. The segmental origin is the primary determinant.

The segmental nature of the embryo is strictly preserved in the nervous system. Each myotome is innervated by the spinal nerve derived from the same somitic segment. This relationship is established early and persists regardless of how far the muscle cells migrate (e.g., into the limbs or body wall).

Both primaxial and abaxial muscles are innervated by spinal nerves. The difference lies in which branch of the spinal nerve they receive: dorsal rami for epaxial (primaxial) and ventral rami for hypaxial (which includes both primaxial and abaxial). Cranial nerves only innervate head and neck muscles from somitomeres.

Innervation begins early, and the nerves actually grow into the muscle-forming areas while the cells are still in the process of migrating or organizing. The nerve-muscle connection is one of the earliest established relationships in the developing musculoskeletal system. It is not a late-stage event.

There is no single 'plexiform nerve' for all myotomes. Each segment has its own pair of spinal nerves. While these nerves may later join to form plexuses (like the brachial or lumbosacral plexuses) for the limbs, the underlying segmental contribution remains distinct.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaxial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in the gut
statement: Which embryonic tissue is the primary source of the smooth muscle found in the wall of the gastrointestinal tract?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Surface ectoderm
correctOption: Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle, not the smooth muscle of the gut. It forms the somites that give rise to the axial skeleton and its associated muscles. The gut wall develops from a more lateral and ventral mesodermal population.

The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm forms the body wall (dermis, bones, and connective tissue) and provides the environment for abaxial skeletal muscles. It does not contribute to the muscular wall of the internal organs. The visceral layer is responsible for the organs themselves.

The visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm surrounds the endodermal gut tube. As the gut develops, this mesoderm differentiates into the smooth muscle and connective tissue of the gut wall. This same layer also gives rise to the cardiac muscle surrounding the heart tube.

Neural crest cells contribute to the enteric nervous system (ganglia) within the gut wall, but they do not form the bulk of the smooth muscle tissue itself. The contractile smooth muscle cells are mesodermal. Neural crest cells do, however, contribute to smooth muscle in large arteries.

Surface ectoderm gives rise to the epidermis and certain glands. While it does form a few specific smooth muscles (like those in the mammary and sweat glands), it is not the source for the extensive smooth muscle found in the gastrointestinal tract. The gut muscle is strictly mesodermal.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube

statement: Which embryonic layer is primarily responsible for the development of the skeletal musculature of the axial skeleton and limbs?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Visceral mesoderm
Ectoderm
Neural crest
Endoderm
correctOption: Paraxial mesoderm
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle is uniquely derived from the paraxial mesoderm, which organizes into somites and somitomeres to provide the cellular basis for the axial and limb musculature. This layer undergoes specific epithelial-to-mesenchymal transitions to form the dermomyotome, the precursor to all skeletal muscle fibers. Other mesodermal layers, such as the lateral plate, contribute to different muscle types or connective tissues but not the primary skeletal muscle mass.

Visceral or splanchnic mesoderm is the primary source for cardiac muscle and most smooth muscle, such as that found in the walls of the gut. It does not contribute to the formation of skeletal muscle fibers in the axial skeleton or limbs. This distinction is critical for understanding the different developmental pathways of striated versus non-striated muscle.

Ectoderm is generally responsible for the nervous system and epidermis, though it does contribute to a very small subset of smooth muscles like the pupillary and sweat gland muscles. It is not a source for the large-scale skeletal muscle systems of the body. Skeletal muscle development is strictly a mesodermal process.

Neural crest cells are highly migratory and contribute to various structures including the peripheral nervous system and certain smooth muscles in large arteries. However, they do not form the skeletal muscle fibers of the trunk or limbs. Their role in the muscular system is limited to specific vascular and craniofacial components.

Endoderm primarily forms the epithelial lining of the respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts and associated glands. It does not have a myogenic potential for skeletal, cardiac, or smooth muscle. Muscle development is almost entirely restricted to the mesodermal and, in rare cases, ectodermal layers.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

statement: During the differentiation of a somite, which specific regions contribute to the formation of the dermomyotome?
options: VLL and DML lips
Sclerotome and dermatome
Neural tube and notochord
Parietal and visceral mesoderm
Ectoderm and endoderm
correctOption: VLL and DML lips
optionExplanations: The dermomyotome is formed by the migration and proliferation of progenitor muscle cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips of the somite. These cells move beneath the dermatome to create a layer that will eventually differentiate into the various skeletal muscles of the body. This process represents the initial organization of myogenic precursors before they migrate to their final anatomical positions.

The sclerotome is the ventral portion of the somite that becomes mesenchymal to form the vertebrae and ribs, while the dermatome forms the dermis. While the dermatome is part of the dermomyotome complex, the sclerotome is distinct and does not contribute to muscle formation. The muscle-forming areas are specifically located at the edges or lips of the upper somite.

The neural tube and notochord are axial structures that provide essential signaling for somite differentiation but do not physically contribute cells to the dermomyotome. They influence the fate of the DML and VLL cells through secreted factors. Their role is inductive rather than structural in the context of the dermomyotome's cellular makeup.

Parietal and visceral mesoderm are divisions of the lateral plate mesoderm, which is distinct from the paraxial mesoderm that forms somites. While some VLL cells eventually migrate into the parietal lateral plate, the initial formation of the dermomyotome occurs within the somite itself. These layers are secondary environments for muscle development rather than the primary source of the dermomyotome.

Ectoderm and endoderm are the outer and inner germ layers, respectively, and do not participate in the formation of the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is a specialized structure within the paraxial mesoderm. Any interaction with the ectoderm is primarily for signaling or the eventual formation of the overlying skin.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome.

statement: A developmental defect in the migration of cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) lip into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm would most likely affect which of the following muscles?
options: Rectus abdominis
Erector spinae
Rhomboids
Levator scapulae
Trapezius
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle derived from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This migration is essential for the formation of the ventral body wall musculature. Failure of this migratory process would specifically result in the absence or malformation of these abdominal muscles.

The erector spinae is a primaxial muscle that develops from cells remaining within the paraxial mesoderm near the neural tube. It does not require migration into the lateral plate mesoderm and is therefore unaffected by defects in the lateral somitic frontier crossing. These muscles are traditionally classified as epaxial muscles of the back.

The rhomboids are part of the primaxial domain, meaning they are derived from somitic cells that do not cross into the lateral plate mesoderm. Although they are located in the back, they receive their developmental cues from the neural tube and notochord. Their development is independent of the abaxial migratory pathway.

The levator scapulae is another example of a primaxial muscle that remains within the somitic environment during its early development. It is not formed by cells that migrate into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Consequently, a defect in VLL cell migration to the abaxial domain would not impact its formation.

The trapezius is a muscle of the neck and back that, while complex in its innervation, is not derived from the abaxial migratory population of the VLL lip. It is generally considered part of the primaxial musculature or derived from cranial somitomeres. It would remain intact even if the abaxial migration into the body wall was compromised.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles.

statement: From which structures do the primaxial muscle cell precursors, which remain within the paraxial mesoderm, primarily receive their developmental signals?
options: Neural tube and notochord
Lateral plate mesoderm
Ectoderm and endoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
Parietal layer of mesoderm
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: Primaxial muscle precursors are defined by their location within the paraxial mesoderm and their reliance on signals from the neural tube and notochord. These axial structures provide the necessary environment for the differentiation of muscles like those of the back and intercostals. This signaling pathway is distinct from the one used by abaxial cells, which migrate further away.

The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial muscle precursors that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. It does not serve as the primary signaling source for the primaxial cells that stay within the paraxial domain. The distinction between these two signaling environments is a hallmark of the primaxial/abaxial model.

While the overlying ectoderm may provide some signals for the dermatome, it is not the primary source of developmental cues for the primaxial muscle precursors. The endoderm is even further removed and does not play a significant role in the patterning of these paraxial muscles. The neural tube and notochord are the dominant inductive centers for this domain.

The sclerotome and dermatome are fellow components of the somite but are not the primary sources of the inductive signals that drive myogenesis in the primaxial domain. Instead, they all respond to signals from the neural tube and notochord to achieve their respective fates. The sclerotome specifically responds to ventral signals to become bone-forming tissue.

The parietal layer of the mesoderm is part of the lateral plate and is the destination and signaling environment for abaxial cells. Primaxial cells, by definition, do not enter this layer and therefore do not receive their primary differentiation signals from it. Their development is governed by the more medial axial structures.
citation: those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord (Fig. 11.3). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves.

coreConcept: Innervation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles
statement: In the context of axial muscle development, which of the following correctly matches the muscle group with its primary innervation?
options: Epaxial muscles - Dorsal primary rami
Hypaxial muscles - Dorsal primary rami
Epaxial muscles - Ventral primary rami
Limb muscles - Dorsal primary rami
Back muscles - Ventral primary rami
correctOption: Epaxial muscles - Dorsal primary rami
optionExplanations: Epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back, are consistently innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves. This relationship is established early in development as the myotome divides into its respective components. This innervation pattern remains a reliable way to identify the developmental origin of these muscles.

Hypaxial muscles, including those of the body wall and limbs, are innervated by the ventral primary rami, not the dorsal rami. The dorsal rami are strictly reserved for the epaxial musculature. This division reflects the anatomical split of the spinal nerve as it exits the intervertebral foramen to reach different muscle territories.

Epaxial muscles are never innervated by ventral primary rami; they are exclusively supplied by the dorsal rami. The ventral rami are much larger and supply the vast majority of the body's musculature, including the limbs and ventral trunk. This clear-cut anatomical division is a fundamental principle of segmental innervation.

Limb muscles are derived from the hypaxial portion of the myotome and are therefore innervated by the ventral primary rami. The dorsal rami do not extend into the limbs to provide motor innervation. The complex plexuses that supply the limbs (brachial and lumbosacral) are entirely composed of ventral rami.

Back muscles that are classified as epaxial (the intrinsic back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami. While some superficial back muscles (like the latissimus dorsi) are innervated by ventral rami, they are developmentally hypaxial muscles that have migrated. The true 'back muscles' of the epaxial group always follow the dorsal ramus pattern.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

statement: At what point during embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: End of the third month
End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the fifth month
At birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: Cross-striations in skeletal muscle result from the highly organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments into sarcomeres. According to embryological timelines, these striations become visible by the end of the third month of development. This follows the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers and the subsequent synthesis of myofibrillar proteins.

The end of the first month is far too early for cross-striations, as the somites are still in the process of segmenting and the myoblasts have just begun to differentiate. At this stage, the muscle precursors are still largely mesenchymal or just beginning to form the dermomyotome. Striated organization requires significantly more time for protein synthesis and assembly.

By the end of the second month, myoblasts are actively fusing to form primary myotubes, but the internal organization of myofibrils is not yet sufficient to produce visible cross-striations. The third month marks a significant milestone in the maturation of the contractile apparatus. Earlier stages show multinucleation but lack the characteristic banding pattern.

While muscle development continues through the fifth month and beyond, the initial appearance of cross-striations occurs earlier, specifically at the end of the third month. Waiting until the fifth month would overlook the significant maturation that occurs during the late first trimester. By the fifth month, fetal movements (quickening) are often already detectable.
