It migrates to the lateral plate mesoderm is incorrect because the sclerotome cells stay relatively close to the midline to form the axial skeleton. It is the VLL cells of the myotome that migrate to the lateral plate.
It fuses with the neural tube is incorrect because while the sclerotome surrounds the neural tube to form the vertebral arch, it does not fuse with the neural tissue itself.
It differentiates into cardiac muscle is incorrect because cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not from the somitic sclerotome.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A). The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs.

coreConcept: Origin of coronary artery smooth muscle
statement: Which cell populations are responsible for the formation of smooth muscle in the coronary arteries?
options: Visceral mesoderm and ectoderm
Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
Paraxial mesoderm and lateral plate mesoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
Endoderm and neural crest cells
correctOption: Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Visceral mesoderm and ectoderm is incorrect because while visceral mesoderm forms the myocardium, it is not the specific source for coronary smooth muscle, and ectoderm does not contribute to these vessels.

Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells are the correct sources. Proepicardial cells contribute to the majority of the coronary smooth muscle, while neural crest cells contribute to the proximal segments of these arteries.

Paraxial mesoderm and lateral plate mesoderm is incorrect because paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle, and while lateral plate mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the aorta, it is not the primary source for the coronary arteries.
Sclerotome and dermatome are incorrect because these are somite derivatives that form bone and skin/muscle, respectively, and have no role in coronary vessel development.
Endoderm and neural crest cells is incorrect because endoderm does not form muscle tissue. While neural crest cells are involved, the other major contributor is the proepicardial cell population.
citation: In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil

coreConcept: General mesodermal origins of the three muscle types
statement: From which primary germ layer does the majority of the muscular system, including skeletal, cardiac, and most smooth muscle, originate?
options: Ectoderm
Endoderm
Mesoderm
Neural crest
Trophoblast
correctOption: Mesoderm
optionExplanations: Ectoderm is incorrect because it only gives rise to a very small number of specific smooth muscles, such as those in the eye and certain glands. The bulk of the muscular system is not ectodermal.
Endoderm is incorrect because it primarily forms the epithelial linings of the internal organs and does not differentiate into muscle tissue.
Mesoderm is the correct answer. It is the primary germ layer that differentiates into paraxial mesoderm (skeletal muscle) and lateral plate mesoderm (cardiac and most smooth muscle).
Neural crest is incorrect because while it contributes to some smooth muscle in the head and large vessels, it is not the primary source for the entire muscular system.
Trophoblast is incorrect because it is an extraembryonic tissue that contributes to the formation of the placenta and does not form any part of the embryo's muscular system.
citation: With the exception of some smooth muscle tissue (see p. 164), the muscular system develops from the mesodermal germ layer and consists of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle. Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head.

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most muscle tissue in the human body is mesodermal in origin, certain specific smooth muscles are derived from the ectodermal germ layer. Which of the following muscles follows this exceptional developmental path?
options: Sphincter pupillae muscle
Rectus abdominis muscle
Myocardium of the left ventricle
Smooth muscle of the ascending aorta
Tracheal smooth muscle
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil are unique because they differentiate from the ectoderm of the optic cup. This is an exception to the general rule that the muscular system develops from the mesodermal germ layer. Other ectodermal muscle derivatives include those found in the mammary and sweat glands.

The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle derived from the abaxial muscle cell precursors of the somites. These cells migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. It is entirely mesodermal and does not have an ectodermal component.

The myocardium is cardiac muscle derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. It follows the standard mesodermal developmental pathway for the circulatory system. It is never derived from the ectodermal germ layer.

Smooth muscle in the dorsal aorta and large arteries originates from a combination of lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. While neural crest cells are ectodermal in origin, the bulk of the vascular smooth muscle is considered mesodermal or neural crest-derived, not surface ectoderm-derived like the pupillary muscles. The context specifically distinguishes these from the ectodermal group.

Tracheal smooth muscle, like the smooth muscle of the gut, is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This mesoderm surrounds the endodermal lung bud as it develops. It is a classic example of splanchnic mesodermal differentiation.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

coreConcept: The Lateral Somitic Frontier and mesodermal domains
statement: The lateral somitic frontier is a critical developmental boundary established during embryogenesis. What is the primary role of this frontier in defining the organization of the mesoderm?
options: It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
It marks the division between the sclerotome and the dermomyotome
It defines the boundary between the paraxial and intermediate mesoderm
It serves as the site for the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers
It separates the endodermal gut tube from the visceral mesoderm
correctOption: It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
optionExplanations: The lateral somitic frontier is the well-defined border between each somite and the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. It separates the primaxial domain, which contains only somite-derived cells, from the abaxial domain, which contains both lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells. This distinction is fundamental to the modern understanding of muscle precursor populations.

The division between the sclerotome and dermomyotome occurs within the somite itself as it differentiates into ventral and dorsal regions. The lateral somitic frontier is an external boundary between the somite and the adjacent lateral plate mesoderm. Therefore, it does not define the internal sclerotome-dermomyotome split.

The boundary between paraxial and intermediate mesoderm is established much earlier during the initial patterning of the mesodermal germ layer. The lateral somitic frontier specifically involves the relationship between the paraxial mesoderm (somites) and the lateral plate mesoderm. Intermediate mesoderm is a separate longitudinal column located between them.

The fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers is a histological differentiation process that occurs within the developing muscle tissue itself. It is not related to the anatomical boundary defined by the lateral somitic frontier. Fusion occurs in both primaxial and abaxial domains regardless of the frontier.

The separation of the endodermal gut tube from the visceral mesoderm is a process related to the formation of the body cavity and the gut wall. The lateral somitic frontier involves the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm and the somites, not the visceral layer or the endoderm. It is located more dorsolaterally in the embryo.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells

coreConcept: Signaling sources for primaxial vs. abaxial precursors
statement: Muscle cell precursors are categorized into primaxial and abaxial populations based on their migration across the lateral somitic frontier. From which structures do the primaxial muscle cell precursors primarily receive their developmental signals for differentiation?
options: Neural tube and notochord
Parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm
Visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm
Surface ectoderm and apical ectodermal ridge
Endodermal lining of the yolk sac
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: Primaxial muscle cell precursors are those that remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. Because of their location, they are positioned to receive inductive signals from the midline structures, specifically the neural tube and the notochord. These signals guide their differentiation into muscles of the back and intercostals.

The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm provides signals to the abaxial muscle cell precursors, not the primaxial ones. Abaxial cells are those that have migrated across the frontier into this specific mesodermal environment. Primaxial cells are sequestered away from these lateral plate signals.

The visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is involved in the development of smooth muscle of the gut and cardiac muscle. It does not provide the primary signals for the differentiation of the skeletal muscle precursors found in the primaxial domain. Primaxial skeletal muscles are strictly somite-derived.

While the surface ectoderm provides some signals for the dermatome and limb development, the primary signaling centers for the primaxial muscle precursors are the neural tube and notochord. The apical ectodermal ridge is specific to limb bud outgrowth and does not govern the general primaxial population. Primaxial cells are defined by their proximity to the axial midline.

The endodermal lining of the yolk sac is not involved in the signaling or differentiation of skeletal muscle precursors. Skeletal muscle is a mesodermal derivative, and its patterning is controlled by adjacent mesodermal and ectodermal/neuroectodermal structures. The yolk sac endoderm is far removed from the somitic domains.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors

coreConcept: Cardiac muscle development and intercalated discs
statement: During the development of cardiac muscle, myoblasts exhibit a specific behavior that distinguishes them from skeletal muscle myoblasts. Which of the following best describes this developmental characteristic?
options: Myoblasts adhere via special attachments but do not fuse into a syncytium
Myoblasts undergo complete fusion to form long multinucleated fibers
Myoblasts differentiate directly from the neural crest cells
Myoblasts lose their myofibrils to become Purkinje fibers
Myoblasts migrate from the paraxial mesoderm to the heart tube
correctOption: Myoblasts adhere via special attachments but do not fuse into a syncytium
optionExplanations: In cardiac muscle development, myoblasts adhere to one another through specialized junctions that eventually become intercalated discs. Unlike skeletal muscle, these myoblasts do not fuse together; instead, they remain as individual cells connected by these discs. This allows for the functional syncytium required for coordinated heart contractions without anatomical fusion.

Complete fusion to form long multinucleated fibers is the hallmark of skeletal muscle development, not cardiac muscle. In skeletal muscle, myoblasts merge their cell membranes to create a single large cell with many nuclei. Cardiac cells remain mononucleated or binucleated and are separated by intercalated discs.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not from neural crest cells. While neural crest cells contribute to other structures in the heart, such as the septa and some smooth muscle in the outflow tract, they do not form the primary myocardium. The myocardium is purely mesodermal.

Purkinje fibers are indeed specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells, but they do not 'lose' their myofibrils; rather, they have irregularly distributed myofibrils. Furthermore, this is a specialized differentiation of a small subset of cells, not the general fate of all cardiac myoblasts. Most cardiac myoblasts become standard contractile cardiomyocytes.

Cardiac muscle originates from the visceral mesoderm, which is part of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscles of the axis and limbs. The heart develops in the splanchnic mesodermal layer ventral to the gut.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

statement: At what point during embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: By the end of the third month
Immediately after the fusion of myoblasts
During the process of somitomere epithelization
At the end of the first month
Only after the muscle receives its final innervation
correctOption: By the end of the third month
optionExplanations: According to the developmental timeline, cross-striations in skeletal muscle appear by the end of the third month of gestation. This follows the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers and the subsequent synthesis of myofibrils within the cytoplasm. These striations represent the organized sarcomeric structure of the mature muscle.

Cross-striations do not appear immediately after myoblast fusion. Fusion creates the multinucleated fiber, but the internal organization of myofibrils into the striated pattern takes additional time. The process of myofibril accumulation and alignment occurs gradually after fusion.

Somitomere epithelization is a very early event occurring shortly after segmentation of the paraxial mesoderm. At this stage, the cells are still epithelial and have not yet differentiated into myoblasts or begun the process of muscle fiber formation. Striations appear much later in the fetal period.

The end of the first month is too early for the appearance of cross-striations. At this stage, the somites are just beginning to differentiate into sclerotomes and dermomyotomes. The complex process of myoblast migration, fusion, and protein synthesis required for striations has not yet reached completion.

While innervation is crucial for the continued growth and maintenance of muscle, the initial appearance of cross-striations is a programmed part of the histological differentiation of the muscle fiber itself. Striations appear by the end of the third month as myofibrils organize, independent of the final functional maturation of the neuromuscular junction.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

coreConcept: Resegmentation of sclerotomes and muscle function
statement: What is the functional consequence of the resegmentation of sclerotomes for the developing axial musculature?
options: It allows myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs and move the spine
It triggers the migration of VLL cells across the lateral somitic frontier
It causes the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers
It determines whether a muscle will be innervated by dorsal or ventral rami
It results in the formation of the dermomyotome from the upper somite
correctOption: It allows myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs and move the spine
optionExplanations: Resegmentation is the process where the caudal half of one sclerotome fuses with the cranial half of the next. This shift ensures that the resulting vertebrae are offset from the original myotomes, allowing the muscles (myotomes) to span the gap between vertebrae (the intervertebral discs). This anatomical arrangement is what enables the muscles to exert force and move the spinal column.

The migration of VLL (ventrolateral lip) cells across the lateral somitic frontier is a separate process driven by signaling from the lateral plate mesoderm. It is not a consequence of sclerotome resegmentation. Resegmentation specifically concerns the skeletal elements of the vertebrae and their relationship to the myotomes.

The fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers is a cellular differentiation process that occurs within the myotome. It is independent of the macroscopic rearrangement of the sclerotomes. Myoblast fusion occurs to create the contractile unit, while resegmentation provides the mechanical leverage.

The innervation of muscles by dorsal or ventral rami is determined by their position relative to the axis (epaxial vs. hypaxial) and their embryological origin (primaxial vs. abaxial). Resegmentation does not change the segmental innervation of the myotome, which always remains linked to its original spinal nerve segment.

The formation of the dermomyotome occurs in the upper region of the somite before resegmentation takes place. Resegmentation is a later event involving the ventral mesenchymal sclerotome. The dermomyotome provides the cells that will eventually form the muscles that bridge the vertebrae created by resegmentation.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells.

coreConcept: Derivatives of the abaxial muscle cell precursors
statement: Which of the following muscle groups is derived from the abaxial muscle cell precursors that have migrated into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Abdominal wall muscles (e.g., rectus abdominis)
Deep muscles of the back (e.g., erector spinae)
Intercostal muscles of the thorax
Muscles of the shoulder girdle (e.g., rhomboids)
Prevertebral muscles of the neck
correctOption: Abdominal wall muscles (e.g., rectus abdominis)
optionExplanations: The abdominal wall muscles, including the rectus abdominis, internal and external obliques, and transversus abdominis, are classic examples of abaxial derivatives. These muscles form from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm. This migration defines the abaxial domain.

The deep muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are primaxial muscles. They develop from cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves.

Intercostal muscles are considered primaxial derivatives because they remain within the somite-derived domain of the thoracic wall. Although they are located in the body wall, they do not involve the migration of cells into the lateral plate mesoderm in the same way as the abdominal or limb muscles. They receive signals from the neural tube and notochord.

Muscles of the shoulder girdle, such as the rhomboids and levator scapulae, are primaxial derivatives. Even though they are associated with the limb skeleton, their muscle precursors do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They are formed by somite cells that stay within the primaxial environment.

Prevertebral muscles are located close to the vertebral column and are derived from the primaxial muscle cell population. They do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. Like other primaxial muscles, they are patterned by signals from the axial midline structures.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles.

coreConcept: Innervation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles
statement: A developmental anomaly results in the failure of the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves to form correctly. Which of the following muscle groups would most likely lack proper innervation as a result?
options: Hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs
Epaxial muscles of the deep back
Erector spinae muscle group
Intrinsic muscles of the vertebral column
Splenius capitis muscle
correctOption: Hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs
optionExplanations: Hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the body wall and the limbs, are specifically innervated by the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves. If these rami fail to develop, the motor supply to these muscles would be absent. This relationship holds true regardless of whether the muscle is primaxial or abaxial in origin.

Epaxial muscles are the deep muscles of the back located 'above the axis.' They are innervated by the dorsal primary rami, not the ventral primary rami. Therefore, a defect in the ventral rami would not directly affect the innervation of the epaxial muscle group.

The erector spinae is a major component of the epaxial muscle group. As such, it receives its motor innervation from the dorsal primary rami of the spinal nerves. It would remain correctly innervated even if the ventral primary rami were absent.

The intrinsic muscles of the vertebral column are epaxial muscles. They develop from the primaxial domain and are located dorsally, receiving their nerve supply from the dorsal primary rami. They are not dependent on the ventral primary rami for innervation.

The splenius capitis is a muscle of the back and neck that belongs to the epaxial category. Like other back muscles, it is supplied by the dorsal primary rami. It would not be impacted by a failure in the development of the ventral primary rami.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment.

coreConcept: Origin of smooth muscle in large arteries
statement: The smooth muscle found in the wall of the dorsal aorta has a complex embryological origin. Which two cell populations contribute to its formation?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Paraxial mesoderm and ectoderm
Visceral mesoderm and endoderm
Proepicardial cells and somites
Sclerotome and dermatome
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries is derived from two primary sources: the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. Neural crest cells are particularly important for the smooth muscle in the proximal segments of the great vessels. This dual origin reflects the complex patterning of the vascular system.

Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle, not the smooth muscle of the large arteries. Ectoderm gives rise to very specific smooth muscles like the pupillary muscles, but it is not a direct contributor to the aortic wall. The aorta is primarily a mesodermal and neural crest derivative.

While visceral mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut and the heart, the dorsal aorta specifically utilizes the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest. Endoderm provides the lining for the gut and respiratory tubes but does not contribute muscle cells to the vascular walls. The aorta develops in the dorsal mesentery area.

Proepicardial cells are specifically involved in the formation of the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, not the dorsal aorta. Somites (paraxial mesoderm) form skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton, not the smooth muscle of the major systemic arteries. The origins of coronary and systemic vascular smooth muscle are distinct.

The sclerotome and dermatome are subdivisions of the somite that form bone/cartilage and dermis/muscle, respectively. They do not contribute to the smooth muscle of the large arteries. Vascular smooth muscle development is independent of the somitic differentiation into sclerotome and dermatome.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer.

statement: The dermomyotome is a transient embryonic structure that gives rise to both dermis and muscle. How is the myotome portion of this structure initially formed?
options: Cells from the VLL and DML lips migrate and proliferate ventral to the dermatome
Cells from the sclerotome migrate dorsally to merge with the surface ectoderm
Myoblasts from the lateral plate mesoderm invade the somite cavity
The entire somite undergoes epithelization to form a single muscle mass
Neural crest cells migrate into the somite and differentiate into myoblasts
correctOption: Cells from the VLL and DML lips migrate and proliferate ventral to the dermatome
optionExplanations: The myotome is formed by progenitor muscle cells that originate from two specific regions of the somite: the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips. These cells migrate and proliferate to form a layer of muscle precursors located just ventral to the dermatome. Together, these layers are referred to as the dermomyotome.

The sclerotome is the ventral portion of the somite that becomes mesenchymal and forms bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. It does not migrate dorsally to form the myotome. The myotome and dermatome are derived from the upper (dorsal) region of the somite.

Myoblasts do not invade the somite from the lateral plate mesoderm to form the dermomyotome. Instead, some cells from the somite (specifically the VLL) migrate *out* of the somite and *into* the lateral plate mesoderm to form abaxial muscles. The dermomyotome itself is a somite-derived structure.

While the somite does undergo epithelization initially, it quickly differentiates into distinct regions (sclerotome and dermomyotome). It does not remain a single epithelial ball or form a single muscle mass. The differentiation into VLL and DML lips is a key step in creating the organized myotome.

Neural crest cells contribute to many structures, including sensory ganglia and some smooth muscle, but they are not the source of the skeletal muscle cells in the dermomyotome. The skeletal muscle of the myotome is derived strictly from the paraxial mesoderm of the somite. Neural crest cells do not form the myotome.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome (Fig. 11.1B,C; Fig. 11.2).

coreConcept: Development of the cardiac conducting system
statement: What are Purkinje fibers, and what is their embryological origin within the heart?
options: Specialized cardiac muscle cells derived from visceral mesoderm
Modified neural crest cells that migrate into the myocardium
Endothelial cells that have undergone mesenchymal transition
Nerve fibers originating from the vagus nerve
Fibrous connective tissue derived from the epicardium
correctOption: Specialized cardiac muscle cells derived from visceral mesoderm
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are specialized cardiac muscle cells that form the heart's conducting system. They are derived from the same visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm as the rest of the myocardium. They are characterized histologically by having fewer and more irregularly distributed myofibrils compared to standard contractile cardiomyocytes.

Purkinje fibers are not derived from neural crest cells. While the autonomic nervous system (which includes neural crest derivatives) innervates the heart, the actual conducting system (SA node, AV node, Purkinje fibers) is composed of modified cardiac muscle cells. They are myogenic, not neurogenic, in origin.

Endothelial cells of the heart tube form the endocardium and contribute to the heart valves via epithelial-mesenchymal transition, but they do not form the Purkinje fibers. Purkinje fibers are part of the muscular layer (myocardium) and thus share a mesodermal origin with cardiomyocytes. They are not endothelial derivatives.

Purkinje fibers are not nerve fibers; they are specialized muscle cells. Although they function to conduct electrical impulses like nerves, their structure and embryological origin are muscular. The vagus nerve provides parasympathetic innervation to the heart but does not form the conducting system itself.

Purkinje fibers are not made of fibrous connective tissue. They are living, excitable muscle cells. Connective tissue in the heart, such as the fibrous skeleton, provides structural support but does not participate in the rapid conduction of electrical impulses like the Purkinje system does.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments.

statement: Immediately following the segmentation of paraxial mesoderm, what morphological transformation do the somitomeres undergo to become somites?
options: Epithelization into a ball of cells with a central cavity
Mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition to form the neural tube
Fusion into a multinucleated syncytium
Delamination into parietal and visceral layers
Migration into the lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: Epithelization into a ball of cells with a central cavity
optionExplanations: Immediately after segmentation, somitomeres undergo epithelization. This process transforms the loose mesenchymal cells into an organized 'ball' of epithelial cells. This epithelial somite contains a small central cavity before it further differentiates into the sclerotome and dermomyotome.

While the process is a mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition, it forms the somite, not the neural tube. The neural tube is formed from the ectodermal neural plate through the process of neurulation. Somites are paraxial mesoderm structures located adjacent to the neural tube.

Fusion into a multinucleated syncytium is a much later step in muscle development where myoblasts merge to form skeletal muscle fibers. It is not part of the initial formation of the somite from somitomeres. The early somite is composed of individual epithelial cells.

Delamination into parietal and visceral layers is a characteristic of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the somites. Somites remain as discrete blocks of paraxial mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm splits to form the intraembryonic coelom, which is a different developmental event.

Migration into the lateral plate mesoderm only occurs later for a specific subset of cells (the abaxial precursors) from the VLL lip of the dermomyotome. The initial formation of the somite via epithelization occurs in situ within the paraxial mesoderm column. It is a prerequisite for subsequent migration.
citation: Musculature of the aXial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites, which initially form as somitomeres and extend from the occipital region to the tail bud. Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center.

coreConcept: Origin of coronary artery smooth muscle
statement: Which embryonic cell populations are responsible for the formation of smooth muscle in the coronary arteries?
options: Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
Visceral mesoderm and endoderm
Paraxial mesoderm and somitomeres
Sclerotome and dermatome
Ectoderm and lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the coronary arteries has a unique dual origin. It is derived from proepicardial cells, which migrate over the heart to form the epicardium and its derivatives, and neural crest cells, which contribute specifically to the proximal segments of these arteries. This differs from the smooth muscle of the gut or the dorsal aorta.

Visceral mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut and the myocardium, but the coronary artery smooth muscle specifically requires proepicardial and neural crest contributions. Endoderm provides the lining for internal organs but does not differentiate into vascular smooth muscle. The coronary vessels develop within the epicardial layer.

Paraxial mesoderm and somitomeres are the sources of skeletal muscle, not the smooth muscle of the coronary circulation. Coronary vessels are part of the heart's own blood supply and develop from specialized cardiac progenitor populations. Somites contribute to the body wall and axial structures.

The sclerotome and dermatome are somite derivatives that form bone and dermis/skeletal muscle. They have no role in the development of the coronary arteries. The coronary vasculature is derived from cells associated with the heart tube and the overlying epicardium.

While neural crest cells are ectodermal in origin, the term 'ectoderm' usually refers to the surface ectoderm, which forms muscles like the pupillary sphincter. The coronary arteries do not receive contributions from the surface ectoderm. Lateral plate mesoderm is a broad category, but the specific source for coronary smooth muscle is the proepicardial organ.
citation: In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil.

statement: Despite the migration of muscle precursors into different domains (primaxial or abaxial), what fundamental rule governs their innervation?
options: They are innervated by spinal nerves from their segment of origin
They are all innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves
They lose their original segmental innervation and are re-innervated by the nearest nerve
They are innervated by cranial nerves if they migrate into the thoracic region
They receive innervation only from the autonomic nervous system
correctOption: They are innervated by spinal nerves from their segment of origin
optionExplanations: A key principle of muscle development is that each myotome retains the innervation from the spinal nerve of its original segment. Even when cells migrate far from their point of origin (such as abaxial cells moving into the limbs), they 'carry' their nerve supply with them. This explains the complex paths of nerves like the phrenic nerve.

Only epaxial muscles (back muscles) are innervated by the dorsal primary rami. Hypaxial muscles (body wall and limbs) are innervated by the ventral primary rami. Therefore, it is incorrect to say all muscles are innervated by the dorsal rami; the rami division depends on the muscle's final position.

Muscle cells do not lose their original segmental innervation. The relationship between a specific somite segment and its corresponding spinal nerve is established early and maintained throughout development. This segmental consistency is a vital diagnostic tool in clinical neurology (dermatomes and myotomes).

Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the pharyngeal arches in the head and neck. Muscles derived from the somites in the trunk and limbs are always innervated by spinal nerves, regardless of where they migrate. Migration into the thoracic region does not change a spinal-derived muscle into a cranial-derived one.

Skeletal muscles, which are the derivatives of the myotomes, are under voluntary control and are innervated by the somatic nervous system (motor neurons in the ventral horn). The autonomic nervous system innervates smooth muscle and cardiac muscle, not the skeletal muscles derived from the somitic myotomes.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles).

statement: Which part of the somite undergoes a transition back to a mesenchymal state to eventually form the vertebrae and ribs?
options: The ventral region (sclerotome)
The dorsomedial lip (DML)
The ventrolateral lip (VLL)
The upper region (dermatome)
The lateral somitic frontier
correctOption: The ventral region (sclerotome)
optionExplanations: The ventral region of the somite is the part that loses its epithelial organization and becomes mesenchymal again to form the sclerotome. These mesenchymal cells then migrate around the notochord and neural tube to differentiate into the bone and cartilage of the vertebrae and ribs. This is the first major differentiation step of the somite.

The dorsomedial lip (DML) is part of the upper region of the somite and contributes to the formation of the myotome. It does not form the sclerotome or the axial skeleton. Cells from the DML stay within the primaxial domain to form back muscles.

The ventrolateral lip (VLL) is also part of the upper region of the somite. While it provides muscle precursors for both primaxial and abaxial domains, it does not form the bone-forming sclerotome. Its primary fate is the production of skeletal muscle cells.

The dermatome is the part of the upper somite that forms the dermis of the back. It remains distinct from the ventral sclerotome. While it is closely associated with the myotome in the dermomyotome, it does not contribute to the formation of the vertebrae or ribs.

The lateral somitic frontier is a boundary between the somite and the lateral plate mesoderm, not a region of the somite itself. It is a geographical marker used to define the primaxial and abaxial domains. It does not undergo mesenchymal transition to form skeletal elements.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively.

statement: Which embryonic tissue layer is the primary source for the development of skeletal muscles throughout the body, including those in the head and the trunk?
options: Visceral mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Surface ectoderm
correctOption: Paraxial mesoderm
optionExplanations: Visceral mesoderm, also known as splanchnic mesoderm, is primarily responsible for the development of cardiac muscle and the smooth muscle surrounding the gut. It does not contribute to the formation of the skeletal muscles of the trunk or limbs. This layer is distinct from the paraxial mesoderm which forms the somites.

Paraxial mesoderm is the correct answer as it organizes into somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head to form all skeletal muscles. These segments eventually differentiate into the dermomyotome, which provides the progenitor cells for skeletal muscle fibers. This process ensures the segmental arrangement of the axial musculature.

Lateral plate mesoderm, specifically the parietal layer, provides the framework for the limbs and body wall but does not directly form the skeletal muscle cells themselves. Instead, muscle precursors from the somites must migrate into this layer to form specific muscle groups. It is the source of smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries.

Neural crest cells contribute to various structures in the head and neck, including some smooth muscle in large arteries, but they are not the primary source of skeletal muscle. Skeletal muscle in the head is derived from somitomeres, which are part of the paraxial mesoderm. Neural crest cells are more involved in forming connective tissues and the peripheral nervous system.

Surface ectoderm gives rise to the epidermis and certain specialized muscles like the pupillary sphincter and dilator muscles. It is not the source of the vast majority of skeletal muscles in the body. Most skeletal muscle is mesodermal in origin, specifically from the paraxial division.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

statement: During the early development of somites, what morphological transformation occurs immediately following segmentation before the formation of the sclerotome?
options: Direct migration into the lateral plate mesoderm
Fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers
Epithelization into a ball of cells with a central cavity
Immediate differentiation into the dermatome
Invagination to form the neural tube
correctOption: Epithelization into a ball of cells with a central cavity
optionExplanations: Direct migration into the lateral plate mesoderm only occurs later for specific muscle precursors from the ventrolateral lip. It is not the immediate step following segmentation of the somitomeres. The initial somite must first undergo structural reorganization before any migration begins.

The fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers is a much later stage of muscle differentiation that occurs after the dermomyotome has formed. This process characterizes the maturation of skeletal muscle tissue rather than the early organization of the somite. It occurs during the third month of development.

Epithelization is the correct answer; immediately after segmentation, the somitomeres organize into a spherical 'ball' of epithelial cells surrounding a small central cavity. This epithelial structure is temporary, as the ventral portion will soon revert to a mesenchymal state to form the sclerotome. This transition is critical for the subsequent patterning of the axial skeleton and muscles.

Immediate differentiation into the dermatome does not occur; the dermatome forms later from the upper region of the somite after the ventral portion has become the sclerotome. The somite must first pass through the epithelial ball stage. The dermatome eventually contributes to the dermis of the skin.

Invagination to form the neural tube is a process of the neuroectoderm (neurulation) and is not a process undergone by the paraxial mesoderm or somitomeres. While the somites form adjacent to the neural tube, their internal reorganization is distinct from the formation of the central nervous system. The neural tube provides signals for somite differentiation but is not formed by them.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A). The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs.

coreConcept: Dermomyotome formation from VLL and DML
statement: Which two specific regions of the somite contribute cells that migrate and proliferate to form the dermomyotome?
options: The sclerotome and the dermatome
The ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips
The neural tube and the notochord
The parietal and visceral layers of lateral plate mesoderm
The occipital and sacral somitomeres
correctOption: The ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips
optionExplanations: The sclerotome is the ventral part of the somite that forms bone, and the dermatome is the dorsal part that forms dermis; they do not combine to form the dermomyotome. Instead, the dermomyotome is a distinct layer formed by muscle precursors. These precursors originate from specific edges of the somite structure.

The ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips are the correct answer; cells from these two edges migrate beneath the dermatome to form the myotome portion of the dermomyotome. The VLL cells are particularly mobile, with some even leaving the somite entirely. Together, these two populations provide the full complement of skeletal muscle progenitors for the trunk.

The neural tube and the notochord are signaling centers that influence the differentiation of the somite, but they do not contribute physical cells to the dermomyotome. They secrete factors that tell the somite cells whether to become muscle or bone. The physical muscle cells are entirely mesodermal in origin.

The parietal and visceral layers of lateral plate mesoderm are separate from the paraxial mesoderm that forms the somites. While some VLL cells migrate into the parietal layer, the lateral plate mesoderm itself does not form the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is strictly a product of the somite's upper region.

Occipital and sacral somitomeres refer to the longitudinal range of somite formation rather than the internal regions of a single somite. While all somites follow a similar pattern, the question asks for the specific internal lips or edges. Somitomeres in the head do not even form organized somites like those in the trunk.
citation: Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively (Fig. 11.1B). Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome

statement: A researcher is studying the migration of muscle precursor cells across the lateral somitic frontier. Which of the following muscles would they find originating from cells that have successfully entered the abaxial domain?
options: Intercostal muscles
Muscles of the back (erector spinae)
Shoulder girdle muscles
Rectus abdominis
Rhomboid muscles
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles are considered part of the primaxial domain because they develop from cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm. They do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. These muscles receive their developmental signals primarily from the neural tube and notochord.

Muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are classic examples of primaxial muscles that stay near the neural tube. They are derived from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and the remaining ventrolateral lip (VLL) cells that do not migrate far. They are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves.

Shoulder girdle muscles are derived from the primaxial domain, meaning their precursors do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Although they are located near the limbs, their embryological origin is distinct from the abaxial limb muscles. They remain under the signaling influence of the paraxial environment.

Rectus abdominis is the correct answer; it is an abaxial muscle formed by VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This group also includes the obliques, transversus abdominis, and infrahyoid muscles. These muscles are characterized by receiving differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm.

Rhomboid muscles are part of the primaxial domain and do not involve migration into the lateral plate mesoderm. Like other shoulder girdle and back muscles, they develop from precursors that remain within the somite-derived territory. They are distinct from the abaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles

statement: What defines the 'abaxial domain' in the context of the lateral somitic frontier?
options: The region containing only cells derived from the neural crest
The area around the neural tube containing only paraxial mesoderm
The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm combined with migrated somite cells
The visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm surrounding the gut
The space between the dermatome and the sclerotome
correctOption: The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm combined with migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The abaxial domain is not defined by neural crest cells, although neural crest cells do contribute to other structures like the peripheral nervous system. The domain is specifically a mesodermal classification based on the lateral somitic frontier. It involves the interaction between somite-derived muscle cells and lateral plate mesoderm.

The area around the neural tube containing only paraxial mesoderm defines the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain. The primaxial domain is characterized by a lack of lateral plate mesoderm influence. Cells in this region receive signals from the notochord and neural tube.

The abaxial domain is the correct answer; it consists of the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm along with the somite cells (from the VLL) that have crossed the lateral somitic frontier. This domain is where muscles like the rectus abdominis and limb muscles develop. It represents a unique environment where somite cells respond to lateral plate signals.

The visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm surrounding the gut is involved in smooth muscle development, but it is not part of the abaxial domain definition. The abaxial domain specifically involves the parietal layer and its interaction with migrating skeletal muscle precursors. Visceral mesoderm is located more medially and ventrally around the endoderm.

The space between the dermatome and the sclerotome is where the myotome initially forms within the somite, but this does not define the abaxial domain. The abaxial domain requires the crossing of the lateral somitic frontier. The internal arrangement of the somite occurs before the establishment of these broader domains.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells

coreConcept: Signaling for Primaxial vs. Abaxial Differentiation
statement: How do the developmental signaling sources differ between primaxial and abaxial muscle cell precursors?
options: Primaxial cells receive signals from the ectoderm, while abaxial cells receive signals from the endoderm
Primaxial cells receive signals from the neural tube and notochord, while abaxial cells receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm
Primaxial cells are self-signaling, while abaxial cells require signals from the neural crest
Primaxial cells receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm, while abaxial cells receive signals from the notochord
Both populations receive identical signals from the yolk sac
correctOption: Primaxial cells receive signals from the neural tube and notochord, while abaxial cells receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: Ectoderm and endoderm provide various signals in the embryo, but they are not the primary distinguishing signaling sources for the primaxial and abaxial muscle domains. The primaxial/abaxial distinction is specifically tied to the proximity to the axial structures versus the lateral plate. The neural tube and notochord are the key axial signaling centers.

Primaxial cells receive signals from the neural tube and notochord, while abaxial cells receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm is the correct answer. This difference in signaling environment is what defines the two domains and directs the specific differentiation of the muscle groups. Primaxial muscles include the back muscles, while abaxial muscles include the abdominal wall.

Muscle cells are not self-signaling in this context; they require extrinsic inductive signals to differentiate into specific muscle types. Neural crest cells do not provide the primary differentiation signals for these skeletal muscle domains. The lateral somitic frontier acts as the boundary between these two distinct signaling environments.

This option reverses the correct signaling sources; primaxial cells are the ones near the notochord, not the lateral plate. Abaxial cells are the ones that have migrated into the lateral plate mesoderm. Therefore, they are the ones influenced by lateral plate signals rather than axial ones.

The yolk sac is an extraembryonic structure and does not provide the localized patterning signals required for somite differentiation into primaxial and abaxial domains. Differentiation is driven by the immediate neighbors of the mesoderm, such as the neural tube and lateral plate. The signals are highly localized and segment-specific.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord

statement: What is the fundamental rule governing the innervation of developing myotomes, regardless of whether they belong to the primaxial or abaxial domain?
options: They are innervated by cranial nerves from the brainstem
They are innervated by spinal nerves from the same embryonic segment
They are innervated by nerves from the adjacent superior segment
They are innervated only after they reach their final anatomical position
They lose their original innervation if they migrate across the lateral somitic frontier
correctOption: They are innervated by spinal nerves from the same embryonic segment
optionExplanations: Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres, but the question refers to myotomes in general, which are primarily associated with spinal nerves. The segmental nature of the trunk is defined by the spinal cord segments. Cranial nerves follow a different developmental logic related to pharyngeal arches.

Innervation by spinal nerves from the same embryonic segment is the correct answer; as muscle cells migrate, they carry their nerve supply with them. This ensures that even if a muscle moves far from its origin, its segmental innervation remains a clue to its embryological source. This is a core principle of vertebrate anatomy.

Innervation does not typically come from the adjacent superior segment; it is strictly tied to the segment of origin. While some overlap can occur in adult plexuses, the developmental rule is segmental matching. This matching occurs early when the myotome and spinal cord are adjacent.

Innervation begins early in development, often while the muscle precursors are still migrating or just after they have reached their domain. It is not delayed until the final adult anatomical position is reached. The relationship between the nerve and the muscle is established very early.

Migration across the lateral somitic frontier does not cause a loss of original innervation. Abaxial muscles, which cross this frontier, still maintain the innervation from the spinal nerve of their segment of origin. This is why limb muscles are innervated by ventral rami of specific spinal segments.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles), which was based on a functional de fi nition of innervation

statement: In the traditional functional classification of muscles, which nerves supply the hypaxial muscles of the body wall and limbs?
options: Dorsal primary rami
Ventral primary rami
Sympathetic chain ganglia
Parasympathetic vagus nerve
White rami communicantes
correctOption: Ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the true muscles of the back. They do not supply the hypaxial muscles of the body wall or the limbs. The division of the spinal nerve into dorsal and ventral rami reflects this functional split.

Ventral primary rami is the correct answer; they supply the hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the limbs and the ventrolateral body wall. This classification remains valid even with the newer primaxial/abaxial domain descriptions. The ventral rami are typically larger as they supply a greater mass of muscle.

Sympathetic chain ganglia are part of the autonomic nervous system and provide motor innervation to smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands. They do not provide the voluntary motor innervation to skeletal hypaxial muscles. Skeletal muscle is always supplied by somatic motor fibers.

The vagus nerve is a cranial nerve (CN X) that provides parasympathetic innervation to thoracic and abdominal viscera. It does not innervate the skeletal muscles of the body wall or limbs. Those muscles are strictly under the control of spinal somatic nerves.

White rami communicantes carry preganglionic sympathetic fibers from the spinal nerve to the sympathetic trunk. They are not involved in the direct motor innervation of skeletal muscles. They are part of the visceral efferent pathway, not the somatic efferent pathway.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

statement: By which point in embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically appear?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
End of the fifth month
At the time of birth
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: The end of the first month is too early for cross-striations; at this stage, the somites are still just beginning to differentiate into dermomyotomes. Myoblasts have not yet fully fused into the multinucleated fibers required for striation. The embryo is still in the early stages of organogenesis.

By the end of the second month, myoblasts are fusing and myofibrils are appearing, but the organized cross-striations are not yet fully visible. The muscle tissue is still maturing and organizing its internal contractile apparatus. This period is characterized by rapid limb and body wall formation.

The end of the third month is the correct answer; this is when the internal organization of myofibrils into sarcomeres becomes sufficiently advanced to be seen as cross-striations under a microscope. This marks a significant milestone in the functional maturation of skeletal muscle. It follows the fusion of myoblasts into long fibers.

The end of the fifth month is much later than the initial appearance of striations. By this time, the fetus is already capable of significant movement (quickening), which requires functional, striated muscle. Striations are well-established long before the second trimester concludes.

Cross-striations appear long before birth, during the first trimester of development. Waiting until birth would mean the fetus had no functional skeletal muscle during gestation. Muscle development is largely completed in terms of basic structure by the end of the embryonic period and early fetal period.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

statement: Which of the following is a key difference between the development of cardiac muscle and skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form multinucleated syncytia
Cardiac muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain as individual cells connected by intercalated discs
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils in its cytoplasm
Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectoderm
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain as individual cells connected by intercalated discs
optionExplanations: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse to form multinucleated syncytia; this is a characteristic of skeletal muscle development. In skeletal muscle, hundreds of myoblasts merge their membranes to form a single long fiber. In contrast, cardiac cells remain distinct units throughout life.

Cardiac muscle is derived from visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm, not paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle. The visceral mesoderm specifically surrounds the heart tube as it forms in the early embryo.

Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain as individual cells connected by intercalated discs is the correct answer. These specialized attachments allow for electrical and mechanical coupling between cells, which is essential for the coordinated contraction of the heart. This structural arrangement is unique to cardiac tissue.

Cardiac muscle definitely contains myofibrils; they are the contractile elements responsible for heart contraction. The development of myofibrils in cardiac muscle is actually very similar to that in skeletal muscle. The main difference lies in the cellular architecture rather than the contractile proteins.

Cardiac muscle is mesodermal in origin, specifically from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. Ectoderm gives rise to the nervous system, skin, and a few specific smooth muscles, but never to cardiac muscle. The heart is one of the first functional organs to develop from the mesoderm.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

coreConcept: Purkinje fibers and the Cardiac Conducting System
statement: What are Purkinje fibers, and what is their developmental significance in the heart?
options: They are specialized nerve cells that migrate from the neural crest to the heart
They are modified muscle cells that form the heart's conducting system
They are connective tissue fibers that support the heart valves
They are the primary contractile cells of the ventricles
They are smooth muscle cells that regulate coronary blood flow
correctOption: They are modified muscle cells that form the heart's conducting system
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are not nerve cells, although they function in electrical conduction. They are embryologically derived from cardiac muscle cells (myocytes) rather than neural tissue. This is a common misconception due to their role in signal propagation.

Purkinje fibers are modified muscle cells that form the heart's conducting system is the correct answer. They are characterized by having fewer and more irregularly distributed myofibrils compared to standard cardiomyocytes. Their primary role is to rapidly transmit electrical impulses to ensure synchronized ventricular contraction.

Purkinje fibers are not connective tissue; they are specialized myogenic cells. Connective tissue in the heart, such as the fibrous skeleton and valve components, has a different histological structure and function. Purkinje fibers are part of the specialized myocardium.

While Purkinje fibers are located in the ventricles, they are not the primary contractile cells. Their myofibril content is reduced because their function has shifted from contraction to conduction. The bulk of the ventricular wall is made of standard contractile cardiomyocytes.

Purkinje fibers are not smooth muscle cells and do not reside in the walls of the coronary arteries. Smooth muscle in the coronary arteries regulates blood flow, but Purkinje fibers are located within the subendocardial layer of the heart chambers. They are part of the heart's internal electrical network.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

statement: Which embryonic sources contribute to the smooth muscle found in the dorsal aorta and other large arteries?
options: Only paraxial mesoderm
Visceral mesoderm and endoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Ectoderm and sclerotome
Proepicardial cells and myotome
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton, but it is not the source of smooth muscle for the large arteries. Smooth muscle has a more diverse set of origins depending on the location of the vessel. The dorsal aorta specifically requires contributions from more lateral and migratory cell populations.

Visceral mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut, but the large arteries like the dorsal aorta receive contributions from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest. Endoderm provides the lining of the gut but does not form muscle tissue. The vascular system has a distinct developmental pathway from the digestive tract.

Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells is the correct answer. The neural crest is particularly important for the smooth muscle in the proximal segments of the great vessels and the aortic arches. This dual origin reflects the complex integration of different cell populations during vasculogenesis.

Ectoderm only forms specific smooth muscles like those in the eye and glands, not the large systemic arteries. The sclerotome forms bone and cartilage, not vascular smooth muscle. This option incorrectly mixes germ layers and somite derivatives.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, but not the dorsal aorta. The myotome is the source of skeletal muscle, not smooth muscle. Each vascular bed has a specific developmental signature based on its location and the surrounding tissues.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm

statement: While most muscles are mesodermal, which of the following is an example of a muscle derived from the ectoderm?
options: Biceps brachii
Dilator pupillae
Gastrocnemius
Diaphragm
Myocardium
correctOption: Dilator pupillae
optionExplanations: Biceps brachii is a skeletal muscle of the limb and is derived from the abaxial domain of the somites (paraxial mesoderm). It is not an ectodermal derivative. Most large voluntary muscles follow this mesodermal pattern.

Dilator pupillae is the correct answer; along with the sphincter pupillae and muscles of the mammary and sweat glands, it is one of the rare exceptions where muscle tissue originates from the ectoderm. These muscles develop in close association with epithelial structures of ectodermal origin. This is a high-yield fact in embryology.

Gastrocnemius is a skeletal muscle of the lower limb, originating from the somitic mesoderm. Like the biceps, it is part of the abaxial muscle group. It is innervated by the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves.

The diaphragm is a complex skeletal muscle derived from several sources, primarily the septum transversum and cervical myotomes (paraxial mesoderm). It is not derived from ectoderm. Its innervation by the phrenic nerve reflects its cervical origin.

The myocardium is the muscular tissue of the heart and is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm. It is never ectodermal. The heart is a purely mesodermal organ in terms of its muscular and endothelial components.
citation: Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm. Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C).

statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation affect the functional relationship between myotomes and the vertebral column?
options: It causes myotomes to fuse into a single continuous muscle sheet
It allows myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, enabling spinal movement
It forces myotomes to disappear and be replaced by tendons
It disconnects the myotomes from their original spinal nerves
It prevents the myotomes from attaching to the ribs
correctOption: It allows myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, enabling spinal movement
optionExplanations: Myotomes do not fuse into a single continuous sheet; they maintain a segmental arrangement, although some may merge to form larger muscle groups. The segmental nature is vital for the fine control of spinal movements. Resegmentation specifically alters the bone-muscle alignment.

Resegmentation allows myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs is the correct answer. By shifting the vertebral boundaries relative to the original somite segments, the muscles (myotomes) now span the gap between two adjacent vertebrae. This anatomical arrangement is what allows muscle contraction to result in the bending or rotation of the spine.

Myotomes do not disappear; they are the precursors to all axial skeletal muscles. Tendons develop to attach these muscles to the bones, but they do not replace the muscle tissue itself. The myotome is the functional contractile unit.

Resegmentation does not disconnect myotomes from their spinal nerves. In fact, the nerves now exit the vertebral column between the new vertebrae to reach the myotomes. The nerve-muscle relationship established in the early embryo is preserved throughout this skeletal reorganization.

Resegmentation is actually what allows for the proper attachment of muscles to the ribs and vertebrae. Without this shift, the muscles would be contained within a single segment and could not exert force across a joint. The process is essential for the development of a functional musculoskeletal system.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head.

statement: What is the embryological origin of the smooth muscle found in the wall of the gastrointestinal tract?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Endoderm
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle, not the smooth muscle of the gut. While it is adjacent to the gut in the early embryo, it differentiates into somites which have a different fate. The gut musculature is part of the autonomic, involuntary system.

The visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm is the correct answer; it surrounds the endodermal heart tube and the endodermal gut tube, giving rise to the cardiac muscle and gut smooth muscle, respectively. This layer is also known as the splanchnic mesoderm. It provides the contractile power for peristalsis.

The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm forms the body wall and the skeletal elements of the limbs, but not the muscle of the gut. It is separated from the gut by the intraembryonic coelom (body cavity). The parietal layer is associated with the ectoderm, while the visceral layer is associated with the endoderm.

Neural crest cells contribute to the enteric nervous system (the nerves within the gut wall) but they do not form the bulk of the smooth muscle tissue itself. The muscle tissue is mesodermal. A failure of neural crest migration leads to conditions like Hirschsprung disease, affecting gut motility.

Endoderm forms the epithelial lining of the gastrointestinal tract and its glands, but it does not form muscle. All muscle tissue in the gut wall is derived from the surrounding mesoderm. The interaction between the endoderm and the visceral mesoderm is crucial for proper gut development.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: Which of the following embryonic structures is the primary source of skeletal muscle in the head region?
options: Lateral plate mesoderm
Somitomeres
Neural crest cells
Visceral mesoderm
Ectodermal placodes
correctOption: Somitomeres
optionExplanations: Lateral plate mesoderm primarily contributes to the smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and large arteries, as well as the skeletal muscles of the limbs and body wall via the abaxial domain. It does not serve as the primary source for the skeletal musculature of the head. Its role is more focused on the appendicular skeleton and vascular structures.

Somitomeres are the segmented clusters of paraxial mesoderm in the head region that do not form distinct somites but differentiate into the cranial musculature. They extend from the occipital region and are the direct precursors to the skeletal muscles of the face and jaw. This paraxial origin is consistent with the development of skeletal muscle throughout the rest of the body.

Neural crest cells contribute significantly to the connective tissues and skeletal elements of the head, as well as some smooth muscle in the proximal segments of the coronary arteries. However, they are not the primary source of skeletal muscle fibers. Skeletal muscle is almost exclusively derived from the mesodermal germ layer.

Visceral or splanchnic mesoderm is the embryonic source for cardiac muscle and the smooth muscle found in the walls of the gut and its derivatives. While it is a mesodermal derivative, it does not form the voluntary skeletal muscles of the head. Its differentiation pathway is distinct from the paraxial mesoderm that forms somitomeres.

Ectodermal placodes are involved in the development of sensory organs such as the ears and nose, but they do not form skeletal muscle. Only very specific smooth muscles, such as the pupillary muscles and those in mammary glands, are derived from ectoderm. Skeletal muscle development is a hallmark of the mesodermal layer.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

statement: Immediately following the segmentation of paraxial mesoderm, what morphological change do the somitomeres undergo to form a 'ball' of cells?
options: Mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition
Apoptotic cavitation
Invagination of the neural plate
Delamination of the dermatome
Epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition
correctOption: Mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition
optionExplanations: Epithelization is a mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition where loosely organized mesenchymal cells organize into a structured epithelial layer. This process is essential for the formation of the somite 'ball' and the creation of a small central cavity.
