It represents the first major morphological reorganization of the paraxial mesoderm after segmentation.

While a small cavity forms in the center of the somite, it is not the result of programmed cell death or apoptotic cavitation. Instead, it is a consequence of the epithelial cells arranging themselves into a spherical structure. Apoptosis is more commonly associated with the removal of vestigial structures or digit separation.

Invagination of the neural plate is the process that forms the neural tube and occurs in the overlying ectoderm, not the paraxial mesoderm. While these processes occur concurrently, they involve different germ layers and distinct morphological mechanisms. The somites form lateral to the neural tube as it closes.

Delamination of the dermatome occurs later in somite development after the initial epithelization has already taken place. The dermatome and myotome form from the upper region of the somite after the ventral portion has already reverted to mesenchyme. It is a secondary differentiation step rather than the immediate result of segmentation.

Epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) is actually the process that occurs later in the ventral region of the somite to form the sclerotome. The initial step after segmentation is the reverse: the formation of an epithelial ball from mesenchyme. EMT allows the sclerotome cells to migrate and surround the notochord to form vertebrae.
citation: Musculature of the aXial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites, which initially form as somitomeres and extend from the occipital region to the tail bud. Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center.

statement: During somite differentiation, which region undergoes a secondary mesenchymal transition to eventually form the vertebrae and ribs?
options: Dorsomedial lip (DML)
Ventrolateral lip (VLL)
Ventral region
Dermatome
Lateral somitic frontier
correctOption: Ventral region
optionExplanations: The dorsomedial lip (DML) is part of the upper region of the somite and is dedicated to forming progenitor muscle cells. These cells remain epithelial for a longer period and eventually contribute to the primaxial muscle domain. They do not form the bone-forming sclerotome.

The ventrolateral lip (VLL) is another muscle-forming area in the upper region of the somite. While some of its cells migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm, they are destined to become skeletal muscle, not bone. The VLL is a key component of the dermomyotome.

The ventral region of the somite loses its epithelial organization and becomes mesenchymal to form the sclerotome. These cells then migrate toward the notochord and neural tube to differentiate into the vertebrae and ribs. This transition is critical for the development of the axial skeleton.

The dermatome is the portion of the upper somite that will eventually form the dermis of the back. It does not undergo the mesenchymal transition to form bone; instead, it remains associated with the muscle-forming lips to form the dermomyotome. Its fate is strictly cutaneous.

The lateral somitic frontier is a boundary between the somite and the lateral plate mesoderm, not a region of the somite itself. It serves as a dividing line for different muscle precursor populations. It does not differentiate into bone-forming cells like the sclerotome does.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively.

statement: Which of the following muscles is derived from the abaxial muscle cell precursors that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier?
options: Intercostal muscles
Shoulder girdle muscles
Rectus abdominis
Erector spinae
Rhomboids
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles are considered primaxial muscles because they develop from cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain. They do not migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Their development is influenced by signals from the neural tube and notochord.

The muscles of the shoulder girdle are derived from the primaxial domain, meaning they originate from somite cells that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. Although they are associated with the limb, their embryological origin is distinct from the distal limb muscles. They receive their developmental cues from the axial environment.

The rectus abdominis is a classic example of an abaxial muscle because its precursors migrate from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the somite into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This migration across the lateral somitic frontier defines the abaxial domain. These muscles are typically innervated by the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves.

The erector spinae muscles are deep back muscles that develop from the primaxial domain. They are derived from the dorsomedial lip and the remaining ventrolateral lip cells that stay within the paraxial mesoderm. As epaxial muscles, they are innervated by the dorsal primary rami.

The rhomboids are part of the shoulder girdle and back musculature that originates from the primaxial domain. They do not involve migration into the lateral plate mesoderm. Like other primaxial muscles, they are formed by cells that remain close to the neural tube and notochord during early development.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles.

statement: Which group of muscles is entirely derived from the primaxial domain, remaining within the paraxial mesoderm throughout development?
options: Infrahyoid muscles
Transversus abdominis
Intercostal muscles
Limb muscles
External oblique
correctOption: Intercostal muscles
optionExplanations: Infrahyoid muscles are formed by cells that migrate from the ventrolateral lip across the lateral somitic frontier. This places them in the abaxial domain, not the primaxial domain. They are part of the ventral body wall musculature that utilizes the lateral plate mesoderm as a substrate.

The transversus abdominis is one of the abdominal wall muscles that originates from abaxial precursors. These cells migrate into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm to differentiate. Therefore, it is not a primaxial muscle.

Intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial domain, as the precursor cells remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They develop in the region around the neural tube and receive signals from axial structures. This distinguishes them from the abdominal wall muscles.

Limb muscles are the quintessential abaxial muscles, formed by cells that migrate extensively into the lateral plate mesoderm of the limb buds. They are far removed from the primaxial domain. Their differentiation is heavily influenced by signals from the lateral plate mesoderm.

The external oblique is a component of the abdominal wall and is derived from the abaxial muscle cell population. Like the rectus abdominis, its precursors must cross the lateral somitic frontier to reach their destination. It is not a derivative of the primaxial domain.
citation: The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles (Table 11.1, p. 162). Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo.

statement: From which structures do the primaxial muscle cell precursors primarily receive their developmental signals for differentiation?
options: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Overlying surface ectoderm
Endodermal lining of the gut
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial muscle precursors, not the primaxial ones. Abaxial cells migrate into this layer and are influenced by its environment. Primaxial cells, by definition, do not enter this domain.

Primaxial muscle precursors remain in the paraxial mesoderm, close to the midline of the embryo. Consequently, they are positioned to receive inductive signals from the neural tube and the notochord. These signals are essential for their commitment to the myogenic lineage within the primaxial domain.

While the surface ectoderm does provide signals for the dermatome and some muscle precursors, the primary signaling environment for the primaxial domain is defined by the axial structures. The neural tube and notochord are the dominant influences for these medial cell populations. The ectoderm is more involved in dorsalizing the somite.

The endodermal lining of the gut is located ventrally and is more closely associated with the visceral mesoderm and smooth muscle development. It does not provide the primary signals for the skeletal muscle precursors in the primaxial domain. Primaxial cells are located dorsally relative to the gut.

The visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm is involved in the development of smooth muscle and cardiac muscle. It is separated from the primaxial skeletal muscle precursors by the intraembryonic coelom and other mesodermal structures. It does not play a role in signaling for the primaxial skeletal muscle domain.
citation: those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord (Fig. 11.3). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves.

statement: What is the primary source of differentiation signals for muscle cells that have crossed the lateral somitic frontier?
options: Notochord
Neural tube
Lateral plate mesoderm
Sclerotome
Dorsomedial lip
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The notochord provides signals primarily to the sclerotome and the primaxial muscle precursors located near the midline. It is too distant from the abaxial cells that have migrated into the lateral plate mesoderm to be their primary signaling source. Its influence is restricted to the axial domain.

The neural tube is a major signaling center for the primaxial domain, but its influence diminishes as cells migrate laterally across the frontier. Abaxial cells move into a new environment where they are exposed to different inductive cues. Therefore, the neural tube is not their primary source of signals.

Once muscle precursors cross the lateral somitic frontier and enter the abaxial domain, they are surrounded by the lateral plate mesoderm. This tissue provides the necessary signals for their further differentiation into specific muscles of the body wall and limbs. This environmental shift is a key feature of the abaxial development model.

The sclerotome consists of bone-forming cells and does not serve as a primary signaling center for the differentiation of abaxial muscle precursors. While it is derived from the same somite, its developmental path is distinct. Sclerotome cells migrate medially, while abaxial muscle cells migrate laterally.

The dorsomedial lip (DML) is a source of muscle progenitor cells, but it is not a signaling center for the abaxial cells. In fact, DML cells stay within the primaxial domain and do not cross the frontier. The abaxial cells originate from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) and then respond to lateral plate signals.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier.

statement: What is the characteristic cellular process that leads to the formation of multinucleated skeletal muscle fibers?
options: Mitotic division without cytokinesis
Fusion of myoblasts
Hypertrophy of a single progenitor
Fragmentation of the sarcoplasm
Syncytial budding from the sclerotome
correctOption: Fusion of myoblasts
optionExplanations: Mitotic division without cytokinesis (forming a coenocyte) is not the mechanism for skeletal muscle formation. While this occurs in some organisms, human skeletal muscle is a true syncytium formed by the merging of individual cell membranes. This allows for the coordinated growth of very long fibers.

The fusion of individual myoblasts is the definitive process that creates the multinucleated state of skeletal muscle fibers. As these precursor cells fuse, they lose their individual cell boundaries and contribute their nuclei to a common cytoplasm. This process is essential for the development of the contractile apparatus.

Hypertrophy refers to the increase in size of an existing cell, but it does not account for the multinucleated nature of skeletal muscle fibers. Skeletal muscle grows through both hyperplasia (increase in cell number via fusion) during development and hypertrophy later in life. The initial fiber formation is strictly a fusion event.

Fragmentation of the sarcoplasm is not a developmental process for muscle formation; it would imply the breakdown of a cell rather than its construction. Muscle development is an additive process where proteins and organelles are organized into myofibrils. Fragmentation is usually associated with pathological states.

Syncytial budding from the sclerotome is incorrect because the sclerotome forms bone and cartilage, not muscle. Muscle precursors come from the myotome portion of the somite. Furthermore, the process is one of fusion of separate cells, not budding from a single source.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

statement: Unlike skeletal muscle, how do cardiac muscle cells maintain their individual cellular integrity during development?
options: They undergo complete fusion into a syncytium
They remain separated by thick collagenous septa
They adhere via specialized intercalated discs
They lack myofibrils entirely
They are derived from ectoderm
correctOption: They adhere via specialized intercalated discs
optionExplanations: Complete fusion into a syncytium is the hallmark of skeletal muscle, but it does not occur in cardiac muscle. Cardiac cells remain as discrete individual units throughout life. This allows for different physiological properties, such as the spread of electrical impulses through junctions rather than a shared cytoplasm.

While connective tissue is present in the heart, it is not the primary mechanism by which cardiac myoblasts maintain their connection and integrity. The cells are directly attached to one another at their ends. Collagenous septa provide structural support but not the functional cell-to-cell coupling.

Cardiac myoblasts adhere to one another through special attachments that eventually mature into intercalated discs. These discs contain gap junctions and desmosomes, allowing for both mechanical adhesion and electrical coupling between individual cells. This ensures the heart can contract as a functional syncytium without being a structural one.

Cardiac muscle cells definitely contain myofibrils, which are the contractile units of the cell. Their development is very similar to that of skeletal muscle, involving the organization of actin and myosin filaments. The presence of myofibrils is what gives cardiac muscle its striated appearance.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not the ectoderm. Ectoderm only contributes to a very small subset of smooth muscles in the body. The mesodermal origin is consistent with the rest of the muscular system.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

coreConcept: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
statement: Which two embryonic sources contribute to the smooth muscle found in the wall of the dorsal aorta?
options: Paraxial mesoderm and ectoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Visceral mesoderm and somitomeres
Proepicardial cells and endoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle, and ectoderm forms only specific smooth muscles like the pupillary muscles. Neither is the primary source for the dorsal aorta's smooth muscle. The aorta requires a different set of precursors suited for vascular development.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries has a dual origin from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. This mixed origin is unique to the vascular system, particularly in the transition from the heart to the great vessels. Neural crest cells are especially important in the aortic arches.

Visceral mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut and the cardiac muscle of the heart, but the large arteries like the dorsal aorta receive contributions from the lateral plate and neural crest. Somitomeres are strictly for head skeletal muscle. This distinction is important for understanding vascular anomalies.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries, not the dorsal aorta. Endoderm provides the epithelial lining of the gut and respiratory tract but does not form muscle tissue. The dorsal aorta's development is independent of the proepicardial organ.

The sclerotome and dermatome are subdivisions of the somite that form bone and dermis/muscle, respectively. They do not contribute to the smooth muscle of the large elastic arteries. Vascular smooth muscle development follows a separate lineage from the segmental somitic structures.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer.

statement: Which of the following muscles is a rare example of a muscle derived from the ectodermal germ layer?
options: Rectus abdominis
Sphincter pupillae
Biceps brachii
Myocardium
Trachealis muscle
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae
optionExplanations: The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle derived from the abaxial domain of the somites (mesoderm). It is not an ectodermal derivative. Most skeletal muscles follow this mesodermal pattern.

The sphincter pupillae, along with the dilator pupillae and muscles of the mammary and sweat glands, are unique because they differentiate from ectoderm. This is an exception to the general rule that the muscular system is mesodermal. These muscles are functionally smooth muscles but have a distinct developmental origin.

The biceps brachii is a skeletal muscle of the limb, originating from the abaxial somitic precursors that migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. It is entirely mesodermal in origin. Limb muscles are never derived from ectoderm.

The myocardium is the muscle layer of the heart and is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm. It is a classic mesodermal derivative. Ectoderm does not contribute to the contractile tissue of the heart.

The trachealis muscle is a smooth muscle found in the wall of the trachea, which is a gut derivative. Therefore, it is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. It follows the standard pattern for smooth muscle in internal organs.
citation: Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm. Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C).

statement: What developmental event allows segmental myotomes to acquire the functional capacity to move the vertebral column?
options: Fusion of adjacent myotomes into a single longitudinal unit
Resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae
Migration of myoblasts into the limb buds
Loss of innervation from the dorsal primary rami
Formation of the lateral somitic frontier
correctOption: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae
optionExplanations: Myotomes do not fuse into a single unit; they remain segmentally arranged. Their functional capacity comes from their relationship to the bones they move, not from losing their segmental nature. The segmental arrangement is preserved in the deep back muscles.

Resegmentation is the process where the caudal half of one sclerotome fuses with the cranial half of the next. This shift causes the myotomes, which were originally aligned with the sclerotomes, to now bridge the space between the new vertebrae (the intervertebral discs). This bridging is what allows a muscle to span a joint and exert force to move the spine.

Migration of myoblasts into the limb buds allows for the movement of the arms and legs, but it does not explain how the axial skeleton is moved. Limb muscles are abaxial and move the appendicular skeleton. Spine movement is a function of the axial (epaxial) muscles.

Loss of innervation would result in paralysis, not functional capacity. Epaxial muscles must retain their innervation from the dorsal primary rami to function. The innervation remains segmental, matching the original myotome level.

The lateral somitic frontier is a boundary that defines muscle domains (primaxial vs. abaxial). While important for muscle identity, it is not the mechanical reason why muscles can move the spine. The mechanical advantage is provided by the resegmentation of the underlying skeletal elements.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). With the exception of some smooth muscle tissue (see p. 164), the muscular system develops from the mesodermal germ layer.

statement: The infrahyoid muscles are categorized as abaxial because their precursor cells do which of the following?
options: Remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain
Migrate across the lateral somitic frontier
Originate from the neural crest
Develop from the visceral mesoderm
Differentiate directly from the sclerotome
correctOption: Migrate across the lateral somitic frontier
optionExplanations: Remaining within the paraxial mesoderm would make the muscles primaxial, such as the intercostals or deep back muscles. The infrahyoid muscles are not in this category. They are part of the ventral body wall musculature.

The infrahyoid muscles are formed by cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This migration into the abaxial domain is what defines them as abaxial muscles. They are part of the same developmental group as the abdominal wall muscles.

Neural crest cells contribute to some smooth muscle and skeletal elements in the head and neck, but they are not the source of the infrahyoid skeletal muscles. These muscles are somitic in origin. The somite provides the myogenic precursors.

Visceral mesoderm is the source of smooth muscle in the gut and cardiac muscle, not the skeletal muscles of the neck. Skeletal muscles are derived from the somites (paraxial mesoderm). The infrahyoid muscles are voluntary skeletal muscles.

The sclerotome is the bone-forming part of the somite and does not produce muscle cells. Muscle cells come from the myotome (DML and VLL). The sclerotome and myotome have distinct cell fates after the somite differentiates.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells

statement: Which components make up the 'abaxial domain' in the developing embryo?
options: Neural tube and paraxial mesoderm
Parietal lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
Visceral lateral plate mesoderm and endoderm
Sclerotome and notochord
Dermatome and overlying ectoderm
correctOption: Parietal lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The neural tube and paraxial mesoderm define the primaxial domain, not the abaxial one. This region is characterized by cells that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. It is the 'original' territory of the somite.

The abaxial domain is defined by the combination of the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm and the somite-derived cells (from the VLL) that have migrated into it. This domain is lateral to the lateral somitic frontier. It is the site of development for limb and body wall muscles.

Visceral lateral plate mesoderm and endoderm are involved in the formation of the gut tube and its associated smooth muscle. They are not part of the abaxial domain, which specifically refers to the somatic body wall and limb components. The abaxial domain is associated with the parietal layer.

The sclerotome and notochord are axial structures involved in the formation of the vertebral column. They are located medially and are part of the primaxial environment. They do not contribute to the abaxial domain.

The dermatome and ectoderm are involved in the formation of the skin (dermis and epidermis). While the dermatome is part of the somite, the abaxial domain specifically refers to the muscle-forming cells and their new environment in the lateral plate mesoderm. The term 'abaxial' is primarily used in the context of muscle development.
citation: The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier. Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals.

statement: A researcher is studying the histological maturation of skeletal muscle in human embryos. At what developmental milestone do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: End of the first month
End of the second month
End of the third month
End of the fourth month
End of the fifth month
correctOption: End of the third month
optionExplanations: The end of the first month is far too early for striations as the somites are only beginning to differentiate into sclerotomes and dermomyotomes. At this stage, the primary focus is on the epithelization of somitomeres and the initial migration of mesenchymal cells.

The end of the second month is characterized by the active fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers and the initial appearance of myofibrils. However, the organized sarcomere arrangement required for visible cross-striations is not yet complete.

By the end of the third month, the fusion of myoblasts into long, multinucleated fibers is well-advanced, and myofibrils have organized sufficiently to display cross-striations. This represents a critical transition from primitive myoblasts to mature, functional skeletal muscle tissue.

The end of the fourth month occurs after the initial appearance of cross-striations, which are already established by the end of the first trimester. While muscle growth and maturation continue, the diagnostic histological feature of striation is already present.

The end of the fifth month is well into the second trimester, long after the initial appearance of skeletal muscle striations. By this time, the fetus is capable of significant spontaneous movement, which relies on the pre-existing striated muscle architecture.
citation: Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs.

coreConcept: Origin of coronary artery smooth muscle
statement: While most vascular smooth muscle originates from lateral plate mesoderm or neural crest cells, the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries has a unique developmental contribution. Which of the following sources contributes to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries?
options: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm only
Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
Ectodermal placodes and paraxial mesoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome derivatives
Endothelial heart tube cells
correctOption: Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: The visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm provides smooth muscle for the gut and its derivatives, but it is not the primary source for the coronary arteries. Coronary artery development involves more specialized cell populations migrating to the heart.

Coronary artery smooth muscle is unique because it originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells, particularly in the proximal segments. This dual origin distinguishes coronary vasculature from the dorsal aorta and other large vessels.

Ectodermal placodes contribute to sensory structures, and paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle, but neither is involved in the formation of coronary artery smooth muscle. These tissues follow entirely different developmental pathways.

Sclerotome and dermatome derivatives form the axial skeleton and dermis, respectively, and do not contribute to the vascular smooth muscle of the heart. Their myogenic potential is restricted to skeletal muscle lineages.

The endothelial heart tube cells give rise to the endocardium and the internal lining of the heart, but they do not differentiate into the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries. The smooth muscle layer is added later from external cell populations.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives

statement: During the differentiation of a somite, the ventral region undergoes a secondary transition from epithelial to mesenchymal tissue. What is the primary fate of the cells resulting from this transition?
options: Formation of the dermis of the back
Development into the muscles of the limbs
Formation of the vertebrae and ribs
Differentiation into the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Development into the autonomic nervous system
correctOption: Formation of the vertebrae and ribs
optionExplanations: The dermis of the back is formed by the dermatome, which is located in the upper region of the somite, not the ventral mesenchymal region. The dermatome remains distinct from the bone-forming sclerotome.

Muscles of the limbs are derived from the ventrolateral (VLL) lip of the dermomyotome, which migrates into the lateral plate mesoderm. These cells are myogenic precursors, not the bone-forming cells of the ventral somite.

The ventral region of the somite becomes mesenchymal to form the sclerotome, which consists of the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. This process is essential for the development of the axial skeleton.

The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm is a separate mesodermal population that exists adjacent to the somites. While some somitic cells migrate into it, the sclerotome itself does not form this layer.

The autonomic nervous system is derived from neural crest cells and the neural tube, not from the mesenchymal sclerotome of the somites. The sclerotome is strictly a mesodermal derivative for skeletal structures.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips

statement: The dermomyotome is a critical intermediate structure in muscle development. How is this structure formed from the initial somite?
options: By the fusion of the sclerotome with the overlying ectoderm
By cells from the VLL and DML lips migrating ventral to the dermatome
By the direct transformation of the entire somitomere into muscle
By the migration of neural crest cells into the dorsal somite
By the proliferation of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: By cells from the VLL and DML lips migrating ventral to the dermatome
optionExplanations: The sclerotome forms the axial skeleton and does not fuse with ectoderm to form the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is a distinct layer formed from the dorsal and lateral parts of the somite.

The dermomyotome forms when cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips of the somite migrate and proliferate beneath the dermatome. This layer provides the progenitor cells for both the primaxial and abaxial muscle domains.
Somitomeres undergo epithelization to form somites, but they do not directly transform into muscle as a single unit. They must first differentiate into sclerotome and dermomyotome layers.

Neural crest cells contribute to many structures, including the peripheral nervous system, but they are not the source of the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is entirely derived from paraxial mesoderm.
The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm receives cells from the VLL region of the dermomyotome, but it does not form the dermomyotome itself. The dermomyotome is a somitic structure.
citation: Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome (Fig. 11.1B,C; Fig. 11.2). Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid

statement: A developmental biologist is tracing the migration of cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) lip of the dermomyotome across the lateral somitic frontier. Which of the following muscles is a direct derivative of these abaxial precursor cells?
options: Intercostal muscles
Muscles of the back
Shoulder girdle muscles
Rectus abdominis
Rhomboid muscles
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles are derived from the primaxial domain, meaning they develop from cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They receive signals from the neural tube and notochord.
Muscles of the back (epaxial muscles) are classic examples of primaxial derivatives. They are formed by cells from the DML and VLL lips that stay within the paraxial mesoderm environment.

Shoulder girdle muscles are classified as primaxial derivatives. Although they are located near the limbs, their embryological origin is from somite cells that do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm.

The rectus abdominis is an abaxial muscle formed by VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This group also includes the obliques and transversus abdominis.
Rhomboid muscles are primaxial derivatives that form part of the shoulder girdle and back musculature. They do not arise from the abaxial cell population that crosses into the lateral plate mesoderm.
citation: Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles (Table 11.1, p. 162). Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite

statement: The differentiation of muscle precursors is heavily dependent on signaling from surrounding tissues. From which structures do the primaxial muscle cell precursors receive their primary developmental signals?
options: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Neural tube and notochord
Overlying surface ectoderm only
Endothelial heart tube
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial muscle precursors, not the primaxial ones. Abaxial cells are those that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

Primaxial muscle precursors remain within the paraxial mesoderm and receive their essential differentiation signals from the neural tube and notochord. This environment is distinct from the signaling milieu of the abaxial domain.

While the surface ectoderm provides some signals for the dermatome and VLL cells, the primary signals for the primaxial population specifically come from the midline structures like the neural tube and notochord.
The endothelial heart tube is involved in the development of cardiac muscle from the surrounding visceral mesoderm. It does not provide signals for the skeletal muscle precursors in the somites.

The visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm is the source of signals and tissue for smooth muscle of the gut and cardiac muscle. It is not involved in the signaling of primaxial skeletal muscle precursors.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross

statement: In contrast to skeletal muscle development, cardiac muscle cells exhibit a unique behavior during differentiation. Which of the following best describes the formation of cardiac muscle fibers?
options: Myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated syncytia
Myoblasts remain as individual cells connected by intercalated discs
Myoblasts differentiate from the neural crest to form the myocardium
Myoblasts undergo apoptosis to create the heart chambers
Myoblasts lose their myofibrils to become Purkinje fibers
correctOption: Myoblasts remain as individual cells connected by intercalated discs
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle myoblasts fuse to form long, multinucleated fibers, but this process does not occur in cardiac muscle. Cardiac muscle maintains individual cellular boundaries throughout development.

Cardiac myoblasts adhere to one another through specialized attachments that eventually mature into intercalated discs. This allows for electrical and mechanical coupling while the cells remain distinct, non-fused units.

Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not from the neural crest. Neural crest cells contribute to the outflow tract and coronary arteries but not the primary myocardium.

Myoblasts do not undergo apoptosis to create chambers; rather, they proliferate and differentiate to form the muscular walls. Chamber formation involves complex folding and septation of the heart tube.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils, but they are a specific subset of the conducting system, not the result of all myoblasts losing their fibrils.
citation: Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form

statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal in origin, certain specific muscles are derived from the ectodermal germ layer. Which of the following is an example of an ectoderm-derived muscle?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
Sphincter pupillae muscle
Smooth muscle of the gut wall
Coronary artery smooth muscle
Rectus abdominis muscle
correctOption: Sphincter pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells, which are not purely ectodermal in the context of general muscle classification.

The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil, along with muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands, are unique because they originate from ectoderm. Most other muscles in the body are mesodermal.
Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and its derivatives is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm. This is the standard origin for most visceral smooth muscle.

Coronary artery smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. While neural crest is ectodermal in origin, the proepicardial cells are mesodermal, and the tissue is not classified as purely ectodermal muscle.
The rectus abdominis is a skeletal muscle derived from the abaxial domain of the somites (paraxial mesoderm). It is not a smooth muscle and is not derived from ectoderm.
citation: Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm. statement: The process of resegmentation in the sclerotomes is vital for the functional anatomy of the spine. What is the direct consequence of this process on the overlying myotomes?
options: Myotomes fuse into a single continuous longitudinal muscle
Myotomes migrate to the limbs and lose their segmental identity
Myotomes bridge the intervertebral discs to allow spinal movement
Myotomes differentiate into the intervertebral discs themselves
Myotomes are obliterated by the developing vertebrae
correctOption: Myotomes bridge the intervertebral discs to allow spinal movement
optionExplanations: Myotomes do not fuse into a single continuous muscle; they maintain a segmental arrangement, although their relationship to the bony segments changes during resegmentation.
While some myotome cells migrate to the limbs, the axial myotomes remain in the trunk. Resegmentation specifically refers to the rearrangement of the axial skeleton and its associated muscles.

Resegmentation of the sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the newly formed intervertebral discs. This anatomical arrangement is what allows the muscles to exert leverage and move the vertebral column.
The intervertebral discs are formed from the sclerotome (specifically the dense cranial part and the notochord), not from the myogenic cells of the myotome.
Myotomes are not obliterated; they are essential for the development of the muscular system. They adapt their position relative to the resegmented vertebrae to become functional muscles.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles)

statement: Despite the modern classification of muscles into primaxial and abaxial domains, the traditional functional division based on innervation remains valid. Which nerves innervate the hypaxial muscles?
options: Dorsal primary rami
Ventral primary rami
Cranial nerves only
Sympathetic chain ganglia
Splanchnic nerves
correctOption: Ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the muscles of the back. This division is based on the position of the muscles relative to the vertebral axis.

Hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the body wall and limbs, are innervated by the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves. This remains a consistent anatomical rule regardless of the embryological domain (primaxial or abaxial).
Cranial nerves innervate muscles of the head and neck derived from somitomeres and branchial arches, but they do not innervate the hypaxial muscles of the trunk and limbs.
Sympathetic chain ganglia provide autonomic innervation to smooth muscles and glands, but they do not provide the motor innervation for the skeletal hypaxial muscles.
Splanchnic nerves carry autonomic fibers to the viscera and do not innervate the skeletal muscles of the body wall or limbs. They are part of the visceral nervous system.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). statement: The paraxial mesoderm organizes into segmented structures that give rise to the majority of the skeletal musculature. What is the longitudinal extent of the somites formed from this layer?
options: From the cervical to the coccygeal regions
From the occipital to the sacral regions
From the cranial to the thoracic regions
Only in the lumbar and sacral regions
From the head to the tail bud exclusively as somitomeres
correctOption: From the occipital to the sacral regions
optionExplanations: While somites do extend into the cervical and coccygeal areas, the standard description of their primary range for skeletal muscle derivation is from the occipital to the sacral regions.
Somites form from the occipital region down to the sacral region, providing the basis for the axial skeleton and musculature. In the head, the paraxial mesoderm forms unsegmented somitomeres instead.
The somites extend far beyond the thoracic region, reaching down to the sacral and coccygeal levels to form the lower trunk and tail structures.
Somites are not restricted to the lower back; they begin in the occipital region and are responsible for the muscles of the neck and the entire trunk.

Somitomeres are the unsegmented precursors in the head, but they undergo segmentation to form somites from the occipital region posteriorly. The entire musculature is not derived from somitomeres alone.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is

statement: The lateral somitic frontier is a significant boundary in embryonic development. What two domains does this frontier separate?
options: The dermatome and the myotome
The sclerotome and the dermomyotome
The primaxial domain and the abaxial domain
The epimere and the hypomere
The endoderm and the ectoderm
correctOption: The primaxial domain and the abaxial domain
optionExplanations: The dermatome and myotome are layers within the dermomyotome itself, both of which are somite-derived. The frontier is a boundary between somitic and lateral plate mesoderm.
The sclerotome and dermomyotome are the two primary divisions of a somite, but the lateral somitic frontier is the border between the somite and the adjacent lateral plate mesoderm.

The lateral somitic frontier separates the primaxial domain (containing only somite-derived cells) from the abaxial domain (containing lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells). This distinction is based on the embryological origin of the cells.
Epimere and hypomere are older terms based on functional innervation (dorsal vs. ventral rami), whereas the frontier defines domains based on cell lineage and signaling environments.
The endoderm and ectoderm are primary germ layers. The lateral somitic frontier is a specialized boundary within the mesodermal layer specifically.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). statement: A medical student is reviewing the embryology of the gastrointestinal tract. What is the developmental origin of the smooth muscle found in the wall of the esophagus, stomach, and intestines?
options: Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm (somites)
Neural crest cells
Surface ectoderm
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm forms the body wall and associated structures, but it does not contribute to the smooth muscle of the gut tube.

The visceral (splanchnic) layer of lateral plate mesoderm surrounds the endodermal gut tube and differentiates into the smooth muscle and connective tissue of the gut wall. This is a fundamental concept in organogenesis.
Paraxial mesoderm gives rise to skeletal muscle, the axial skeleton, and the dermis of the back, but it is not the source of visceral smooth muscle.
Neural crest cells contribute to the enteric nervous system (ganglia) within the gut wall, but the actual smooth muscle tissue is derived from the visceral mesoderm.
Surface ectoderm gives rise to the epidermis and certain specialized muscles like those in the sweat glands, but it does not form the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm. Resegmentation of sclerotomes into

statement: The modern understanding of muscle development emphasizes the origin of precursor cells over their final innervation. How does the primaxial population differ from the abaxial population?
options: Primaxial cells migrate across the lateral somitic frontier, while abaxial cells do not
Primaxial cells receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm
Primaxial cells remain in the paraxial mesoderm and receive signals from the neural tube
Primaxial cells only form limb muscles, while abaxial cells form back muscles
Primaxial cells are derived from ectoderm, while abaxial cells are mesodermal
correctOption: Primaxial cells remain in the paraxial mesoderm and receive signals from the neural tube
optionExplanations: It is the abaxial cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm; primaxial cells are defined by their failure to cross this boundary.
Abaxial cells receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm because they migrate into that environment. Primaxial cells are shielded from these signals by their location.
Primaxial muscle cell precursors are those that remain within the paraxial mesoderm. Because of their proximity to the midline, they receive developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord.
The opposite is true: primaxial cells form muscles of the back and shoulder girdle, while abaxial cells contribute to the muscles of the limbs and ventral body wall.
Both primaxial and abaxial populations are derived from the paraxial mesoderm (somites). The difference lies in their migration patterns and the signaling environments they encounter.
citation: The new description is based on the actual embryological origin of muscle cells from two different populations of muscle cell precursors, the abaxial and primaxial cells, and not their innervation. The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami

statement: The conducting system of the heart includes specialized cells known as Purkinje fibers. What is the embryological origin of these fibers?
options: Neural crest cells migrating into the heart
Modified cardiac muscle cells
Endocardial cushion tissue
The epicardium
The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: Modified cardiac muscle cells
optionExplanations: While neural crest cells contribute to the heart's outflow tract and some vascular smooth muscle, they are not the source of the Purkinje fibers. Purkinje fibers are myogenic in origin.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells that develop irregularly distributed myofibrils. They differentiate from the same visceral mesoderm as the rest of the myocardium to form the heart's conducting system.
Endocardial cushion tissue is involved in the formation of the heart valves and septa, but it does not differentiate into the Purkinje fibers of the conducting system.
The epicardium is the outer layer of the heart and contributes to the coronary vessels, but the Purkinje fibers are located within the myocardial layer and are derived from myoblasts.
The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm forms the serous membranes and body wall, whereas the heart and its conducting system develop from the visceral (splanchnic) layer.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the

statement: During the early development of the axial musculature, what morphological change occurs to the somitomeres immediately following their segmentation?
options: They undergo immediate fusion to form multinucleated myotubes
They undergo epithelization to form a ball of cells with a central cavity
They migrate directly to the lateral plate mesoderm to form limb buds
They differentiate into sclerotome without an epithelial intermediate
They lose their segmental arrangement to form a continuous mesodermal sheet
correctOption: They undergo epithelization to form a ball of cells with a central cavity
optionExplanations: Fusion into multinucleated myotubes is a later stage of muscle differentiation (myogenesis) and does not occur immediately after the segmentation of somitomeres. This process requires the prior formation of myoblasts from the dermomyotome.

Immediately after segmentation, somitomeres undergo epithelization, transforming into a spherical 'ball' of epithelial cells that contains a small central cavity. This epithelial structure is the precursor to further differentiation into the sclerotome and dermomyotome.

While some cells from the somites eventually migrate to the lateral plate mesoderm, this occurs after the formation of the dermomyotome and the establishment of the lateral somitic frontier, not immediately after segmentation.
The sclerotome forms only after the ventral region of the epithelial somite undergoes a secondary transition back into mesenchymal cells; it is not the immediate post-segmentation state.
Somitomeres maintain their segmental arrangement throughout early development, which is critical for the segmental innervation and organization of the axial skeleton and musculature.
citation: Musculature of the aXial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites, which initially form as somitomeres and extend from the occipital region to the tail bud. Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center.

coreConcept: Old vs New concept of muscle domains
statement: How does the modern embryological classification of muscle domains (primaxial and abaxial) differ fundamentally from the traditional classification into epimeres and hypomeres?
options: The modern classification is based on the functional type of muscle fiber (fast vs slow twitch)
The modern classification is based on the actual embryological origin of muscle cells rather than innervation
The modern classification defines domains based on the adult position relative to the transverse processes
The modern classification relies strictly on the vascular supply to the developing myotome
The modern classification groups muscles based on their derivation from either ectoderm or endoderm
correctOption: The modern classification is based on the actual embryological origin of muscle cells rather than innervation
optionExplanations: Muscle fiber type (fast vs slow twitch) is a physiological characteristic determined later in development and is not the basis for the primaxial/abaxial domain classification.

The modern classification into primaxial and abaxial domains is based on the embryological origin of the muscle cells and whether they cross the lateral somitic frontier. This differs from the old epimere/hypomere system, which was defined functionally by innervation from dorsal or ventral primary rami.

While the position relative to the axis is relevant to the terms 'epaxial' and 'hypaxial', the primaxial/abaxial distinction specifically tracks the migration of somitic cells into different mesodermal environments.

Vascular supply is not the primary criteria for defining these developmental domains; the domains are defined by the interaction between somitic cells and the surrounding mesoderm (paraxial vs lateral plate).
Skeletal muscles are almost exclusively derived from the mesodermal germ layer (specifically paraxial mesoderm), so a classification based on ectoderm or endoderm would be incorrect.
citation: The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles), which was based on a functional de fi nition of innervation: Epimeric muscles were innervated by dorsal primary rami, and hypomeric muscles by ventral primary rami.

coreConcept: Origin of coronary artery smooth muscle
statement: What are the specific embryological sources for the smooth muscle found within the walls of the coronary arteries?
options: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm only
Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
Paraxial mesoderm and endoderm
Ectoderm and somatic mesoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
correctOption: Proepicardial cells and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: While the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm provides smooth muscle for the gut and heart tube, the coronary arteries have a more complex origin involving specific cell populations.

Smooth muscle in the coronary arteries is uniquely derived from proepicardial cells and, in the proximal segments of the vessels, from neural crest cells. This distinguishes them from the smooth muscle of the gut or the dorsal aorta.
Paraxial mesoderm primarily forms skeletal muscle, and endoderm forms the epithelial lining of the gut, not the smooth muscle of the coronary vasculature.
Ectoderm contributes to smooth muscle in specific areas like the pupil and mammary glands, but it is not the source for coronary artery smooth muscle.
Sclerotome and dermatome are subdivisions of the somite that form bone and dermis/muscle precursors, respectively, but they do not contribute to the smooth muscle of the coronary arteries.
citation: In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments). Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures.

statement: Which characteristic of cardiac muscle development distinguishes it from the development of skeletal muscle fibers?
options: Cardiac myoblasts undergo extensive fusion to form syncytia
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain attached via intercalated discs
Cardiac muscle is derived from the ectodermal germ layer
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils during the first three months of development
Cardiac muscle cells lose their nuclei during the process of maturation
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain attached via intercalated discs
optionExplanations: Skeletal muscle fibers are formed by the fusion of myoblasts into long, multinucleated syncytia, whereas cardiac muscle cells do not undergo this fusion process.

In cardiac muscle development, myoblasts adhere to each other through specialized attachments that eventually become intercalated discs. Unlike skeletal muscle, these individual myoblasts do not fuse into a single multinucleated fiber.
Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube, not from the ectoderm.
Myofibrils develop in cardiac muscle in a manner similar to skeletal muscle; they are present and essential for the early contractile function of the heart tube.
Cardiac muscle cells remain nucleated throughout their lifespan; they do not lose their nuclei like red blood cells or undergo the extreme multinucleation seen in skeletal muscle fibers.
citation: Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

statement: What is the significance of the 'lateral somitic frontier' in the development of the musculoskeletal system?
options: It marks the boundary where the neural tube separates from the surface ectoderm
It is the border between the somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
It defines the transition point where smooth muscle becomes skeletal muscle
It is the site where the sclerotome fuses with the notochord to form vertebrae
It represents the division between the occipital and cervical somites
correctOption: It is the border between the somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The separation of the neural tube from the surface ectoderm is a process of neurulation and is unrelated to the lateral somitic frontier.

The lateral somitic frontier is a well-defined border that separates the somite-derived cells from the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This frontier is crucial because it defines two distinct developmental environments: the primaxial and abaxial domains.
The frontier does not represent a transition between muscle types; both primaxial and abaxial domains primarily produce skeletal muscle, though from different precursor populations.
The formation of vertebrae involves the resegmentation of the sclerotome around the notochord, which is a medial process, not a lateral frontier event.
The division between different segmental somites (e.g., occipital vs cervical) is transverse, whereas the lateral somitic frontier is a longitudinal boundary.
citation: The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles (Table 11.1, p. 162). Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B).

coreConcept: Signaling sources for abaxial vs primaxial
statement: According to the domain-based model of muscle development, what is the primary source of differentiation signals for the abaxial muscle cell precursors?
options: The neural tube and notochord
The lateral plate mesoderm
The surface ectoderm only
The endodermal lining of the yolk sac
The apical ectodermal ridge of the limb bud
correctOption: The lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The neural tube and notochord provide the primary developmental signals for the primaxial muscle cell precursors, which remain within the paraxial mesoderm.

Abaxial muscle cell precursors are those that cross the lateral somitic frontier and enter the lateral plate mesoderm. Consequently, they receive many of their essential signals for differentiation from the lateral plate mesoderm itself.
While the surface ectoderm may provide some signals to the underlying mesoderm, the lateral plate mesoderm is the specific environment defining the abaxial domain's signaling.
The endodermal lining is involved in gut development and does not serve as the primary signaling center for the differentiation of abaxial skeletal muscle precursors.
The apical ectodermal ridge is a specialized structure for limb outgrowth, but the broader abaxial domain (including abdominal muscles) relies on signals from the lateral plate mesoderm.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier

statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal in origin, which of the following muscles are derived from the ectoderm?
options: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and large arteries
Sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil
Smooth muscle in the wall of the gastrointestinal tract
Cardiac muscle fibers of the ventricles
Skeletal muscles of the abdominal wall
correctOption: Sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil
optionExplanations: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells, not from the surface ectoderm.

The sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil, along with the smooth muscle in the mammary and sweat glands, are unique exceptions to the rule of mesodermal muscle origin, as they are derived from the ectoderm.
Smooth muscle in the gut wall is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds the endodermal tube.
Cardiac muscle is derived from the visceral mesoderm surrounding the heart tube and is never ectodermal in origin.
Skeletal muscles of the abdominal wall are derived from the abaxial domain of the somites (paraxial mesoderm) that migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

statement: Following the epithelization of a somite, what is the fate of its ventral region as it continues to differentiate?
options: It remains epithelial and forms the dermis of the back
It becomes mesenchymal and forms the sclerotome
It migrates to the lateral plate to form the limb muscles
It differentiates into the dorsomedial lip of the myotome
It forms the endothelial lining of the dorsal aorta
correctOption: It becomes mesenchymal and forms the sclerotome
optionExplanations: The dermis of the back is formed by the dermatome, which is located in the upper (dorsal) region of the somite, not the ventral region.
The ventral region of the somite undergoes a transition from epithelial back to mesenchymal cells to form the sclerotome. These cells are the precursors for the vertebrae and the ribs.
Migration to the lateral plate for limb muscle formation involves cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) lip of the dermomyotome, which is in the upper region of the somite.
The dorsomedial lip (DML) is one of the two muscle-forming areas located in the upper region of the somite, distinct from the ventral sclerotome.
The endothelial lining of the dorsal aorta is not derived from the ventral sclerotome; the sclerotome specifically forms the skeletal elements of the axial skeleton.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips

statement: Which principle governs the innervation of developing myotomes, irrespective of whether they belong to the primaxial or abaxial domains?
options: They are innervated by the nearest cranial nerve
They receive innervation from spinal nerves of the same segment
They are innervated only after they reach their final anatomical destination
They lose their original segmental innervation during migration
They are innervated by autonomic fibers from the sympathetic chain
correctOption: They receive innervation from spinal nerves of the same segment
optionExplanations: Cranial nerves innervate muscles derived from the head somitomeres, but the myotomes of the trunk are innervated by spinal nerves.

A fundamental rule of muscle development is that each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves derived from the same embryonic segment as the muscle cells. This segmental relationship is maintained even if the cells migrate far from their origin.
Innervation is established early and follows the muscle cells during their migration; it does not wait until the muscle reaches its final adult position.
Muscle cells do not lose their original segmental innervation; this is why the nerve supply can be used to trace the embryological origin of a muscle.
The primary innervation of skeletal muscle is via somatic motor neurons in the spinal nerves, not autonomic fibers from the sympathetic chain.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. The new description of muscle development characterized by primaXial and abaxial domains differs from the old concept of epimeres (back muscles) and hypomeres (limb and body wall muscles)

statement: At what point during human embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically appear?
options: By the end of the first month
By the end of the third month
Immediately upon the fusion of myoblasts
Only after birth when the infant begins to move
During the second week of gestation
correctOption: By the end of the third month
optionExplanations: By the end of the first month, the somites are still differentiating and myoblasts are just beginning to form; striations are not yet visible.
Cross-striations, which are the histological hallmark of mature skeletal muscle, appear in the cytoplasm of the developing multinucleated muscle fibers by the end of the third month of development.
Fusion of myoblasts forms the multinucleated fiber, but the organization of myofibrils into visible cross-striations is a subsequent maturation process that takes more time.
Skeletal muscle is well-differentiated and histologically recognizable with striations long before birth, allowing for fetal movements in utero.
The second week of gestation is the stage of the bilaminar germ disc; somites and muscle precursors do not even exist at this early stage.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

statement: What is the functional consequence of the resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae for the overlying myotomes?
options: It causes the myotomes to degenerate and be replaced by tendons
It allows the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs
It forces the myotomes to fuse into a single longitudinal muscle mass
It disconnects the myotomes from their original segmental nerves
It prevents the myotomes from exerting any force on the vertebral column
correctOption: It allows the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs
optionExplanations: Resegmentation does not lead to degeneration; rather, it is a critical step in the functional maturation of the axial musculoskeletal system.

The resegmentation of sclerotomes into definitive vertebrae shifts the vertebral boundaries relative to the myotomes. This results in myotomes bridging the intervertebral discs, which is essential for the muscles to be able to move the spine.
Myotomes remain segmented (as seen in the intercostal muscles), although some may combine to form larger muscle groups; they do not fuse into a single undifferentiated mass.
Resegmentation does not disrupt the nerve supply; the spinal nerves emerge between the new vertebrae to reach their corresponding myotomes.
The entire purpose of bridging the discs is to enable the myotomes to exert force across the joints of the spine, allowing for movement and stability.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: Which embryological tissues contribute to the formation of smooth muscle in the dorsal aorta and other large arteries?
options: Paraxial mesoderm and endoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Ectoderm and visceral mesoderm only
Sclerotome and dermatome
Proepicardial cells and paraxial mesoderm
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle, and endoderm forms the lining of the gut; neither is the primary source for the smooth muscle of the large arteries.
The smooth muscle in the walls of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries has a dual origin, arising from both the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.
While ectoderm forms some specific smooth muscles (like the pupil), it is not a contributor to the large systemic arteries like the aorta.
Sclerotome and dermatome are somitic derivatives that form bone, dermis, and skeletal muscle, but they do not form the smooth muscle of the vasculature.
Proepicardial cells contribute to the coronary arteries, but they are not the source for the smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large systemic vessels.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments).

statement: Which of the following best describes the composition of the 'abaxial domain' during embryonic development?
options: It contains only cells derived from the neural tube
It consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
It is composed exclusively of paraxial mesoderm that remains near the notochord
It is the region of the somite that forms the vertebrae and ribs
It consists of the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm surrounding the gut
correctOption: It consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells
optionExplanations: The neural tube is a neuroectodermal structure and does not contribute cells to the abaxial muscle domain.

The abaxial domain is defined as the region consisting of the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm combined with the somite cells (from the VLL lip) that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.
The region composed exclusively of paraxial mesoderm that remains near the neural tube and notochord is the primaxial domain, not the abaxial domain.
The region of the somite that forms the vertebrae and ribs is the sclerotome, which is part of the primaxial environment.
The visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm surrounds the gut and forms smooth muscle, but it is not the 'abaxial domain' associated with skeletal muscle precursors.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

statement: Which group of muscles is formed by the somitic cells that migrate into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Muscles of the back (erector spinae)
Infrahyoid and abdominal wall muscles
Intercostal muscles and shoulder girdle muscles
Sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil
Smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract
correctOption: Infrahyoid and abdominal wall muscles
optionExplanations: Muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are derived from the primaxial domain and do not involve migration into the lateral plate mesoderm.

Cells from the ventrolateral (VLL) lip of the dermomyotome migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm to form the infrahyoid, abdominal wall, and limb muscles.
Intercostal and shoulder girdle muscles are derived from the primaxial domain (cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm), according to the modern classification.
The pupillary muscles are derived from the ectoderm, not from somitic cells migrating into the lateral plate mesoderm.
Smooth muscle of the gut is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not from the migrating skeletal muscle precursors of the somite.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles.

statement: In the context of axial muscle development, how is the innervation of epaxial and hypaxial muscles organized?
options: Both are innervated by the dorsal primary rami
Epaxial by dorsal primary rami; hypaxial by ventral primary rami
Epaxial by ventral primary rami; hypaxial by dorsal primary rami
Both are innervated by the ventral primary rami
They are innervated by cranial nerves rather than spinal rami
correctOption: Epaxial by dorsal primary rami; hypaxial by ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami only innervate the epaxial muscles; they do not provide the primary motor supply to the hypaxial muscles.

The traditional functional definition of innervation remains valid: epaxial muscles (those above the axis, like back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, while hypaxial muscles (those below the axis, like limb and body wall muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami.
