Cross-striations, which result from the highly organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments into sarcomeres, appear by the end of the third month of development. This follows the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers and the synthesis of myofibrils. It marks a significant stage in the functional maturation of skeletal muscle.

While myoblast fusion occurs earlier, the appearance of cross-striations requires further cytoplasmic organization and the accumulation of contractile proteins. Fusion creates the multinucleated syncytium, but the 'striated' appearance is a subsequent histological milestone. It does not happen immediately upon fusion.

Skeletal muscle is well-developed and striated long before birth. Fetal movements, which can be felt by the mother (quickening), are evidence of functional, striated muscle in utero. Waiting until after birth for striations would mean the fetus would have no contractile ability during development.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

coreConcept: Origin of infrahyoid and abdominal muscles
statement: Which group of muscles is formed by cells that migrate from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the somite into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Intercostal muscles and muscles of the shoulder girdle
Deep muscles of the back (erector spinae)
Infrahyoid and abdominal wall muscles
Smooth muscle of the gut and respiratory tract
Cardiac conducting system (Purkinje fibers)
correctOption: Infrahyoid and abdominal wall muscles
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles and muscles of the shoulder girdle are derived from the primaxial domain. These cells remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are distinct from the abaxial muscles formed in the body wall.

The deep muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are primaxial muscles derived from the dorsomedial lip (DML) and the remaining VLL cells. They do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. These muscles are innervated by the dorsal primary rami of spinal nerves.

The infrahyoid, abdominal wall muscles (including the rectus abdominis and obliques), and limb muscles are all abaxial muscles. They originate from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm. This migration is a key step in the development of the ventral body wall.

Smooth muscle of the gut and respiratory tract is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. It does not involve the migration of somitic VLL cells. This tissue is involuntary and non-striated, unlike the skeletal muscles of the abdominal wall.

Purkinje fibers are specialized cardiac muscle cells that form the heart's conducting system. They develop from the visceral mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. They are not skeletal muscles and do not originate from the somitic VLL migration into the parietal mesoderm.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles.

statement: What are Purkinje fibers in the heart, and what is their developmental origin?
options: Nerve fibers derived from the neural crest that innervate the myocardium
Specialized cardiac muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils derived from visceral mesoderm
Smooth muscle cells derived from the proepicardial organ
Connective tissue strands that anchor the heart valves
Endothelial cells that have undergone epithelial-mesenchymal transition
correctOption: Specialized cardiac muscle cells with irregularly distributed myofibrils derived from visceral mesoderm
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are not nerve fibers, although they function in electrical conduction. They are modified cardiac muscle cells. While the neural crest does contribute to some cardiac structures, it is not the source of the Purkinje fibers themselves.

Purkinje fibers are specialized bundles of cardiac muscle cells characterized by irregularly distributed myofibrils. They originate from the same visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm that forms the rest of the myocardium. Their primary role is to form the rapid conducting system of the heart.

Purkinje fibers are not smooth muscle cells; they are a specialized form of cardiac (striated) muscle. Smooth muscle in the heart's coronary vessels may come from the proepicardial organ, but the conducting system does not. The two tissue types have different histological and functional properties.

Connective tissue strands in the heart, such as the chordae tendineae, anchor the valves but do not conduct electrical impulses. Purkinje fibers are cellular and contractile (though specialized), not purely fibrous connective tissue. They are essential for the coordinated contraction of the ventricles.

While endothelial cells in the heart can undergo epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) to form the heart valves (endocardial cushions), they do not form the Purkinje fibers. Purkinje fibers are myogenic in origin. They represent a specialized differentiation pathway within the cardiac muscle lineage.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

statement: Immediately following the segmentation of somitomeres into somites, what morphological change occurs to the somite structure?
options: The cells immediately become mesenchymal to form the sclerotome
The somite undergoes epithelization to form a hollow ball of cells
The somite fuses with the adjacent lateral plate mesoderm
The cells undergo apoptosis to create the intraembryonic coelom
The somite divides into three distinct layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm
correctOption: The somite undergoes epithelization to form a hollow ball of cells
optionExplanations: The transition to mesenchymal cells (the epithelial-mesenchymal transition) occurs later for the ventral portion of the somite to form the sclerotome. It is not the immediate change following segmentation. The initial somite structure is epithelial, not mesenchymal.

Immediately after segmentation, somitomeres undergo epithelization, organizing into a 'ball' of epithelial cells with a small central cavity. This epithelial stage is a prerequisite for the subsequent organized differentiation into sclerotome and dermomyotome. This process occurs in a cranio-caudal sequence along the embryo's axis.

The somite does not fuse with the lateral plate mesoderm; instead, a clear boundary called the lateral somitic frontier is established between them. While some cells later migrate across this frontier, the somite remains a distinct paraxial structure. Fusion would destroy the segmental organization of the body.

Apoptosis is not the primary mechanism for forming the somite or the coelom. The intraembryonic coelom forms by the splitting of the lateral plate mesoderm into parietal and visceral layers. The somite's central cavity is a small, transient space within the epithelial ball.

The somite is already a mesodermal structure and does not divide into the three primary germ layers. Ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm are established during gastrulation, well before somite segmentation. The somite itself differentiates into sclerotome, dermatome, and myotome.
citation: Musculature of the aXial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites, which initially form as somitomeres and extend from the occipital region to the tail bud. Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center.

coreConcept: Smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta
statement: Which embryonic cell populations contribute to the smooth muscle found in the wall of the dorsal aorta?
options: Visceral mesoderm and endoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
Paraxial mesoderm and ectoderm
Proepicardial cells and visceral mesoderm
Sclerotome and dermatome
correctOption: Lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Visceral mesoderm forms the smooth muscle of the gut, and endoderm forms the epithelial lining, but neither is the primary source for the dorsal aorta's smooth muscle. The dorsal aorta is a major vessel of the body wall and trunk. Its development involves different mesodermal and ectomesenchymal inputs.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large systemic arteries is derived from a combination of the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. Neural crest cells are particularly important for the vessels near the heart and in the head and neck. This dual origin reflects the complex integration of different cell lineages in the vascular system.

Paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle and bone, while ectoderm forms the skin and nervous system (and a few specific smooth muscles like the pupil). They do not contribute to the dorsal aorta's muscular wall. The aorta's development is tied to the lateral plate mesoderm.

Proepicardial cells contribute to the coronary arteries, not the dorsal aorta. Visceral mesoderm surrounds the heart and gut but is not the source for the large systemic arteries like the aorta. The aorta develops in the dorsal body wall, associated with the lateral plate.

The sclerotome and dermatome are derivatives of the somite (paraxial mesoderm) and form bone and dermis, respectively. They do not differentiate into the smooth muscle of the large arteries. The vascular system has its own distinct mesodermal origins.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments).

statement: Which of the following is a defining characteristic of the 'primaxial domain' in muscle development?
options: It contains a mixture of somite cells and lateral plate mesoderm
It is located far from the neural tube in the ventral body wall
It contains only cells derived from the paraxial mesoderm (somites)
It gives rise to the muscles of the limbs and abdominal wall
It receives all its developmental signals from the lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: It contains only cells derived from the paraxial mesoderm (somites)
optionExplanations: A mixture of somite cells and lateral plate mesoderm defines the abaxial domain, not the primaxial domain. The primaxial domain is 'pure' in its somitic origin. This distinction is fundamental to the new model of muscle development.

The primaxial domain is located in the region immediately surrounding the neural tube and notochord, not in the ventral body wall. Its proximity to these axial structures allows it to receive specific patterning signals. The ventral body wall is part of the abaxial domain.

The primaxial domain is characterized by containing only cells derived from the paraxial mesoderm (somites) that have not crossed the lateral somitic frontier. These cells form the muscles of the back, ribs, and shoulder girdle. This domain is defined by its strictly somitic cell population.

The muscles of the limbs and abdominal wall are derived from the abaxial domain, where somite cells have migrated into the lateral plate mesoderm. Primaxial muscles are those of the axial skeleton and back. The two domains produce different sets of skeletal muscles.

The primaxial domain receives its developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord, not the lateral plate mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm provides signals for the abaxial domain. This difference in signaling environment leads to different muscle fates.
citation: The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells 2. The abaxial domain that consists of the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm together with somite cells that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier.

statement: What is the relationship between a myotome and its nerve supply during development?
options: Myotomes are innervated by nerves from the adjacent superior segment
Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from the same segment as the muscle cells
Abaxial muscles lose their segmental innervation after crossing the frontier
All myotomes are innervated exclusively by the dorsal primary rami
Innervation is determined by the final position of the muscle, not its segment of origin
correctOption: Each myotome is innervated by spinal nerves from the same segment as the muscle cells
optionExplanations: Myotomes are not innervated by superior segments; they maintain a strict relationship with the spinal nerve of their own segment. This segmental pattern is established early in embryogenesis. It is a key principle used in clinical neurology to map dermatomes and myotomes.

Regardless of whether muscle cells belong to the primaxial or abaxial domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same embryonic segment. This ensures that as muscles migrate (like those in the limbs), they carry their original nerve supply with them. This is why the nerve supply can be used to trace the embryological origin of a muscle.

Abaxial muscles do not lose their segmental innervation; they are still supplied by the spinal nerves of their segment of origin (via the ventral primary rami). The migration across the lateral somitic frontier does not sever the connection with the spinal cord. This is why limb muscles are innervated by specific spinal levels.

Only epaxial (back) muscles are innervated by dorsal primary rami. Hypaxial (body wall and limb) muscles are innervated by ventral primary rami. Both rami are branches of the same segmental spinal nerve. Therefore, not all myotomes use the dorsal rami.

Innervation is determined by the segment of origin, not just the final position. While the final position determines whether a dorsal or ventral ramus is used, the specific spinal level (e.g., C5, L2) is fixed by the segment where the myotome first formed. This is a fundamental rule of vertebrate development.
citation: Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves derived from the same segment as the muscle cells. ... The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami.

statement: During the early development of the muscular system, what morphological change occurs immediately after the segmentation of somitomeres into somites?
options: The cells undergo apoptosis to create space for the neural tube
The cells undergo epithelization to form a hollow ball-like structure
The cells immediately differentiate into multinucleated myotubes
The cells migrate to the lateral plate mesoderm before forming a cavity
The cells lose their epithelial characteristics to become mesenchymal sclerotome
correctOption: The cells undergo epithelization to form a hollow ball-like structure
optionExplanations: Apoptosis is not the primary mechanism for early somite organization; instead, the cells organize into a structured epithelial layer. This process is essential for establishing the initial boundaries of the somite before further differentiation into specific lineages. The neural tube develops adjacent to the somites but does not rely on somitic apoptosis for its space.

Immediately after segmentation, the mesenchymal cells of the somitomeres undergo epithelization, organizing into a spherical epithelial structure with a central cavity. This epithelial somite is the precursor to all subsequent somitic derivatives, including the sclerotome and dermomyotome. This organization is a critical step in the segmental patterning of the vertebrate body axis.

The formation of multinucleated myotubes is a much later stage of muscle differentiation called histogenesis, occurring after the myotome has formed. At the stage of initial segmentation, the cells are still multipotent epithelial or mesenchymal precursors. Striations and fusion do not occur until the myoblast stage later in development.

Migration to the lateral plate mesoderm only occurs for a specific subset of cells (the VLL cells) after the somite has already differentiated into a dermomyotome. The initial epithelization and cavity formation occur in situ within the paraxial mesoderm. The lateral plate mesoderm remains a distinct layer separated by the lateral somitic frontier at this early stage.

While the ventral region of the somite does eventually become mesenchymal to form the sclerotome, this occurs after the initial epithelization process. The epithelization is the immediate step following segmentation from somitomeres. The transition back to mesenchyme (epithelial-mesenchymal transition) is a secondary event that defines the sclerotome.
citation: Musculature of the aXial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites, which initially form as somitomeres and extend from the occipital region to the tail bud. Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center.

coreConcept: Somite Differentiation into Sclerotome and Dermomyotome
statement: Which region of the somite is responsible for forming the bone-forming cells of the vertebrae and ribs, and what is its cellular characteristic?
options: The dorsal region, which remains epithelial
The ventrolateral lip, which becomes mesenchymal
The ventral region, which becomes mesenchymal
The dorsomedial lip, which remains epithelial
The central cavity, which becomes the nucleus pulposus
correctOption: The ventral region, which becomes mesenchymal
optionExplanations: The dorsal region of the somite does not form the vertebrae; instead, it gives rise to the dermatome and the muscle-forming lips. The dorsal region maintains an epithelial organization longer than the ventral region. Bone formation is specifically a function of the ventral somitic derivatives.

The ventrolateral lip (VLL) is one of the two muscle-forming areas of the dermomyotome, not the primary source of the vertebrae and ribs. While VLL cells are migratory, they are destined to become muscle precursors rather than bone-forming cells. The sclerotome is the specific population that forms the axial skeleton.

The ventral region of the somite undergoes an epithelial-mesenchymal transition to become the sclerotome. These mesenchymal cells then migrate around the notochord and neural tube to form the vertebrae and ribs. This transition is a hallmark of the differentiation between the skeletal and muscular precursors within the somite.

The dorsomedial lip (DML) is a muscle-forming area located in the upper region of the somite, contributing to the myotome. It does not form the sclerotome or the ribs. Like the VLL, its primary fate is the production of myogenic progenitor cells for the axial musculature.

The central cavity of the epithelial somite is a transient structure and does not directly form the nucleus pulposus. The nucleus pulposus is derived from the notochord, while the surrounding annulus fibrosus and vertebrae come from the sclerotome. The cavity disappears as the somite differentiates into its sub-regions.
citation: The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs. Cells in the upper region of the somite form the dermatome and two muscle-forming areas at the ventrolateral (VLL) and dorsomedial (DML) lips (or edges), respectively.

statement: What is the significance of the lateral somitic frontier in the organization of the embryonic mesoderm?
options: It marks the boundary where the neural tube separates from the surface ectoderm
It separates the somite-derived cells from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm
It defines the limit between the primaxial domain and the abaxial domain
It acts as a physical barrier that prevents any somite cells from migrating laterally
It serves as the primary site for the formation of the heart tube and pericardium
correctOption: It defines the limit between the primaxial domain and the abaxial domain
optionExplanations: The lateral somitic frontier is a boundary within the mesodermal layer, not between the ectoderm and the neural tube. Neurulation is a separate process involving the folding of the neural plate. The frontier specifically organizes the paraxial and lateral plate mesodermal contributions to the body wall.

The frontier separates the somite from the parietal (somatic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the visceral (splanchnic) layer. The visceral layer is associated with the gut tube and internal organs. The parietal layer contributes to the body wall and limbs, which is where the frontier is relevant.

The lateral somitic frontier is the defining boundary between the primaxial domain (containing only paraxial mesoderm) and the abaxial domain (containing lateral plate mesoderm and migrated somite cells). This distinction is crucial for understanding the different signaling environments and developmental fates of muscle precursors. It represents a shift from the traditional epimere/hypomere classification.

The frontier is not an impenetrable barrier; in fact, cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the somite specifically cross this frontier to enter the abaxial domain. These cells then differentiate into muscles of the limbs and ventral body wall. The frontier defines the domains but allows regulated cellular migration.

The heart tube and pericardium develop from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm in the cardiogenic area, far from the lateral somitic frontier. The frontier is located more laterally and dorsally in the trunk region. It is primarily involved in the development of the musculoskeletal system of the body wall and limbs.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells

statement: Which statement correctly describes the abaxial muscle cell precursors regarding their origin and signaling?
options: They originate from the DML and receive signals from the notochord
They originate from the VLL and receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm
They originate from the sclerotome and receive signals from the neural tube
They originate from the ectoderm and receive signals from the somite
They originate from the visceral mesoderm and receive signals from the gut
correctOption: They originate from the VLL and receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The dorsomedial lip (DML) cells remain in the primaxial domain and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. Consequently, they receive their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord rather than the lateral plate. The DML contributes primarily to the muscles of the back.

Abaxial muscle precursors originate from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) of the dermomyotome and migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the parietal lateral plate mesoderm. Once in this new environment, they rely on signals from the lateral plate mesoderm for their differentiation. This population gives rise to the infrahyoid, limb, and abdominal wall muscles.

The sclerotome gives rise to the vertebrae and ribs, not muscle precursors. Furthermore, sclerotome cells receive signals from the notochord and neural tube to initiate chondrogenesis. Muscle precursors are derived from the dermomyotome, specifically the VLL and DML regions.

Muscle tissue is almost entirely mesodermal in origin, with very few exceptions like the pupillary muscles. Abaxial precursors are definitely mesodermal, originating from the paraxial mesoderm (somite) before migrating. They do not originate from the ectoderm.

The visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm gives rise to smooth muscle of the gut and cardiac muscle, not the abaxial skeletal muscles. Abaxial muscles are skeletal muscles of the body wall and limbs. Their signaling environment is the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, not the visceral layer.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier

statement: A developmental defect affecting the migration of cells across the lateral somitic frontier would most likely result in the malformation of which muscle group?
options: Intercostal muscles
Muscles of the back (erector spinae)
Rectus abdominis muscle
Rhomboid muscles
Shoulder girdle muscles
correctOption: Rectus abdominis muscle
optionExplanations: Intercostal muscles are considered primaxial derivatives because they develop from cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm domain. They do not require migration across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. Therefore, a migration defect at the frontier would not typically affect them.

The muscles of the back, such as the erector spinae, are classic primaxial muscles derived from the DML and the remaining VLL cells that do not migrate. They receive signals from the neural tube and notochord. Their development is independent of the lateral plate mesoderm environment.

The rectus abdominis is a member of the abdominal wall muscles, which are derived from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the abaxial domain. These cells enter the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm to differentiate. A failure in migration across this frontier would directly impact the formation of the rectus abdominis and other abdominal wall components.

The rhomboids are part of the shoulder girdle musculature that is classified as primaxial. Although they are located in the limb/girdle region, their embryological origin is from cells that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They are innervated by nerves corresponding to their primaxial origin.

Most muscles of the shoulder girdle, including the rhomboids and levator scapulae, are derived from the primaxial domain. These cells remain in the paraxial mesoderm and do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. Thus, they would remain unaffected by a frontier migration defect.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles.

coreConcept: Innervation of Epaxial and Hypaxial Muscles
statement: Regardless of whether a muscle is primaxial or abaxial in origin, how is the innervation of 'hypaxial' muscles characterized?
options: They are innervated by dorsal primary rami
They are innervated by ventral primary rami
They are innervated by cranial nerves only
They are innervated by the sympathetic trunk
They are innervated by the phrenic nerve exclusively
correctOption: They are innervated by ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the true muscles of the back. This functional definition is distinct from the embryological primaxial/abaxial classification. Epaxial muscles are located 'above the axis' of the vertebral column.

Hypaxial muscles, which include the muscles of the body wall and limbs, are consistently innervated by the ventral primary rami of spinal nerves. This remains true even though some hypaxial muscles (like the intercostals) are primaxial in origin while others (like the abdominal wall) are abaxial. The innervation pattern follows the anatomical position 'below the axis'.

While some muscles in the head and neck are innervated by cranial nerves, the term 'hypaxial' specifically refers to body wall and limb muscles innervated by spinal nerves. Cranial nerve innervation is associated with branchiomeric muscles or specific head somitomeres. Hypaxial muscles are defined by their spinal nerve supply.

The sympathetic trunk provides autonomic innervation to smooth muscles and glands, not motor innervation to skeletal muscles. Skeletal muscles, including all hypaxial groups, are under somatic motor control. The ventral primary rami carry these somatic motor fibers to the target muscles.

The phrenic nerve is indeed a ventral ramus derivative (C3-C5) that innervates the diaphragm, which is a hypaxial muscle. However, it is not the exclusive nerve for all hypaxial muscles. Every spinal level contributes ventral rami to various hypaxial muscles throughout the body wall and limbs.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4).

statement: At what point in embryonic development do the characteristic cross-striations of skeletal muscle typically become visible?
options: By the end of the first month
By the end of the second month
By the end of the third month
During the fifth month of gestation
Only after birth when the muscle is used
correctOption: By the end of the third month
optionExplanations: By the end of the first month, the somites are still in the process of segmenting and differentiating into sclerotome and dermomyotome. Myoblasts have not yet fused into multinucleated fibers at this early stage. Striations require the organized assembly of myofibrils, which takes more time.

During the second month, myoblasts are actively proliferating and beginning to fuse into myotubes. While myofibrils start to appear in the cytoplasm, the highly organized sarcomere structure that produces visible cross-striations is not yet fully established. The process is ongoing but not complete.

Cross-striations, which are the hallmark of mature skeletal muscle, appear by the end of the third month of development. This follows the fusion of myoblasts into multinucleated fibers and the subsequent synthesis and alignment of myofibrillar proteins. This timeline marks a significant milestone in the functional maturation of the muscular system.

The fifth month is well after the initial appearance of cross-striations. By this time, the fetus is capable of significant movement (quickening), which requires functional, striated muscle. The structural foundation for these movements is laid down by the end of the first trimester.

Skeletal muscle does not require postnatal activity to develop its striated appearance. The genetic and developmental program for myofibril assembly is completed in utero. While muscle fibers grow and strengthen after birth, their basic histological features are present by the end of the third month of gestation.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear.

statement: What is a key histological difference between the development of cardiac muscle and skeletal muscle?
options: Cardiac myoblasts fuse to form syncytia, while skeletal myoblasts do not
Cardiac muscle lacks myofibrils, whereas skeletal muscle has them
Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain attached via intercalated discs
Cardiac muscle is derived from ectoderm, while skeletal muscle is mesodermal
Cardiac muscle develops cross-striations much later than skeletal muscle
correctOption: Cardiac myoblasts do not fuse but remain attached via intercalated discs
optionExplanations: This statement is the opposite of the truth; skeletal myoblasts fuse to form a multinucleated syncytium, whereas cardiac myoblasts remain as individual cells. The syncytial nature of skeletal muscle allows for long, continuous fibers. Cardiac muscle functions as a functional syncytium due to gap junctions, not cellular fusion.

Both cardiac and skeletal muscles contain myofibrils, which are the contractile units responsible for striations. The internal organization of actin and myosin is very similar in both tissues. The presence of myofibrils is what allows both muscle types to generate force and appear striated under a microscope.

Unlike skeletal muscle where myoblasts fuse into long multinucleated fibers, cardiac myoblasts remain separate entities. They adhere to each other through specialized cell-to-cell junctions that eventually mature into intercalated discs. These discs contain desmosomes and gap junctions, allowing for mechanical and electrical coupling without cytoplasmic fusion.

Both cardiac and skeletal muscles are derived from the mesodermal germ layer. Specifically, cardiac muscle comes from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm, and skeletal muscle comes from the paraxial mesoderm. Ectoderm only contributes to a very small number of specific smooth muscles.

Both muscle types develop myofibrils and striations during embryonic life. While the exact timing might vary slightly, the fundamental process of sarcomere assembly is a shared feature of all striated muscles. The primary difference lies in the cellular architecture (fusion vs. attachment) rather than the presence of striations.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse.

statement: From which embryonic source does the smooth muscle in the wall of the stomach and intestines originate?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Surface ectoderm
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is the source of skeletal muscle (via somites) and the axial skeleton. It does not contribute to the smooth muscle of the gut. The gut's muscularis externa is derived from the mesoderm immediately adjacent to the endodermal tube.

The parietal (somatic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm forms the dermis of the body wall, the bones of the limbs, and the sternum. It also provides the environment for abaxial skeletal muscle development. It is not the source of the visceral smooth muscle surrounding the internal organs.

The visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm surrounds the endodermal gut tube and its derivatives (like the stomach and intestines). This mesodermal layer differentiates into the smooth muscle and connective tissue of the organ walls. This is a fundamental distinction between the body wall (parietal) and the viscera (visceral).

Neural crest cells contribute to the enteric nervous system (ganglia) within the gut wall, but they do not form the bulk of the smooth muscle tissue itself. The muscle is mesodermal. Neural crest cells do, however, contribute smooth muscle to certain large arteries.

Surface ectoderm gives rise to the epidermis and certain specialized smooth muscles like those in the mammary and sweat glands. It does not contribute to the internal smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract. The gut is an endodermal tube surrounded by visceral mesoderm.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: Which of the following is a unique feature of the smooth muscle in the dorsal aorta compared to the smooth muscle of the gut?
options: It is derived entirely from the paraxial mesoderm
It receives a significant contribution from neural crest cells
It is the only smooth muscle derived from ectoderm
It does not contain any contractile proteins
It is formed by the fusion of myoblasts into syncytia
correctOption: It receives a significant contribution from neural crest cells
optionExplanations: Neither the aorta nor the gut smooth muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm. Paraxial mesoderm is reserved for skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton. Vascular smooth muscle has a more complex, heterogeneous origin including lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest.

The smooth muscle of the dorsal aorta and other large arteries (especially in the head and neck) has a dual origin from the lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In contrast, the smooth muscle of the gut is derived almost exclusively from the visceral lateral plate mesoderm. This neural crest contribution is a specific feature of the great vessels.

Ectoderm-derived smooth muscles are limited to the pupil, mammary glands, and sweat glands. While neural crest cells are ectodermal in origin (neuroectoderm), the smooth muscle of the aorta is usually described as having a neural crest 'contribution' rather than being purely 'ectodermal' like the pupillary muscles. The gut muscle is purely mesodermal.

All smooth muscle, by definition, contains contractile proteins like actin and myosin. Without these, the vessels would not be able to regulate their diameter or maintain vascular tone. The presence of these proteins is universal across all muscle types, though their organization varies.

Fusion into syncytia is a characteristic of skeletal muscle, not smooth muscle. Smooth muscle cells remain as individual, spindle-shaped cells connected by gap junctions. This allows for slow, sustained contractions and the ability to be stretched, which is vital for arterial function.
citation: Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. In the coronary arteries, smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells (see Chapter 13) and neural crest cells (proximal segments).

coreConcept: Ectodermal Origin of Specific Smooth Muscles
statement: Which of the following muscles is an exception to the rule that muscle tissue is derived from the mesodermal germ layer?
options: Biceps brachii muscle
Dilator pupillae muscle
External oblique muscle
Myocardium of the left ventricle
Pyloric sphincter of the stomach
correctOption: Dilator pupillae muscle
optionExplanations: The biceps brachii is a skeletal muscle of the limb, derived from the abaxial muscle precursors of the somites (paraxial mesoderm). It follows the standard mesodermal origin for the muscular system. It is not an exception to the germ layer rule.

The dilator pupillae and sphincter pupillae muscles of the eye are among the very few muscles in the human body derived from the ectoderm (specifically the neuroectoderm of the optic cup). This makes them a classic embryological exception, as nearly all other muscle tissue is mesodermal. Other exceptions include muscles of the mammary and sweat glands.

The external oblique is a skeletal muscle of the abdominal wall. Like other body wall muscles, it is derived from the VLL cells of the somites that migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. It is entirely mesodermal in origin.

The myocardium is the muscular layer of the heart and is derived from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. It is a primary example of mesoderm-derived muscle tissue. It is never derived from ectoderm.

The pyloric sphincter is a smooth muscle structure in the gut. Like the rest of the gastrointestinal tract's musculature, it is derived from the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. It is not one of the ectodermal exceptions.
citation: Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm. Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs

statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation facilitate the functional movement of the vertebral column by the muscles?
options: It allows each myotome to attach to only one vertebra, increasing stability
It causes myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs by shifting their relative position
It ensures that each muscle is innervated by at least three different spinal nerves
It converts the myotome into a ligamentous structure that supports the discs
It prevents the spinal nerves from exiting the vertebral canal
correctOption: It causes myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs by shifting their relative position
optionExplanations: If a myotome attached to only one vertebra, it would be unable to produce movement across a joint. For a muscle to move a joint (or the spine), it must span across the articulation between two bones. Resegmentation is the mechanism that ensures this spanning occurs.

During resegmentation, the caudal half of one sclerotome fuses with the cranial half of the next. This shift in the skeletal framework means that the myotomes, which originally aligned with the centers of the sclerotomes, now bridge the space between the newly formed definitive vertebrae (the intervertebral discs). This anatomical arrangement is what allows the muscles to exert leverage and move the spine.

While some muscles are multi-segmental, resegmentation is primarily about the physical relationship between the muscle and the bone, not the number of nerves. Each myotome still fundamentally receives innervation from the spinal nerve of its original segment. The 'bridging' is a mechanical necessity for movement.

The myotome remains muscular and differentiates into the various muscles of the back and body wall. It does not become a ligament. The ligaments of the spine are derived from the sclerotome itself, not the myotome. The myotome's role is active contraction, not passive support.

Resegmentation actually creates the pathways (intervertebral foramina) through which the spinal nerves exit the vertebral canal. Without this process, the nerves would be trapped or blocked by the developing bone. It coordinates the exit of nerves with the position of the muscles they innervate.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). Regardless of their domain, each myotome receives its innervation from spinal nerves

statement: Which of the following correctly pairs a muscle precursor population with its primary source of developmental signaling?
options: Abaxial precursors — Neural tube and Notochord
Primaxial precursors — Lateral plate mesoderm
DML-derived cells — Neural tube and Notochord
VLL cells crossing the frontier — Surface ectoderm
Sclerotome cells — Lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: DML-derived cells — Neural tube and Notochord
optionExplanations: Abaxial precursors are those that have migrated into the lateral plate mesoderm. Consequently, they receive their signals from the lateral plate mesoderm itself, not the midline structures like the neural tube. The neural tube and notochord are too distant to provide the primary signals for these migrated cells.

Primaxial precursors are the cells that remain in the paraxial mesoderm environment near the midline. Therefore, they are under the influence of signals from the neural tube and notochord. They do not receive signals from the lateral plate mesoderm, which is separated from them by the lateral somitic frontier.

The dorsomedial lip (DML) cells are a core component of the primaxial muscle precursor population. Because they remain in the paraxial domain adjacent to the axial structures, they receive their essential differentiation signals from the neural tube and the notochord. This signaling environment dictates their fate as muscles of the back and certain other axial structures.

While the surface ectoderm does provide some signals to the underlying mesoderm, the specific signals for the differentiation of abaxial VLL cells come from the lateral plate mesoderm they have entered. The lateral plate mesoderm provides a unique molecular environment that differs from the paraxial domain. The frontier marks this transition.

Sclerotome cells, which form the vertebrae, are located ventrally in the somite and receive their primary induction signals from the notochord and the floor plate of the neural tube. They are not signaled by the lateral plate mesoderm. Their development into bone is strictly regulated by these midline structures.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their developmental signals from the neural tube and notochord

statement: How is the dermomyotome specifically formed during somite differentiation?
options: By the fusion of the sclerotome with the overlying surface ectoderm
By cells from the VLL and DML migrating and proliferating ventral to the dermatome
By the migration of neural crest cells into the central cavity of the somite
By the direct transformation of the entire somite into a single muscle mass
By the proliferation of the endoderm into the paraxial mesoderm
correctOption: By cells from the VLL and DML migrating and proliferating ventral to the dermatome
optionExplanations: The sclerotome and the dermomyotome are distinct populations that move in opposite directions; the sclerotome moves ventrally to surround the notochord, while the dermomyotome remains dorsal. They do not fuse. The surface ectoderm is a separate germ layer that does not contribute cells to the dermomyotome.

The dermomyotome is formed when cells from the two muscle-forming lips of the somite—the ventrolateral lip (VLL) and the dorsomedial lip (DML)—proliferate and migrate to form a new layer of progenitor muscle cells (the myotome) immediately beneath the remaining epithelial dermatome. This combined structure is essential for the subsequent development of both the skin's dermis and the skeletal muscles.

Neural crest cells migrate through the somites (specifically the cranial half of the sclerotome), but they do not form the dermomyotome. The dermomyotome is a purely somitic (paraxial mesoderm) derivative. Neural crest cells contribute to the peripheral nervous system and other non-muscle structures.

The somite does not become a single muscle mass; it must differentiate into the sclerotome (bone), dermatome (dermis), and myotome (muscle). This regionalization is critical for the organized development of the musculoskeletal system. A single mass would not allow for the complex patterning of the trunk.

The endoderm forms the lining of the gut and respiratory systems and does not contribute cells to the somites or the dermomyotome. The muscular system is almost entirely mesodermal. There is no proliferation of endoderm into the paraxial mesoderm during this stage of development.
citation: Cells from these two areas migrate and proliferate to form progenitor muscle cells ventral to the dermatome, thereby forming the dermomyotome (Fig. 11.1B,C; Fig. 11.2). Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B).

statement: Which of the following embryonic tissues is the primary source for the development of the skeletal musculature of the axial skeleton and limbs?
options: Paraxial mesoderm
Visceral mesoderm
Neural crest
Ectoderm
Lateral plate mesoderm
correctOption: Paraxial mesoderm
optionExplanations: Paraxial mesoderm is the correct answer because it organizes into somites and somitomeres, which are the primary precursors for all skeletal muscles of the axial skeleton and limbs. This tissue undergoes segmentation to form the building blocks of the musculoskeletal system. Other mesodermal layers contribute to different muscle types or connective tissues.

Visceral mesoderm is incorrect as it primarily gives rise to cardiac muscle and the smooth muscle of the gut wall rather than skeletal muscle. It surrounds the heart tube and the primitive gut during development. It does not form the somites required for skeletal muscle production.

Neural crest cells contribute to some smooth muscle in large arteries and specific head structures but do not form the bulk of the skeletal muscle system. They are primarily known for forming peripheral nerves and craniofacial structures. Their role in myogenesis is limited to very specific regions.

Ectoderm is responsible for specific smooth muscles like the pupillary, mammary, and sweat gland muscles but not skeletal muscle. Most muscle tissue is mesodermal in origin. Ectoderm primarily forms the nervous system and the epidermis.

Lateral plate mesoderm provides the environment and some signals for abaxial muscles, but the muscle cells themselves originate from the paraxial mesoderm. While it forms the parietal and visceral layers, it is not the primary source of skeletal muscle fibers. It contributes to the dermis and bones of the limbs instead.
citation: Skeletal muscle is derived from paraxial mesoderm, which forms somites from the occipital to the sacral regions and somitomeres in the head. Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles).

statement: During the early development of a somite, which specific region undergoes a transition back to mesenchymal tissue to eventually form the vertebrae and ribs?
options: Ventral region
Dorsomedial lip
Ventrolateral lip
Dermatome
Lateral somitic frontier
correctOption: Ventral region
optionExplanations: The ventral region of the somite is the correct answer because it undergoes an epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition to form the sclerotome. These mesenchymal cells then migrate to surround the notochord and neural tube to form the vertebral column. This process is essential for the development of the axial skeleton.

The dorsomedial lip is incorrect because it is one of the two muscle-forming areas of the somite that contributes to the myotome. It specifically gives rise to primaxial muscle precursors that stay near the neural tube. It does not contribute to the bone-forming sclerotome.

The ventrolateral lip is incorrect as it is the other muscle-forming area that produces abaxial muscle precursors. These cells often migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm to form body wall and limb muscles. Like the dorsomedial lip, its primary fate is myogenic, not osteogenic.

The dermatome is the dorsal part of the somite that gives rise to the dermis of the back. It remains epithelial longer than the ventral region and does not form the vertebrae or ribs. It is distinct from the sclerotome and myotome layers.

The lateral somitic frontier is not a region of the somite itself but a boundary between the somite and the lateral plate mesoderm. It defines the separation between primaxial and abaxial developmental domains. It does not undergo mesenchymal transition to form bone.
citation: Immediately after segmentation, these somitomeres undergo a process of epithelization and form a “ball” of epithelial cells with a small cavity in the center (Fig. 11.1A). The ventral region of each somite then becomes mesenchymal again and forms the sclerotome (Fig. 11.1B—D), the bone-forming cells for the vertebrae and ribs.

statement: What is the significance of the lateral somitic frontier in the developing embryo regarding the organization of mesodermal domains?
options: It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
It divides the sclerotome from the dermatome
It marks the boundary between the ectoderm and endoderm
It separates the neural tube from the notochord
It defines the limit of the neural crest cell migration
correctOption: It separates the primaxial domain from the abaxial domain
optionExplanations: The lateral somitic frontier is the correct answer because it serves as the boundary between the primaxial domain (somite-only cells) and the abaxial domain (somite cells mixed with lateral plate mesoderm). This frontier is crucial for determining the signaling environment and final destination of muscle precursors. It represents a fundamental shift in how we understand muscle patterning.

Dividing the sclerotome from the dermatome is incorrect because these are internal subdivisions of the somite itself. The frontier is an external boundary between the somite and the adjacent lateral plate mesoderm. Sclerotome and dermatome are defined by their dorsal-ventral position within the somite.

Marking the boundary between ectoderm and endoderm is incorrect as the frontier is strictly a mesodermal landmark. Ectoderm and endoderm are different germ layers entirely. The frontier exists within the mesodermal layer to organize muscle and connective tissue precursors.

Separating the neural tube from the notochord is incorrect because these are midline structures of the embryo. The lateral somitic frontier is located more laterally, where the paraxial mesoderm meets the lateral plate mesoderm. It has no role in the separation of axial midline structures.

Defining the limit of neural crest cell migration is incorrect because neural crest cells follow specific pathways that are not primarily defined by this frontier. While they interact with somites, the frontier's primary definition relates to the mixing of somitic and lateral plate mesodermal cells. It is a myogenic and osteogenic boundary.
citation: Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo: 1. The primaxial domain that comprises the region around the neural tube and contains only somite-derived (paraXial mesoderm) cells

statement: Which group of muscles is derived from the abaxial muscle cell precursors that have migrated across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Abdominal wall muscles
Deep back muscles
Intercostal muscles
Shoulder girdle muscles
Rhomboid muscles
correctOption: Abdominal wall muscles
optionExplanations: Abdominal wall muscles, such as the rectus abdominis and obliques, are correct because they are formed by VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier. These cells enter the abaxial domain and receive differentiation signals from the lateral plate mesoderm. This migration is a key feature of hypaxial muscle development.

Deep back muscles are incorrect because they belong to the primaxial domain. They are derived from cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm and do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. They receive signals primarily from the neural tube and notochord.

Intercostal muscles are incorrect as they are classified as primaxial muscles despite their location in the body wall. They develop from somite cells that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. They are part of the primaxial domain along with the shoulder girdle muscles.

Shoulder girdle muscles are incorrect because they are derived from primaxial precursors. Although they are located near the limbs, their embryological origin is from cells that stay within the paraxial mesoderm domain. They do not migrate across the frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm.

Rhomboid muscles are incorrect as they are part of the primaxial muscle group. Like other muscles of the back and shoulder girdle, they develop from precursors that remain in the paraxial mesoderm. They are not considered abaxial muscles.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors

statement: Which of the following muscles is categorized as belonging to the primaxial domain, consisting of cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm?
options: Shoulder girdle muscles
Rectus abdominis
External oblique
Infrahyoid muscles
Limb muscles
correctOption: Shoulder girdle muscles
optionExplanations: Shoulder girdle muscles are the correct answer because they are derived from primaxial muscle precursors that do not cross the lateral somitic frontier. These cells remain in the paraxial mesoderm and receive signals from the neural tube. This group also includes the deep back muscles and intercostals.

Rectus abdominis is incorrect because it is an abaxial muscle. It forms from VLL cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. It is part of the ventral body wall musculature derived from the abaxial domain.

External oblique is incorrect as it is another example of an abaxial muscle. Like the rectus abdominis, its precursors migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm. It receives its developmental signals from the lateral plate rather than the neural tube.

Infrahyoid muscles are incorrect because they are derived from the abaxial domain. These muscles of the neck form from somitic cells that have migrated across the frontier. They are not part of the primaxial population that stays near the axis.

Limb muscles are incorrect as they are the classic example of abaxial muscles. Precursors from the VLL migrate extensively into the limb buds, which are formed by lateral plate mesoderm. They are entirely abaxial in their developmental origin.
citation: The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles (Table 11.1, p. 162). Initially, there is a well-de fi ned border between each somite and the parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm called the lateral somitic frontier (Fig. 11.1B). This frontier separates two mesodermal domains in the embryo.

statement: A developmental biologist is studying the innervation of the axial skeleton. Which nerve branches are responsible for the innervation of the epaxial (back) muscles?
options: Dorsal primary rami
Ventral primary rami
Sympathetic chain ganglia
Phrenic nerve
Intercostal nerves
correctOption: Dorsal primary rami
optionExplanations: Dorsal primary rami are the correct answer because they specifically innervate the epaxial muscles, which are the deep muscles of the back. This relationship is established early in development as the spinal nerve divides. Each myotome segment is served by the corresponding segmental nerve.

Ventral primary rami are incorrect because they innervate the hypaxial muscles, which include the limb and body wall muscles. While they originate from the same spinal nerve, they follow a different path to reach the ventral and lateral structures. They do not supply the true deep back muscles.

Sympathetic chain ganglia are incorrect as they are part of the autonomic nervous system and provide visceral motor innervation. They do not provide somatic motor innervation to skeletal muscles like the epaxial group. Their role is related to smooth muscle and glands.

The phrenic nerve is incorrect because it is a specific nerve (C3-C5) that innervates the diaphragm. While the diaphragm is a skeletal muscle, it is not classified as an epaxial muscle of the back. The phrenic nerve is derived from ventral rami.

Intercostal nerves are incorrect because they are the ventral primary rami of the thoracic spinal nerves. They innervate the intercostal muscles and the skin of the thorax, which are hypaxial structures. They do not innervate the epaxial muscles located dorsal to the vertebrae.
citation: The description does not preclude the fact that epaxial (above the axis) muscles (back muscles) are innervated by dorsal primary rami, whereas hypaxial (below the axis) muscles (body wall and limb muscles) are innervated by ventral primary rami (Fig. 11.4). During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers.

statement: In contrast to skeletal muscle development, which of the following is a characteristic feature of cardiac muscle differentiation?
options: Myoblasts do not fuse
Myoblasts form multinucleated fibers
Derived from paraxial mesoderm
Lack of myofibrils
Derived from the neural crest
correctOption: Myoblasts do not fuse
optionExplanations: The fact that myoblasts do not fuse is the correct answer and a defining difference between cardiac and skeletal muscle. Instead of forming a syncytium, cardiac myoblasts remain as individual cells. They are linked by intercalated discs to allow for coordinated contraction.

Myoblasts forming multinucleated fibers is incorrect because this is the hallmark of skeletal muscle development. In skeletal muscle, myoblasts fuse to form long, syncytial fibers. Cardiac muscle cells typically remain mononucleated or binucleated.

Derived from paraxial mesoderm is incorrect because cardiac muscle originates from the visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. Paraxial mesoderm is the source for skeletal muscle. These two muscle types have distinct embryological origins.

Lack of myofibrils is incorrect because cardiac muscle cells do develop myofibrils, similar to skeletal muscle. These myofibrils are responsible for the striations seen in cardiac tissue. Only the specialized Purkinje fibers have irregularly distributed or fewer myofibrils.

Derived from the neural crest is incorrect as the primary myocardium is mesodermal. While neural crest cells contribute to the septation of the heart and some smooth muscle in the great vessels, they do not form the cardiac muscle of the heart walls. The heart wall is purely mesodermal.
citation: Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube. Myoblasts adhere to one another by special attachments that later develop into intercalated discs. Myo fi brils develop as in skeletal muscle, but myoblasts do not fuse. During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible.

coreConcept: Ectodermal origin of specific smooth muscles
statement: While most smooth muscle is mesodermal in origin, which of the following smooth muscles is uniquely derived from the ectodermal germ layer?
options: Pupillary muscles
Dorsal aorta wall
Coronary artery wall
Gut wall muscles
Bronchial tree muscles
correctOption: Pupillary muscles
optionExplanations: Pupillary muscles (sphincter and dilator pupillae) are the correct answer as they are among the few muscles derived from ectoderm. Specifically, they arise from the ectoderm of the optic cup. This is a rare exception to the general rule that muscle is mesodermal.

The dorsal aorta wall is incorrect because its smooth muscle is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells. It is not an ectodermal derivative. The neural crest is often considered a 'fourth germ layer' but is distinct from the surface ectoderm that forms pupillary muscles.

The coronary artery wall is incorrect as its smooth muscle originates from proepicardial cells and neural crest cells. These are mesodermal and neural crest origins, respectively. They do not develop from the ectodermal germ layer.

Gut wall muscles are incorrect because they differentiate from the visceral (splanchnic) layer of the lateral plate mesoderm. This mesoderm surrounds the endodermal gut tube during development. It is the standard source for most visceral smooth muscle.

Bronchial tree muscles are incorrect as they are derived from the splanchnic mesoderm surrounding the lung buds. Like the gut, the respiratory system's smooth muscle is mesodermal. It follows the same developmental pattern as the rest of the gastrointestinal derivatives.
citation: Smooth muscle differentiates from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the gut and its derivatives and from ectoderm (pupillary, mammary gland, and sweat gland muscles). Cardiac muscle is derived from Visceral (splanchnic) mesoderm surrounding the heart tube. Musculature of the aXial skeleton, body wall, and limbs is derived from somites.

statement: What is the embryological origin and function of Purkinje fibers within the developing heart?
options: Specialized cardiac muscle cells for conduction
Neural crest cells for contraction
Ectodermal cells for rhythmicity
Splanchnic mesoderm for valve formation
Paraxial mesoderm for septation
correctOption: Specialized cardiac muscle cells for conduction
optionExplanations: Purkinje fibers are specialized cardiac muscle cells, making this the correct answer. They are modified myocytes that have fewer myofibrils and are specialized for the rapid conduction of electrical impulses. They are not nerves, despite their role in the conducting system.

Neural crest cells for contraction is incorrect because Purkinje fibers are of myogenic (mesodermal) origin, not neural crest. While neural crest cells are involved in heart development, they do not form the conducting myocytes. Their role is more structural and regulatory in the outflow tract.

Ectodermal cells for rhythmicity is incorrect because the heart and its conducting system are derived from mesoderm. Ectoderm does not contribute to the internal tissues of the heart. Rhythmicity is a property of these specialized mesodermal cells.

Splanchnic mesoderm for valve formation is incorrect because, while the heart is derived from splanchnic mesoderm, Purkinje fibers are not involved in valve formation. Valves are formed by endocardial cushions. Purkinje fibers are strictly part of the electrical conduction pathway.

Paraxial mesoderm for septation is incorrect because paraxial mesoderm forms skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton, not heart structures. Septation involves the endocardium and neural crest cells. The heart develops from the lateral plate (visceral) mesoderm.
citation: During later development, a few special bundles of muscle cells with irregularly distributed myo fi brils become visible. These bundles, Purkinje fi bers, form the conducting system of the heart. Smooth muscle for the dorsal aorta and large arteries is derived from lateral plate mesoderm and neural crest cells.

coreConcept: Smooth muscle of the gut wall
statement: From which specific embryonic tissue does the smooth muscle found in the wall of the gastrointestinal tract originate?
options: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm
Paraxial mesoderm
Neural crest cells
Ectoderm
correctOption: Visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm
optionExplanations: The visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm is the correct answer as it directly surrounds the endodermal gut tube. As the gut develops, this mesodermal layer differentiates into the smooth muscle and connective tissue of the organ walls. This is also known as splanchnic mesoderm.

The parietal layer of lateral plate mesoderm is incorrect because it forms the dermis of the body wall, the bones of the limbs, and the sternum. It does not contribute to the internal organ walls. It lines the intraembryonic cavity rather than the gut tube.

Paraxial mesoderm is incorrect because its primary fate is to form somites, which give rise to skeletal muscle and the axial skeleton. It does not contribute to the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract. Its derivatives are primarily somatic, not visceral.

Neural crest cells are incorrect because, while they migrate into the gut wall to form the enteric nervous system (ganglia), they do not form the smooth muscle itself. The muscle tissue is entirely mesodermal. The neural crest provides the innervation, not the contractile tissue.

Ectoderm is incorrect because it only forms a few specific smooth muscles like those in the eye and skin glands. The vast majority of smooth muscle, including that of the gut, is mesodermal. Ectoderm primarily forms the epithelial lining of the skin and the nervous system.
citation: Smooth muscle in the wall of the gut and gut derivatives is derived from the visceral layer of lateral plate mesoderm that surrounds these structures. Only the sphincter and dilator muscles of the pupil and muscle tissue in the mammary and sweat glands are derived from ectoderm.

statement: How does the process of sclerotome resegmentation facilitate the functional movement of the vertebral column by the axial muscles?
options: It causes myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs
It allows myotomes to fuse into a single unit
It prevents the formation of spinal nerves
It converts skeletal muscle into smooth muscle
It separates the epaxial and hypaxial muscles
correctOption: It causes myotomes to bridge intervertebral discs
optionExplanations: The correct answer is that resegmentation causes myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs. Because the vertebrae form from the halves of two adjacent sclerotomes, the muscles (myotomes) that were originally aligned with the sclerotomes now span the gap between the new vertebrae. This arrangement is what allows muscle contraction to result in spinal movement.

Allowing myotomes to fuse into a single unit is incorrect because myotomes must remain segmented to allow for localized movement and flexibility of the spine. Fusion would result in a rigid, immobile rod. The segmental nature of the muscles is preserved to match the vertebral segments.

Preventing the formation of spinal nerves is incorrect because resegmentation actually allows spinal nerves to exit the vertebral column. The nerves pass between the vertebrae to reach their respective myotomes. Without this process, the nerves would be trapped within the bone.

Converting skeletal muscle into smooth muscle is incorrect because the axial muscles remain skeletal throughout development and adulthood. Smooth muscle has a different embryological origin and function. The axial muscles are responsible for voluntary movement and posture.

Separating the epaxial and hypaxial muscles is incorrect because this separation is determined by the lateral somitic frontier and the migration of VLL cells. Resegmentation is a process specifically involving the sclerotome and its relationship to the myotome. It does not define the epaxial/hypaxial split.
citation: Resegmentation of sclerotomes into de fi nitive vertebrae causes the myotomes to bridge the intervertebral discs, and this alteration gives them the capacity to move the spine (Fig. 10.16C). With the exception of some smooth muscle tissue (see p. 164), the muscular system develops from the mesodermal germ layer and consists of skeletal, smooth, and cardiac muscle.

statement: Which morphological change is characteristic of skeletal muscle differentiation by the end of the third month of development?
options: Appearance of cross-striations
Formation of intercalated discs
Loss of multinucleation
Transition to mesenchymal cells
Development of Purkinje fibers
correctOption: Appearance of cross-striations
optionExplanations: The appearance of cross-striations is the correct answer as it signifies the organized arrangement of actin and myosin filaments into sarcomeres. This occurs by the end of the third month of fetal development. It is the definitive histological feature of mature skeletal muscle.

Formation of intercalated discs is incorrect because these are unique to cardiac muscle. They represent the specialized cell-to-cell junctions that allow for electrical coupling in the heart. Skeletal muscle fibers are syncytial and do not possess these discs.

Loss of multinucleation is incorrect because skeletal muscle fibers become more multinucleated as more myoblasts fuse. The multinucleated state is a permanent and essential feature of skeletal muscle fibers. It allows for the maintenance of the large cytoplasmic volume of the fiber.

Transition to mesenchymal cells is incorrect because the process of myogenesis involves the transition of mesenchymal cells into specialized myoblasts and then into muscle fibers. It is a process of differentiation away from the mesenchymal state. Mesenchymal cells are the precursors, not the end product.

Development of Purkinje fibers is incorrect because these are specialized conducting cells found only in the heart. They are a derivative of cardiac muscle development, not skeletal muscle. Skeletal muscle does not have a specialized internal conduction system of this type.
citation: During differentiation, precursor cells, the myoblasts, fuse and form long, multinucleated muscle fi bers. Myo fi brils soon appear in the cytoplasm, and by the end of the third month, cross-striations, typical of skeletal muscle, appear. Cardiac muscle develops from visceral mesoderm surrounding the endothelial heart tube.

statement: Which of the following muscles is formed by cells from the ventrolateral lip (VLL) that migrate into the parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm?
options: Rectus abdominis
Erector spinae
Rhomboid major
Levator scapulae
Trapezius
correctOption: Rectus abdominis
optionExplanations: Rectus abdominis is the correct answer because it is a member of the abaxial muscle group. Its precursors originate in the VLL of the somite and migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm. This migration is characteristic of the ventral body wall muscles.

Erector spinae is incorrect because it is a deep back muscle belonging to the primaxial domain. These muscles develop from cells that remain within the paraxial mesoderm. They do not migrate into the lateral plate mesoderm during development.

Rhomboid major is incorrect as it is classified as a primaxial muscle. Although it is a muscle of the shoulder region, its embryological origin is from the DML and VLL cells that stay within the somitic domain. It does not cross the lateral somitic frontier.

Levator scapulae is incorrect because it is also a primaxial muscle. Like the rhomboids and the shoulder girdle muscles, it is derived from precursors that do not enter the abaxial domain. It receives its signals from the neural tube and notochord.

Trapezius is incorrect because it has a unique origin, partly from branchial arch mesoderm and innervated by a cranial nerve (Accessory nerve). It is not a typical somite-derived muscle of the VLL migration pathway. It is often grouped with the muscles of the head and neck.
citation: Some cells from the VLL region also migrate into the adjacent parietal layer of the lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.1B). Here, they form infrahyoid, abdominal wall (rectus abdominis, internal and external oblique, and transversus abdominis), and limb muscles. The remaining cells in the myotome form muscles of the back, shoulder girdle, and intercostal muscles.

statement: From which structure do primaxial muscle cell precursors, which remain in the paraxial mesoderm, primarily receive their developmental signals?
options: Neural tube and notochord
Lateral plate mesoderm
Ectoderm
Endoderm
Splanchnic mesoderm
correctOption: Neural tube and notochord
optionExplanations: The neural tube and notochord are the correct answer because they provide the primary inductive signals for the primaxial domain. These midline structures secrete factors that direct the differentiation of the DML and the remaining VLL cells. This ensures the proper patterning of the axial muscles.

Lateral plate mesoderm is incorrect because it provides signals for the abaxial muscle precursors, not the primaxial ones. Cells that migrate across the lateral somitic frontier into the lateral plate mesoderm are influenced by its specific signaling environment. Primaxial cells are shielded from these signals by the frontier.

Ectoderm is incorrect as it primarily provides signals for the differentiation of the dermatome and some aspects of the limb bud. While it interacts with the underlying mesoderm, it is not the primary signaling source for the primaxial muscle precursors. The neural tube is the dominant influence for the medial somite.

Endoderm is incorrect because it is located ventrally and primarily influences the development of the gut and its derivatives. It does not play a major role in signaling the dorsal paraxial mesoderm where the primaxial muscles form. Its signaling is more relevant to the visceral mesoderm.

Splanchnic mesoderm is incorrect because it is the visceral layer of the lateral plate mesoderm, which forms cardiac and smooth muscle. It is not involved in the signaling of the skeletal muscle precursors in the paraxial domain. It is geographically and functionally distinct from the primaxial environment.
citation: Muscle cells that cross this frontier (those from the VLL edge of the myotome) and enter the lateral plate mesoderm comprise the abaxial muscle cell precursors and receive many of their signals for differentiation from lateral plate mesoderm (Fig. 11.3); those that remain in the paraXial mesoderm and do not cross the frontier (the remaining VLL cells and all of the DML cells) comprise the primaxial muscle cell precursors.

statement: Which nerve branches provide the motor innervation for the hypaxial muscles, including those of the body wall and limbs?
options: Ventral primary rami
Dorsal primary rami
Cranial nerves
Splanchnic nerves
Gray rami communicantes
correctOption: Ventral primary rami
optionExplanations: Ventral primary rami are the correct answer as they supply all hypaxial muscles. This includes the muscles of the limbs and the ventral and lateral body wall. This division occurs as the spinal nerve exits the intervertebral foramen and splits into two main branches.

Dorsal primary rami are incorrect because they are dedicated to the innervation of the epaxial muscles (the deep back muscles). They also provide sensory innervation to the skin of the back. They do not travel to the limbs or the ventral body wall.

Cranial nerves are incorrect because they innervate muscles of the head and neck derived from branchial arches or pre-otic somitomeres. They do not provide the segmental innervation for the body wall and limb muscles. Those are strictly the domain of spinal nerves.
